sessions.title: Structure-informed Uncertainty Quantification in Machine Learning

talks.title: Statistical Learning Guarantees for Group-Invariant Barron Functions
talks.abstract:
.
We study the generalization error of group-invariant neural networks within the Barron framework and show that incorporating group-invariant structure leads to an approximation rate modulated by a group-dependent factor $(\delta_{G,\Gamma,\sigma} \le 1)$. When this factor is small, group invariance yields substantial improvements in approximation accuracy. On the estimation side, we establish that the Rademacher complexity of the group-invariant function class is no larger than that of its non-invariant counterpart, implying that the estimation error is not adversely affected by the inclusion of symmetry. As a result, the overall generalization error can be significantly improved when learning target functions with inherent group symmetries. We further present illustrative examples demonstrating both favorable cases, where $(\delta_{G,\Gamma,\sigma} \approx \vert G\vert ^{-1})$, and unfavorable cases, where $(\delta_{G,\Gamma,\sigma} \approx 1)$. Overall, our results provide a rigorous theoretical foundation showing that encoding group-invariant structures in neural networks leads to clear statistical advantages for symmetric target functions.
Authors
Yahong Yang, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., yyang3194@gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Canonical Bayesian System Identification

speakers.name: Andrey Bryutkin
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Matthew Levine, Broad Institute, U.S.; Iñigo Urteaga, Basque Center for Applied Mathematics, Spain; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Standard Bayesian approaches for linear time-invariant (LTI) system identification are hindered by parameter non-identifiability; the resulting complex, multi-modal posteriors make inference inefficient and impractical. We solve this problem by embedding canonical forms of LTI systems within the Bayesian framework. We rigorously establish that inference in these minimal parameterizations fully captures all invariant system dynamics (e.g., transfer functions, eigenvalues, predictive distributions of system outputs) while resolving identifiability. This approach unlocks the use of meaningful, structure-aware priors (e.g., enforcing stability via eigenvalues) and ensures conditions for a Bernstein–von Mises theorem&#8212a link between Bayesian and frequentist large-sample asymptotics that is broken in standard forms. Extensive simulations with modern MCMC methods highlight advantages over standard parameterizations: canonical forms achieve higher computational efficiency, generate interpretable and well-behaved posteriors, and provide robust uncertainty estimates, particularly from limited data.
Authors
Andrey Bryutkin, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., bryutkin@mit.edu
Matthew Levine, Broad Institute, U.S., mattlevine22@gmail.com
Iñigo Urteaga, Basque Center for Applied Mathematics, Spain, iurteaga@bcamath.org
Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., ymarz@mit.edu

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Synchronization Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Synchronization problems—recovering group elements from noisy pairwise measurements—arise in computer vision, structural biology, and graph matching. A central challenge is not only to achieve accurate recovery but also to quantify uncertainty under severe corruption.
I will present cycle-consistency–based methods, in particular Cycle-Edge Message Passing (CEMP), which provide both robust recovery and principled corruption estimates. CEMP offers corruption estimation and recovery guarantees for rotations and, more generally, for compact groups. These ideas extend naturally to partial permutations and to translations (without distance information), with applications to multiple image matching and camera pose estimation. Together, these results yield simple yet rigorous uncertainty scores on edges—both provable and practically effective.
Authors
Yunpeng Shi, University of California, Davis, U.S., ypshi@ucdavis.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Data Augmentation: A Fourier Analysis Perspective
talks.abstract:
.
Data augmentation, which supplements a dataset with transformed copies of each datum according to a known symmetry group, provides a model-agnostic approach to enforcing invariances, in contrast to methods that encode symmetries directly into the model. Although data augmentation has proven effective in theory and practice, full group-sized augmentation is often computationally infeasible, prompting the question: Can sublinear augmentation still achieve the same performance as full augmentation in terms of generalization bounds and sample complexity? In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework based on Fourier analysis, showing that sublinear data augmentation can achieve the full statistical benefits achieved via full data augmentation. To our knowledge, this is the first proof of the efficacy of sublinear augmentation, highlighting an underexplored aspect of why augmentation remains a powerful and widely applicable strategy, even when performed only partially.
Authors
Melanie Weber, Harvard University, U.S., mweber@seas.harvard.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Probabilistic Manifold Learning and Deep Embeddings for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of III

talks.title: Probabilistic Learning on Manifolds with Double Diffusion Maps

speakers.name: Nikolaos Evangelou
speakers.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, U.S.; Roger Ghanem, University of Southern California, U.S.; Ioannis G. Kevrekidis , Johns Hopkins University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present a generative learning framework for probabilistic sampling based on an extension of the Probabilistic Learning on Manifolds (PLoM) approach, which is designed to generate statistically consistent realizations of a random vector in a finite-dimensional Euclidean space, informed by a limited (yet representative) set of observations. In its original form, PLoM constructs a reduced-order probabilistic model by combining three main components: (a) kernel density estimation to approximate the underlying probability measure, (b) Diffusion Maps to uncover the intrinsic low-dimensional manifold structure, and (c) a reduced-order Itô Stochastic Differential Equation (ISDE) to sample from the learned distribution. A key  challenge arises, however, when the number of available data points $N$ is small and the dimensionality of the diffusion-map basis approaches $N$, resulting in overfitting and loss of generalization. To overcome this limitation, we propose an enabling extension that implements  a synthesis of Double Diffusion Maps—a technique capable of capturing multiscale geometric features of the data—with Geometric Harmonics (GH), a nonparametric reconstruction method that allows smooth nonlinear interpolation in high-dimensional ambient spaces. This  approach enables us to solve a full-order ISDE directly in the latent space, preserving the full dynamical complexity of the system, while leveraging its reduced geometric representation.
Authors
Dimitris Giovanis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., dgiovan1@jhu.edu
Nikolaos Evangelou, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., nevange2@jh.edu.
Roger Ghanem, University of Southern California, U.S., ghanem@usc.edu
Ioannis G. Kevrekidis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., yannisk@jhu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Witnessing Discrepancies in Imaging Modalities
talks.abstract:
.
Inspection of integrated circuits (advanced packaging) to identify defects and adversarial objects relies on a subjective examination of complicated images generated by X-ray computed tomography (xCT). The measurement is both time-intensive and human-dependent. We explore a method of visualizing and automatically quantifying discrepancies in random distributions of shapes given boundary segmentations of microchip xCT imaging. By motivating rigid invariant feature spaces as dual evaluation functionals informing a natural decomposition of segmented curves, we explore a kernel-based metric for comparing distributions of Separable Shape Tensors (SST's) over a learned product submanifold. These formalisms facilitate a framework testing a hypothesis that “trusted” images and a “discovered” images contain shapes from common distributions of generalized, anisotropic scale and nonlinear undulation. If we reject this hypothesis, subsequent “witness functions” can then be used to visualize subsets of shapes that correspond to significant discrepancies to inform rapid inspection and quantify differences.
Authors
Zach Grey, National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S., zachary.grey@nist.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: On the Edge: Generative Model Assisted Sampling of Separtrices
talks.abstract:
.
A central challenge in modeling dynamical systems is the characterization of basins of attraction and the barriers, or separatrices, that divide them. Simulations in practice often become trapped in local minima over long timescales, and critical points of interest frequently lie along free energy barriers that are sparsely sampled. Reconstructing these separatrices is important not only for identifying tipping points of a system, but also for providing plausible data in regions of the phase space that are otherwise poorly populated. In parallel, advances in machine learning have introduced generative models that can learn to sample from complex target distributions and detect underlying low-dimensional structures. These models share conceptual parallels with physics-based enhanced sampling techniques, and can be used to sample densities from underlying manifolds present in systems of interest. In this work, we present a framework that integrates machine learning–based classification of basins of attraction with generative modeling to characterize, reconstruct, and visualize separatrices in dynamical systems, as well as quantify the uncertainty present in these steps.
Authors
Ellis R. Crabtree, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., ecrabtr2@jhu.edu
Dimitris Giovanis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., dgiovan1@jhu.edu
Ioannis G. Kevrekidis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., yannisk@jhu.edu

sessions.title: Gradient Flows for Uncertainty Quantification: New Algorithms and Applications - Part I of II

talks.title: Dynamical Sampling with Affine Invariant Gradient Flows and Beyond

speakers.name: Yifan Chen
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The framework of gradient flows has played an important role in generating efficient dynamics for sampling probability distributions. I will present recent work on affine invariant gradient flows and their parametric versions for addressing sampling problems in high dimensions that are often anisotropic. This includes a new affine invariant exact ensemble MCMC sampler that achieves improved dimensional scaling, and an efficient approximate sampler in the context of variational inference with applications in Bayesian inverse problems. I will also briefly discuss a new affine invariant Hamiltonian sampler beyond gradient flows with state-of-the-art dimensional scaling.
Authors
Yifan Chen, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S., yifanchen@math.ucla.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Weighted Quantization Using MMD: From Mean Field to Mean Shift Via Gradient Flows
talks.abstract:
.
Approximating a probability distribution using a set of particles is a fundamental problem in machine learning and statistics, with applications including clustering and quantization. Formally, we seek a weighted mixture of Dirac measures that best approximates the target distribution. While much existing work relies on the Wasserstein distance to quantify approximation errors, maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) has received comparatively less attention, especially when allowing for variable particle weights. We argue that a Wasserstein&#8211Fisher–Rao gradient flow is well-suited for designing quantizations optimal under MMD. We show that a system of interacting particles satisfying a set of ODEs discretizes this flow. We further derive a new fixed-point algorithm called mean shift interacting particles (MSIP). We show that MSIP extends the classical mean shift algorithm, widely used for identifying modes in kernel density estimators. Moreover, we show that MSIP can be interpreted as preconditioned gradient descent and that it acts as a relaxation of Lloyd's algorithm for clustering. Our unification of gradient flows, mean shift, and MMD-optimal quantization yields algorithms that are more robust than state-of-the-art methods, as demonstrated via high-dimensional and multi-modal numerical experiments.
Authors
Ayoub Belhadji, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., abelhadj@mit.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: HK Gradient Flows for Sampling: Two New Machine Learning Applications

speakers.name: Jia-Jie Zhu
speakers.affiliation: KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Sweden

talks.abstract:
.
This talk features two recent works on gradient flows for MCMC sampling in machine learning. The first part of this talk focuses on the global exponential decay of the entropy functionals along the unbalanced-transport gradient flows, a.k.a. Hellinger-Kantorovich (HK) or Wasserstein-Fisher-Rao. As an example, we will construct a new formal dissipation geometry, which we term the interaction-force transport, that guarantees convergence of minimizing the maximum-mean discrepancy. This then results in a new algorithm for sampling and generative modeling. In the second part, I will introduce a gradient flow formulation to enhance the robustness of learning and optimization algorithms under data distribution shifts. Previously, researchers have interpreted these problems as interacting gradient flows. In comparison, our new formulation unifies the analysis and motivates the design of new and flexible algorithms. In particular, I will show using entropy-regularized optimal transport for learning robustly under distribution shifts.
Authors
Jia-Jie Zhu, KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Sweden, zplusj@gmail.com
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Gaussian Particles Through Wasserstein Gradient Flow
talks.abstract:
.
Variational inference (VI) is a widely used framework in Bayesian analysis, where the posterior distribution is approximated within a chosen parametric family by minimizing a discrepancy measure—typically the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence. In this work, we introduce a novel regularization of the VI objective using the Wasserstein distance over the variational family. We show that this regularization allows the variational inference problem to be reformulated as a projected Wasserstein gradient flow, which admits closed-form solutions when the parametric familly is a Gaussian distribution. We further show how the problem can be lifted to a Gaussian particle representation, allowing for efficient approximation of multimodal posterior distributions.
Authors
Marc Lambert, Inria, France, marc.lambert@inria.fr
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Advances in Digital Twin Calibration and Validation

talks.title: Dynamic Parameter Calibration of Digital Twins Using Surrogate-Based Kalman Filter
talks.abstract:
.
Digital twins—virtual representations of physical systems that evolve alongside their real-world counterparts—are transforming engineering, manufacturing, energy, and healthcare. Digital twins complement physical assets by maintaining historical, current, and predicted states and behaviors. Unlike conventional simulations, digital twins are continuously updated using routinely collected sensor data to enable continuous monitoring, predictive maintenance, and informed decision-making. Their development often relies on physics-based first principles, with fidelity critically dependent on the accurate calibration of several parameters. We propose a dynamic calibration method that integrates a Kalman filter with a surrogate model, enabling real-time updates without costly simulator runs.
Authors
Eunshin Byon, University of Michigan, U.S., ebyon@umich.edu
Jaeshin Park, University of Michigan, U.S., jaeshin@umich.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Dynamic Calibration Framework for Digital Twins Using Active Learning and Conformal Prediction

speakers.name: Ozge Surer
speakers.affiliation: Miami University, U.S.; Xi Chen, Virginia Tech, U.S.; Sara Shashaani, North Carolina State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This work presents an adaptive framework for dynamically calibrating digital twins (DTs) in response to evolving real-world (RW) conditions. Traditional simulation-based models often rely on fixed parameter estimates, limiting their adaptability over time. To address this, our approach integrates active learning (AL) with a dynamic calibration mechanism that keeps the DT aligned with RW observations. At each time step, a new data point is received, and a conformal prediction-based monitoring system assesses whether recalibration is needed. When a change in the RW system state is detected, DT parameters are updated using an efficient AL strategy. The framework reduces computational overhead by avoiding unnecessary DT evaluations while maintaining accurate system representation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in achieving adaptive, cost-efficient DT calibration over time.
Authors
Ozge Surer, Miami University, U.S., surero@miamioh.edu
Xi Chen, Virginia Tech, U.S., xchen6@vt.edu
Sara Shashaani, North Carolina State University, U.S., sshasha2@ncsu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Validating a Stochastic Digital Twin with Multi-Epoch, Multi-Variate Data

speakers.name: Linyun He
speakers.affiliation: HEC Montréal, Canada; Luke Rhodes-Leader, Lancaster University, United Kingdom ; Eunhye Song , Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a nonparametric validation framework for a stochastic Digital Twin (DT) to verify if the distributions of the simulated and system-observed key performance indicators (KPIs) match. The system KPIs are non-stationary and non-identically distributed across epochs and only one realization is observed in each epoch rendering any validation method that requires independent and identically distributed data not applicable. We propose a hypothesis testing framework that consists of a set of marginal tests and one joint inter-correlation test. Each marginal test examines if the marginal distributions of the DT and system KPI within each epoch align in all observed epochs. For each epoch, the system KPI is converted via probability integral transform using the empirical marginal cumulative distribution function of the DT KPIs. The converted marginal KPIs from all epochs are then tested against a standard uniform distribution. If the marginal tests fail to reject, the joint test determines whether the inter-correlation of the DT and system KPIs, measured by their respective copula functions, match. We prove that our tests deliver a valid Type-I error upon appropriately controlling the simulation sample size with respect to the number of epochs. We further improve the finite-sample performance by adopting a step-down multiple hypothesis testing framework where a set of critical values are calculated using bootstrap.
Authors
Linyun He, HEC Montréal, Canada, linyun.he@hec.ca
Luke Rhodes-Leader, Lancaster University, United Kingdom , l.rhodes-leader@lancaster.ac.uk
Eunhye Song, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., eunhye.song@isye.gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Root-Finding for Fast Digital Twin Calibration
talks.abstract:
.
We pose the digital twin calibration problem as a simulation optimization where a sample-efficient stochastic metamodel is used to optimize for the best parameters. To accelerate the search, we propose to find the root of a signed discrepancy measure. We fit the metamodel on this signed metric and derive new acquisition functions and their gradients that empirically prove significant computational gains in a number of data-driven and physics-driven examples. We also analyze this framework when a root may not exist and provide error bounds for this approximate solution for calibration parameters.
Authors
Sara Shashaani, North Carolina State University, U.S., sshasha2@ncsu.edu
Yongseok Jeon, North Carolina State University, U.S., yjeon@ncsu.edu

talks.title: Active Learning for Nonlinear Computer Model Calibration
talks.abstract:
.
Combining computer models with real observations is a powerful approach for analyzing complex physical systems. One of the key challenges in model calibration is accounting for uncertainty arising from model inadequacy. The Kennedy–O’Hagan (KOH) framework provides a principled way to address this issue when computer model outputs are linearly related to physical observations. In this work, we extend the KOH framework by introducing a nonlinear wrapping function that jointly models the relationship between design inputs and computer model outputs. We develop the corresponding statistical inference procedures and propose active learning strategies that simultaneously guide the collection of physical and computer model data. These strategies exploit closed-form expressions for the posterior mean and variance under specific kernel structures, leading to efficient and adaptive experimental design.
Authors
Andrews Boahen, Michigan State University, U.S., boahenan@msu.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Data Assimilation in Complex Dynamical Systems - Part I of III

talks.title: Uncertainty Propagation in Intermittent Data Assimilation with Control-Based Importance Sampling
talks.abstract:
.
Intermittent data assimilation, whereby observations arrive at discrete-times, requires the propagation of a conditional distribution until the next observation occurrence. Incorrect or inaccurate approximation of the true initial distribution, modeling errors, stochastic forcing, and nonlinear dynamics combine to degrade the distribution approximation at the observation time. In this talk we focus on how to improve the uncertainty representation of the transition distribution in the finite sample case of particle filtering, and in light of the aforementioned difficulties. The central technique to achieve the improvement is the inclusion of a control-based technique to adjust the flow of the transition distribution, which can be understood as an active importance sampling strategy for the duration of the transition propagation and which provides samples from an optimal proposal. We discuss and illustrate with simple models the ability to counter the aforementioned difficulties and implicitly adjust for nonlinearities and account for model error.
Authors
Ryne Beeson, Princeton University, U.S., ryne@princeton.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Reduced Representations of Empirical Probability Distributions

speakers.name: David M. Rogers
speakers.affiliation: Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S.; Zezhong Zhang, Auburn University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce a methodology for the reconstruction of empirical distributions from reduced representations of the same. The application we have in mind is in transmitting massive data sets when these consist of samples from high dimensional distributions, and our goal is to make the transmission process more  efficient by requiring less data be used in the reconstruction. The strategy exploits the property of Bayesian inference and stochastic interpolation. It is assumed that the receiver of the reduced data set will have the requisite recovery map, which we formulate and compute. This talk will present the general methodology and encouraging preliminary results that   Indicate that the strategy is viable and can be made practical on sizable, high dimensional distributions.
Authors
Juan M. Restrepo, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., juan.m.restrepo@gmail.com
Jorge Ramirez, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., ramirezosojm@orn.gov
David M. Rogers, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., rogersdm@ornl.gov
Zezhong Zhang, Auburn University, U.S., zez0002@auburn.edu

talks.title: Quantum Mechanical Closure of Partial Differential Equations with Symmetries
talks.abstract:
.
We develop a framework for the dynamical closure of spatiotemporal dynamics governed by partial differential equations. Employing the mathematical framework of quantum mechanics, we use the space of quantum states to model the unresolved degrees of freedom of the original dynamics and the method of quantum measurement to predict their contributions to the resolved dynamics. The embedded dynamics is projected to finite dimension by a positivity preserving discretization process. Relying on kernel methods and time delay embedding, the compressed finite dimensional representation of the dynamics is invariant under the spatial symmetries of the original dynamics. We apply the data driven formulation of our scheme to closure problems for the shallow water and Kuramoto-Sivashinsky equations, demonstrating that it can accurately predict the main features of the true dynamics for out of sample initial conditions.
Authors
Chris Vales, Dartmouth College, U.S., chris.vales@dartmouth.edu
David Freeman, Dartmouth College, U.S., david.c.freeman.gr@dartmouth.edu
Joanna M. Slawinska, Dartmouth College, U.S., joanna.m.slawinska@dartmouth.edu
Dimitrios Giannakis, Dartmouth College, U.S., Dimitrios.Giannakis@dartmouth.edu

talks.title: Bridging the Model Hierarchy: A Physics-Guided Machine Learning Framework for High-Resolution Climate Simulation Enhancement

speakers.name: Charlotte Moser
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Sibo Cheng, École des Ponts ParisTech, France; Nan Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Operational models are high-resolution and contain many crucial variables. Despite numerous successes, biases persist in most of these models, particularly in identifying extreme events and accurately reproducing statistics. However, it is challenging to directly modify these models to improve their performance. Conceptual and intermediate coupled models accurately characterize certain features of nature. Yet, these coarse-grained models contain only a subset of variables within a specific domain. By leveraging the strengths of models of varying complexities, we develop a robust physics-driven machine learning modeling framework that bridges the model hierarchy through effective latent space data assimilation. The resulting model not only inherits operational model benefits, including high resolution and a comprehensive set of variables, but also obtains globally enhanced the accuracy through local dynamical and statistical improvements from simpler models. The latent space technique identifies the dominant nonlinear features of the underlying dynamics, which facilitates effective communication between models. Finally, the machine learning representation of the model significantly enhances simulation efficiency, providing high-quality datasets and advancing the quantification of uncertainty in extreme events. The framework has been applied to improve the performance of CMIP6 models in characterizing El Niño complexity by utilizing simpler yet statistically accurate models.
Authors
Pouria Behnoudfar, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., p.behnoud@gmail.com
Charlotte Moser, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., crmoser2@wisc.edu
Sibo Cheng, École des Ponts ParisTech, France, sibo.cheng@enpc.fr
Nan Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., chennan@math.wisc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Theory and Simulation of Failure Probabilities and Rare Events - Part I of II

talks.title: Mathematical Analysis of Variance-Based Reliability Sensitivity Indices
talks.abstract:
.
In this work we analyze the sensitivity of indicator random variables associated with a failure event that occurs with a small probability. This is a classical problem in engineering risk and reliability analysis. For the sensitivity analysis we use the well known Sobol' indices defined in terms of the variance decomposition of the failure indicator random variable. We derive tight two-sided bounds for the closed and total  Sobol' indices of affine linear limit-state functions and standard Gaussian inputs. In addition we show that the closed and total Sobol' indices approach either zero or one in the limit of the failure probability going to zero. This proves rigorously an empirical observation in previous studies. Moreover, we study second-order approximations of reliability sensitivity indices and show their asymptotic behavior under certain conditions. We illustrate the theoretical results by a simple test problem with an affine linear limit-state function. Moreover, we consider a practical engineering test problem with a nonlinear limit-state function.
Authors
Elisabeth Ullmann, Technische Universität München, Germany, elisabeth.ullmann@tum.de
Iason Papaioannou, Technische Universität München, Germany, iason.papaioannou@tum.de
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Failure Probability Estimation Using Physics-Based Surrogate Models

speakers.name: Zhouzhou Song
speakers.affiliation: Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany; Chao Dang, Technical University of Dortmund, Germany; Marcos Valdebenito

talks.abstract:
.
It is important to evaluate the reliability of structural dynamical systems under various uncertainties. Surrogate models are widely employed to enable efficient reliability analysis in complex, computationally intensive engineering problems. However, building high-accuracy surrogate models for dynamic reliability estimation with limited computational resources remains a significant challenge. This work presents a novel active learning-based surrogate modeling method for the efficient estimation of first-passage failure probabilities of stochastic structural dynamical systems. In this approach, Kriging surrogate models are constructed in a latent functional space obtained through functional dimension reduction. By leveraging the prediction uncertainty and the concept of misclassification probability, a new learning function is then developed to guide the selection of the best next sample for model enhancement. Furthermore, an error-based stopping criterion is proposed to judge the convergence of the active learning process. Through numerical examples of varying dimensionality and complexity, it is shown that the proposed method is efficient and accurate for dynamic reliability analysis.
Authors
Zhouzhou Song, Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany, zhouzhou.song@tu-dortmund.de
Chao Dang, Technical University of Dortmund, Germany, chao.dang@tu-dortmund.de
Marcos Valdebenito, Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany, mailto:marcos.valdebenito@tu-dortmund.de
Matthias Faes, Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany, matthias.faes@tu-dortmund.de

talks.title: Active Learning-based Surrogate Modeling Method for Efficient Reliability Analysis of Stochastic Structural Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
It is important to evaluate the reliability of structural dynamical systems under various uncertainties. Surrogate models are widely employed to enable efficient reliability analysis in complex, computationally intensive engineering problems. However, building high-accuracy surrogate models for dynamic reliability estimation with limited computational resources remains a significant challenge. This work presents a novel active learning-based surrogate modeling method for the efficient estimation of first-passage failure probabilities of stochastic structural dynamical systems. In this approach, Kriging surrogate models are constructed in a latent functional space obtained through functional dimension reduction. By leveraging the prediction uncertainty and the concept of misclassification probability, a new learning function is then developed to guide the selection of the best next sample for model enhancement. Furthermore, an error-based stopping criterion is proposed to judge the convergence of the active learning process. Through numerical examples of varying dimensionality and complexity, it is shown that the proposed method is efficient and accurate for dynamic reliability analysis.

talks.title: Intrepid MCMC Sampler for Structural Reliability Estimation Via Subset Simulation
talks.abstract:
.
Monte Carlo simulation augmented with sampling variance reduction schemes are among the most widely adopted techniques in computational reliability (failure probability) estimation. When limit state functions (LSFs) possess geometric features such as (a) discontinuities or sharp nonlinearities, or (b) several, possibly disconnected regions of failure, even advanced variance reduction techniques, such as the subset simulation method, may fail to deliver accurate estimates of failure probability. In such cases, the underlying Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler may fail to detect and adequately sample from all modes of the conditional pdf that contribute to the probability of failure. This study introduces an improved MCMC sampler, termed Intrepid MCMC, which explores the parameter space more effectively, leading to improved sampling from important failure regions. The key idea is to utilize two Markov transition pdfs, (a) one focusing on sampling from the mode of the conditional pdf in which the Markov chain currently resides, and (b) another dedicated to exploring the parameter space in search of previously undetected modes. These transition pdfs are combined to yield a composite transition pdf which leads to improved sampling from the target conditional pdf. Intrepid MCMC has previously shown to significantly outperform conventional random walk Metropolis methods for multimodal distributions, and we now demonstrate its effectiveness for complex reliability problems.

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for PDEs under Uncertainty: Propagation, Inference, Optimal Control, and Optimization - Part I of III

talks.title: Multilevel Methods for Optimal Control of Elliptic Equations with Stochastic Coefficients Discretized Using Stochastic Collocation

speakers.name: Andrei Draganescu
speakers.affiliation: University of Maryland, Baltimore County, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this work, we extend multilevel methods - originally developed for linear-quadratic distributed optimal control of elliptic equations - to the problem of identifying a stochastic diffusivity model in heat transfer from state measurements. Using synthetic experiments with applied heat sources and multiple measurements of temperature and heat flux, our goal is to recover the diffusivity tensor. For this study, the tensor is assumed isotropic and represented by a truncated Karhunen–Loève expansion. Because the problem is ill-posed, we formulate it as a regularized least-squares optimization, which is nonlinear and non-convex. To solve it, we use a hierarchy of geometric grids and multilevel techniques to precondition the Hessian in the Newton–CG process.

Andrei Draganescu, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, U.S., draga@umbc.edu

talks.title: A Low-Rank Monolithic Stochastic Galerkin Solver For The Unsteady Navier-Stokes Equations

speakers.name: Ahmet Kaan Aydin
speakers.affiliation: University of Maryland, Baltimore County, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We develop a low-rank iterative solver for the stochastic unsteady Navier-Stokes equations with incompressible flow and uncertain viscosity. The problem is discretized using a stochastic Galerkin method and formulated in an all-at-once (monolithic) framework, where the systems across all-time steps are solved simultaneously. Nonlinearity is handled via Picard linearization, and low-rank tensor-train representations are employed to reduce memory and computational costs. To control the rapid rank growth induced by the convection operator, we approximate its CANDECOMP/PARAFAC (CP) decomposition using an alternating least-squares (ALS) procedure. This enables the construction of structure-preserving preconditioners that more effectively capture both stochastic and nonlinear dynamics compared to traditional mean-based approaches.
Authors
Ahmet Kaan Aydin, University of Maryland, Baltimore County, U.S., aaydin1@umbc.edu

talks.title: Nonlinear Reduction Model for Solving Parameterized PDE Problems Using Machine Learning Techniques
talks.abstract:
.
In many engineering systems, solving parameterized partial differential equations (PDEs) over many parameter values is inevitable. Reduced order modelling (ROM) replaces the full-order model by a model with smaller dimension. Linear ROMs, e.g., reduced basis methods often perform poorly when the Kolmogorov n-width of a solution manifold decays slowly. To address this, we propose a new method that consists of two stages: first, finding a low-dimensional space and second, use a feedforward neural network to map this space to high-fidelity solutions. Thus, our surrogate model uses two nonlinear mappings. The first mapping is parameter-independent and maps from the reduced basis space to a low-dimensional space. The second mapping is parameter-dependent and maps the low-dimensional space to the solution space. We discuss the above solution strategy and apply it to some parameterized PDEs for which linear ROMs do not perform well. In the first test, a 1D Burgers' equation with solutions exhibit strong oscillations provides a difficult example. The second test is a 2D lid-driven cavity problem over a high dimensional parameter domain. Results show that our approach improves the accuracy compared to linear ROMs. Furthermore, the nonlinear mapping that constructs a low-dimensional representation also improves the training efficiency in the second stage. These advantages make our strategy suitable for fast simulations of parameterized PDEs requiring multiple solves.

talks.title: Stochastic Galerkin Solvers for Linear Stability Analysis under Parametric Uncertainty: A Surrogate-Learning Outlook

speakers.name: Ahmet Kaan Aydin
speakers.affiliation: University of Maryland, Baltimore County, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
We study linear stability under parametric uncertainty in a stochastic Galerkin framework. Our model problem is the Navier–Stokes equations with stochastic viscosity. Linearization about a steady state yields a nonsymmetric stochastic eigenvalue problem whose key quantity is the rightmost eigenvalue, possibly a complex conjugate pair. We discuss the stochastic Galerkin formulation and its solution by an inexact Newton method with preconditioned GMRES, together with preconditioning strategies for the linear systems arising in the nonlinear iteration. Numerical experiments show the accuracy of the stochastic Galerkin approach relative to Monte Carlo and stochastic collocation and demonstrate the effectiveness of the solver. We close with brief remarks on surrogate-based approximations of stability indicators.

sessions.title: Structure-preserving Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of III

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Uncertainty Quantification of Dynamical Systems Using Structure-Preserving Operator-Inference Neural Networks
talks.abstract:
.
We present an approach to operator inference using neural network (NN) models and demonstrate it in the context of multi-fidelity uncertainty quantification (UQ). We infer a composite NN operator comprised of a series of structure-preserving terms — e.g., matrices with symmetry and definiteness properties — from state observation data. As opposed to classic operator inference approaches, the use of an NN model class enables representation of generic non-linearities at the expense of non-convex inference. We demonstrate that this approach provides superior accuracy over several existing NN-based inference procedures and demonstrate its ability to reduce variance in a forward-propagation UQ problem with uncertain parameters.

talks.title: Entropy Stable Reduced Order Modeling of Nonlinear Conservations Laws Using Entropy Correction Artificial Viscosity
talks.abstract:
.
Reduced order models (ROMs) are inexpensive surrogate models that reduce costs associated with many-query scenarios, but face challenges of robustness and stability when applied to nonlinear conservation laws. These can be addressed by constructing ROMs which are "entropy stable"; however, existing constructions rely on relatively invasive and expensive flux differencing formulations. In this work, we propose an alternative method of constructing an entropy stable ROM based on "entropy correction artificial viscosity" (ECAV), which adds a minimally dissipative correction to an arbitrary ROM to enforce a semi-discrete entropy inequality. We also propose a conservative hyper-reduction technique for finite volume FOMs, which, when combined with ECAV, results in a conservative and entropy stable ROM.

Ray Qu, Rice University, U.S., rq10@rice.edu

talks.title: Preconditioning for Adjoint Sensitivity Analysis with Application to Optimization of Radiation Transport

speakers.name: Brian K. Tran
speakers.affiliation: University of Colorado Boulder, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Achieving robust control and optimization in high-fidelity physics simulations is extremely challenging, especially for evolutionary systems whose solutions span vast scales across space, time, and physical variables. In conjunction with gradient-based methods, adjoint systems are widely used in the optimization of systems subject to differential equation constraints. In optimization, gradient-based methods are often transformed using suitable preconditioners to stabilize and accelerate the convergence of the optimization algorithm. Inspired by preconditioned gradient descent methods, we introduce a framework for the preconditioning of adjoint systems associated to evolution equations, which allows one to reshape the dynamics of the adjoint system. Using techniques from symplectic geometry and Hamiltonian mechanics, we show that these preconditioned adjoint systems preserve the property that the adjoint system backpropagates the derivative of an objective function. We then apply this framework to the setting of coupled evolution equations, where we develop a notion of scale preconditioning of the adjoint equations when the state dynamics exhibit large scale-separation. We demonstrate the proposed scale preconditioning on an inverse problem for the radiation diffusion equations.

talks.title: Rare Event Estimation Using Tensor Trains and Large Deviation Theory

speakers.name: Alex De Beer
speakers.affiliation: University of Sydney, Australia; Karina Koval, Universität Heidelberg, Germany; Tiangang Cui, University of Sydney, Australia

talks.abstract:
.
Recently, algorithms for approximating and sampling from arbitrary multivariate probability densities using low-rank tensor-based surrogates have been proposed. In particular, the deep inverse Rosenblatt transport (DIRT) algorithm, which uses a layered composition of functional tensor train approximations, has found success in a range of uncertainty quantification tasks. In this work, we propose some practical strategies to accelerate the application of the DIRT algorithm to rare event estimation problems. When the uncertainty in the system of interest is characterised by a complex distribution without a known analytical form (e.g., a Bayesian posterior), we use the DIRT algorithm to construct a coupling between this distribution and the standard Gaussian distribution. This allows us to apply ideas from large deviation theory to design preconditioning techniques to efficiently construct a DIRT approximation to the optimal importance sampling distribution for the rare event probability. We illustrate our methodology using several numerical examples.

talks.title: Low-Rank Approximations for Large-Scale Nonlinear Feedback Control

speakers.name: Sergey Dolgov
speakers.affiliation: University of Bath, United Kingdom; Dante Kalise, Imperial College London, United Kingdom; Luca Saluzzi, University of Rome I, Italy

talks.abstract:
.
Computation of the optimal feedback law for general (nonlinear/unstable/stochastic) dynamical systems requires solving the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Partial Differential Equation (PDE), which suffers from the curse of dimensionality.
We develop a unified framework for computing a fast surrogate model of the feedback control function based on low-rank decompositions of matrices and tensors.
Firstly, we propose a Statistical Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (SPOD) for Model Order Reduction of very high-dimensional systems, such as the discretized Navier-Stokes equation or other PDEs, by compressing snapshots corresponding to random samples of all parameters in the system, initial condition and time.
Secondly, we compute a low-rank Functional Tensor Train (TT) approximation of the feedback control function for the reduced model.
Thus pre-trained TT representation of the control function of the reduced state can be used for real-time online generation of the control signal.
Using the proposed SPOD and TT approximations, we demonstrate a controller computable in milliseconds that achieves lower vorticity of the Navier-Stokes flow with random inflow compared to using the mean inflow to produce reduced bases or controllers.

sessions.title: Innovations in Practical OED: Recent Advances in Methods and Applications - Part I of III

talks.title: Optimal Design of Bayesian Inverse Problems with Mobile Sensor Paths

speakers.name: John N. Neuberger
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S.; Bart Waanders, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Alen Alexanderian, North Carolina State University, U.S.; Ahmed Attia, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We study optimal experimental design (OED) for inverse problems governed by PDEs, where the inversion parameter lies in an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space. In our framework, we parameterize sensor trajectories and treat the path parameter as the experimental design. This yields a general formulation compatible with standard OED criteria, including the c-optimality criterion. The problem is discretized with finite elements and solved with gradient-based methods. Numerical experiments on an advection–diffusion equation demonstrate the approach: we solve OED problems with two different path parameterizations and show large improvements over random or user-selected paths. Our framework is general and extensible to mobile sensing in real applications, with relevance to wildfire prediction and control.

talks.title: Novel Optimal Criteria in Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Tapio Helin
speakers.affiliation: Lappeenranta University of Technology, Finland; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Rodrigo Rojo-Garcia, Lappeenranta University of Technology, Finland

talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian optimal experimental design (OED) provides a principled framework for choosing experimental settings that maximize the informativeness of collected data. Traditional Bayesian OED has relied heavily on the expected information gain (EIG), based on Kullback–Leibler divergence, or variance-reduction criteria such as A-optimality. In this talk, we introduce a new design criterion grounded in optimal transport: the expected Wasserstein distance between prior and posterior distributions. This criterion inherits the fully Bayesian character of EIG while offering geometric and stability advantages. For Gaussian regression models, the Wasserstein-2 criterion yields explicit formulas akin to classical optimality measures, making it computationally tractable. A central contribution of our work is a stability analysis of the Wasserstein-1 criterion, demonstrating robustness under perturbations of the prior and likelihood and enabling rigorous error control when empirical priors are used.

talks.title: Sequential Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design in Infinite Dimensions via Policy Gradient Reinforcement Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Sequential Bayesian optimal experimental design (SBOED) for PDE-governed inverse problems is computationally demanding for infinite-dimensional random field parameters, due to repeated forward and adjoint solves within nested Bayesian inversion. We formulate SBOED as a finite-horizon Markov decision process and learn an amortized design policy using policy-gradient reinforcement learning (PGRL), enabling online design selection without repeatedly solving optimization problems. We combine active subspace projection for parameters and principal component analysis for states with a derivative-informed latent attention neural operator (LANO) surrogate that predicts both the parameter-to-solution map and its Jacobian. We adopt a Laplace-based D-optimality reward and introduce an eigenvalue-based evaluation strategy that uses prior samples as proxies for MAP points. Numerical experiments on sequential multi-sensor placement for contaminant source tracking show approximately $100\times$ speedup over high-fidelity finite element methods, improved performance over random placement, and interpretable “upstream” tracking policies.

talks.title: Bayesian Experimental Design for Prediction Using Transport Approaches

speakers.name: Karina Koval
speakers.affiliation: Universität Heidelberg, Germany; Tiangang Cui, University of Sydney, Australia

talks.abstract:
.
We consider Bayesian optimal experimental design (BOED) with a focus on maximizing expected information gain (EIG), a task that is often computationally challenging due to the dependence of the EIG on intractable densities. In many settings, samples from the joint distribution of parameters and data are available, and these can be used to construct transport-map-based approximations to intractable densities (e.g., posteriors, evidence, or priors) by minimizing a suitable loss function. We present a sample-based transport approach for approximating the EIG in such settings. The methodology is illustrated through numerical examples.

sessions.title: Inverse Problems and Uncertainty Quantification in Biological Systems

talks.title: Multilevel Bayesian Modeling of Local Growth Probabilities in Intracranial Aneurysms

speakers.name: Kostiantyn kondratiuk
speakers.affiliation: Purdue University, U.S.; David Saloner, University of California, San Francisco, U.S.; Vitaliy L Rayz, Ilias Bilionis

talks.abstract:
.
Cerebral aneurysms cause nearly half million deaths annually worldwide, yet rupture risk assessment remains subjective. Monitoring relies on manual 2D MRA annotations, inconsistent across operators and insensitive to subtle 3D changes. We propose a Bayesian framework that quantifies growth from longitudinal 3D segmentations of aneurysm and vessel. Vessel walls are assumed rigid with zero-mean displacement over time, and their distribution captures uncertainty from resolution, segmentation, and registration. At the global level, aneurysm displacement distribution are compared against the vessel distribution using Wasserstein distance. The distance is then modeled with a Bayesian soft threshold, turning noisy measurements into global growth probabilities with posterior uncertainty. This yields interpretable cut-offs, slopes, and error terms that update as data accumulate. At the patient level, we extend growth probabilities locally to surface vertices. Rigid vessel vertices define the uncertainty model, while aneurysm vertices follow patient-specific log-normal growth distributions. The global probability acts as a prior for each vertex, producing maps that highlight high-risk regions. These maps guide borderline cases, reveal growth hotspots, and link with hemodynamic metrics for biomarker discovery. By combining global thresholds with local maps, our multilevel Bayesian model delivers risk scores and surgical guidance, reducing subjectivity and improving confidence.

talks.title: Fokker-Planck-Inverse Reinforcement Learning: a Physics-Constrained Approach to Markov Decision Process Models of Cell Dynamics

speakers.name: Chengyang Huang
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Siddhartha Srivastava, Auburn University, U.S.; Kenneth Ho, Katherine Luker, Gary Luker

speakers.name: Xun Huan
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Krishna Garikipati , University of Southern California, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) seeks to estimate the unknown reward function of a Markov Decision Process (MDP) from observed agent trajectories. IRL needs a transition function, and most algorithms assume it is known or can be estimated from data. It therefore becomes challenging when such transition dynamics is not known a-priori, since it enters the estimation of the policy. When the dynamics of agents in the state-action space is described by stochastic differential equations (SDE) in It\^{o} calculus, these transitions can be inferred from the associated Fokker-Planck (FP) equation. We conjecture an equivalence between the time-discrete FP and MDP. We identify specific manifestations of this equivalence and use them to create a novel physics-aware IRL algorithm, FP-IRL, which can simultaneously infer the transition and reward functions using only observed trajectories.  We employ PDE inference for the potential function in FP, which consequently allows the evaluation of reward, transition, and policy by leveraging the conjecture. We demonstrate the effectiveness of FP-IRL by applying it to a synthetic benchmark and a biological problem of cancer cell dynamics, where the transition function is inaccessible.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Complex Engineering Systems with Machine Learning

talks.title: Fourier Neural Operator-based Emulators for Coupling Engineering and Environmental System Simulations: Evaluating the Environmental Impact of Advanced Nuclear Reactor Spent Fuel
talks.abstract:
.
Tackling environmental challenges requires the understanding and models in both engineering systems – in which waste and toxic substances are created – and environmental systems – in which contaminants can migrate and affect the public health. The integrated analysis of both engineering and environmental systems is critical for quantifying the actual environmental impact and health consequences. At the same time, the integration allows us to identify the engineering and design parameters and other factors that can potentially reduce and minimize the environmental impact.   In this study, we develop an engineering/environmental system integration framework through deep-learning (DL)-based emulators, particularly using the Fourier Neural Operators (FNO). Each model and emulator are developed separately for an engineering system and an environmental assessment model, and then fast running emulators enable the rapid integration. We demonstrate our approach to evaluate multiple advanced nuclear reactors from the environmental impact of their spent nuclear fuel (SNF). This integrated system analysis will quantify the impact of different reactor/fuel designs on the waste repository performance and potential health risk as well as optimize reactor/processing designs for minimizing the environmental impact.
Authors
Haruko M. Wainwright, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., hmwainw@mit.edu

talks.title: Kernel Density Estimation Adaptive Importance Sampling with Gaussian Processes for Rare-Events Probability Estimation

speakers.name: Ashwin Renganathan
speakers.affiliation: Pennsylvania State University, U.S.; Annie Booth, Virginia Tech, U.S.

talks.abstract:
Abstract.Estimating rare–event probabilities for expensive black–box models is notoriously difficult because failures are scarce, structured, and a priori unknown. We present an adaptive importance sampling strategy that learns a proposal nonparametrically from data via a kernel density surrogate, allowing it to track multi–modal and anisotropic failure regions without committing to a parametric family. The method interfaces with user–defined input laws and simple domain constraints, and it is designed to be plug–and–play within existing UQ workflows. From a theoretical standpoint, we show that the resulting estimator is unbiased at every iteration and consistent as the design adapts. Under mild regularity conditions, we establish a central limit theorem with an explicit asymptotic variance that contracts toward the oracle variance associated with the ideal failure–tilted density. We also provide finite–sample diagnostics and stability guarantees that prevent weight degeneracy during adaptation. Across reliability benchmarks, the approach delivers substantial variance reduction relative to static IS while maintaining conceptual and implementational simplicity.

Authors
  • Ashwin Renganathan, Pennsylvania State University, U.S., ashwin.renganathan@psu.edu
  • Annie Booth, Virginia Tech, U.S., annie_booth@vt.edu

talks.title: Quantifying Unparameterized Spatial Uncertainties in Deep Geologic Repositories for Nuclear Waste

speakers.name: Dusty Brooks
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Laura P. Swiler, Retired (Sandia National Laboratories), U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Spatially heterogeneous uncertainties are prevalent in geophysical modeling applications, such as probabilistic post-closure performance assessment (PA) of deep geologic repositories for nuclear waste. Such uncertainties are often highly influential to model outputs, so it is desirable to identify the most important mechanisms by which they influence model predictions. However, these uncertainties are often unparameterized in the sense that there is no set of parameters that can be specified to yield a particular realization of the uncertainty. Additionally, the uncertainty is not intrinsically endowed with a parameterization that captures a realization’s mechanistic influence on model outputs. Therefore, in this talk we present a novel methodology to develop and assess a set of proxy variables that aim to represent this influence. We show how they can be computed, downselected, and incorporated into the construction of statistical surrogate models mapping model inputs to outputs. We present our methodology in the context of a motivating application problem in deep geologic repository PA and discuss the challenges in capturing the effects of these spatial heterogeneities in uncertainty analyses. SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525

talks.title: Scalable Gaussian Processes with Low-rank Deep Kernel Decomposition
talks.abstract:
.
Kernels are key to encoding prior beliefs and data structures in Gaussian process (GP) models. The design of expressive and scalable kernels has garnered significant research attention. Deep kernel learning enhances kernel flexibility by feeding inputs through a neural network before applying a standard parametric form. However, this approach remains limited by the choice of base kernels, inherits high inference costs, and often demands sparse approximations. Drawing on Mercer's theorem, we introduce a fully data-driven, scalable deep kernel representation where a neural network directly represents a low-rank kernel through a small set of basis functions. This construction enables highly efficient exact GP inference in linear time and memory without invoking inducing points. It also supports scalable mini-batch training based on a principled variational inference framework. We further propose a simple variance correction procedure to guard against overconfidence in uncertainty estimates. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate the advantages of our deep kernel GP in terms of predictive accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and computational efficiency.
Authors
Yao Xie, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., yao.xie@isye.gatech.edu

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for UQ in Earth Science - Part I of III

talks.title: Randomized Blocky Occam for Generating 2D Resistivity Models with Sharp Contrasts from Electromagnetic Data

speakers.name: Eliana Vargas Huitzil
speakers.affiliation: Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S.; Matthias Morzfeld

speakers.name: Steven Constable
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Geological boundaries such as sedimentary-volcanic contacts, groundwater tables, magmatic bodies, and faults produce sharp resistivity contrasts critical for subsurface characterization. Standard electromagnetic (EM) inversion methods generate smooth models that blur these features. Existing approaches for generating sharp resistivity contrasts are computationally expensive and difficult to use.
We present Blocky Occam, a new inversion method that enforces sharp resistivity contrasts using total variation (TV) regularization. TV regularization has been applied successfully in compressed sensing and image deblurring, and we show how to use it efficiently in EM inversion. We integrate TV regularization within the established Occam’s inversion:  Linearize the model, solve a sequence of linear TV regularized problems efficiently via split Bregman to determine an appropriate regularization, and iterate this process until convergence.
Randomized blocky Occam (RamBO) adapts the randomize-then-optimize (RTO) for uncertainty quantification using edge-preserving Laplace priors.
We demonstrate the robust efficiency of blocky Occam and RamBO on synthetic and real (field data) 2D EM inverse problems. Our results indicate that RamBO improves the resolution of geological boundaries and produces reliable uncertainties. The algorithm is computationally efficient, parallelizable, and enables practical application of blocky inversions to complex geological settings.

Eliana Vargas Huitzil, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S., evargashuitzil@ucsd.edu
Matthias Morzfeld, University of California, San Diego, U.S., mmorzfeld@ucsd.edu

talks.title: Accelerating Uncertainty Quantification of Magnetotelluric Data Using Neural Operators
talks.abstract:
.
The magnetotelluric (MT) method is a passive source geophysical method to image the conductivity distribution of the subsurface. This is achieved by measuring the natural electric field and the magnetic field at the earth's surface and then obtaining the transfer function between the two measurements. The imaging problem involves obtaining the conductivity distribution that can reproduce the transfer functions. It is an ill-posed and underdetermined system solved with the aid of a regularizer.
Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods have been used widely to quantify the uncertainties in the model estimates obtained from the inverse problem. This requires solving the governing differential equations multiple times. The computation, usually done through traditional numerical methods is expensive, limiting their usage to 1D. Neural operators can be used to approximate the differential equation operator, in our case the operator mapping the conductivity distribution to the electric and the magnetic field.
Since the regularizer informs the
a priori
distribution, usually one consisting of smooth models, limiting ourselves to this family guarantees the accuracy of learned surrogates while providing interpretable subsurface features. We validate our results against the ones obtained using MCMC and show that the obtained surrogate, being orders of magnitude faster than the traditional methods, enables us to perform uncertainty quantification efficiently.

talks.title: Earthquake Forecasting with Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Jean Philippe Avouac
speakers.affiliation: California Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Earthquake forecasting is critical for hazard assessment but remains extremely challenging due to unobservable state variables, sparse and low-resolution observations, and the multiscale dynamics of the earthquake cycle. To address these difficulties, we develop machine-learned reduced-order models (ROMs) of physics-based earthquake simulations and use them within ensemble-based data assimilation. With the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), we estimate the evolving system states from partial observations, enabling probabilistic forecasts of the timing and spatial distribution of future events together with uncertainty quantification. In a related direction, we apply Ensemble Kalman Inversion (EnKI) to infer uncertain physical parameters from sparse paleoseismic records, obtaining both parameter estimates and their uncertainties. These efforts illustrate how reduced-order modeling, data assimilation, and inversion methods can tackle the dual challenges of forecasting earthquake events and constraining the governing physical models.

Hojjat Kaveh, California Institute of Technology, U.S., hkaveh@caltech.edu

sessions.title: Bridging Theory and Machine Learning for Improved Bayesian Filtering - Part I of II

talks.title: A Bayesian Characterization of Ensemble Kalman Updates
talks.abstract:
.
The Ensemble Kalman Update (EnKU), an update in the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF), is widely used for Bayesian inference in inverse problems and data assimilation. At each step, it solves a likelihood-free Bayesian inversion problem using an ensemble of particles drawn from a joint measure and a single observation. The empirical posterior is formed by transporting the particles through a linear map given by the Kalman gain. Although EnKU is exact for Gaussian joints in the mean-field, exactness alone does not determine a unique update. Many affine maps send a Gaussian joint to the same posterior, so which affine map should we use? We give a new characterization of EnKU among all such maps. First, we identify the set of laws for which EnKU yields exact conditioning and show that it is much larger than the Gaussian family. Next, we prove that, except for a small class of highly symmetric distributions (including Gaussians), EnKU is the unique exact affine conditioning map. Finally, we ask for the largest possible collection of measures for which any measure-dependent affine transport could be exact. We show that EnKU’s exactness set is nearly maximal, differing only by a small symmetry class. Thus, among affine transports, EnKU is near-optimal for exact distributional conditioning beyond the Gaussian case, and it remains uniquely exact except for a narrow subclass of highly symmetric distributions.
Authors
Frederic Jorgensen, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., fjorgen@mit.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Long-Time Accuracy of Ensemble Kalman Filters with Machine-Learned Surrogate Models
talks.abstract:
.
The first part of this talk will study the long-time accuracy of ensemble Kalman filters. We introduce conditions on the dynamics and the observations under which the state estimation error remains small in the long-time horizon. Our theory covers a wide class of dissipative chaotic dynamical systems, which includes the Navier-Stokes equations and Lorenz models. In addition, we prove long-time accuracy of ensemble Kalman filters with surrogate dynamics, thus validating the use of machine-learned forecast models in ensemble data assimilation. The second part of this talk concerns the sample complexity of learning surrogate models for evolution equations with Fourier neural operators, motivated by the use of such surrogates in data assimilation tasks. Our results demonstrate that the sample complexity of learning a broad class of evolution equations is polynomial, not exponential, as is the case for generic Lipschitz operators. Further, our theory captures how the difficulty of learning depends on the structure of the PDE, including the degree of nonlinearity, the order of spatial derivatives, and the smoothness of solutions. This result helps to understand the success of operator learning techniques for learning PDE’s and provides theoretical foundations for the use of neural-network surrogate models in filtering algorithms.
Authors
Nathan Waniorek, University of Chicago, U.S., waniorek@uchicago.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Ensemble Kalman Updates: a Non-Asymptotic Approach
talks.abstract:
.
Filtering is concerned with online estimation of the state of a dynamical system from partial and noisy observations. In applications where the state of the system is high dimensional, ensemble Kalman filters are often the method of choice. These algorithms rely on an ensemble of interacting particles to sequentially estimate the state as new observations become available. Despite the practical success of ensemble Kalman filters, theoretical understanding is hindered by the intricate dependence structure of the interacting particles. This talk will discuss ensemble Kalman filters that incorporate an additional resampling step to break the dependency between particles. The new algorithm is amenable to a non-asymptotic and dimension-free theoretical analysis that extends and improves upon those available for filters without resampling, while also performing well in numerical examples. Papers: https://arxiv.org/abs/2208.03246 https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.08751.pdf
Authors
Omar H. Al-Ghattas, Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard University, U.S., oag@broadinstitute.org

sessions.title: Model Reduction in Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of II

talks.title: Likelihood-Informed Model Reduction for Bayesian Inference of Static Structural Loads

speakers.name: Jakob Scheffels
speakers.affiliation: Technische Universität München, Germany; Elizabeth Qian, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Iason Papaioannou

talks.abstract:
.
The Bayesian approach to solving inverse problems updates a prior to a posterior distribution of the parameters through data [Stuart and Dashti,
The Bayesian Approach to Inverse Problems
, 2017]. The likelihood of the data needed to obtain the posterior distribution depends on a computational model for the numerical solution of a partial differential equation. Standard  procedures for solving the Bayesian inference are based on multiple evaluations of the likelihood function and, hence, the underlying computational model. The application of model order reduction enables a cheaper solution of the inverse problem for resource demanding models.
We focus on Bayesian linear inverse problems with Gaussian parameter priors and sparse data. As limited data is only informative in a low-dimensional subspace of the parameter space, the posterior can be expressed as a low-rank update of the prior [Spantini et al,
Optimal Low-Rank Approximations of Bayesian Linear Inverse Problems
, 2015]. In this work, we introduce a framework that leverages the low-rank nature of the mapping between the data and the parameter itself to construct a reduced-order model for inverse problems. This approach is based on the eigendirections of the Fisher information and the prior precision and is referred to as likelihood-informed subspace (LIS). Through numerical examples, we compare the posterior approximation using the LIS reduced-order model with state-of-the-art approximation methods.
Authors
Jakob Scheffels, Technische Universität München, Germany, jakob.scheffels@tum.de
Elizabeth Qian, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., eqian@gatech.edu
Iason Papaioannou, Technische Universität München, Germany, iason.papaioannou@tum.de
Elisabeth Ullmann, Technische Universität München, Germany, elisabeth.ullmann@tum.de

talks.title: Prior-Driven Balancing for Bayesian Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Josie König
speakers.affiliation: Universität Potsdam, Germany; Elizabeth Qian, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Han Cheng Lie

talks.abstract:
.
Solving data assimilation (DA) problems typically requires expensive forward model simulations to compute the expected output for multiple initial conditions. Since this quickly becomes infeasible in high dimensions, reduced models can play a key role in rendering such computations tractable. In particular, model reduction methods from control (systems) theory are well established for obtaining efficient reduced models while preserving the map from control input to observed output. In this talk, we present prior-driven balancing as a system-theoretic model order reduction method adapted for use in DA. This includes a new interpretation of the Bayesian inverse problem, which is essential in DA, as a control problem and its reduction.
Authors
Josie König, Universität Potsdam, Germany, josie.koenig@uni-potsdam.de
Elizabeth Qian, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., eqian@gatech.edu
Han Cheng Lie, Universität Potsdam, Germany, han.lie@uni-potsdam.de
Melina A. Freitag, Universität Potsdam, Germany, melina.freitag@uni-potsdam.de

talks.title: Goal-Oriented Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Inverse Problems Governed by Autonomous Dynamical Systems

speakers.name: Omar Ghattas
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present a goal-oriented dimension reduction algorithm that extends our recent work on real-time, extreme-scale Bayesian inversion for linear autonomous dynamical systems. In that work, an offline-online decomposition enabled real-time predictions of time-dependent quantities of interest (QoIs) with uncertainties. However, the underlying inverse problem is extremely ill-posed given sparse and noisy data, and computing the full posterior covariance for the high-dimensional spatiotemporal parameter field remains computationally intractable.
To overcome these challenges, we introduce a goal-oriented dimension reduction strategy that constructs a basis for the parameter field by retaining only the directions most informative to the QoIs. By leveraging the fast, FFT-based algorithms from our prior work for the parameter-to-observable and parameter-to-QoI maps, solving the full Bayesian inverse problem for this reduced parameter field becomes computationally viable.
We establish theoretical error bounds for the proposed reduction strategy on model problems and demonstrate its effectiveness with numerical results for the acoustic-gravity model governing tsunami dynamics.

Sreeram R. Venkat, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., srvenkat@utexas.edu

talks.title: A Sequential Framework of Dimension-Reduced Expectation-Maximization with Optical Flow for Scalable Dynamic X-Ray Ct
talks.abstract:
.
Large-scale dynamic inverse problems are typically ill-posed and suffer from complexity of the model constraints and large dimensionality of the parameters. A common approach to overcome ill-posedness is through regularization that aims to add constraints on the desired parameters. In this talk, we describe a method for reconstructing a dynamic sequence of undersampled images from computerized tomography data. One approach is a prior-based dimension reduction Kalman filter with smoothing. However, while this method is suitable for image reconstruction, the challenge lies in the realization of the full model for applications, especially when the image sequence is non-stationary. To address this, we utilize a regularized Optical Flow and Expectation Maximization methods alongside the Kalman filter for robust construction of model realizations. We illustrate the effectiveness of the prescribed approach through numerical experiments from a wide range of limited angle and single shot computerized tomography.
Authors
Aryeh Keating, Virginia Tech, U.S., aryehkeating@vt.edu
Mirjeta Pasha, Virginia Tech, U.S., mpasha@vt.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Scientific Computing - Part I of II

talks.title: Data-Driven Closures with Quantified Uncertainty to Enhance Model Prediction Reliability

speakers.name: Rileigh Bandy
speakers.affiliation: Teresa Portone, Mohammad Khalil, Paul Smith, Eric Crislip

talks.abstract:
.
Computational simulations of dynamical systems often require closure models to represent unresolved phenomena, enhance computational efficiency, or correct model-form error. While data-driven approaches offer a promising path to improving the accuracy and generality of closure models, a systematic, robust, and scalable methodology for constructing them has remained elusive. In this work, we introduce a novel framework:  Scalable, Trustworthy, Agile, Goal-Oriented, Efficient, and Data-Assimilated Data-Driven Closure Models (STAGED DDCM). This two-step approach first decouples the forward computational model from closure learning via data assimilation, enabling flexible and scalable integration of observational data. In the second step, machine learning techniques are employed to learn the closure terms from the data generated via data assimilation, capturing complex, nonlinear relationships while preserving model efficiency and scalability.
We demonstrate the end-to-end utility of the STAGED DDCM framework through two representative examples: (1) an epidemiological model, illustrating fast and accurate closure modeling for disease spread; and (2) a structural dynamics case involving a two-mass oscillator calibrated with real experimental data, showcasing the method's ability to capture unknown forces in predictive scenarios in physical systems where subject-matter expertise is limited.
SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525.
