talks.title: A Score-Based Diffusion Model Approach for Adaptive Learning of Stochastic Partial Differential Equation Solutions
talks.abstract:
.
We propose a novel framework for adaptively learning the time-evolving solutions of stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) using score-based diffusion models within a recursive Bayesian inference setting. SPDEs play a central role in modeling complex physical systems under uncertainty, but their numerical solutions often suffer from model errors and reduced accuracy due to incomplete physical knowledge and environmental variability. To address these challenges, we encode the governing physics into the score function of a diffusion model using simulation data and incorporate observational information via a likelihood-based correction in a reverse-time stochastic differential equation. This enables adaptive learning through iterative refinement of the solution as new data becomes available. To improve computational efficiency in high-dimensional settings, we introduce the ensemble score filter, a training-free approximation of the score function designed for real-time inference. Numerical experiments on benchmark SPDEs demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of the proposed method under sparse and noisy observations.
Authors
Feng Bao, Florida State University, U.S., fbao@fsu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Assessing the Quality of Uncertainty Estimates from Conformal Classification Methods
talks.abstract:
.
Conformal prediction is becoming widely used as a distribution-free approach to quantifying uncertainty. The minimal assumptions, flexibility in application to different types of models and data, and guarantee of valid uncertainty estimates make conformal methods attractive for researchers and practitioners alike. Because, under some weak assumptions, conformal methods are guaranteed to produce valid uncertainty quantification (UQ), the quality of the resulting UQ is typically assessed by simply considering efficiency. For two conformal approaches that both produce valid UQ, the smaller interval or set is more efficient. While a straightforward concept, the assessment of efficiency presents some challenges. In this talk, we present an overview of conformal prediction for classification and discuss how to compare conformal methods in terms of UQ quality. We introduce several novel measures of efficiency and compare them to existing approaches through simulation studies. We argue that this represents a more holistic approach to efficiency assessment than current common practices. Finally, we discuss how our approach to evaluating efficiency using simulation studies may be carried out with real data sets and provide some potential avenues for future work in this direction.
Authors
Jason Adams, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., jradams@sandia.gov
Shengzhu Yin, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., syin@sandia.gov
Brandon Berman, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., bjberma@sandia.gov

talks.title: Conditional Diffusion Models for High-speed Boundary Layer Reconstructions

speakers.name: Hojin Kim
speakers.affiliation: Purdue University, U.S.; Dibyajyoti Chakraborty, Pennsylvania State University, U.S.; Takahiko Toki

speakers.name: Carlo Scalo
speakers.affiliation: Purdue University, U.S.; Romit Maulik, Pennsylvania State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
A complete resolution of turbulent boundary layers for high-speed flows is impossible for realistic applications in aerospace engineering. Direct numerical simulations (DNS) are available for canonical geometries and reveal information about the behavior of the near-wall flow. In this work, we use DNS data for high Mach turbulent Couette flow to train a diffusion-based super-resolution model for recovering the near-wall structure of the flow, given conditional information such as distance from the wall, Mach number, Reynolds number, etc. Our model demonstrates that the instantaneous flow structures of the boundary layer are recovered accurately, including derived quantities such as the wall-shear stress and wall heat flux with uncertainty estimates obtained through diffusion model sampling. This opens up a promising new pathway for the wall-modeling of large eddy simulations of turbulent boundary layers in hypersonic applications.
Authors
Hojin Kim, Purdue University, U.S., hojin.kim@purdue.edu

Romit Maulik, Pennsylvania State University, U.S., rmm7011@psu.edu

sessions.title: Probabilistic Manifold Learning and Deep Embeddings for Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of III

talks.title: Generalized Least Volume for Structured Representation Learning

speakers.name: Qiuyi Chen
speakers.affiliation: GE Vernova Research, U.S.; Mark Fuge, ETH Zurich, Switzerland; Cashen Diniz, University of Maryland, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce Generalized Least Volume (GLV), a regularization framework for autoencoders that extends geometric ideas of latent volume minimization beyond Euclidean settings. GLV incorporates label or side information into the latent representation, yielding contrastive effects for discrete labels and smooth interpolations for continuous ones. We show that GLV preserves the dimension-reduction benefits of Least Volume while providing theoretical guarantees under Lipschitz continuity assumptions. Empirically, GLV reveals dataset-specific topological structure and enhances stability in downstream tasks: on labeled benchmarks it induces label-aware disentanglement, while on a continuous-label airfoil dataset it produces representations that correspond to smooth aerodynamic variations, enabling more robust optimization. These results highlight GLV as a principled approach to integrating geometric and statistical structure in latent-variable models.

Qiuyi Chen, GE Vernova Research, U.S., Qiuyi.Chen@gevernova.com

talks.title: From Smooth to Nonsmooth: Minimax Optimal Regression with Laplacian Eigenmaps
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, I will discuss nonparametric regression under varying smoothness assumptions, ranging from classical Sobolev spaces to fractional, nonsmooth regimes. In the smooth setting, we develop Laplacian Eigenmap–based estimators and establish both adaptive and non-adaptive minimax optimality. Our analysis covers a broad family of weighted graph Laplacians and shows that principal components regression on their eigenfunctions achieves the optimal rate, with adaptation accomplished via a Lepski-type procedure. In the nonsmooth setting, where the regression function belongs to a fractional Sobolev space, we introduce Principal Component Regression with Fractional Laplacian Eigenmaps (PCR–FLE). We prove that this estimator attains the minimax optimal rate for fractional smoothness, and complement the upper bounds with matching lower bounds. Taken together, these results provide a unified spectral framework for minimax optimal regression via Laplacian embedding, bridging the gap between smooth and nonsmooth function classes and providing a theoretical foundation for (fractional) Laplacian involved high-dimensional nonparametric learning.
Authors
Zhaoyang Shi, Harvard University, U.S., zshi@fas.harvard.edu
Krishnakumar Balasubramanian, University of California, Davis, U.S., kbala@ucdavis.edu

talks.title: Unsupervised Neural Operators for Optimal Control: Scaling Laws and Applications
talks.abstract:
.
Optimal control provides a principled framework for transforming dynamical system models into intelligent decision-making, yet classical computational approaches are often too expensive for real-time deployment in dynamic or uncertain environments. In this work, we propose a neural operator learning framework designed for optimal control problems. It offers a new paradigm by directly approximating the mapping from system conditions to optimal control strategies, enabling instantaneous inference across diverse scenarios once trained. We investigate both the promise and limitations of neural operators for optimal control. Theoretically, we establish scaling laws that quantify how sample and model complexity depend on the intrinsic dimension and regularity of the optimal control function, thus clarifying the price to pay for efficiency. Numerically, we demonstrate case studies where neural operators achieve remarkable gains in real-time performance, as well as regimes where complexity barriers persist. Together, these results provide a balanced perspective: neural operators represent a powerful new tool for high-performance control when hidden low-dimensional structure can be exploited, yet remain fundamentally constrained by dimensional complexity in more challenging settings.
Authors
Wuzhe Xu, Purdue University, U.S., xu2224@purdue.edu
Rongjie Lai, Purdue University, U.S., lairj@purdue.edu

talks.title: Feature-to-feature Data Assimilation: Application to High-speed Reactive Flow
talks.abstract:
.
We describe recent algorithms to evaluate the distribution of spatio-temporal features conditional on samples of other, related, features.  In particular, we evaluate the flow morphology inside a channel with high-speed reactive flow given temporal observations at sparse boundary locations.  The approach consists of a reverse diffusion process conditioned by a polynomial chaos expansion. The PCE encodes the joint dependence between features and signals and the reverse diffusion process serves to filter and sharpen the features.
Authors
Zheming Gou, University of Southern California, U.S., zgou@usc.edu
Roger Ghanem, University of Southern California, U.S., ghanem@usc.edu

sessions.title: Gradient Flows for Uncertainty Quantification: New Algorithms and Applications - Part II of II

talks.title: Particle Rates for Stein Variational Gradient Descent

speakers.name: Sayan Banerjee
speakers.affiliation: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) is an algorithm for sampling from a target distribution using a deterministic flow of N particles (started from a possibly random initial distribution) which interact with each other through a positive-definite kernel. In comparison with traditional Markov Chain Monte Carlo schemes, SVGD requires less randomness, is easier to implement and is seen to empirically perform better in many situations. SVGD can be thought of as a `discretization’ of a kernelized gradient flow for the relative entropy on Wasserstein-2 space. Thus, its convergence properties are well understood in the mean-field limit. However, due to relative entropy having no suitable analogue for the empirical measure of particle locations, obtaining rates of convergence for the actual particle system has been a long-standing open problem. The few previously obtained rates of order $1/\sqrt{\log(\log(N))}$ were believed to be highly sub-optimal. In the current work, we make the observation that the relative entropy of the joint distribution of particle locations with respect to the $N$-fold product of the target distribution can be used as a `Lyapunov function’ which furnishes near-optimal rates of convergence of order $1/\sqrt{N}$. We also obtain long-time propagation of chaos results and marginal convergence rates for the particle laws.
Authors
Sayan Banerjee, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S., sayan@email.unc.edu

talks.title: Using Probability Paths to Improve the Efficiency of Sampling and Estimation Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Modern algorithms for sampling, estimating normalizing constants, and density-ratio estimation often rely on a probability path that connects a complex target distribution to a simple base distribution such as a Gaussian. In this talk, I will discuss the limitations of classical methods, show how using probability paths can improve their efficiency, sometimes dramatically, and present principled strategies for designing the probability path.
Authors
Omar Chehab, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., ochehab@andrew.cmu.edu

talks.title: Blind Denoising Diffusion Models and the Blessings of Dimensionality
talks.abstract:
.
We analyze, theoretically and empirically, the performance of generative diffusion models based on
blind denoisers
, in which the denoiser is not given the noise amplitude in either the training or sampling processes. Assuming that the data distribution has low intrinsic dimensionality, we prove that blind denoising diffusion models (BDDMs), despite not having access to the noise amplitude,
automatically
track a particular
implicit
noise schedule along the reverse process. Our analysis shows that BDDMs can accurately sample from the data distribution in polynomially many steps as a function of the intrinsic dimension. Empirical results corroborate these mathematical findings on both synthetic and image data, demonstrating that the noise variance is accurately estimated from the noisy image. Remarkably, we observe that schedule-free BDDMs produce samples of higher quality compared to their non-blind counterparts. We provide evidence that this performance gain arises because BDDMs correct the mismatch between the true residual noise (of the image) and the noise assumed by the schedule used in non-blind diffusion models. This is joint work with Zahra Kadkhodaie, Sinho Chewi, and Eero Simoncelli.
Authors
Aram-Alexandre Pooladian, Yale University, U.S., aram-alexandre.pooladian@yale.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Uniform-in-Time Propagation of Chaos for Consensus-Based Optimization

speakers.name: Dohyeon Kim
speakers.affiliation: California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Nicolai Gerber, Ulm University, Germany ; Franca Hoffmann, California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Urbain Vaes, Inria

talks.abstract:
.
Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO) is a class of derivative-free global optimization algorithms governed by interacting stochastic particle systems. These systems are characterized by a non-local structure where particles are attracted toward a weighted consensus point via a Laplace-principle-based approximation of the global minimizer. This mechanism allows for global optimization without gradient information, and causes mathematical challenges for a rigorous mean-field analysis, necessitating new approaches.   >   > In this talk, we establish uniform-in-time propagation of chaos for CBO. By leveraging a novel stability estimate for the weighted mean and quantitative concentration inequalities, we prove that the microscopic particle system tracks the mean-field limit with the classical Monte Carlo rate over infinite time horizons. Notably, for CBO with anisotropic noise, the error prefactor is independent of the problem dimension. We also present results on the almost sure exponential convergence of the microscopic system.
Authors
Dohyeon Kim, California Institute of Technology, U.S., dohyeon@caltech.edu
Nicolai Gerber, Ulm University, Germany , nicolai.gerber@uni-bonn.de
Franca Hoffmann, California Institute of Technology, U.S., franca.hoffmann@caltech.edu
Urbain Vaes, Inria and Ecole des Ponts ParisTech, France, urbain.vaes@inria.fr

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Uncertainty Quantification for Scientific Machine Learning and Digital Twins - Part I of III

talks.title: Shallow Universal Polynomial Networks
talks.abstract:
.
Deep neural networks (DNNs) and Kolmogorov–Arnold networks (KANs) are popular  methods for function approximation due to their flexibility and expressivity. However, they typically  require a large number of trainable parameters to produce a suitable approximation. Beyond making  the resulting network less transparent, overparameterization creates a large optimization space, likely  producing local minima in training that have quite di?erent generalization errors. In this case,  network initialization can have an outsize impact on the model’s out-of-sample accuracy. For these  reasons, we propose shallow universal polynomial networks (SUPNs). These networks replace all  but the last hidden layer with a single layer of polynomials with learnable coe?cients, leveraging  the strengths of DNNs and polynomials to achieve su?cient expressivity with far fewer parameters.  We prove that SUPNs converge at the same rate as the best polynomial approximation of the same  degree, and we derive explicit formulas for quasi-optimal SUPN parameters. We complement theory  with an extensive suite of numerical experiments involving SUPNs, DNNs, KANs, and polynomial  projection in one, two, and ten dimensions, consisting of over 13,000 trained models. On the target  functions we numerically studied, for a given number of trainable parameters, the approximation  error and variability are often lower for SUPNs than for DNNs and KANs by an order of magnitude.  In our examples, SUPNs even outperform polynomial projection on non-smooth functions.
Authors
John D. Jakeman, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., jdjakem@sandia.gov

talks.title: Robust Parameter and State Estimation in Multiscale Neuronal Systems Using Physics-Informed Neural Networks
talks.abstract:
.
Inferring biophysical parameters and hidden state variables from partial and noisy observations is a fundamental challenge in computational neuroscience. This problem is particularly difficult for fast–slow spiking and bursting models, where strong nonlinearities, multiscale dynamics, and limited observational data often lead to severe sensitivity to initial parameter guesses and convergence failure in the methods replying on the traditional numerical forward solvers. In this work, we developed a physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for the joint reconstruction of unobserved state variables and the estimation of unknown biophysical parameters in neuronal models. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the method on biophysical neuron models, including the Morris–Lecar model across multiple spiking and bursting regimes and a respiratory model neuron. The method requires only partial voltage observations over short observation windows and remains robust even when initialized with non-informative parameter guesses. These results suggest that PINN can deliver robust and accurate parameter inference and state reconstruction, providing a promising alternative for inverse problems in multiscale neuronal dynamics, where traditional techniques often struggle.

talks.title: Flow Map Learning of Unknown Stochastic Systems
talks.abstract:
.
We present a computational framework for modeling unknown stochastic systems using observation data. The method utilizes data on the system trajectories and constructs a stochastic flow map of the unknown system. The stochastic flow map takes the form of generative model, e.g., GANs, autoencoder, normalizing flow, etc. Once the generative stochastic flow map is constructed, it allows us to create a predictive model of the known stochastic system. A set of numerical examples, including complex chemical reaction networks, are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the method. By using short bursts of data, the method is able to construct accurate models for the unknown system for very long-term system prediction.
Authors
Yuan Chen, Ohio State University, U.S., chen.11050@osu.edu

sessions.title: Generative AI Surrogates, Efficient Sampling and Optimization for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of II

talks.title: GaussNet: A Gaussian Process Network Accelerated by the Inverse Kalman Filter

speakers.name: Mengyang Gu
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Santa Barbara, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In the first part of the talk, we introduce the inverse Kalman filter, which enables exact matrix-vector multiplication between a covariance matrix from a dynamic linear model, including the Matérn covariance matrix with a half-integer roughness parameter, and a real-valued vector with a linear computational cost to the dimension of the vector. We integrate the inverse Kalman filter with the conjugate gradient algorithm, which substantially accelerates the computation of matrix inversion for an additive summation of covariance matrices, where other approximation approaches may not be directly applicable. In the second part of the talk, we introduce GaussNet, a new neural network architecture that requires much less data for predicting nonlinear maps than conventional neural networks. We derive the closed-form expressions of the predictive distribution and gradients to train GaussNet, which can be substantially accelerated by the inverse Kalman filter. We demonstrate the scalability and accuracy of GaussNet for predicting nonlinear relationships with general multi-dimensional inputs compared with approximation approaches of Gaussian processes and neural networks. Other applications include nonparametric estimation of particle interaction functions using trajectories of cells from simulation and microscopy videos.

Mengyang Gu, University of California, Santa Barbara, U.S.,  mengyang@pstat.ucsb.edu

talks.title: FunDiff: Diffusion Models over Function Spaces for Physics-Informed Generative Modeling
talks.abstract:
.
Recent advances in generative modeling – particularly diffusion models and flow matching – have achieved remarkable success in synthesizing discrete data such as images and videos. However, adapting these models to physical applications remains challenging, as the quantities of interest are continuous functions governed by complex physical laws. Here, we introduce FunDiff, a novel framework for generative modeling in function spaces. FunDiff combines a latent diffusion process with a function autoencoder architecture to handle input functions with varying discretizations, generate continuous functions evaluable at arbitrary locations, and seamlessly incorporate physical priors. These priors are enforced through architectural constraints or physics-informed loss functions, ensuring that generated samples satisfy fundamental physical laws. We theoretically establish minimax optimality guarantees for density estimation in function spaces, showing that diffusion-based estimators achieve optimal convergence rates under suitable regularity conditions. We demonstrate the practical effectiveness of FunDiff across diverse applications in fluid dynamics and solid mechanics. Empirical results show that our method generates physically consistent samples with high fidelity to the target distribution and exhibits robustness to noisy and low-resolution data.

Sifan Wang, Yale University, U.S., sifan.wang@yale.edu

talks.title: Embedding Machine Learning into a Numerical Cimate Models: Computational, Probabilistic and Scientific Advances

speakers.name: James Briant
speakers.affiliation: University College London, United Kingdom; debiprasad bhuyan

talks.abstract:
.
Embedding Machine Learning into a numerical climate models: computational, probabilistic and scientific advances
This talk addresses the design, development and deployment of using Gaussian processes to improve convection within a climate model. The climate model's temperature and humidity profiles are stochastically perturbed every 6 hours at runtime based on a Gaussian process trained on data from a high-resolution weather model with realistic convection. A proof-of-concept study shows the effects of the approach on a simplified climate model's output (SPEEDY). The precipitation pattern is improved globally with the most significant reductions in the Indian Ocean and tropical Pacific Ocean off the Mexican coast which yields a reduced area-weighted RMSE in the tropics by 20% (Giles et al. 2024). A second project looks at the engineering challenges of compiling the Gaussian process deployment into a Fortran-based climate model at runtime with minimal overhead. Initial findings conclude that running on an NVIDIA GH200 device reduces the overheads from 3x to only 3% in compute time but yields similar modelling improvements. This extension also uses a more sophisticated climate model, CESM 2.1, with a moderate resolution. Finally we will discuss the first steps towards including coastal wave modelling into a climate model, using Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics as well as air-sea exchanges representation.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Data Assimilation in Complex Dynamical Systems - Part II of III

talks.title: Can the Generative AI Shark Eat the Entire Data Assimilation System?

speakers.name: Matthias Morzfeld
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.; Daniel Hodyss, US Naval Research Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The recent surge in machine learning (ML) methods for geophysical modeling has raised the question of how these methods might be applied to data assimilation (DA). We focus on diffusion modeling (a form of generative artificial intelligence) for systems that can perform the entire DA process, rather than on ML-based tools used within a conventional DA system. We identify at least three distinct types of diffusion-based DA systems and show that they differ in the posterior distribution they target for sampling. These posterior distributions correspond to different priors and/or likelihoods, which in turn result in unique training datasets, computational requirements, and state estimate qualities. Our analysis further shows that a diffusion DA system designed to target the same posterior distribution as current ensemble DA algorithms requires re-training at each DA cycle, which is computationally costly. We discuss the implications of these findings for the use of diffusion modeling in DA.
Authors
Matthias Morzfeld, University of California, San Diego, U.S., mmorzfeld@ucsd.edu
Daniel Hodyss, US Naval Research Laboratory, U.S., daniel.hodyss@nrl.navy.mil

talks.title: Response Theory Via Score Modeling
talks.abstract:
.
Predicting how small forcings reshape the full probability distribution of complex systems is essential for design, risk, and science. I will present a data-driven framework that combines the Generalized Fluctuation–Dissipation Theorem (GFDT) with score-based generative modeling to forecast distributional responses—mean, variance, skewness, and kurtosis—in nonlinear, non-Gaussian dynamics. The key is to estimate the score (the gradient of the log steady-state density) directly from data: for low-dimensional effective dynamics we use a clustering-based estimator (KGMM), while for high-dimensional fields we train a denoising U-Net. Across a hierarchy of testbeds—scalar and multiscale reduced-order models, a barotropic atmospheric regime model, and a 2-D Navier–Stokes turbulence example with localized forcing—the method reproduces “ground-truth” responses and markedly outperforms Gaussian approximations, including in the tails relevant for extremes.
Authors
Ludovico T. Giorgini, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., giorgini.ludovico@gmail.com

talks.title: Learning Complex Spatio-temporal Models in Latent Space with Uncertainty Quantification Improves Forecast Accuracy and Realism
talks.abstract:
.
Over the last few years, we have witnessed a transformation in our capacity to predict complex chaotic and non-linear spatio-temporal dynamics with machine learning. However many of those models tend to become fuzzy after some time or lose realistic spectra. We demonstrate that by learning the training in the latent space rather than in the model space along with the inclusion of proper uncertainty quantification, we can better predict complex spatio-temporal systems even at long time horizons. Further, the flows remain physically realistic and sound unlike models trained in the physical space. We then explain why this is the case.

sessions.title: Theory and Simulation of Failure Probabilities and Rare Events - Part II of II

talks.title: Advances in Importance Sampling for Rare Event Probability Estimation
talks.abstract:
.
Rare event simulation and probability estimation for safety-critical systems under computationally expensive simulators is a central UQ task. Several variance-reduction methods, based on subset/splitting, cross-entropy, sequential Monte Carlo, etc., can mitigate the computational cost. However, in high-dimensional spaces with strongly nonlinear forward maps, disconnected modes, and general input distributions, available methods often fail to perform adequately and efficiently. We present here a novel two-stage importance sampling (IS) estimator addressing these challenges. A flexible, unnormalized target is first constructed, in a general fashion for all cases. It intrinsically concentrates probability mass on the domain of interest, while relaxing the optimal yet intractable IS density, enabling effective sampling with diverse MCMC methods in gradient-based and gradient-free settings, e.g., QNp-HMCMC and pCN. The normalizing constant of the approximate target is then estimated using density estimators trained on the MCMC samples. Neural density estimators can further boost performance, particularly our Score Aligned Neural Density Estimator (SANDE). SANDE aligns the score field of the learned density with that of the unnormalized target, enabling accurate and scalable density estimation at competitive cost. The proposed framework is validated on challenging benchmarks and applications of complex autonomous and engineered systems involving several hundred random variables.

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for PDEs under Uncertainty: Propagation, Inference, Optimal Control, and Optimization - Part II of III

talks.title: Structure-Preserving Discontinuous Galerkin Methods for Stochastic Shallow Water Equations
talks.abstract:
.
Shallow water equations (SWE) are fundamental models in fluid dynamics that are  essential for studying a wide range of geophysical and engineering phenomena. Therefore, stable and accurate numerical methods for SWE are needed. Although some  algorithms are well studied for deterministic SWE, more effort should be devoted to  handling the SWE with uncertainty, e.g., from initial value and bottom topography.  Building from the hyperbolicity-preserving stochastic Galerkin formulation for SWE  [Dai, Epshteyn, Narayan, 2021 SISC] and the entropy stable discontinuous Galerkin for skew-symmetric SWE [Fu, 2022 JSC], we develop a structure-preserving, entropy conservative, and entropy stable discontinuous Galerkin method for the stochastic Galerkin shallow water system. We demonstrate the efficacy, applicability and robustness of these structure-preserving algorithms through  several numerical experiments.
Keywords: stochastic shallow water equation, stochastic Galerkin, discontinuous Galerkin, structure-preserving, entropy stable.
Authors
Yinqian Yu, University of Utah, U.S., yinqian.yu@utah.edu

talks.title: Worst-Case Learning of Adverse Quantities of Interest
talks.abstract:
.
After a brief rundown on Optimal Recovery, viewed as an analytical learning theory focused on worst-case guarantees, I will present new works whose common theme is the estimation of quantities of interest that are not simple linear maps. In one direction, the quantity of interest is a nonlinear functional (e.g. the maximum of a function, a piecewise affine functional, etc.), but recovery procedures that are genuinely worst-case optimal can be constructed. In another direction, the quantity of interest may be linear but is not explicit (e.g. the solution operator in partial differential equations), so the existing theory does not directly apply.
Authors
Simon Foucart, Texas A&M University, U.S., foucart@tamu.edu

sessions.title: Structure-preserving Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of III

talks.title: Hard-Constrained Neural Networks for Learning Chaotic PDEs with Boundedness Guarantees
talks.abstract:
.
Machine learning models often fail to respect known structure, such as physical laws, and soft-constraining penalty methods are often inadequate, especially in out-of-distribution scenarios. We present a new paradigm: hard-constrained neural networks (HardNet). HardNet appends a differentiable enforcement layer to a network’s outputs to guarantee satisfaction of input-output constraints during both training and inference. It preserves the expressive power of neural networks and allows for end-to-end training with standard unconstrained optimization methods. I will then specialize the idea to learning chaotic partial differential equations, where existing machine learning models often generate unbounded trajectories, leading to invalid statistics. We jointly learn a dissipative dynamics emulator and an energy-like function whose algebraic conditions imply convergence to an invariant level set. Enforcing those conditions via an explicit convex quadratic projection layer—a HardNet-style enforcement layer—provides formal boundedness guarantees and an outer estimate of the strange attractor. On Kuramoto–Sivashinsky and Navier–Stokes benchmarks, the method delivers bounded long-horizon forecasts and, as a result, preserves invariant statistics. Together, these results show the effectiveness of enforcement layers as a general recipe for structure-preserving learning with provable guarantees and strong empirical performance in scientific, or otherwise high-stakes, applications.
Authors
Navid Azizan, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., azizan@mit.edu
Andrea Goertzen, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., agoertz@mit.edu

talks.title: Quantifying Model Uncertainties in Exact Conservation Law-Embedded Reduced Dynamics Models
talks.abstract:
.
Reduced/Latent state dynamics approaches for parameterized PDEs offer a viable method where high-fidelity data and machine learning techniques are used to reduce the system's dimensionality and estimate the dynamics of low-dimensional reduced states. These reduced state dynamics approaches rely on high-quality data and struggle with highly sparse spatiotemporal noisy measurements typically obtained from experiments. Furthermore, there is no guarantee that these models satisfy governing physical conservation laws, especially for parameters that are not a part of the model learning process. We propose a reduced state dynamics approach, which we refer to as ECLEIRS, that satisfies conservation laws exactly even for parameters unseen in the model training process. We compare ECLEIRS with other reduced state dynamics approaches, those that do not enforce any physical constraints and those with physics-informed loss functions, for three shock-propagation problems: 1-D advection, 1-D Burgers and 2-D Euler equations. The numerical experiments conducted in this study demonstrate that ECLEIRS provides the most accurate prediction of dynamics for unseen parameters even in the presence of highly sparse and noisy data. We also demonstrate that ECLEIRS yields solutions and fluxes that satisfy the governing conservation law up to machine precision for unseen parameters, while the other methods yield much higher errors and do not satisfy conservation laws.
Authors
Aviral Prakash, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., aviralp@lanl.gov

talks.title: Semi-Explicit Neural DAEs: Learning Long-Horizon Dynamical Systems with Algebraic Constraints

speakers.name: Avik Pal
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Despite the promise of scientific machine learning (SciML) in combining data-driven techniques with mechanistic modeling, existing approaches for incorporating hard constraints in neural differential equations (NDEs) face significant limitations. Scalability issues and poor numerical properties prevent these neural models from being used for modeling physical systems with complicated conservation laws. We propose Manifold-Projected Neural ODEs (PNODEs), a method that explicitly enforces algebraic constraints by projecting each ODE step onto the constraint manifold. This framework arises naturally from semi-explicit differential-algebraic equations (DAEs), and includes both a robust iterative variant and a fast approximation requiring a single Jacobian factorization. We further demonstrate that prior works on relaxation methods are special cases of our approach. PNODEs consistently outperform baselines across six benchmark problems achieving a mean constraint violation error below $10^{-10}$. Additionally, PNODEs consistently achieve lower runtime compared to other methods for a given level of error tolerance. These results show that constraint projection offers a simple strategy for learning physically consistent long-horizon dynamics.
Authors
Avik Pal, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., avikpal@mit.edu

sessions.title: Matrix and Tensor Computation Meets Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of III

talks.title: Optimal Tensor Algebras Via Variable Projection
talks.abstract:
.
As high-dimensional, complex data becomes more accessible, tensor-based approaches become increasingly attractive for multiway data analysis. However, many multilinear methods suffer from a so-called “curse of multidimensionality;” that is, that fundamental mathematical properties break down in high dimensions. Recent advances in matrix-mimetic tensor algebra in have made it possible to preserve linear algebraic properties and, as a result, to obtain optimal representations of multiway data. Matrix mimeticity arises from interpreting tensors as operators, which in turn are parameterized by invertible linear transformations. The choice of transformation is critical to representation quality and computational efficiency, and thus far, has been made heuristically. In this talk, we will learn data-dependent, orthogonal transformations by leveraging the optimality of matrix-mimetic representations. In particular, we will exploit the coupling between transformations and optimal tensor representations using variable projection. We will highlight the efficacy of our proposed approach on multiway data compression and regression tasks.

talks.title: The Tensor-Train Stochastic Finite Volume Method for Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Steven Walton
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Gianmarco Manzini, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Many problems in physics and engineering are modeled by systems of partial  differential equations such as the shallow water equations of hydrology,  the Euler equations for inviscid, compressible flow, and the MHD equations. The initial data, boundary conditions, and coefficients of these models may be uncertain due to measurement, prediction, or modeling errors. The stochastic finite volume (SFV) method offers an efficient one-pass approach for assessing uncertainty in hyperbolic conservation laws. The SFV method has shown great promise as a weakly-intrusive PDE solver for uncertainty quantification. However, in many relevant applications, the dimension of the stochastic space can make traditional implementations of the SFV method infeasible or impossible due to the so-called curse of dimensionality. We introduce the Tensor-Train SFV (TT-SFV) method within the tensor-train framework to manage the curse of dimensionality. This integration, however, comes with its own set of difficulties, mainly due to the propensity for shock formation in hyperbolic systems. To overcome these issues, we have developed a tensor-train-adapted stochastic finite volume method that employs a global WENO reconstruction, making it suitable for such complex systems. This approach represents the first step in designing efficient tensor-train techniques for uncertainty quantification in hyperbolic systems and conservation laws involving shocks.

sessions.title: Innovations in Practical OED: Recent Advances in Methods and Applications - Part II of III

talks.title: Sensitivities, Singular Values, and Optimally Learned Quantities of Interest

speakers.name: Troy Butler
speakers.affiliation: University of Colorado at Denver, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Traditional approaches for optimal experimental design (OED) typically require solving a large number of computationally intensive inverse problems to find the data that maximizes a utility function. We introduce two novel OED criteria specifically crafted for the data consistent inversion (DCI) framework that avoid approximating inverse problem solutions. DCI is a specific approach for solving a class of stochastic inverse problems by constructing a pullback measure on uncertain parameters from an observed probability measure on the outputs of a quantities of interest (QoI) map. While expected information gain (EIG) has been used for both DCI and Bayesian based OED, the characteristics and properties of DCI solutions differ from those of solutions to Bayesian inverse problems which should be reflected in the OED criteria. The new design criteria developed in this study, called the expected scaling effect and the expected skewness effect, leverage the geometric structure of pre-images associated with observable data sets, allowing for an intuitive and direct approach to OED. These criteria utilize singular value computations derived from sampled and approximated Jacobians of the experimental designs. We present both simultaneous and sequential (greedy) formulations of OED based on these innovative criteria. Numerical results demonstrate the effectiveness in our approach for DCI solutions to stochastic inverse problems.
Authors
Troy Butler, University of Colorado at Denver, U.S., troy.butler@ucdenver.edu

talks.title: Bayesian D-Optimal Experimental Designs Via Column Subset Selection

speakers.name: Srinivas Eswar
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Arvind Saibaba, North Carolina State University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
This work tackles optimal sensor placement for Bayesian linear inverse problems, a popular version of the more general Optimal Experimental Design (OED) problem, using the D-optimality criterion. This is done by establishing connections between sensor placement and Column Subset Selection Problem (CSSP), which is a well-studied problem in Numerical Linear Algebra (NLA). In particular, we use the Golub-Klema-Stewart (GKS) approach which involves computing the truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) followed by a pivoted QR factorization on the right singular vectors. The algorithms are further accelerated by using randomization to compute the low-rank approximation as well as for sampling the indices. The resulting algorithms are robust, computationally efficient, amenable to parallelization, require virtually no parameter tuning, and come with strong theoretical guarantees. One of the proposed algorithms is also adjoint-free which is beneficial in situations, where the adjoint is expensive to evaluate or is not available. Additionally, we develop a method for data completion without solving the inverse problem. Numerical experiments on model inverse problems involving the heat equation and seismic tomography in two spatial dimensions demonstrate the performance of our approaches.

sessions.title: Inverse Problems and Uncertainty Quantification in Biological Systems - Part II of II

talks.title: Amortized Inference of Physiologic Boundary Conditions

speakers.name: Chloe Choi
speakers.affiliation: Stanford University, U.S.; Daniele E. Schiavazzi, University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Alison L. Marsden, Stanford University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Amortized probabilistic inference uses neural networks to learn parametric families of posterior distributions. Despite an increased offline training cost, online generation of posterior samples is extremely fast and, unlike traditional Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, the system does not need to be retrained every time new observations become available. Amortized inference is therefore particularly interesting for patient-specific cardiovascular modeling, where boundary condition tuning is a necessary prerequisite that can quickly become intractable when combining three-dimensional cardiovascular models with sample-based approaches to inverse problems. Given high clinical demands and limited computational resources typically available, it is common practice to use low-fidelity (zero- or one-dimensional) models to capture flow and pressure results along a centerline or at discrete locations in lumped-parameter circuit models. We introduce a simulation-free amortized inference framework based on conditional density estimation. The framework predicts the posterior distribution of patient-specific boundary conditions using neural flow models conditioned on embeddings of complex patient-specific anatomies, in addition to typical clinical targets. We test the framework on patient-specific models that are readily available through the open source Vascular Model Repository (VMR).
*Supported by Yansouni Family Stanford Graduate Fellowship and NSF grant 2105345.
Authors
Chloe Choi, Stanford University, U.S., chloe1@stanford.edu
Daniele E. Schiavazzi, University of Notre Dame, U.S., dschiavazzi@nd.edu
Alison L. Marsden, Stanford University, U.S., amarsden@stanford.edu

talks.title: Estimating Lung Stiffness from CT Using PDE-Constrained Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Tissue elasticity tells us a great deal about how diseases begin and progress, but measuring it inside the body isn’t always straightforward. Techniques like Magnetic Resonance Elastography have made it possible to non-invasively assess tissue stiffness in organs such as the liver, yet the same approach struggles in the lungs because of their complex anatomy and air-filled structure. In this talk, I’ll introduce our effort to estimate lung stiffness directly from standard CT scans acquired during regular breathing. The idea is to link image-derived lung motion to underlying tissue mechanics. We start by estimating how the lung deforms between respiratory phases using image registration, then use a physics-based model to relate that deformation to tissue elasticity. Instead of solving this challenging inverse problem separately for each patient, we train a deep learning model to learn this mapping. Importantly, the model is guided by physics: its predictions must reproduce observed deformations when plugged into a physical simulation. I’ll discuss how this physics-constrained learning framework can recover spatial patterns of lung stiffness and what it reveals about the feasibility of performing “virtual elastography” directly from CT.
Authors
Kayhan Batmanghelich, Boston University, U.S., batman@bu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Reconstructing Boundary Waveform of Cerebrospinal Fluid Flow with Coupled Fluid-Solid Simulation and Surrogate Modeling
talks.abstract:
.
Intrathecal injection delivers medication directly to the central nervous system, with transport governed by cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) flow within the thecal space. CSF flow is periodic and influenced by the stiffness of surrounding soft tissues, with waveform shape and period exhibiting significant patient variability that can affect drug delivery. Although coupled fluid–solid solvers in spinal geometries can accurately model CSF dynamics, their high computational cost limits their use in inverse analysis. Here, we develop a surrogate model of CSF flow that maps the foramen magnum (FM) inlet boundary condition and elastic tissue properties directly to displacement and velocity fields. FM boundary conditions are sampled using a Gaussian process conditioned on patient-specific flow data. A Karhunen–Loève expansion combined with Sobol sequence sampling enables efficient exploration of the boundary condition and material parameter space for fluid–solid simulations. Gaussian process regression is then performed on a reduced-dimensional subspace obtained via principal component analysis. The resulting surrogate enables Bayesian inverse methods, including Markov chain Monte Carlo, and pose an inverse problem to reconstruct the CSF waveform at the FM. This approach demonstrates that rapid analysis of fluid–solid interactions in a realistic spinal geometry is feasible and can inform pharmacokinetics in the nervous system.

Mark Wilkinson, Purdue University, U.S., wilki114@purdue
Aditya Shankar Paspunurwar, Purdue University, U.S., apaspunu@purdue.edu

Ilias Bilionis, Purdue University, U.S., ibilion@purdue.edu

talks.title: Surrogate Models for Handling Global and Local Shape Variations in Aortic Hemodynamics

speakers.name: Richard Schussnig
speakers.affiliation: Technische Universität, Graz, Austria; Francesco Romor, WIAS, Berlin, Germany; Sascha Ranftl, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.; Alfonso Caiazzo, Weierstrass Institute for Applied Analysis

speakers.name: Stochastics
speakers.affiliation: Germany; Gerhard A. Holzapfel, Graz University of Technology, Austria

talks.abstract:
.
This work addresses the efficient handling of shape variability in aortic geometries, where we distinguish between local and global shape variations. Local shape variations can be used to describe uncertainty from medical imaging and the algorithm chain that leads to a simulation-ready discrete representation. Global shape variations capture the inter-patient variability (see, e.g., the SynthAorta dataset [Bošnjak et al., SynthAorta: A 3D Mesh Dataset of Parametrized Physiological Healthy Aortas, IEEE Trans. Med. Imag., 2025]).
Our investigations are based on modeling the geometrical perturbation and the resulting mesh generation, combined with computational fluid dynamics simulation. Variability across shapes is handled using an automated registration based on ResNet-LDDMM. This approach allows to use the simulation data to train shape-informed graph neural network models [Romor et al., Data assimilation performed with robust shape registration and graph neural networks: application to aortic coarctation, arxiv, 2025] to create efficient surrogates. The approach can be used to quantify the effect of shape variability on relevant biomarkers associated with clinical outcomes in numerous vascular pathologies, reducing the computational costs associated with numerical simulations.

talks.title: Semi-Intrusive Surrogate Modelling of the Anodizing Process for Surface Morphology Control Under Uncertainty
talks.abstract:
.
Semi-intrusive UQ balances intrusiveness and non-intrusiveness by avoiding reformulations of governing equations while selectively replacing costly sub-models. This yields computational efficiency with no loss of model structure or fidelity, enabling applications in material science particularly corrosion models.  We apply semi-intrusive UQ to study the growth of anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) films, a multistage electrochemical process progressing from barrier layer formation through pore initiation, nucleation, and steady-state self-organization. While early and final stages are relatively well characterized, transitions through pore initiation (Phase 2) and nucleation (Phase 3) remain major sources of mechanistic uncertainty. By integrating physics-based models with stochastic representations, we estimate uncertainties in nucleation kinetics and morphology under varied scenarios and spatial distributions. Semi-intrusive surrogates are built for subprocesses, enabling efficient parameter space exploration and systematic uncertainty propagation.  Our results identify parameters controlling pore initiation, quantify their importance to film morphology, and provide insight into the dynamics of self-organized AAO growth. Tracking uncertainty through coupled sub-models reveals the interaction driving honeycomb structure formation. This work highlights semi-intrusive UQ ability to bridge experiments and simulation, as well as quantifying epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties.
Authors
Tamadur Albaraghtheh, Helmholtz-Zentrum Hereon, Germany, tamadur.albaraghtheh@hereon.de

talks.title: Propagation of Quantum Scale Uncertainty Via Multivariate Conformal Prediction

speakers.name: Katharine Fisher
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
In recent years, machine learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) have become key tools for efficiently incorporating quantum scale data into a multiscale workflow. The typical representations of uncertainty of MLIPs prediction have been the posterior predictive distribution from Gaussian process regression or the standard deviation of neural network ensembles, but these indicators can exhibit unpredictable behavior on chemistry data sets. By contrast, conformal prediction provides confidence sets with coverage guarantees with minimal assumptions on the underlying data and surrogate model. Thus far, conformal prediction has been applied to quantum scale predictions component-wise to construct an predictive interval for each scalar quantity of interest (QoI). However, many downstream QoIs depend on multiple related properties, for instance both energies and forces, and on multiple molecular configurations. Gaussian process surrogates have the ability to represent these relationships and predict the covariance of vector valued QoIs for multiple configurations. This talk introduces multivariate conformal prediction for quantum chemistry and demonstrates its advantages for downstream QoI compared with component-wise conformal prediction.
Authors
Katharine Fisher, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., kefisher@mit.edu

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for UQ in Earth Science - Part II of III

talks.title: Vecchia Approximation and Its Potential Applications in Geophysics

speakers.name: Eagan Kaminetz
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The Vecchia approximation is a computationally efficient algorithm to approximate large matrices. It doesn't even have to look at all the entries in the matrix once! Our recent work has investigated the Vecchia approximation and its mathematical optimality properties. Because the Vecchia approximation uses just a limited number of entries, it may be especially robust to the curse of dimensionality. Specifically, in geophysics it may be suitable for estimating covariance matrices where the ensemble size is many orders of magnitude smaller than the dimensionality of the state space.
Authors
Eagan Kaminetz, University of California, San Diego, U.S., ekaminetz@ucsd.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: An Alternative Approach to Ensemble-based Covariance Propagation for Data Assimilation Applications
talks.abstract:
.
The propagation, or evolution, of the estimation error covariance is an important aspect of the statistical estimation of dynamical systems, such as data assimilation. Ensemble-based data assimilation schemes, such as the ensemble Kalman filter, approximate the evolution of the covariance through the propagation of the individual ensemble members. Thus, it is typically assumed that if the discrete state propagation and resulting mean state estimates are accurate, then the ensemble-based discrete covariance propagation will be accurate as well, apart from sampling errors due to limited ensemble size. Through a series of numerical experiments supported by analytical results, we demonstrate that this assumption is false when correlation length scales approach grid resolution. We show for states satisfying advective dynamics, that while the discrete state propagation and ensemble mean state estimates are accurate, the corresponding ensemble covariances can be remarkably inaccurate, well beyond that expected from sampling errors or typical numerical discretization errors. Motivated by these results, we propose an alternative method of discrete covariance propagation, derived from the continuum covariance dynamics, that directly addresses these errors observed during discrete  covariance propagation while remaining computationally tractable in large-scale systems. Aspects of this work can then be extended to new methods of covariance modeling and covariance localization.
Authors
Shay Gilpin, University of Arizona, U.S., sgilpin@arizona.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: State Estimation and Sensitivity Analysis of the Tropical Pacific Ocean with the Adjoint Method
talks.abstract:
.
Estimation and prediction of the tropical Pacific Ocean is critical to forecasting both weather and climate. This region is home to the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), which is the dominant driver of interannual climate variability. Strong El Niños have especially severe ecosystem, human health, and economic impacts. ENSO dynamics are also notoriously challenging to model due to their asymmetric, nonlinear nature. Therefore, estimating the tropical Pacific Ocean state is a high-dimensional, nonlinear data assimilation problem, made especially challenging by limited observations.
In this work we use 4D-Variational Data Assimilation to estimate the tropical Pacific Ocean state by constraining the MIT General Circulation Model (MITgcm) to observations. Using the adjoint model we pursue questions such as: 1) which observations constrain the model best, 2) what limits our ability to control the forward model run, and 3) how can we use maps of adjoint sensitivities to identify regions of high uncertainty? As an example, we will show the impact of assimilating moored velocity measurements on the representation of Equatorial phenomena like Tropical Instability Waves.
Authors
Ellen Davenport, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S., edavenport@ucsd.edu

sessions.title: Bridging Theory and Machine Learning for Improved Bayesian Filtering - Part II of II

talks.title: On the Estimation Error of Optimal Transport Particle Filters
talks.abstract:
.
Optimal Transport Filters (OTFs) are a new family of algorithms that utilize conditional optimal transport maps for filtering of highly non-Gaussian processes. While these algorithms have demonstrated interesting practical properties their theoretical foundation is scarce. In this talk we will provide an initial attempt in this direction by outlining a series of error bounds in terms of sample complexities of a training process as well as approximation biases due to discretization. We show that a slow rate can be achieved for broad families of problems while a fast rate of convergence is contingent on theoretical constraints that are hard to satisfy and lead to interesting open questions in the theory of optimal transportation.
Authors
Bamdad Hosseini, University of Washington, U.S., bamdadh@uw.edu
Michele Martino, University of Washington, U.S., michem29@uw.edu

talks.title: A Contrastive Learning Approach to Data Assimilation

speakers.name: Ricardo Baptista
speakers.affiliation: University of Toronto, Canada; Andrew Stuart, California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Son Tran, Amazon, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Multimodal contrastive learning is a methodology for linking different data modalities, such as images and text. It is typically framed as the identification of a set of encoders—one for each modality—that align representations within a common latent space. In this presentation, we interpret contrastive learning as the optimization of encoders that define conditional probability distributions, for each modality conditioned on the other, in a way consistent with the available data. This probabilistic perspective suggests two natural generalizations of contrastive learning: (i) the introduction of novel probabilistic loss functions, and (ii) the use of alternative metrics for measuring alignment in the common latent space. We investigate these generalizations of the classical approach in the multivariate Gaussian setting by viewing latent space identification as a low-rank matrix approximation problem. The proposed framework is further studied through numerical experiments on multivariate Gaussians, the labeled MNIST dataset, and a data assimilation application in oceanography.
Authors
Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada,  r.baptista@utoronto.ca
Andrew Stuart, California Institute of Technology, U.S., astuart@caltech.edu
Son Tran, Amazon, U.S., sontran@amazon.com

talks.title: Transformers for Nonlinear Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
Attention mechanisms, the core of transformer architectures that power modern large language models, have been widely successful at modeling nonlocal correlations in data. Recent interest in applying the transformer architecture to scientific machine learning motivates the development of rigorous mathematical analysis for this methodology. In this talk we show how the mathematical properties of the attention mechanism enable the design of transformer architectures well suited for data assimilation tasks. Namely, we demonstrate that the formulation of attention as an operator mapping between spaces of functions allows for transformer-based methodology for state estimation in nonlinear dynamical systems. On the other hand, a formulation of attention as a map between spaces of probability measures allows for methodology performing stochastic filtering. We show how this novel filtering approach presents a first step towards overcoming the Gaussian assumptions in popular model-driven data assimilation methods for nonlinear filtering.
Authors
Edoardo Calvello, California Institute of Technology, U.S., e.calvello@caltech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: The Ensemble Score Filter for High-Dimensional Nonlinear Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
We propose an ensemble score filter (EnSF) for solving high-dimensional nonlinear filtering problems with superior accuracy. A major drawback of existing filtering methods, e.g., particle filters or ensemble Kalman filters, is the low accuracy in handling high-dimensional and highly nonlinear problems. EnSF addresses this challenge by exploiting the score-based diffusion model, defined in a pseudo-temporal domain, to characterize the evolution of the filtering density. EnSF stores the information of the recursively updated filtering density function in the score function, instead of storing the information in a set of finite Monte Carlo samples (used in particle filters and ensemble Kalman filters). Unlike existing diffusion models that train neural networks to approximate the score function, we develop a training-free score estimation method that uses a mini-batch-based Monte Carlo estimator to directly approximate the score function at any pseudo-spatial–temporal location, which provides sufficient accuracy in solving high-dimensional nonlinear problems while also saving a tremendous amount of time spent on training neural networks. High-dimensional Lorenz-96 systems are used to demonstrate the performance of our method. EnSF provides superior performance, compared with the state-of-the-art LEnTKF, in reliably and efficiently tracking extremely high-dimensional Lorenz systems (up to 1,000,000 dimensions) with highly nonlinear observation processes.
Authors
Guannan Zhang, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., zhangg@ornl.gov

sessions.title: Model Reduction in Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of II

talks.title: Nested Operator Inference for Multifidelity Uncertainty Quantification in Ice Sheet Simulations

speakers.name: Karen E. Willcox
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Highly accurate full-order models are often too expensive computationally to evaluate in predictive, real-time, or many-query applications. Projection-based model order reduction methods exploit the intrinsic low-dimensionality of the full-order solution manifold. These reduced-order models (ROMs) typically achieve significant computational savings while remaining physically interpretable through the governing equations. Operator Inference (OpInf) is a data-driven learning technique to construct projection-based ROMs without accessing the full-order operators. Nested OpInf exploits the inherent hierarchy within the reduced space to iteratively construct initial guesses for the OpInf learning problem that prioritize the interactions of the dominant modes. The initial guess computed for any target reduced dimension corresponds to a ROM with provably smaller or equal snapshot reconstruction error than with standard OpInf, thereby alleviating the need for careful regularization. We demonstrate the nested OpInf algorithm on a large-scale, parameterized model of the Greenland ice sheet in the context of a multifidelity uncertainty quantification study.
Authors
Nicole Aretz, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., nicole.aretz@austin.utexas.edu
Karen E. Willcox, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., kwillcox@oden.utexas.edu

talks.title: Tackling Uncertainty in Model Reduction and Scientific Machine Learning for a Catalytic CO2 Methanation Reactor

speakers.name: Ion Victor Gosea
speakers.affiliation: Max Planck Institute, Magdeburg, Germany; Peter Benner, Max-Planck-Institute for Dynamics of Complex Technical Systems, Germany; Kai Sundmacher, Max Planck Institute for Dynamics of Complex Technical Systems, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
In recent years, model-based approaches have become computationally intensive, limiting their application in demanding, real-time scenarios. This highlights the need for surrogate models that are computationally efficient and capable of accurately capturing the essential dynamics of complex systems. However, for such approaches, robustness to noisy and uncertain data is required, especially in the context of processing data generated on the spot. In this study, we examine several techniques for reduced-order modeling of complex processes, including operator inference with stability guarantees and sparse identification with greedy sampling. These methods are evaluated for their ability to model the dynamic operation and control of our application, e.g., a catalytic carbon dioxide methanation reactor. We show how artificially-generated data, together with real data (measured directly from the physical entity), can be used to extract valuable information for controlling the complex processes that describe the reactor's physics under a variety of parameters and conditions. The latter category includes the excitation of the mass inlet flows using tailored inputs to mimic load variations from fluctuating hydrogen availability. By evaluating the accuracy and computational efficiency of the proposed methods, we aim to identify suitable reduced-order predictive surrogate models tailored to the challenging application under study.
Authors
Luisa Peterson, Max Planck Institute, Magdeburg, Germany, peterson@mpi-magdeburg.mpg.de
Alexander Geschke, Max Planck Institute, Magdeburg, Germany, geschke@mpi-magdeburg.mpg.de
Ion Victor Gosea, Max Planck Institute, Magdeburg, Germany, gosea@mpi-magdeburg.mpg.de
Peter Benner, Max-Planck-Institute for Dynamics of Complex Technical Systems, Germany, benner@mpi-magdeburg.mpg.de
Kai Sundmacher, Max Planck Institute for Dynamics of Complex Technical Systems, Germany, sundmacher@mpi-magdeburg.mpg.de

talks.title: Variationally Stable Reduced Basis Neural Operator

speakers.name: Yuan Qiu
speakers.affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Wolfgang Dahmen, South Carolina State University, U.S.; Peng Chen, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Minimizing PDE-based residual losses is a common way to softly enforce physical consistency in neural operators. However, the link between small residuals and small solution errors is often unclear. We show with two canonical examples, Poisson and linear elasticity, how to construct variationally correct first-order least-squares residuals that are provably equivalent to errors in PDE-compliant energy norms rather than in a generic L2 norm. This yields training objectives that directly control accuracy and naturally accommodate mixed Dirichlet/Neumann data via variational lifts. To alleviate optimization challenges and reduce training costs, we apply proper orthogonal decomposition (POD) to high-fidelity snapshots, training the neural operator to predict only the POD coefficients. This approach preserves residual–error equivalence while enabling efficient evaluation of the quadratic loss in the reduced space. Numerical studies compare this approach with pointwise training- and evaluation-based neural operators and, importantly, show that the proposed variationally correct loss serves as a reliable a posteriori error estimator, exhibiting a tight correlation with the solution error in the appropriate PDE energy norms.
Authors
Yuan Qiu, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., yuan.qiu@gatech.edu
Wolfgang Dahmen, South Carolina State University, U.S., dahmen@math.sc.edu
Peng Chen, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., pchen402@gatech.edu

talks.title: Efficient Greedy Sampling for High-dimensional Parameter Spaces

speakers.name: Karen Veroy-Grepl
speakers.affiliation: Technische Universiteit Eindhoven, The Netherlands; Evie Nielen

talks.abstract:
.
Configuration Optimization Problems (COPs), which involve minimizing a loss function over a set of discrete points $\gamma \subset P$, are common in areas like Model Order Reduction, Active Learning, and Optimal Experimental Design. While exact solutions are often infeasible, heuristic methods such as the Greedy Sampling Method (GSM) provide practical alternatives, particularly for low-dimensional cases. GSM recursively updates $\gamma$ by solving a continuous optimization problem, which is typically approximated by a search over a discrete sample set $S\subset P$. However, as the dimensionality grows, the sample size suffers from the curse of dimensionality. To address this, we introduce the Polytope Division Method (PDM), a scalable greedy-type approach that adaptively partitions the parameter space and targets regions of high loss. PDM achieves linear scaling with problem dimensionality and offers an efficient solution approach for high-dimensional COPs, overcoming the limitations of traditional methods.
Authors
Karen Veroy-Grepl, Technische Universiteit Eindhoven, The Netherlands, k.p.veroy@tue.nl
Evie Nielen, Eindhoven University of Technology, Netherlands, e.t.e.nielen@tue.nl
Oliver Tse, Eindhoven University of Technology, Netherlands, o.t.c.tse@tue.nl

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Scientific Computing - Part II of II

talks.title: Stochastic Sensitivity Analysis for Dynamical Systems

speakers.name: Nishant Panda
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Jehanzeb H. Chaudhry, University of New Mexico, U.S.; Natalie Klein, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; James Carzon, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S.; Troy Butler, National Science Foundation, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We derive the evolution equation for local sensitivities of statistical quantities of interest with respect to model parameters in dynamical systems with uncertain initial conditions or parameters.  Our main contribution is the extension of adjoint-based a posteriori analysis for differential operators of generic dynamical systems acting on states to the Liouville operator acting on probability densities of the states. This results in theoretically rigorous estimates of sensitivity and error for a broad class of computed quantities of interest while propagating uncertainty through dynamical systems.  We also derive Monte-Carlo type estimators to make these estimates computationally tractable using spatio-temporal normalizing flows and exploiting the hyperbolic nature of the Liouville equation. This work was published in the Proceeding of Machine Learning Research at Artificial Intelligence and Statistics conference in 2025.

talks.title: Bayesian Inference of Hybrid Physics – Machine Learning Models for Monitoring and Identification of Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian filtering techniques are a powerful tool to monitor the behavior of dynamical systems. The Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) is particularly well suited for damage detection, enabling both state and parameter estimation in nonlinear structural systems. However, the UKF assumes that the system dynamics are fully known, making it sensitive to model inaccuracies, unmodeled dynamics, and environmental noise. We present a hybrid framework that integrates data-driven probabilistic models (e.g., Gaussian Processes GPs or Bayesian neural networks) to model the unknown system dynamics and uncertainties without requiring explicit formulation. This hybrid framework thus aims to combine the interpretability of physics-based methods and the adaptability and robustness of data-driven approaches, while maintaining the computational efficiency of the UKF for training. We will more specifically discuss some of the challenges that arise when integrating data-driven models, which are traditionally trained off-line via optimization, within sequential learning frameworks. Also, contrary to methods that fully represent the dynamics via data-driven models without integrating known physics, the combination of both physics- and data-driven models can generate additional issues of identifiability. We will illustrate the need for robust estimation of uncertainties in this hybrid modeling context using several examples related to structural identification.
Authors
Audrey Olivier, University of Southern California, U.S., audreyol@usc.edu
Nihan Bilgin, University of Southern California, U.S., nbilgin@usc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Characterizing Errors in Fine-Tuned Scientific Foundation Models via Stochastic Attention
talks.abstract:
.
Inspired by the success of foundation models in language modeling, scientific foundation models (SciFMs) are being developed rapidly across many domains. These large-scale AI models are trained on broad scientific data and can be adapted to downstream tasks via fine-tuning. Yet for scientific deployment, we still lack practical error characterization and robust uncertainty quantification for these models.
In this talk, we introduce
stochastic attention
, a mechanism that perturbs attention layers in transformer-based SciFMs to induce a controlled distribution over predictions. This provides a probabilistic reinterpretation of attention and can be viewed as a generative mechanism for modeling long-range dependencies. We use stochastic attention to quantify post–fine-tuning model error with negligible runtime and storage overhead, without requiring changes to the fine-tuning procedure.
We demonstrate the approach on a SciFM for weather and climate prediction, and compare it against several probabilistic fine-tuning baselines, including parametric estimations and approximate Bayesian methods.
Authors
Akash Yadav, University of Houston, U.S., ayadav4@uh.edu
Taiwo Adebiyi, University of Houston, U.S., taadebiyi2@uh.edu
Ruda Zhang, University of Houston, U.S., rudaz@uh.edu

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification Across the Lifecycle of Foundation Models for Scientific Machine Learning

speakers.name: Ponkrshnan Thiagarajan
speakers.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, U.S.; Souvik Chakraborty, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, India; Michael D. Shields, Johns Hopkins University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The recent development of data-driven foundation models for scientific machine learning (SciML) has enabled generalizable modelling of complex physical systems, but it has also amplified the challenge of understanding and managing uncertainty. Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) for foundation models must be considered at all stages of the model development lifecycle. In this work, we present a systematic perspective on UQ across four critical stages: (1) Data and Dataset Curation; (2) Tokenization and discretization; (3) Model Architecture; and (4) Training and Fine-Tuning to quantify epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties. We also perform a comparative study of SciML foundation models spanning several orders of magnitude in number of parameters (ranging from specialised architectures with a few thousand parameters to large-scale foundation models with billions of parameters). Through our study, we highlight how uncertainty evolves with model and data scale and task complexity during pretraining and finetuning. Our proposed approach will enable researchers and foundation model developers to effectively probe their development pipelines and create reliable models.

.

talks.title: Learning Stochastic Reduced-Order Models for Closure Terms and Model Error in High-Dimensional Plasma
talks.abstract:
.
High-dimensional plasma simulations, such as those performed with particle-in-cell (PIC) methods, provide critical insights into kinetic plasma behavior but are computationally prohibitive for large-scale or long-term studies. Fluid plasma models offer a more tractable alternative, yet suffer from limited physical fidelity due to inherent closure approximations and loss of key kinetic information. We presents a data-driven framework for constructing stochastic reduced-order models (ROMs) that systematically exploit the underlying low-dimensional structure of high-fidelity plasma data. By projecting rich kinetic behavior available in PIC simulations onto a lower-dimensional manifold, we hope to enable the direct learning of closure terms—such as heat fluxes, anisotropic pressure tensors, and collisional dynamics—with greater accuracy and physical consistency. We investigate both linear methods, including Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), and nonlinear machine learning approaches, notably Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs), to discover accurate and interpretable representations of closure physics. Furthermore, we employ stochastic modeling strategies to rigorously quantify uncertainties and model errors introduced by dimensionality reduction and other physical assumptions. We demonstrate that by exploiting low-dimensional structure in high-fidelity PIC simulations, data-driven ROMs provide a promising path for accelerating plasma simulations and building reliable fluid closures.
Authors
Josh Eckels, University of Michigan, U.S., eckelsjd@umich.edu
Alex Gorodetsky, University of Michigan, U.S., goroda@umich.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Fokker-Planck-Based Inverse Reinforcement Learning

speakers.name: Chengyang Huang
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Siddhartha Srivastava, University of Southern California, U.S.; Xun Huan , University of Michigan, U.S.; Krishna Garikipati, University of Southern California, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce Fokker-Planck inverse reinforcement learning (FP-IRL), a physics-constrained IRL framework for systems governed by Fokker-Planck dynamics. Unlike standard IRL, FP-IRL infers both reward and transition functions directly from trajectory data without sampled transitions. By leveraging a conjectured equivalence between MDPs and the FP equation, we recover full MDP components via variational system identification. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark problems demonstrate accurate, interpretable, and efficient recovery of agent incentives.
Authors

Xun Huan, University of Michigan, U.S., xhuan@umich.edu
Krishna Garikipati, University of Southern California, U.S., garikipa@usc.edu
