sessions.title: Measure Flows for Inverse Problems and Machine Learning - Part I of III

talks.title: Measure Theoretic Approaches for Uncertainty Propagation
talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty is ubiquitous: both data and physical models inherently contain uncertainty. Therefore, it is crucial to identify the sources of uncertainty and control its propagation over time. In this talk, I will introduce two approaches to address this uncertainty propagation problem—one for the inverse problem and one for the forward problem. The main idea is to work directly with probability measures, treating the underlying PDE as a pushforward map. In the inverse setting, we will explore various variational formulations, focusing on the characterization of minimizers and their stability. In the forward setting, we aim to propose a new approach to tackle high-dimensional uncertainties.
Authors
Li Wang, University of Minnesota, U.S., wang8818@umn.edu

talks.title: Score-Based Generative Models for Posterior Sampling in Function Space

speakers.name: Lorenzo Baldassari
speakers.affiliation: Universität Basel, Switzerland; Ali Siahkoohi, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Josselin Garnier, Ecole Polytechnique, France; Knut Solna, University of California, Irvine, U.S.; Maarten V. de Hoop, Rice University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
A central challenge in Bayesian inverse problems—particularly those governed by partial differential equations—is that the unknowns are functions living in infinite-dimensional spaces. It is therefore crucial to design inference methods that are both theoretically sound and computationally effective in arbitrarily high dimensions.   Recently, score-based generative models (SGMs) have been successfully applied to Bayesian inverse problems. However, existing approaches typically assume that the posterior is supported on a finite-dimensional space, leaving the challenges of infinite dimensions to heuristics and ad hoc solutions.  In this talk, we address this gap by developing a theoretical framework for applying SGMs directly to infinite-dimensional Bayesian inverse problems. We formulate SGMs at the level of function spaces and analyze their stability and convergence under refinement of the discretization. This provides a principled foundation for the use of SGMs in high- and infinite-dimensional Bayesian inference.
Authors
Lorenzo Baldassari, Universität Basel, Switzerland, lorenzo.baldassari@unibas.ch
Ali Siahkoohi, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., alisk@ucf.edu
Josselin Garnier, Ecole Polytechnique, France, josselin.garnier@polytechnique.edu

Maarten V. de Hoop, Rice University, U.S., mdehoop@rice.edu

talks.title: Interacting Particle Systems in Machine Learning and Inference
talks.abstract:
.
The convergence rate of the standard Stein Variational Gradient Descent (SVGD) is a well-known open problem. We address this gap by introducing a second-order, accelerated SVGD and proving its exponential convergence to the target distribution. Our analysis, based on a hypocoercivity framework, establishes the first general convergence rate for an SVGD-type algorithm.

Trevor Teolis, Rice University, U.S., tt111@rice.edu

talks.title: An Eulerian Perspective on Straight-Line Sampling
talks.abstract:
.
We study dynamic measure transport for generative modeling: specifically, flows induced by stochastic processes that bridge a specified source and target distribution. The conditional expectation of the process' velocity defines an ODE whose flow map achieves the desired transport. We ask
which processes produce straight-line flows
—i.e., flows whose pointwise acceleration vanishes and thus are exactly integrable with a first-order method? We provide a concise PDE characterization of straightness as a balance between conditional acceleration and the divergence of a weighted covariance (Reynolds) tensor. Using this lens, we fully characterize affine-in-time interpolants and show that straightness occurs exactly under deterministic endpoint couplings.
We then generalize this framework to stochastic processes satisfying Ito-typo SDEs, showing they obey the exact same PDE thus allowing us to study straightness for a much broader class of flows. Finally, we use our PDE framework to derive geometric conditions for straight-line inducing general processes, offering broad guidance for designing transports that are easier to integrate.
Authors
Panos Tsimpos, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., ptsimpos@mit.edu
Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., ymarz@mit.edu

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Uncertainty Quantification for Scientific Machine Learning and Digital Twins - Part II of III

talks.title: Epistemic Physics-Informed Neural Networks

speakers.name: Panos Stinis
speakers.affiliation: Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have demonstrated promise as a framework for solving forward and inverse problems involving partial differential equations. Despite recent progress in the field, it remains challenging to quantify uncertainty in these networks. While approaches such as Bayesian PINNs (B-PINNs) provide a principled approach to capturing uncertainty through Bayesian inference, they can be computationally expensive for large-scale applications. We propose Epistemic Physics-Informed Neural Networks (E-PINNs), a framework that leverages a small network, the
epinet
, to efficiently quantify uncertainty in PINNs. The proposed approach works as an add-on to existing, pre-trained PINNs with a small computational overhead. We demonstrate the applicability of the proposed framework in various test cases and compare the results with B-PINNs using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) posterior estimation and dropout-equipped PINNs (Dropout-PINNs). Our experiments show that E-PINNs provide similar coverage to B-PINNs, with often comparable sharpness, while being computationally more efficient. This observation, combined with E-PINNs' more consistent uncertainty estimates and better calibration compared to Dropout-PINNs for the examples presented, indicates that E-PINNs offer a promising approach in terms of accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
Authors
Panos Stinis, Pacific Northwest National Laboratory, U.S., panagiotis.stinis@pnnl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Sparse Gaussian Process Operator for Uncertainty Quantification
talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian processes (GPs) are powerful machine learning models that have been successfully used in many applications that involve clustering, density estimation, system identification, regression, and classification. The majority of recent works on GPs are aimed at increasing their scalability to high dimensions and/or large datasets but GPs are getting increasing attention in operator learning and solving forward/inverse problems involving PDEs. In this talk, we will focus on some of the fundamental limitations of kernels in the context of scientific machine learning. By addressing these limitations, we demonstrate that GPs can provide computationally efficient and competitive performance in critical applications such as topology optimization and operator learning.

Ramin Bostanabad, University of California, Irvine, U.S.,  raminb@uci.edu

talks.title: Deep Operator Gaussian Process for Uncertainty Quantification of Operator Learning

speakers.name: Jaeyong Lee
speakers.affiliation: Chung-Ang University, Korea; Yeonjong Shin, North Carolina State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present a Gaussian process-based uncertainty quantification (UQ) framework for operator learning, namely, Deep Operator Gaussian Processes (DeepOGP), which generalizes Gaussian processes (GPs) from functions to operators. Our approach consists of a hierarchical two-stage construction: a high-dimensional data GP first models input–output mappings of operators, providing predictive means and covariances, and a function GP with deep kernel learning subsequently conditions on these uncertain outputs to produce continuous predictions. This formulation yields a closed-form predictive distribution whose variance naturally decomposes into intrinsic function uncertainty and propagated data uncertainty. We demonstrate the method on a range of problems, including a toy differential operator, Burgers equations, Vlasov–Poisson (VP) systems, and climate modeling data, showing improved accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty estimates for high-dimensional scientific problems.

talks.title: Metropolis-Adjusted Subdifferential Langevin Algorithm
talks.abstract:
.
The Metropolis-Adjusted Langevin Algorithm (MALA) is a widely used Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method for sampling from high-dimensional distributions. However, MALA relies on differentiability assumptions that restrict its applicability. In this paper, we introduce the Metropolis-Adjusted Subdifferential Langevin Algorithm (MASLA), a generalization of MALA that extends its applicability to distributions whose log-densities are locally Lipschitz, generally non-differentiable, and non-convex. We evaluate the performance of MASLA by comparing it with other sampling algorithms in settings where they are applicable. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of MASLA in handling a broader class of distributions while maintaining computational efficiency.
Authors
Ning Ning, Texas A&M University, U.S., patning@tamu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Optimal Transport-Based Generative Models for Bayesian Posterior Sampling
talks.abstract:
.
We investigate the problem of sampling from posterior distributions with intractable normalizing constants in Bayesian inference. Our solution is a new generative modeling approach based on optimal transport (OT) that learns a deterministic map from a reference distribution to the target posterior through constrained optimization. The method uses structural constraints from OT theory to ensure uniqueness of the solution and allows efficient generation of many independent, high-quality posterior samples. The framework supports both continuous and mixed discrete-continuous parameter spaces, with specific adaptations for latent variable models and near-Gaussian posteriors. Beyond computational benefits, it also enables new inferential tools based on OT-derived multivariate ranks and quantiles for Bayesian exploratory analysis and visualization. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through multiple simulation studies and a real-world data analysis.
Authors
Yuexi Wang, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, yxwang99@illinois.edu

sessions.title: Dimensionality Reduction in Bayesian Analysis and Uncertainty Quantification

talks.title: Continuous Adaptive Nonlinear Optimal Design
talks.abstract:
.
Identifying informative measurements is a central challenge in computational inverse problems. This task is often framed as an optimal experimental design (OED) problem. In this talk, we explore OED in the context of a continuously-indexed design space, as opposed to traditional approaches that choose measurements from a finite set. This continuous design formulation better reflects practical scenarios where measurements are seamlessly distributed over spatial or temporal domains. To address the associated computational and optimization challenges, we leverage gradient flow methods, incorporated by adaptive algorithms for models with nonlinear parameter-to-observation (PtO) maps. The proposed algorithm not only captures the most informative measurements but also adaptively leads to qualified reconstruction of inverse problems. This novel OED framework has shown effectiveness across a range of physical models, from quantum systems to dynamical models.
Authors
Ruhui Jin, University of Wisconsin, U.S., rjin18@jh.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Efficient Optimization of Expensive Black-Box Simulators Via Marginal Means
talks.abstract:
.
With advances in scientific computing, computer experiments are increasingly used for optimizing complex systems. However, for modern applications, e.g., the optimization of nuclear physics detectors, each experiment run can require hundreds of CPU hours, making the optimization of its black-box simulator $f$ over a high-dimensional space a challenging task. Given limited runs at inputs $\mathbf{x}_1, \cdots, \mathbf{x}_n$, the best solution from these evaluated inputs can be far from optimal, particularly as dimensionality increases. Existing black-box methods, however, largely employ this "pick-the-winner" (PW) solution, which leads to mediocre optimization performance. To address this, we propose a new Black-box Optimization via Marginal Means (BOMM) approach. The key idea is a new estimator of a global optimizer $\mathbf{x}^*$ that leverages the so-called marginal mean functions, which can be efficiently inferred with limited runs in high dimensions. Under mild conditions, we prove that the BOMM estimator not only is consistent for optimization, but also has an optimization rate that tempers the "curse-of-dimensionality" faced by existing methods, thus enabling better performance as dimensionality increases. We present a practical framework for implementing BOMM using the transformed Gaussian process surrogate model.
Authors
Hwanwoo Kim, Duke University, U.S., ghksdn1227@gmail.com
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Reducing the Dimension of Single-Cell Data: Multi-Group Structures and Periodicity
talks.abstract:
.
In single-cell data analysis, recent progress on dimension reduction has focused on leveraging group structures and periodicity. Advanced statistical methods are utilized to identify unique structures in distinct groups, aiding in distinguishing between healthy and diseased cells. This is key for uncovering complex cellular behaviors and understanding disease mechanisms. The unique feature of periodicity in the cell data enhances the interpretability of visualizations in complex biological data. These advancements mark strides in statistical methodologies for single-cell data analysis, emphasizing the need for precision and sophisticated algorithms, ultimately leading to our new methodologies.
Authors
Hengrui Luo, Rice University, U.S., hengruiluo@gmail.com

talks.title: Sanvi: A Fast Spectral-Assisted Network Variational Inference Method with An Extended Surrogate Likelihood Function
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian inference has been broadly applied to statistical network analysis, but suffers from the expensive computational costs due to the nature of Markov chain Monte Carlo sampling algorithms. This paper proposes a novel and computationally efficient Spectral-Assisted Network Variational Inference (SANVI) method within the framework of the generalized random dot product graph. The key idea is a cleverly designed extended surrogate likelihood function that enjoys two convenient features. Firstly, it decouples the generalized inner product of latent positions in the random graph model. Secondly, it relaxes the complicated domain of the original likelihood function to the entire Euclidean space. Leveraging these features, we design a computationally efficient Gaussian variational inference algorithm via stochastic gradient descent. Furthermore, we show the asymptotic efficiency of the maximum extended surrogate likelihood estimator and the Bernstein-von Mises limit of the variational posterior distribution. Through extensive numerical studies, we demonstrate the usefulness of the proposed SANVI algorithm compared to the classical Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithm, including comparable estimation accuracy for the latent positions and less computational costs.
Authors
Fangzheng Xie, Indiana University, U.S., fxie@iu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Data Assimilation in Complex Dynamical Systems - Part III of III

talks.title: Learning Enhanced Ensemble Filters
talks.abstract:
.
The filtering distribution in hidden Markov models evolves according to the law of a mean-field model in state-observation space. The ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) approximates this mean-field model with an ensemble of interacting particles, employing a Gaussian ansatz for the joint distribution of the state and observation at each observation time. These methods are robust, but the Gaussian ansatz limits accuracy. This shortcoming is addressed by approximating the mean-field evolution using a novel form of neural operator taking probability distributions as input: a measure neural mapping (MNM). A MNM is used to design a novel approach to filtering, the MNM-enhanced ensemble filter (MNMEF), which is defined in both the mean-field limit and for interacting ensemble particle approximations. The ensemble approach uses empirical measures as input to the MNM and is implemented using the set transformer, which is invariant to ensemble permutation and allows for different ensemble sizes. The derivation of methods from a mean-field formulation allows a single parameterization of the algorithm to be deployed at different ensemble sizes. In practice fine-tuning of a small number of parameters, for specific ensemble sizes, further enhances the accuracy of the scheme. The promise of the approach is demonstrated by its superior root mean-square-error performance relative to leading methods in filtering the Lorenz 96 and Kuramoto-Sivashinsky models.
Authors
Bohan Chen, California Institute of Technology, U.S., bhchen@caltech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Observation Network Impacts of the Ensemble Score Filter in the Surface Quasi-geostrophic System
talks.abstract:
.
Recent advances in data assimilation (DA) have focused on devel-  oping more flexible approaches that can better accommodate nonlinearities in  models and observations. However, it remains unclear how the performance of  these advanced methods depends on the observation network characteristics.  In this study, we present initial experiments with the surface quasi-geostrophic model, in which we compare a recently developed AI-based ensemble filter with the standard Local Ensemble Transform Kalman Filter (LETKF). Our results show that the analysis solutions respond differently to the number, spatial distribution, and nonlinear fraction of assimilated observations. We also find notable changes in the multiscale characteristics of the analysis errors. Given that standard DA techniques will be eventually replaced by more advanced methods, we hope this study sets the ground for future efforts to reassess the value of Earth observation systems in the context of newly emerging algorithms.
Authors
Zixiang Xiong, Florida State University, U.S., zx18@fsu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Data Assimilation Model for Multiscale Probability Distributions of Turbulent Systems
talks.abstract:
.
We present a new strategy for the statistical forecasts and uncertainty quantification of multiscale nonlinear turbulent systems involving non-Gaussian probability distributions. The capability of using reduced-order models to capture key statistical features is investigated. A closed stochastic-statistical modeling framework is proposed using a high-order statistical closure enabling accurate prediction of leading-order statistical moments and probability density functions in multiscale complex turbulent systems. A new efficient ensemble forecast algorithm is developed dealing with the nonlinear multiscale coupling mechanism as a characteristic feature in high-dimensional turbulent systems. Effective nonlinear ensemble filters are developed based on the particular nonlinear coupling structures of the explicit stochastic and statistical equations, which satisfy an infinite-dimensional Kalman-Bucy filter with conditional Gaussian dynamics. It is demonstrated that crucial principal statistical quantities in the most important large scales can be captured efficiently with accuracy using the new reduced-order model in various dynamical regimes of the flow field with distinct statistical structures.
Authors
Di Qi, Purdue University, U.S., qidi@purdue.edu

sessions.title: Global Sensitivity Analysis and Interpretability of Black-box Models - Part I of II

talks.title: Large-Dimensional Shapley Effects' Estimation for Reliability-oriented Sensitivity Analysis

speakers.name: Lucas Monteiro
speakers.affiliation: Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France; Jérôme Morio, ONERA, France; Julien Demange-Chryst, Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France; François Bachoc, Universite de Lille, France

talks.abstract:
.
In the study of physical systems, reliability analysis aims at estimating failure probabilities and identifying the input variables most responsible for failures. Reliability-oriented sensitivity analysis complements probability estimation by quantifying the contribution of each input to the occurrence of rare events.
When dealing with models involving many correlated inputs, classical Sobol indices are not appropriate, while Shapley effects provide a consistent framework. However, existing estimation procedures have mainly been restricted to low-dimensional models or require prohibitive computational budgets, especially when failure probabilities are very small.
We propose a novel methodology for estimating reliability-oriented Shapley effects in higher-dimensional models by leveraging Normalizing Flows. Their ability to approximate high-dimensional conditional densities, combined with an efficient aggregation strategy, enables accurate estimation of Shapley effects for correlated inputs without additional simulations beyond those required for failure probability estimation.
The proposed approach is validated on numerical case studies with more than 20 input variables, illustrating its effectiveness and scalability in high-dimensional and correlated settings.
Authors
Lucas Monteiro, Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France, lucas.monteiro@onera.fr
Jérôme Morio, ONERA, France, jerome.morio@onera.fr
Julien Demange-Chryst, Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France, julien.demange-chryst@onera.fr
François Bachoc, Universite de Lille, France, francois.bachoc@univ-lille.fr

talks.title: Conditional Feature Importance Revisited: Double Robustness, Efficiency and Inference

speakers.name: Institut de Mathémathiques de Toulouse
speakers.affiliation: France; Bertrand Thirion, INRIA, Université Paris Saclay, France; Pierre Neuvial, Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
In this presentation, we begin by introducing a Generalized Total Sobol Index for a studied feature $j$. This index comes from Sensitivity Analysis and can be adapted to Feature Importance. It provides an interpretable measure that helps us carry out Conditional Independence Testing with statistical control.
We then discuss three model-agnostic estimation procedures, meaning they can be applied to any black-box model:
$\mathbf{1. Leave One Covariate Out (LOCO):}$ compares the performance of the original model with that of a refitted version excluding feature $j$.
$\mathbf{2. Conditional Feature Importance (CFI):}$ compares the performance of the same black-box model when the $j$-th coordinate is conditionally perturbed against the original input. We highlight its performance in variable selection through a
double robustness
property: identifying null covariates requires either a well-specified conditional sampler or a well-specified model.
$\mathbf{3. Sobol-CFI:}$ a generalization of CFI based on marginalization, i.e., the performance decay of the original model compared with its conditionally averaged version. For this procedure, we correct the bias and present a nonparametric efficiency result.
Finally, we discuss type-I error control for the presented procedures and provide numerical experiments illustrating the estimation findings. The results show that Sobol-CFI retains the double robustness property in practice.
Authors
Angel D. Reyero Lobo, INRIA Paris Saclay and Institut de Mathémathiques de Toulouse, France, angel.reyero-lobo@inria.fr
Bertrand Thirion, INRIA, Université Paris Saclay, France, bertrand.thirion@inria.fr
Pierre Neuvial, Institut de Mathématiques de Toulouse, France, pierre.neuvial@math.univ-toulouse.fr

sessions.title: Structure-preserving Uncertainty Quantification - Part III of III
Matrix and Tensor Computation Meets Uncertainty Quantification - Part III of III

talks.title: Subspace Accelerated Measure Transport for Fast and Scalable Sequential Experimental Design

speakers.name: Karina Koval
speakers.affiliation: Universität Heidelberg, Germany; Tiangang Cui, University of Sydney, Australia; Roland Herzog, Universität Heidelberg, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a novel approach for sequential optimal experimental design (sOED) for Bayesian inverse problems involving expensive models with large-dimensional unknown parameters. The focus of this work is on designs that maximize the expected information gain (EIG) from prior to posterior, which is a computationally challenging task in the non-Gaussian setting. This challenge is amplified in sOED, as the incremental expected information gain (iEIG) must be approximated multiple times in distinct stages, with both prior and posterior distributions often being intractable. To address this, we derive a derivative-based upper bound for the iEIG, which not only guides design placement but also enables the construction of projectors onto likelihood-informed subspaces, facilitating parameter dimension reduction. By combining this approach with conditional measure transport maps for the sequence of posteriors, we develop a unified framework for sOED, together with amortized inference, scalable to high- and infinite-dimensional problems. Numerical experiments for two inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) demonstrate the effectiveness of designs that maximize our proposed upper bound.

Robert Scheichl, Universität Heidelberg, Germany, r.scheichl@uni-heidelberg.de

talks.title: Cross-DEIM for Nonlinear PDEs in Tensor Network Format
talks.abstract:
.
Some of the most computationally demanding problems in science and engineering involve  large-scale and high-dimensional matrix and tensor differential equations (MDEs and TDEs).  These arise in applications ranging from the Schr¨odinger equation and kinetic transport  models (Fokker–Planck and Boltzmann equations) to probability density function transport  in turbulent combustion. Low-rank tensor network approximations provide a principled  framework to address this challenge by exploiting intrinsic low-dimensional structure and  multi-dimensional correlations in the solution manifold. In this presentation, we focus on  computational strategies for solving nonlinear MDEs and TDEs directly in adaptive lowrank  form. We present a novel formulation based on adaptive cross interpolation and develop  CUR-type decompositions grounded in the Discrete Empirical Interpolation Method (DEIM)  for matrix, tensor-train, and Tucker representations. These DEIM-CUR algorithms enable  on-the-fly reduced-order modeling with near-optimal accuracy for broad classes of nonlinear  dynamical systems. We demonstrate the effectiveness of these approaches across applications  in turbulent combustion, sensitivity analysis, uncertainty quantification, and both highdimensional  and large-scale partial differential equations.

sessions.title: Innovations in Practical OED: Recent Advances in Methods and Applications - Part III of III

talks.title: Step-Dad: Semi-Amortized Policy-Based Bayesian Experimental Design
talks.abstract:
.
We develop a semi-amortized, policy-based, approach to Bayesian experimental design (BED) called Stepwise Deep Adaptive Design (Step-DAD). Like existing, fully amortized, policy-based BED approaches, Step-DAD trains a design policy upfront before the experiment. However, rather than keeping this policy fixed, Step-DAD periodically updates it as data is gathered, refining it to the particular experimental instance. This test-time adaptation improves both the flexibility and the robustness of the design strategy compared with existing approaches. Empirically, Step-DAD consistently demonstrates superior decision-making and robustness compared with current state-of-the-art BED methods.
Authors
Marcel Hedman, University of Oxford, United Kingdom, marcel.hedman@jesus.ox.ac.uk

talks.title: Wildfire Perimeter Estimation from Emergency Call Data

speakers.name: Mohammad Farazmand
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Wildfires are increasingly reaching populated areas, heightening the need for accurate forecasts. Because real-time observations are often limited, predictions first require estimating the fire perimeter from sparse data. Among existing state reconstruction methods, Discrete Empirical Interpolation Method (DEIM) is notable for its computational efficiency and interpretability. However, DEIM may return unphysical values if the true state variable possesses upper or lower bound constraints. More specifically, DEIM estimates may contain values outside the physical bounds, such as negative mass density. In this talk, we present Constrained DEIM (C-DEIM), a method designed to accurately reconstruct states with known upper and lower bounds. C-DEIM employs soft constraints to enforce these bounds, generating realistic reconstructions suitable for downstream tasks. We demonstrate the effectiveness of C-DEIM by reconstructing and forecasting the 2023 Maui wildfire perimeter from emergency call data.
Authors
Louisa Ebby, North Carolina State University, U.S., lbebby@ncsu.edu
Mohammad Farazmand, North Carolina State University, U.S., farazmand@ncsu.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification at the Interface of Kinetic Theory and Applications

talks.title: Particle Methods for Plasma Equations with Uncertainties
talks.abstract:
.
Plasma models are studied at the kinetic level by the Landau equation and have gained a lot of interest due to the important applications related to fusion reactors and ongoing projects. Moreover, the importance of considering uncertainties into the PDEs is growing, partly due to the increased presence of real-world data.  The construction of numerical methods for such equation with random inputs is a challenging problem, thanks to the high dimensional structure of the equation, involving both phase space variables and stochastic parameters, and the formation of multiscale structures that must be captured by the numerical schemes. Furthermore, a numerical solver must be able  to preserve the structural physical properties such as the non-negativity of the distribution function, the main conservations of invariant quantities, the entropy dissipation, and the equilibrium states. Besides, the regularity of the solution with respect to the random parameter is crucial to derive efficient numerical methods, and must be investigated.  Recently, a new class of numerical methods that combine a particle-based approximation of the distribution function in the phase space together with a stochastic Galerkin expansion of the particles in the random space has been proposed. These methods are spectrally accurate in the space of the random parameters, thanks to the sG formulation, and conserve the structural properties of the equation, because of the particle nature.
Authors
Andrea Medaglia, Universita' di Ferrara, Italy, andrea.medaglia@unife.it
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Control of Magnetized Plasmas by External Electric Fields
talks.abstract:
.
The stability of magnetized plasmas is highly sensitive to model assumptions and perturbations, making quantitative analysis and control a challenging task. We investigate a control problem for uniformly magnetized plasmas with external electric fields. Linear analysis around general equilibria is carried out using a Laplace–Fourier transform framework, and the associated Penrose-type stability condition for Bernstein modes is derived and numerically verified. Building on this characterization, we introduce a general control strategy that recovers the free-streaming solution as a benchmark case. Several case studies, including Gaussian equilibria and the Dory–Guest–Harris instability, illustrate how the proposed control strategy can robustly suppress instabilities. These results highlight a pathway for including the discussion of UQ in inverse and control problems for kinetic equations. There are interesting directions to extend this problem by bringing data-driven approaches and machine learning into the picture.
Authors
Peiyi Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., pchen345@wisc.edu
Rogerio Jorge, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., rogerio.jorge@wisc.edu
Qin Li, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., qinli@math.wisc.edu
Yukun Yue, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., yyue24@wisc.edu

talks.title: Convergence of Fourier-Galerkin Spectral Method for the Boltzmann Equation with Uncertainties

speakers.name: Kunlun Qi
speakers.affiliation: Michigan State University, U.S.; Liu Liu, Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we will present the results about the Boltzmann equation with uncertainties and prove that the spectral convergence of the semi-discretized numerical system holds in a combined velocity and random space, where the Fourier spectral method is applied for approximation in the velocity space, whereas the generalized polynomial chaos (gPC)-based stochastic Galerkin (SG) method is employed to discretize the random variable. Our proof is based on a delicate energy estimate for showing the well-posedness of the numerical solution as well as a rigorous control of its negative part in our well-designed functional space that involves high-order derivatives of both the velocity and random variables.

Kunlun Qi, Michigan State University, U.S., kunlunqi.math@gmail.com
Liu Liu, Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, liuliu@cuhk.edu.hk

sessions.title: UQ for Multiscale Modeling in Computational Chemistry - Part II of II

talks.title: Interatomic Potential Uncertainty Estimation and Propagation Via Embedded Model Error

speakers.name: Cristian Lacey
speakers.affiliation: Mary Alice Cusentino, Habib N. Najm

talks.abstract:
.
Novel materials capable of withstanding the extreme environments in fusion energy devices are often explored via molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, enabling researchers to probe material properties and behavior under representative loading conditions. In the interest of computational efficiency, such MD simulations often involve a choice of interatomic potential (IAP) tuned to match data from more expensive, higher-fidelity approaches (i.e. density functional theory), though this choice inevitably introduces uncertainty. The present work aims to estimate the uncertainty associated with two IAPs: the Spectral Neighbor Analysis Potential (SNAP) and Atomic Cluster Expansion (ACE). Uncertainty associated with SNAP is estimated via an embedded model error approach, in which uncertainty is embedded into the model form by prescribing a distribution to the model coefficients as well as the moments that parameterize that distribution. The maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate of the model covariance is obtained from the resulting Bayesian inverse problem, accounting for model form in uncertainty estimates in derived energies, forces, and stresses across multiple materials. The embedded model error approach is compared to traditional Bayesian linear regression. Further efforts explore the construction of a polynomial chaos surrogate for more efficient forward propagation of uncertainties in elastic properties, binding energies, and defect properties obtained with an ACE potential.
Authors
Cristian Lacey, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., celacey@sandia.gov
Mary Alice Cusentino, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., mcusent@sandia.gov
Habib N. Najm, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., hnnajm@sandia.gov
Khachik Sargsyan, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ksargsy@sandia.gov

talks.title: Dimension Reduction on Hilbert Spaces of Velocity Fields of SDEs

speakers.name: Joanna Zou
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Han Cheng Lie, Universität Potsdam, Germany; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
In a controlled stochastic differential equation (SDE), the control is a velocity field which serves to guide the solution of the SDE toward a chosen terminal time-marginal probability measure, or alternatively, to represent uncertainty in the drift coefficient of the SDE. Many problems involve optimization or sampling over a function space of controls, where either task proves challenging when the control is defined in infinite dimensions. To address this challenge, we take a dimension reduction approach of solving for an optimal rank-r subspace of a Hilbert space of permissible controls and use it to construct efficient ridge approximations of target functionals of the control. We articulate sufficient conditions on the Hilbert space of controls such that the corresponding controlled SDEs have weak solutions and that one can specify a valid Cameron-Martin space embedded in the Hilbert space. Finally, we evaluate the advantages of our dimension reduction approach for two applications, 1) minimization of the objective functional of a stochastic optimal control problem, and 2) forward uncertainty quantification of functionals which represent reaction rates in molecular dynamical systems.
Authors
Joanna Zou, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., jjzou@mit.edu
Han Cheng Lie, Universität Potsdam, Germany, hanlie@uni-potsdam.de
Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., ymarz@mit.edu

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for UQ in Earth Science - Part III of III

talks.title: Quantifying and Propagating Uncertainty in Cloud and Precipitation Processes in Weather and Climate Simulations Via Deterministic and Stochastic Methods

speakers.name: Kaitlyn Loftus
speakers.affiliation: Columbia University, U.S.; Hugh Morrison, National Center for Atmospheric Research, U.S.; Adele Igel, University of California, Davis, U.S.; Mikael Witte, Naval Postgraduate School, U.S.; Arthur Hu, Columbia University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Cloud processes are a leading source of uncertainty in climate projections owing to the difficulty in representing the microscale processes that produce clouds, rain, and snow with the limited degrees of freedom available in Earth System Models (ESMs). I will present recent work addressing parametric uncertainties, quantifying them in both high resolution and global models using ML-assisted Bayesian inference. This ability is largely novel, and allows for uncertainty in climate projections to be provided, facilitating risk assessment, as well as allowing for quantification of observational information content. However, there are are also structural sources of uncertainty in representation of clouds in ESMs – these require a different approach. Machine learning has provides some benefits, with some difficulties regarding implementation. However, more fundamentally there is strong reason to believe that many uncertainties, in particular structural uncertainties, should be represented \em{stochastically}, with spatiotemporal covariability that matches natural variations. I will present preliminary work in this direction, and discuss future steps.
Authors
Marcus Van Lier-Walqui, Columbia University, U.S., mv2525@columbia.edu
Kaitlyn Loftus, Columbia University, U.S., kaitlyn.loftus@columbia.edu

Adele Igel, University of California, Davis, U.S., aigel@ucdavis.edu
Mikael Witte, Naval Postgraduate School, U.S., mikael.witte@nps.edu
Arthur Hu, Columbia University, U.S., zh2611@columbia.edu

talks.title: Accelerations to Climate Model Development Using An Ensemble Kalman Inversion Toolkit

speakers.name: Tapio Schneider
speakers.affiliation: California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Eviatar Bach, University of Reading, United Kingdom; Costa Christopolous, California Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Ensemble Kalman Processes (EKP) can be applied to calibrate model parameters of expensive computer codes from indirect statistical data. Ensembles of simulations at different model parameter configurations are iteratively updated to fit a fixed indirect statistical observation using Kalman-type updates, as seen with the ensemble Kalman filter in data assimilation (DA) literature. Viewing EKP as applications of DA methodology to inverse problems has lead to enhancements such as localization (of covariances/observations/domain) to increase scalability, and inflation to help with model error. Meanwhile, viewing EKP as an iterative black-box optimizer yields enhancements such as adaptive learning rates and momentum acceleration to prevent stagnation.
We showcase how this workhorse can provide a variety of improvements to Climate model development for the CliMA (Climate Modeling Alliance) model. We showcase its use to adapt training point selection for accelerated sampling algorithms for parametric uncertainty (Calibrate Emulate Sample). We also showcase how it enables online learning for model-error diagnosis and improvement of closures for parameterizations (Calibration-assisted Development).
Authors

talks.title: Simulation-based Uncertainty Quantification for Remote Sensing Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Amy Braverman
speakers.affiliation: Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology

talks.abstract:
.
Remote sensing data provide a vast trove of information about Earth systems. These data are inferred from electromagnetic spectra observed by Earth-orbiting instruments using computational algorithms that approximately solve the following inverse problem. Given a spectrum, find the best estimate of the underlying true geophysical state that gave rise to it. The algorithms typically start with an initial guess for the true state, and iteratively try to minimize cost functions based on differences between predicted spectra from a deterministic forward model, and spectra actually observed. This set-up incorporates many important aspects related to uncertainty quantification for complex physical-statistical systems including intractable likelihoods, unknown physics, parameter calibration, computational demands, etc. The remote sensing problem is also inherently spatial, which is both a curse, in that it usually involves massive amounts of data, and a blessing in that it makes dimension reduction possible if we can learn and quantify spatial structure. In this talk I will discuss recent work that demonstrates how we leverage simulation-based inference, spatial statistical modeling with Gaussian processes, and conditional spatial simulation to obtain robust uncertainty estimates for the output of a remote sensing data processing pipeline. If time permits, I will also show results of some preliminary work to quantify and incorporate model discrepancy.
Authors
Amy Braverman, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, Amy.Braverman@jpl.nasa.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Improving Uncertainty Quantification in Atmospheric Data Assimilation with Machine Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Atmospheric data assimilation (DA) integrates observations with model forecasts to produce optimized atmospheric states, which is critical for stable weather forecasting and reliable climate research. Modern DA is posed in a Bayesian framework that combines a model-based prior (background) with an observation likelihood. However, the high dimensionality of the atmospheric state and evolving cross-variable dependencies make estimating uncertainty in the background-error prior particularly challenging. This study leverages machine learning to address these challenges.First, we propose Latent Data Assimilation (LDA), which performs variational DA in a latent space learned from multivariate global atmospheric data via an autoencoder. The autoencoder decorrelates atmospheric variables and captures nonlinear physical relationships, enabling LDA to produce balanced analyses even with a diagonal error covariance matrix. Second, we introduce Diffusion-DA, a score-based framework that learns a generative,  non-Gaussian background prior and incorporates observations to guide denoising, yielding approximate posterior samples. Beyond this, we employ preference-based reinforcement learning to calibrate the background prior’s uncertainty, promoting analyses that align with DA objectives such as physical consistency and improved forecast performance.
Authors
Hang Fan, Columbia University, U.S., hf2526@columbia.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Generative AI for Data Assimilation - Part I of II

talks.title: Improving the Fidelity of PDE Data with Diffusion Models

speakers.name: Yulong Lu
speakers.affiliation: University of Minnesota, Twin Cities, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Solving partial differential equations (PDEs) on fine spatio-temporal scales for high-fidelity solutions is critical for scientific discoveries, but can be prohibitively expensive.  As an alternative, super-resolution techniques enable the enhancement of low-fidelity, low-resolution simulations. In this talk, I will present a family of diffusion-based super-resolution methods that leverage score-based generative modeling to infer sub-grid information while respecting the governing physics. Through extensive numerical experiments, we demonstrate that these approaches retain the accuracy of full-resolution solvers while significantly reducing computational expense.
Authors
Yulong Lu, University of Minnesota, Twin Cities, U.S., yulonglu@umn.edu

talks.title: Generative Modeling in Partially Observed Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
We present a measure transport approach for solving inverse problems sequentially in stochastic dynamical systems. By utilizing a Bayesian framework, we construct a generative modeling method that maps the prior processes to amortized posterior processes. This can then generate data-assimilated trajectories and perform downstream tasks such as time series prediction and system identification.
Authors
Nicole Yang, University of Tennessee, Knoxville, U.S., nicoley031@gmail.com

talks.title: A Diffusion Model-Based Data Assimilation Framework for Uncertainty Reduction in Learning Data-Driven Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
We present a hybrid data assimilation framework that combines long short-  term memory (LSTM) neural networks with the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF)  for nonlinear dynamical systems and operator learning. LSTM models provide  accurate short-term forecasts, but their performance deteriorates over longer horizons due to error accumulation. To overcome this limitation, observational data are assimilated using EnSF, which updates the ensemble of LSTM forecasts while accommodating nonlinear and non-Gaussian state distributions. Numerical experiments with the 20-dimensional Lorenz 96 system demonstrate that the LSTM–EnSF framework effectively corrects long-term forecast errors and yields robust state estimation, highlighting its promise for improving data-driven prediction in complex dynamical systems. Our results demonstrate EnSF’s comparative effectiveness in the realm of  traditional scientific machine learning.
Authors
Jingqiao Tang, Florida State University, U.S., jt21v@fsu.edu
Feng Bao, Florida State University, U.S., fbao@fsu.edu

talks.title: Conditional Flow-Matching for Data Assimilation

speakers.name: Brianna Binder
speakers.affiliation: University of Southern Carolina, U.S.; Agnimitra Dasgupta

talks.abstract:
.
Traditional data assimilation techniques face significant challenges in high-dimensional problems where process dynamics and observation operators are nonlinear, and where both process and observation noise are non-Gaussian and non-additive. In this talk, we will explore the use of flow-matching algorithms to address these challenges. Conditional flow-matching algorithms enable the transport of samples from the prior distribution to the posterior distribution using knowledge of samples from the joint distribution. We will demonstrate how such algorithms can be applied to approximate the update step in the Bayes filter for data assimilation. Furthermore, we will show how they facilitate the use of black-box forward dynamics and observation models, thereby accommodating a broad class of stochastic models for both the forward process and the observation operator. Finally, we will discuss the computational costs associated with training and applying these models, as well as their application in solving challenging data assimilation problems.
Authors

Adaptive Data Acquisition Methods In Scientific Machine Learning - Part I of III

talks.title: Scalable and Practical Bayesian Deep Learning with Variational Last Layers: Methods and Applications
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian deep learning methods have had minimal impact on the largest, most impactful neural network models. In this talk, I will describe progress on highly scalable Bayesian methods that can be easily built into existing architectures. By focusing only on uncertainty in the last (few) layers, combined with a minimal set of maximally impactful architectural interventions, we achieve scalable and practical uncertainty quantification methods that are well suited to modern neural network design. I will describe use of these models in active learning tasks including Bayesian optimization, showing state of the art performance on various scientific problems.
Authors
James Harrison, Google DeepMind, U.S., jamesharrison@google.com
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Covering Multiple Objectives with a Small Set of Solutions Using Bayesian Optimization
talks.abstract:
.
In multi-objective black-box optimization, the goal is typically to find solutions that optimize a set of T black-box objective functions simultaneously. Traditional approaches often seek a single Pareto-optimal set that balances trade-offs among all objectives. In our recent work, we consider a problem setting that departs from this paradigm: finding a smaller set of K solutions, where K < T, that collectively "covers" the T objectives. A set of solutions is defined as "covering" if, for each objective, there is at least one good solution. A motivating example for this problem setting occurs in drug design. For example, we may have T pathogens and aim to identify a set of K < T antibiotics such that at least one antibiotic can be used to treat each pathogen. To address this problem, we propose Multi-Objective Coverage Bayesian Optimization (MOCOBO), a principled algorithm designed to efficiently find a covering set of solutions.
Authors
Natalie T. Maus, University of Pennsylvania, U.S., nmaus@seas.upenn.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Direct Regret Optimization in Bayesian Optimization
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian optimization is a powerful paradigm for optimizing expensive black-box functions. Traditional BO methods typically rely on separate hand-crafted acquisition functions and surrogate models for the underlying function and often operate in a myopic manner. We propose a novel direct regret optimization approach that jointly learns the optimal model and non-myopic acquisition by distilling from a set of candidate models and acquisitions,  and explicitly targets minimizing the multi-step regret. Our framework leverages an ensemble of Gaussian Processes to generate simulated BO trajectories, each guided by an acquisition function chosen from a pool of conventional choices, until a Bayesian early stop criterion is met. These simulated trajectories, capturing multi-step exploration strategies, are used to train an end-to-end decision transformer that directly learns to select next query points aimed at improving the ultimate objective. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world benchmarks suggest that our method consistently outperforms BO baselines, achieving lower simple regret and demonstrating more robust exploration in high-dimensional or noisy settings.
Authors
Fengxue Zhang, University of Chicago, U.S., zhangfx@uchicago.edu
Yuxin Chen, University of Chicago, U.S., chenyuxin@uchicago.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Mowe : A Mixture of Weather Experts

speakers.name: Romit Maulik
speakers.affiliation: Pennsylvania State University, U.S.; Sanjay Choudhry, Peter Harrington, Mohammad A. Nabian

talks.abstract:
.
Data-driven weather models have recently achieved state-of-the-art performance, yet progress has plateaued in recent years. This paper introduces a Mixture of Experts (MoWE) approach as a novel paradigm to overcome these limitations, not by creating a new forecaster, but by optimally combining the outputs of existing models. The MoWE model is trained with significantly lower computational resources than the individual experts. Our model employs a Vision Transformer-based gating network that dynamically learns to weight the contributions of multiple "expert" models at each grid point, conditioned on forecast lead time. This approach creates a synthesized deterministic forecast that is more accurate than any individual component in terms of Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE). Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of this method, achieving up to a 10% lower RMSE than the best-performing AI weather model on a 2-day forecast horizon, significantly outperforming individual experts as well as a simple average across experts. This work presents a computationally efficient and scalable strategy to push the state of the art in data-driven weather prediction by making the most out of leading high-quality forecast models.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Scientific Machine Learning: Surrogates, Algorithms, and Applications

talks.title: Bayesian NNs with Meaningful Functional Priors
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) quantify the uncertainty in the output of a neural network by constructing a Bayesian posterior distribution over the network parameters. However, the prior, which is of key importance in any Bayesian setting, is rarely meaningful for BNNs. This is because the complexity of the input-to-output map of a BNN makes it difficult to understand how certain distributions enforce any interpretable constraint on the output space. Gaussian processes (GPs), on the other hand, are often preferred in uncertainty quantification tasks due to their interpretability. The drawback is that GPs are limited to small datasets without considerable effort. To combine the two techniques, we introduce a new class of priors for BNNs which approximates a desired GP. Unlike previous approaches, our method works by defining a prior directly over the network parameters from the Mercer representation of the covariance kernel, and does not rely on the network having a specific structure. In doing so, we can exploit the scalability of BNNs in a meaningful Bayesian way.
Authors
Alex Alberts, Purdue University, U.S., albert31@purdue.edu
Ilias Bilionis, Purdue University, U.S., ibilion@purdue.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Constrained Conditional Flow Matching Algorithms for the Solution of Probabilistic Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Flow matching models are generative models that use neural networks to approximate the velocity field corresponding to fixed conditional probability paths between a reference distribution and a desired target distribution. This velocity field can be shown to satisfy the appropriate continuity equation that describes the transport of the probability density functions of the reference distribution and the posterior distribution. In this work, we show that flow matching is a powerful tool for solving inverse problems within the Bayesian paradigm. Flow matching offers several advantages, such as the flexibility of choosing the reference distribution, including complex data-driven priors, accommodating intractable likelihoods, and amortizing the cost of inference. Specifically, we compare flow matching models built using the Gaussian distribution and the prior for the inverse problem as the reference distribution, where the latter represents the prior to posterior transport map. In this work, we also explore how constraints can be enforced within the framework of flow matching such that the final solution continues to be faithful to the posterior distribution for the inverse problem. Results show that flow matching can be an effective framework for solving probabilistic inverse problems with constraints.
Authors
Assad A. Oberai, University of Southern California, U.S., aoberai@usc.edu
Ali Fardisi, University of Southern California, U.S., fardisi@usc.edu
Mehrnegar Aminy, University of Southern California, U.S., maminy@usc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Uncertainty-aware Virtual Sensing for Critical Energy Infrastructure with Distribution-free Guarantees

speakers.name: Kazuma Kobayashi
speakers.affiliation: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, U.S.; Shailesh Garg, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, India; Seid Koric, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, U.S.; Syed Bahauddin Alam , University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

talks.abstract:
.
Neural operator networks provide efficient surrogates for complex physical systems, but their adoption in safety-critical domains is restricted by the lack of calibrated, spatially resolved uncertainty quantification (UQ). Accuracy alone is insufficient: an incorrect prediction with unwarranted confidence can be catastrophic. Risks grow under sparse sensing, distributional shifts, and unseen conditions, where even advanced operators may fail silently. Existing UQ methods fall short. Bayesian neural networks are costly and fragile to priors. Deep ensembles improve coverage but require heavy training and memory. Quantile regression is lightweight yet uncalibrated. What good is accuracy if predictions cannot be trusted? We introduce the Conformalized Monte Carlo Operator (CMCO), the first framework to deliver distribution-free, spatially resolved UQ for neural operators with guaranteed conditional coverage using a single model. CMCO combines Monte Carlo dropout with split conformal prediction to yield calibrated intervals without retraining or ensembling, and applies directly to DeepONet and S-DeepONet. Demonstrations on turbulent flows, elastoplastic solids, and global radiation mapping confirm robust coverage. By embedding distribution-free calibration into operator learning, CMCO makes UQ a core capability of neural surrogates for safety-critical science and engineering.
Authors
Kazuma Kobayashi, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, U.S., kazumak2@illinois.edu
Shailesh Garg, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, India, amz218308@am.iitd.ac.in
Seid Koric, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, U.S., koric@illinois.edu
Syed Bahauddin Alam, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, alams@illinois.edu

talks.title: Neural \& Polynomial Chaos: Spectral Stochastic Methods for Operator Learning

speakers.name: Himanshu Sharma
speakers.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, U.S.; Bahador Bahmani, Northwestern University, U.S.; Lukas Novak, Brno University of Technology, Canada; Yannis Kevrekidis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Building surrogate models for uncertainty quantification is essential for many applications where randomness is unavoidable. The Polynomial Chaos Expansion (PCE) is widely recognized as a to-go method for constructing stochastic solutions in both intrusive and non-intrusive ways. However, to date, its application as a surrogate model has been limited to a functional regression setting. In this work, we first extend the PCE formulation to operator learning&#8212demonstrating that PCE can provide an extremely computationally efficient alternative to neural network-based operator learning methods. However, PCE may not afford the flexibility that neural networks provide. For example, PCE requires specialized treatments to manage random variables that do not follow specified families of distributions or are not marginally independent. In this work, we then adopt the same formalism as the spectral expansion used in PCE; however, we replace the classical polynomial basis functions with neural networks to leverage their expressivity. To achieve this, we propose an algorithm that identifies orthogonal neural network-based basis functions in a purely data-driven manner, without any prior assumptions about the joint distribution of the random variables involved. This approach may offer greater flexibility for complex stochastic models. We demonstrate these schemes through several numerical examples of varying complexity.
Authors
Michael D. Shields, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., michael.shields@jhu.edu

Yannis Kevrekidis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., yannisk@jhu.edu

sessions.title: UQ for Transformers & Transformers for UQ - Part I of II

talks.title: Transformers for Hybrid Dynamics: A UQ Perspective
talks.abstract:
.
Transformers have recently emerged as foundation models for time series prediction, but their application to hybrid dynamics—where discrete transitions interact with continuous flows—remains an open challenge. We propose a transformer-based framework designed with structures suited for capturing both types of dynamics. From an uncertainty quantification perspective, our study will compare how uncertainty propagates through interpolation and extrapolation in hybrid dynamics, relative to existing models. A further focus is on how incorporating Hamilton–Jacobi–Bellman (HJB) formulations with regularizations and noise injections can provide mathematically informed structure, potentially improving robustness and offering new insight into uncertainty in predictive modeling.
Authors
Hyemin Gu, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, U.S., hgu@umass.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Optimal Control for Transformer Architectures: Enhancing Generalization, Robustness and Efficiency

speakers.name: Kelvin Kan
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We study Transformers through the perspective of optimal control theory, using tools from continuous-time formulations to derive actionable insights into training and architecture design. This framework improves the performance of existing Transformer models while providing desirable theoretical guarantees, including generalization and robustness. Our framework is designed to be plug-and-play, enabling seamless integration with established Transformer models and requiring only slight changes to the implementation. We conduct seven extensive experiments on tasks motivated by text generation, sentiment analysis, image classification, and point cloud classification. Experimental results show that the framework improves the test performance of the baselines, while being more parameter-efficient. On character-level text generation with nanoGPT, our framework achieves a 46% reduction in final test loss while using 42% fewer parameters. On GPT-2, our framework achieves a 5.6% reduction in final test loss, demonstrating scalability to larger models. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work that applies optimal control theory to both the training and architecture of Transformers. It offers a new foundation for systematic, theory-driven improvements and moves beyond costly trial-and-error approaches.
Authors
Kelvin Kan, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S., kaifung0620@gmail.com

talks.title: Icon to Genicon: Probabilistic Operator Learning with Uncertainty Quantification
talks.abstract:
.
In-context operator networks (ICON) learn solution operators for ODEs/PDEs by conditioning on example initial/boundary data and their solutions. I’ll present a probabilistic interpretation: ICON implicitly computes the posterior predictive mean given the context. Using random differential equations, this connects ICON to Bayesian inference and naturally leads to a generative variant, GenICON, that samples from the posterior predictive for principled uncertainty quantification. The framework unifies operator learning under a Bayesian lens and delivers uncertainty-aware predictions.
Authors
Siting Liu, University of California Riverside, siting.liu1@ucr.edu

sessions.title: Measure Flows for Inverse Problems and Machine Learning - Part II of III

talks.title: Generative Modeling without Clean Data: Self-Consistent Transport under Black-Box Corruptions
talks.abstract:
.
Generative modeling aims to learn an underlying data distribution from samples. In many scientific and engineering settings, however, clean samples are never observed; instead, data are available only after passing through a noisy, possibly nonlinear and ill-conditioned corruption channel. The challenge is therefore to learn a generative model for the clean distribution using only corrupted observations and access to the forward process.
In this talk, I introduce the Self-Consistent Stochastic Interpolant (SCSI), a transport-based framework that inverts such corruption channels at the level of distributions. The method iteratively refines a transport map so that, when composed with the forward model, it reproduces the observed corrupted distribution. This fixed-point formulation yields an efficient and flexible algorithm requiring only black-box evaluations of the forward operator. We establish convergence guarantees under suitable assumptions and demonstrate strong empirical performance on high-dimensional problems in imaging and scientific reconstruction.
Joint work with Chirag Modi, Eric Vanden-Eijnden, and Joan Bruna (arXiv:2512.10857).
Authors
Jiequn Han, Flatiron Institute, U.S., jhan@flatironinstitute.org
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: On the Use of Diffusion Priors for Scientific Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
This talk focuses on algorithms that utilize pretrained diffusion models as priors for inverse problems. The first section introduces InverseBench, a study of various plug-and-play diffusion prior algorithms developed recently. We assess and compare these algorithms across five distinct scientific inverse problems, offering new insights into their effectiveness and limitations. The second section addresses the often-overlooked aspect of uncertainty calibration. We present Blade, a derivative-free method for calibrated Bayesian inversion. We establish convergence analysis and provide explicit error bounds to quantify how approximations in the learned score function and statistical linearization affect the sampling. On both controlled linear setting and challenging nonlinear fluid dynamics problems, we show that Blade is capable of producing well-calibrated posterior samples while existing derivative-free methods fail.
Authors
Hongkai Zheng, California Institute of Technology, U.S., hzzheng@caltech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: GenFocal: Generative Downscaling for Climate Risk Assessment and Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
Assessing climate risk is critical to decision-making across many social and economic domains, but it requires accurate, high-resolution information that coarse global climate models, the workhorse of climate research, cannot provide. Bridging this resolution gap in a computationally efficient way is a major scientific challenge.
In this talk we introduce GenFocal, an AI framework coupling flow-matching and diffusion models to generate statistically accurate, fine-scale weather from coarse climate projections, without requiring paired simulated and observed events during training. GenFocal synthesizes complex and long-lived hazards, such as heat waves and tropical cyclones, even when they are not well represented in the coarse climate projections. It also samples high-impact, rare events more accurately than leading methods.
We will introduce the framework, including its rationale, and we will show several examples showcasing its performance, which hinges on translating large-scale climate projections into actionable, localized information that can be used to assess regional climate risks.
Authors
Leonardo Zepeda-Núñez, Google, Inc., U.S., lzepedanunez@google.com
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Diffeomorphism Invariant Measure Flows in Bayes Hilbert Spaces
talks.abstract:
.
Measure flows—time-parametrized evolutions of measures—are now central to generative modeling, sampling, and Bayesian inverse problems; however, many practically useful dynamics fall outside the classical metric gradient-flow formalism. In this work, we develop an alternative framework based on Bayes Hilbert spaces. We first extend the theory of Bayes Hilbert spaces by giving a new construction using a “Gelfand triple” which brings singular Radon measures into the theory while retaining a compatible Hilbert structure. We apply this framework to study diffeomorphism invariant flows, which enjoy convergence rates that are independent of the target measure. We prove two structural results: (i) pushforward by a diffeomorphism is an isometric isomorphism between Bayes Hilbert spaces, meaning the geometry of Bayes Hilbert spaces itself is diffeomorphism invariant, and (ii) the Fisher–Rao gradient flow of Kullback–Leibler divergence, the only diffeomorphism invariant flow in the classical metric gradient flow framework, coincides with straight-line interpolation in a Bayes Hilbert space. Building on these, we introduce new diffeomorphism-invariant dynamics—q-flows and Bayes Hilbert splines—that are more flexible than the Fisher–Rao flow but still preserve full diffeomorphism invariance. Numerical simulations for sampling and inverse problems demonstrate the practical utility of these new flows.
Authors
David Mis, Rice University, U.S., david.mis@rice.edu
Maarten V. de Hoop, Rice University, U.S., mdehoop@rice.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Uncertainty Quantification for Scientific Machine Learning and Digital Twins - Part III of III

talks.title: Physics-Informed Laplace Neural Operator for Learning Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
Differential equations are fundamental for modeling complex scientific and engineering processes. Operator learning offers a powerful framework for approximating the mapping from parametric inputs such as initial/boundary conditions or forcings to the corresponding solutions. However, purely data-driven operator learning methods such as the Laplace Neural Operator (LNO) typically require large training datasets and exhibit limited extrapolation capabilities. We propose the Physics-Informed Laplace Neural Operator (PILNO), which embeds the governing physical laws directly within the learning process. This physics-informed approach reduces data dependency, enabling effective learning in small-data regimes and enhancing generalization to out-of-distribution inputs where purely data-driven LNOs often fail. By leveraging both data (when available) and physics, PILNO offers a more robust and data-efficient pathway to learning solution operators. We demonstrate advantages of PILNO across diverse parametric differential equation problems, highlighting its improved data efficiency and extrapolation performance.

Minseok Choi, POSTECH Mathematics Institute, Korea |, mchoi@postech.ac.kr

talks.title: A Multi-Fidelity Method for Velocity Discretization of Boltzmann Equations
talks.abstract:
.
This talk is concerned with the approximation of probability distributions known up to normalization constants, with a focus on Bayesian inference for large-scale inverse problems in scientific computing. In this context, key challenges include costly repeated evaluations of forward models, multimodality, and inaccessible gradients for the forward model. To address them, we develop a variational inference framework that combines Fisher-Rao natural gradient with specialized quadrature rules to enable derivative free updates of Gaussian mixture variational families. The resulting method, termed Derivative Free Gaussian Mixture Variational Inference (DF-GMVI), guarantees covariance positivity and affine invariance, offering a stable and efficient framework for approximating complex posterior distributions. The effectiveness of DF-GMVI is demonstrated through numerical experiments on challenging scenarios, including distributions with multiple modes, infinitely many modes, and curved modes in spaces with up to 100 dimensions. The method's practicality is further demonstrated in a large-scale application, where it successfully recovers the initial conditions of the Navier-Stokes equations from solution data at positive times.
Authors
Zhengyu Huang, Peking University, China, huangdz@bicmr.pku.edu.cn

talks.title: Structure-Informed Uncertainty Quantification in Generative Models

speakers.name: Ziyu Chen
speakers.affiliation: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
I will present a novel framework for learning unknown data distributions with known underlying structures. I will rigorously demonstrate how incorporating the structure into models can reduce the model uncertainty along with a new Wasserstein Uncertainty Propagation result on unbounded domains with sample complexity. This framework applies to both SDE-based generative models and deterministic flow models, and may fit in broader scenarios with physical constraints. As a particular example, I will show that equivariant diffusion models can provably learn distributions with symmetries efficiently. This is a joint work with Markos Katsoulakis and Benjamin Zhang.

Ziyu Chen, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S., ziyuchen@unc.edu

sessions.title: Gaussian Processes for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of II

talks.title: Bilevel Optimization for Learning Hyperparameters: Application to Solving Pdes and Inverse Problems with Gaussian Processes
talks.abstract:
.
In the context of Gaussian conditioning, greedy algorithms iteratively select the most informative measurements, given an observed Gaussian random variable. However, the convergence analysis for conditioning Gaussian random variables remains an open problem. We adress this by introducing an operator M that allows us to transfer convergence rates of the observed Gaussian random variable approximation onto the conditional Gaussian random variable. Furthermore we apply greedy methods from approximation theory to obtain convergence rates. These greedy methods have already demonstrated optimal convergence rates within the setting of kernel based function approximation. In this paper, we establish an upper bound on the convergence rates concerning the norm of the approximation error of the conditional covariance operator.

talks.title: Fast and Accurate Spectral Methods for Irregularly Sampled Data

speakers.name: Christopher Geoga
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Paul G. Beckman, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
A common problem in exploratory data analysis is wanting to use Fourier analysis and the FFT, but being unable to due to gaps or irregularities in sampling. Many intuitive options, like smoothing to a grid and transforming or simple window-based weighted Fourier sums, have very poor properties. In this work, I will introduce a new estimator that in many cases works as well as Fourier analysis of gridded data with very few caveats: the resulting estimator can be computed in quasilinear time, analysis of finite-sample bias is natural and direct, and the approach is equally applicable in multiple dimensions. After discussing the conceptual shift used in designing this estimator and the computational apparatus to rapidly and robustly compute it, I will close with some dramatic demonstrations where this method works well and alternative irregular data approaches completely fail to give correct or informative results.

sessions.title: Scientific Machine Learning Methods and Applications for Inverse Problems and Data Assimilation - Part I of III

talks.title: The Ensemble Kalman Inversion Race

speakers.name: Matthias Morzfeld
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.; Oliver Dunbar

talks.abstract:
.
Iterative ensemble methods were initially created to solve data assimilation in the geosciences but are now popular in applications beyond their original use cases.  One application of particular interest is calibrating climate model parameters (e.g., cloud microphysics parameterizations) to climate statistics.  Many variations of iterative ensemble methods exist, including variants of ensemble Kalman inversion and the ensemble smoother.  Given the high computational cost associated with learning climate model parameters and assuming that the various ensemble methods have different relative performances, an important question one should ask is: Which iterative ensemble method should I use for climate model calibration?  We consider several ensemble methods and compare them on a series of systematic numerical experiments where we estimate model parameters from statistical observations.  All test problems are characterized by noisy loss functions, with a noise level too high for successful gradient-based optimization.  Our results indicate “unscented” methods are best for problems with low-dimensional parameter spaces.  In high-dimensional parameter spaces, the iterative ensemble Kalman filter appears to be most efficient when informative prior information is also available.  With uninformative prior information, ensemble transform methods are the most robust and efficient, making ensemble transform methods promising for learning climate model parameters from statistics.

talks.title: Information Field Theory Approach to State Estimation and Calibration of Stochastic Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
Current smoothing methods for stochastic differential equations (SDEs) scale poorly with the curse of dimensionality. In this work, we introduce an information field theory (IFT) approach for smoothing SDEs. We identify a formal prior probability measure in function space which describes the law of the SDE. Under IFT this prior can be conditioned on observations to solve the smoothing problem. To numerically characterize the associated posterior, we restrict the prior to a finite-dimensional function space in the form of basis functions. In doing so, our method scales with the number of observations, rather than the number of time steps, mitigating the curse of dimensionality.

talks.title: Multistage Neural Network for Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Yongji Wang
speakers.affiliation: Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.; Ching-Yao Lai, Stanford University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Deep learning techniques are increasingly being applied to scientific challenges, particularly inverse problems, where network precision is crucial. In practice, however, existing scientific machine learning approaches often struggle to model complex, multi-scale dynamical systems with the high precision required in scientific contexts. To address this issue, we developed Multi-Stage Neural Networks (MSNN), a method that divides the training process into sequential stages. Each stage introduces a new network optimized to fit the residual error from the previous one. In this talk, I will use illustrative examples to explain the principles and features of our method for both standard regression tasks and physics-informed neural networks. Finally, I will present a concrete example to demonstrate the potential of this approach for solving challenging inverse problems.
