sessions.title: Global Sensitivity Analysis and Interpretability of Black-box Models - Part II of II

talks.title: Explaining Models under Multivariate Bernoulli Distribution via Hoeffding Decomposition

speakers.name: Baptiste Ferrere
speakers.affiliation: Université of Toulouse, France; Nicolas Bousquet, EDF & Sorbonne Université, France; Fabrice Gamboa, University of Toulouse, France; Jean-Michel Loubes, Université of Toulouse, France; Joseph Muré, EDF R&D, France

talks.abstract:
.
The analysis and explainability study of predictive models with random inputs can be achieved by decomposition sub-models having easier interpretable features. Arising from the uncertainty quantification community, recent results have demonstrated the existence and uniqueness of a generalized Hoeffding decomposition for such predictive models when the stochastic input variables are correlated, based on concepts of oblique projection onto $\mathbb{L}^2$ subspaces. This work focuses on the case where the input variables have Bernoulli distributions and provides a complete tool for description of this decomposition. We show that in this case the underlying $\mathbb{L}^{2}$ subspaces are one-dimensional and that the functional decomposition is explicit. This leads to a complete interpretability and allows reverse engineering.   Numerous explicit indicators  that help to understand the influence of inputs on the output prediction (such as Sobol' indices, Shapley effects, etc.) are explicitely derived. Illustrated by numerical experiments, this type of analysis proves useful for addressing decision-support problems, based on binary decision diagrams, Boolean networks or binary neural networks. Beyond the case of binary inputs, the paper outlines perspectives for extending these findings to models with finite countable inputs.

talks.title: The Csiszár Index and Variable Ranking

speakers.name: Michael Baudin
speakers.affiliation: EDF R&D, France; Cristina Butucea, ENSAE ParisTech, France; Jean-Francois Delmas, École des Ponts ParisTech, France; Anne Dutfoy, EDF R&D, France; Antoine Schoonaert , Ecole Nationale des Ponts et Chaussées, France

talks.abstract:
.
We first introduce the class of $f$-divergences between two probability measures along with several classical examples. We then define the Csiszár index for a pair of random variables $(X, Y)$ as the $f$-divergence between the product of their marginals and their joint distribution. This index can be seen as a sensitivity index based on a measure of the dependence.
We show that the $f$-divergence is invariant under a general class of transformations. From this result, we derive a condition for the invariance of the Csiszár’s index. For random vectors $(X,Y)$ with atoms, the associated copula is no longer unique. We establish that the Csiszár’s index can be reformulated  as an $f$-divergence between the checkerboard product copula and the checkerboard joint copula. More generally, for any copula of $(X,Y)$, the Csiszár’s index is greater than or equal to the $f$-divergence between the product copula and the joint copula.
We develop the case where $(X, Y)$ is a Gaussian vector. When both $X$ and $Y$ are univariate, the Csiszár index is a non decreasing function of the absolute value of their correlation, for any suitable function $f$. This result is then extended to higher-dimensional settings and applied to variable ranking.

Anne Dutfoy, EDF R&D, France, Anne.Dutfoy@edf.fr

talks.title: Gradient-Enhanced Global Sensitivity Analysis with Poincar\'e Chaos Expansions

speakers.name: Olivier Roustant
speakers.affiliation: INSA Toulouse, France; Nora Luethen, ETH Zurich, Switzerland; David Heredia , INSA Toulouse, France; Bruno Sudret, ETH Zurich, Switzerland Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Chaos expansions are widely used in global sensitivity analysis (GSA), as they leverage orthogonal bases of $L^2$ spaces to efficiently compute Sobol indices, particularly in data-scarce settings. When derivatives are available, we argue that a desirable property is for the derivatives of the basis functions to also form an orthogonal basis. We demonstrate that the only basis satisfying this property is the one associated with weighted Poincar\'e inequalities and Sturm–Liouville eigenvalue problems, which we refer to as the Poincar\'e basis.
We then introduce a comprehensive framework for gradient-enhanced GSA that integrates recent advances in sparse, gradient-enhanced regression for surrogate modeling with the construction of weighting schemes for derivative-based sensitivity analysis. The proposed methodology is applicable to a broad class of probability measures and supports various choices of weights. We illustrate the effectiveness of the approach on a challenging flood modeling case study, where Sobol indices are accurately estimated using limited data.

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for Differential Equations with Random Inputs

talks.title: Active Learning Methods for Multi-Output Uncertainty Quantification Based on Gaussian Process Regression and Principal Component Analysis
talks.abstract:
.
In recent years, surrogate models have been increasingly proposed to speed-up uncertainty propagation tasks in many domains of science and technology. Among the various methods, Gaussian process regression (GPR) stands as a premier tool, providing accurate models and an indication of their prediction confidence. The confidence information also supports the iterative acquisition of an optimal sequence of training points in so-called active learning (AL) schemes. This aspect is of paramount importance in complex engineering problems, since the computational cost incurred by a single simulation often requires the number of training samples to be minimized. However, standard AL methods mostly address deterministic or optimization scenarios, while becoming non-optimal when input parameters are equipped with a probability distribution. In this work, we introduce an active learning (AL) framework specifically designed for uncertainty propagation, in which the distribution of the input parameters is incorporated in the definition of the acquisition function that selects the optimal sampling points. Moreover, to deal with multi-output problems, the training dataset is compressed using principal component analysis (PCA) and the acquisition criterion is applied to the least-accurate principal component. The method is applied to partial differential equations with stochastic coefficients, as well as to the frequency-domain simulation of an electromagnetic structure.
Authors
Michele Cusano, Politecnico di Torino, Italy, michele.cusano@polito.it
Paolo Manfredi, Politecnico di Torino, Italy, paolo.manfredi@polito.it

talks.title: Predictive Reduced-Order Modeling in Coupled Systems of PDEs
talks.abstract:
.
Fluid structure interaction problems can be highly sensitive to the values of physical parameters. For instance, solvers may experience instabilities due to the added mass effect when fluid and structure densities are similar, and a poroelastic structure modeled by Biot’s equations can exhibit pressure oscillations or Poisson locking with small permeability constants or large values of the Lamé parameter $\lambda$.    We present a strongly coupled partitioned method for the solution of coupled PDEs based on a well-posed monolithic formulation of the system which employs one or more Lagrange multipliers (LMs) representing interfacial flux quantities. The partitioned scheme is centered around a Schur complement equation, whose solution at each time step allows for the decoupling of the respective subproblems, making the method non-iterative between subdomains.
To increase computational efficiency, projection-based reduced order models (ROMs) are introduced into one or both subdomains. We examine the method’s performance with both linear elastic and poroelastic structures over a range of reproductive and predictive parametric tests, demonstrating the robustness of the coupling scheme as well as the viability of ROMs to improve computational performance.
Authors
Amy de Castro, University of Utah, U.S., u6067603@utah.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Methods Based on Polynomial Chaos for Delay Differential Equations Including Random Variables
talks.abstract:
.
We consider initial value problems of systems consisting of delay differential equations (DDEs). Physical parameters of a system are replaced by random variables to conduct an uncertainty quantification. Consequently, the solution of a DDE system becomes a random process, which has to be computed numerically. We expand the random process into a series of the generalized polynomial chaos, where predetermined basis polynomials as well as time-dependent coefficient functions are included. Now the aim is to compute a finite set of the unknown coefficient functions. On the one hand, stochastic collocation techniques produce approximations by solving the DDEs for a finite number of realizations of the random variables. On the other hand, a stochastic Galerkin method yields a larger deterministic system of DDEs, which has to be solved once to obtain an approximation of the coefficient functions. We investigate the application of both approaches to solve the stochastic model. If the stochastic Galerkin method is structure-preserving, then the larger system of DDEs can be solved by the same numerical methods as the original DDEs. We present results of numerical computations using an illustrative example.
Authors
Roland Pulch, University of Greifswald, Germany, pulchr@uni-greifswald.de

sessions.title: Efficient Sampling Strategies for High-Dimensional Problems

talks.title: Low Discrepancy Drop-In Replacements for IID

speakers.name: Larysa Matiukha
speakers.affiliation: Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S.; Jimmy Nguyen, University of California, Irvine, U.S.; Alexander Pride, Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Low discrepancy (LD) sequences facilitate faster convergence for computing expectations or integrals of high dimensional functions.  Unfortunately, the best low discrepancy sequences have preferred sample sizes, $n$, such as integer powers of 2.  This may be inconvenient for practitioners who are accustomed to using independent and identically distributed (IID) samples that have no restrictions on $n$.
This talk describes recent work on lattice, Kronecker, and other LD sequences where the $n$ may be any positive integer, and the dimension, $d$, is also allowed to be of arbitrary size.  We use component-by-component constructions based on a figure of merit that includes the discrepancies for all values of $n$ up to a maximum value, $N$.  Because these discrepancies are based on carefully chosen kernels, the cost of the constructions is at most $\mathcal{O}(d N \log(N))$.
Authors
Fred J. Hickernell, Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S., hickernell@illinoistech.edu
Claude Hall, Jr., Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S., chall14@illinoistech.edu

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Quasi-Monte Carlo Sampling

speakers.name: Pieterjan Robbe
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Aleksei Sorokin, Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S.; Gianluca Geraci

talks.abstract:
.
Multifidelity Monte Carlo (MFMC) methods provide an effective framework for reducing the computational cost of uncertainty quantification by combining information from inexpensive low-fidelity models with corrections from more accurate high-fidelity simulations. Traditionally, MFMC relies on standard Monte Carlo sampling, and its efficiency is therefore limited by the slow convergence rate of Monte Carlo estimators. It is well known that quasi-Monte Carlo (QMC) techniques can significantly accelerate convergence when applied to smooth integrands. In this work, we extend the multilevel quasi-Monte Carlo methodology to the multi-fidelity setting, while avoiding costly stochastic replications for error estimation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed multi-fidelity QMC approach can achieve substantial gains in accuracy per unit cost compared to both standard MFMC and classical QMC.

Pieterjan Robbe, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., pmrobbe@sandia.gov

talks.title: ESPs: Cost-Efficient Sampling of Computationally Expensive Posterior Distributions
talks.abstract:
.
Computationally expensive posterior distributions arise in modern scientific settings. One such setting is Bayesian inverse problems with computer simulators, where each evaluation of the (unnormalized) posterior $p$ requires a forward run of the expensive simulator that can take hundreds of CPU hours. This cost poses a challenge for existing posterior samplers, where each sample requires at least one evaluation of the posterior $p$. Given a computational budget, a "cost-efficient" sampler is desired for effective posterior exploration with limited posterior evaluations. We propose a new sampling method called cost-Efficient Stein Points (ESPs). ESPs extend the Stein points in Chen et al. (2018), which employ the sequential minimization of the kernel Stein discrepancy to generate a sequence of posterior samples $x_1, x_2, \cdots$. To reduce posterior evaluations, we leverage a Gaussian process surrogate on the kernel discrepancy that guides the selection of evaluation points. This facilitates the optimization of samples $x_1, x_2, \cdots$ in a cost-efficient manner, where all previous posterior evaluations can be re-used for optimizing the current sample. We prove that, under mild conditions, ESPs converge in distribution to the desired posterior distribution. Finally, we demonstrate the cost-efficiency of ESPs over the state-of-the-art in numerical experiments and an application for Bayesian calibration of a biological oscillator process.
Authors
Simon Mak, Duke University, U.S., sm769@duke.edu

sessions.title: Advancing Uncertainty Quantification Through Open-Source Software - Part I of II
Optimal Experimental Design for Digital Twins - Part I of III

talks.title: Towards Digital Twin for Design of Experiments with Moving Sensor

speakers.name: Sven Leyffer
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Nicole Aretz, University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Thomas F. Lynn, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We optimize the path of a mobile sensor to minimize the posterior uncertainty of a Bayesian inverse problem. Along its path, the sensor continuously takes measurements of the state, which is a physical quantity modeled as the solution of a partial differential equation (PDE) with uncertain parameters. We derive the closed-form expression of the posterior covariance matrix of the model parameters as a function of the path, and formulate the optimal experimental design problem for minimizing the posterior's uncertainty. We discretize the problem such that the cost function remains consistent under temporal refinement. Our formulation allows the addition of constraints that model the dynamics of the sensor platform and ensure that the path avoids obstacles and remains physically interpretable through a control parameterization. We present computational results for a convection-diffusion equation with unknown initial condition.

Sven Leyffer, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., leyffer@anl.gov
Nicole Aretz, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., nicole.aretz@austin.utexas.edu

talks.title: Optimal Experimental Design for Biomanufacturing Digital Twin Calibration and Process Control
talks.abstract:
.
Biomanufacturing processes involve intricate interactions among biological, chemical, and physical factors across multiple scales. Designing optimal control strategies for such systems is challenging due to their nonlinear, stochastic nature, and limited data availability. To address this, we introduce Actor-Simulator, a novel framework that integrates digital twin calibration into model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) for optimal control of stochastic systems with complex dynamics and delay effects. Unlike traditional model-based approaches that rely on restrictive assumptions and overlook parameter uncertainty, Actor-Simulator jointly calibrates a multi-scale mechanistic model—formulated as stochastic differential equations (SDEs)—and learns an optimal control policy. This dual-component strategy balances exploration and exploitation by using policy performance to guide data collection, targeting the most informative regions of the state-action space. To deepen mechanistic understanding, we further quantify uncertainty propagation in stochastic reaction networks using gradient-based analysis of their SDE representations. This enables rigorous sensitivity analysis of multivariate output trajectories with respect to perturbations in model parameters and decision inputs, while addressing SDE discretization bias. Our method provably converges to the optimal policy and demonstrates superior performance in biopharmaceutical manufacturing simulations.
Authors
Wei Xie, Northeastern University, U.S., w.xie@northeastern.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Goal-Oriented OED for Bayesian Inverse Problems Governed by Autonomous Dynamical Systems

speakers.name: Omar Ghattas
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present a goal-oriented algorithm for optimal experimental design (OED) that extends our recent work on real-time, extreme-scale Bayesian inversion for linear autonomous dynamical systems. That work utilized a predetermined set of observation points (sensors) to perform real-time parameter inference and predict quantities of interest (QoIs). In practice, sensor deployment is often constrained by a budget, making the strategic placement of a limited number of sensors a critical design challenge. We address this challenge with a framework for optimal sensor subset selection. Our approach employs a greedy algorithm that, given a set of candidate locations, iteratively selects sensors to maximize a measure of expected information gain. This information gain objective is tailored to be goal-oriented, prioritizing sensor locations that most reduce uncertainty in specific QoIs. The efficiency of this framework is enabled by the fast, FFT-based operators from our prior work, and the greedy selection process is readily parallelizable on multi-GPU clusters. The goal-oriented OED framework is applied to a digital twin (DT) for tsunami early warning in the Cascadia subduction zone. This DT assimilates observations from seafloor pressure sensors during an earthquake to inform a billion-parameter inverse problem for the seafloor motion and issue uncertainty-equipped predictions of tsunami wave heights in real time. Sensor locations are optimized to enable accurate tsunami forecasts.
Authors
Stefan Henneking, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., stefan@oden.utexas.edu
Sreeram R. Venkat, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., srvenkat@utexas.edu

talks.title: A Generic Digital Twin-Enhanced Framework for Partial Discharge Diagnosis
talks.abstract:
.
Accurate prognostics of complex systems has become an imperative research area in the field of reliability engineering. In particular, an optimal sensor network can enhance monitoring of high-risk systems across many fields. Our study focuses on partial discharge detection and localization, which is a severe fault in many high power applications. Specifically, we aim to develop a data fusion framework enhanced with a digital twin, which can integrate data from numerous sources to achieve optimal system analysis. The essentials of this study lie within the scope of data selection for machine learning and data fusion models. However, the challenge of efficient data selection has yet to be formulated in the context of optimal design and sensor placement. Specifically, the proposed method can simultaneously determine the sensor network while optimizing the predictor or monitoring system. This is accomplished by using “proxy” models for data selection, and explainable AI for model transparency. The global and local explainability are provided via SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) analyses, respectively. Various search algorithms are implemented to solve the generally applicable mathematical formulation, which utilizes predictor accuracy and interpretability as the fitness function. Overall, this proposed method is demonstrated on partial discharge detection and localization within electric motors.

Sara Kohtz, Binghamton University, U.S., skohtz1@binghamton.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty and Sparsity in Inverse Problems in Imaging

talks.title: Making the Most of Every Matvec: Projection-Based Methods for Efficient UQ in Sparsity-Promoting Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Efficient and reliable sample-based UQ for imaging inverse problems remains a challenging task. Sampling algorithms require the solution of a sequence of high-dimensional least squares problems with changing matrices and changing righthand sides. The usage of a standard algorithm such as CGLS/LSQR to solve each problem in the sequence may be prohibitively expensive due to the large number of inner iterations required, which translates to many matrix-vector products (matvecs) with the (possibly costly) forward model $A$ and/or the sparsifying transformation $L$. Here, we propose a new paradigm for the efficient solution of the sequence of least squares problems based on dimensionality reduction via subspace projection. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach when applied to several sparsity-promoting imaging inverse problems involving the LASSO (Laplace) prior, sparse bayesian learning priors, and the horseshoe prior.
Authors
Jonathan Lindbloom, Dartmouth College, U.S., jonathan.t.lindbloom.gr@dartmouth.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Signal Recovery Using a Complex-Valued Bayesian Lasso
talks.abstract:
.
Recovering complex-valued images from noisy indirect data is important in applications such as ultrasound and synthetic aperture radar imaging. While there are many effective algorithms to recover point estimates of the magnitude, fewer are designed to recover the phase. Quantifying uncertainty in the estimate can also provide valuable information for real-time decision making. This investigation therefore proposes a new Bayesian inference method that recovers point estimates while also quantifying the uncertainty for complex-valued signals or images given noisy and indirect observation data. Our method is motivated by the Bayesian LASSO approach for real-valued sparse signals, and we demonstrate that the Bayesian LASSO can be effectively adapted to recover complex-valued images whose magnitude is sparse in some (e.g., the gradient) domain. We present numerical examples to demonstrate our algorithm’s robustness to noise as well as its computational efficiency.
Authors
Dylan Green, Trinity College, U.S., dylan.green@trincoll.edu
Jonathan Lindbloom, Dartmouth College, U.S., jonathan.t.lindbloom.gr@dartmouth.edu
Anne Gelb, Dartmouth College, U.S., annegelb@math.dartmouth.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Sparsity-Promoting Priors for 3D Radar Imaging
talks.abstract:
.
Three-dimensional radar imaging presents a large-scale, complex-valued inverse problem, where even applying the forward operator is computationally demanding. Incorporating a third spatial dimension has the potential to dramatically improve automated target recognition (ATR), which often employs shape-based object detection algorithms. This improvement would stem from the fact that height information provides discriminative features that are difficult to capture in 2D projections. To leverage this potential, 3D reconstructions must preserve the structural integrity of objects while suppressing spurious scatterers. We investigate sparsity-promoting priors and regularization strategies that encourage faithful recovery of target shapes, including penalties in both image and transform domains. In addition, we explore techniques that enforce connectedness and neighborhood consistency after thresholding, aiming to improve interpretability and robustness.
Authors
Victor Churchill, Trinity College, U.S., victor.churchill@trincoll.edu

sessions.title: UQ for Personalized Cardiac Models - Part I of II

talks.title: Multifidelity Bayesian Inference for Personalized Cardiac Electrophysiology Models
talks.abstract:
.
Personalized digital twins of the heart—patient-specific computational models of heart function—offer a powerful framework for predictive medicine and treatment planning. However, clinical data of cardiac electrophysiology are often sparse and noisy, leading to substantial uncertainty in model calibration. At the same time, detailed electrophysiology simulations are computationally demanding and involve high-dimensional parameter spaces, which makes traditional Bayesian inference methods such as Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) infeasible.
To address these challenges, we investigate a multifidelity Bayesian inference approach that combines surrogate models with detailed simulations inside a Metropolis–Hastings algorithm. The goal is to reduce the computational burden of posterior sampling while maintaining sufficient accuracy for uncertainty quantification.
The talk will focus on the formulation of this methodology and its application to synthetic data as a testbed for scalable Bayesian inference in cardiac digital twin models.
Authors
Marie Cloet, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium, marie.cloet@kuleuven.be
Maarten Volkaerts, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium, maarten.volkaerts@kuleuven.be
Giovanni Samaey, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium, giovanni.samaey@kuleuven.be

talks.title: Robust and Efficient Uncertainty Quantification and Software Tools for Digital Twin-Scale Biomedical Models
talks.abstract:
.
Digital twin (DT) technologies are rapidly becoming a cornerstone of modern computational science and engineering workflows. From aerospace to manufacturing to healthcare, DTs offer a powerful way to simulate, monitor, and optimize complex systems by creating virtual replicas that evolve in real time with data. A pressing challenge in DT technologies is accurate and actionable information about uncertainty. We discuss advances in UQ methodology in practice in the context of cardiac digital twins (CDT), i.e., personalized models built from patient-specific clinical data, and virtual heart populations (VHP), considering heart function and outcomes across populations. These technologies require UQ tools that address model calibration, sensitivity analysis, and assessments of uncertainty that inform decision-making. Our procedures tackle high dimensionality of parameters involved in these models, epistemic uncertainty stemming from model errors, and finite population sampling errors, and we present translational software tools that we are building to address modeling and simulation of CDTs and VHPs, but that are also applicable across broader DT domains.
Authors
Akil Narayan, University of Utah, U.S., akil@sci.utah.edu
Karli Gillette, University of Utah, U.S., karli.gillette@utah.edu
Asher Merrill, University of Utah, U.S., asher.merrill@utah.edu
Eli Child, University of Utah, U.S., eli.child@utah.edu

talks.title: Promoting Confidence in Patient-Specific Finite Element Models of the Vascular System Through Uncertainty Quantification
talks.abstract:
.
Multiphysics-based simulations of the cardiovascular system are increasingly being integrated into clinical decision-making, surgical planning, medical device design, and regulatory affairs. We have incorporated advancements in uncertainty quantification (UQ) in a series of studies to evaluate the variability in the output of computational simulations of the fluid and solid coronary artery biomechanical environments due to model input uncertainty. Probability density functions were fit to population-derived or patient-specific values and sampled. Idealized and patient-specific coronary artery computational models of the fluid and solid domains were constructed and solved in the finite element software suite, FEBio, to predict fluid wall shear stress and tissue first principal stress. Parameter-to-model-output emulators were constructed via polynomial chaos expansion using the UQ software, UncertainSCI. Results highlight the variability in predicted mechanical metrics due to input parameter uncertainties, and particularly the impact of patient-specific versus population-derived data on model uncertainty. These findings underscore the role of UQ in evaluating model robustness and present uncertainty-aware frameworks to guide model refinement and improve reliability, interpretability, and clinical translation.
Authors
Lucas Timmins, Texas A&M University, U.S., lucas.timmins@tamu.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Nuclear Physics

talks.title: Emulation of Stochastic Simulation Model Describing Heavy-Ion Collisions
talks.abstract:
.
The simulation of relativistic heavy-ion collisions is critical in understanding the evolution of Quark-Gluon Plasma during the early phases. In particular, the viscous anisotropic hydrodynamics (VAH) simulations provide an important component for transitioning smoothly across time. The Bayesian calibration of VAH is challenging due to multiple factors, posing a need for an efficient and accurate emulator. First, the VAH outputs are stochastic, in which the statistical variability can be studied via replications. Second, the VAH outputs are high-dimensional ($d \approx 100$), corresponding to potentially correlated observables. Third, one VAH simulation requires $\sim 1000$ core-hours to complete, making direct calibration infeasible. We propose the use of a novel Gaussian process-based methodology to construct an effective emulator. The model makes use of a flexible representation of the underlying functional components, while handling the correlated outputs by selecting a data-driven transformation. The construction of emulator that accurately represents the uncertainty facilitates the calibration of the VAH simulations. Computational experiments show that the improved quality of the new method, measured by predictive accuracy, statistical coverage, and proper scoring rules.
Authors
Moses Chan, Northwestern University, U.S., moses.chan@northwestern.edu
Evan Barnett, Northwestern University, U.S., evanbarnett2027@northwestern.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Model Combination and Orthogonalization for Controlled Extrapolations
talks.abstract:
.
Some of the most interesting applications of data from physics lies at the extremes. In nuclear physics, questions surrounding both the formation of everyday elements in the hearts of stars and in the most exotic and ephemeral systems produced only in terrestrial labs all rely on data that we sometimes have no way to access experimentally. This is where physicists turn to models to propagate the information in the experimentally accessible region off to the extremes. Of course, these models have flaws and shortcomings, though a substantial amount of effort has been spent in recent years to ground these predictions in principled uncertainty quantification frameworks. This talk will discuss one such approach to this issue by considering the whole zoo of models and predictions that have been made by experts over the years and combining them in a way that minimizes overlap between related models. This leads to an efficient UQ framework for multi-model combination that can be easily scaled to include more models and more predictions without a substantial cost in either the training or the inference stage.
Authors
Kyle Godbey, Michigan State University, U.S., godbey@frib.msu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Implicit Neural Representations for Solving Parametrized PDEs with Uncertainty Quantification: Towards Applications in Nuclear Theory

speakers.name: Pablo Giuliani
speakers.affiliation: Michigan State University, U.S.; Edgard Bonilla, Stanford University, U.S.; Tyler Bagwell

talks.abstract:
.
Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) offer a powerful framework for learning compact, continuous representations of a variety of signal types ranging from images to solutions of partial difference equations (PDEs). This presentation explores how INRs can be leveraged to model high-dimensional solution spaces of parametrized PDEs without reliance on high fidelity solutions or dense coordinate grids for training data. By opting for flexible operator learning through hypernetworks, our INR approach can efficiently capture solutions across diverse parameter regimes, enabling scalable approximation of those PDE families. To address reliability in scientific applications, we incorporate Bayesian uncertainty quantification, focusing on Laplace approximation style methods to derive posterior distributions over network weights and propagate them into credible solution intervals. Additionally, we provide methods that adapt classical Laplace approximation techniques to training regimes where the loss landscape is not smooth or twice differentiable, accounting for nuanced physics-based constraints. As case studies, we examine the quantum harmonic oscillator and a nonlinear, non-affine system, showing how INRs capture eigenstates, integrate physics-informed losses, and handle challenging parameter dependencies. We conclude with directions for scaling to more complex systems, refining UQ methods, and extending applications in nuclear physics.
Authors
Sophia Zorek, Rice University, U.S., saz2@rice.edu
Kyle Godbey, Michigan State University, U.S., godbey@frib.msu.edu
Pablo Giuliani, Michigan State University, U.S., giulia27@msu.edu
Edgard Bonilla, Stanford University, U.S., edgard@stanford.edu

Frederi Viens, Rice University, U.S., fv15@rice.edu

talks.title: Dealing with Uncertainties in Modeling Nuclear Reactions
talks.abstract:
.
We discuss the development of an uncertainty-quantified phenomenological model for nuclear reactions, a parameterization of the quantum-mechanical interaction between the nucleons and the nucleus, calibrated to many experimental data sets of angular cross sections. We primarily focus on the development of the likelihood model and the Bayesian calibration. We discuss the challenge of mitigating overconfident posteriors in the presence of experimental constraints consisting of many data points without provided covariances. We discuss treatment of correlated model discrepancy using a low-rank covariance structure in Legendre polynomial space over angles. We also discuss the novel computational tools developed, which allow for this massively-parallel calibration to be computationally tractable.
Authors
Kyle Beyer, Michigan State University, U.S., beyerk@frib.msu.edu

sessions.title: Generative AI for Data Assimilation - Part II of II

talks.title: Nonlinear Estimation of Cloud Parameters with Ensemble Conjugate Transform Inversion Methods
talks.abstract:
.
One of the major challenges in cloud and precipitation modeling is the presence of bounded quantities. For example, precipitation amounts are inherently non-negative and often exhibit highly skewed distributions near their lower bounds. Similar constraints apply to cloud microphysical parameters, which govern the evolution of cloud processes in Earth system models. As a result, parameter estimation methods based on linear-Gaussian assumptions frequently struggle to accurately calibrate these microphysical parameters from cloud-sensitive observations.     In this talk, we investigate the feasibility of a newly developed inversion algorithm — the Ensemble Conjugate Transform Inversion (ECTI) — for estimating microphysical parameters within a column cloud model. ECTI builds on a recent generalization of Kalman filter theory to non-Gaussian distributions (Chipilski 2025: https://doi.org/10.1175/JAS-D-24-0171.1) and leverages invertible transport maps. We will highlight the advantages of ECTI implementations that incorporate normalizing flows, a class of generative AI methods, and compare their performance against the benchmark Ensemble Kalman Inversion (EKI) and other nonlinear generalizations.
Authors
Hristo G. Chipilski, Florida State University, U.S., hchipilski@fsu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Diffusion-Based Supervised Learning of Generative Models for Efficient Sampling of Multimodal Distributions

speakers.name: Hoang A. Tran
speakers.affiliation: Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S.; Feng Bao, Florida State University, U.S.; Guannan Zhang

speakers.name: Dan Lu
speakers.affiliation: Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S.; Zezhong Zhang, Auburn University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a hybrid generative model for efficient sampling of high-dimensional, multimodal probability distributions for Bayesian inference. Traditional Monte Carlo methods, such as the Metropolis-Hastings and Langevin Monte Carlo sampling methods, are effective for sampling from single-mode distributions in high-dimensional spaces. However, these methods struggle to produce samples with the correct proportions for each mode in multimodal distributions, especially for distributions with well separated modes. To address the challenges posed by multimodality, we adopt a divide-and-conquer strategy. We start by minimizing the energy function with initial guesses uniformly distributed within the prior domain to identify all the modes of the energy function. Then, we train a classifier to segment the domain corresponding to each mode. After the domain decomposition, we train a diffusion-model-assisted generative model for each identified mode within its support. Once each mode is characterized, we employ bridge sampling to estimate the normalizing constant, allowing us to directly adjust the ratios between the modes. Our numerical examples demonstrate that the proposed framework can effectively handle multimodal distributions with varying mode shapes in up to 100 dimensions. An application to Bayesian inverse problem for partial differential equations is also provided.
Authors
Hoang A. Tran, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., tranha@ornl.gov

Guannan Zhang, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., zhangg@ornl.gov
Dan Lu, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S., lud1@ornl.gov

talks.title: Latent Ensemble Score Filters for High-dimensional Data Assimilation with Sparse Observations
talks.abstract:
.
Accurate modeling and prediction of complex physical systems often rely on data assimilation techniques to correct errors inherent in model simulations. Traditional methods like the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) and its variants as well as the recently developed Ensemble Score Filters (EnSF) face significant challenges when dealing with high-dimensional and nonlinear Bayesian filtering problems with sparse observations, which are ubiquitous in real-world applications. In this paper, we propose a novel data assimilation method, Latent-EnSF, which leverages EnSF with efficient and consistent latent representations of the full states and sparse observations to address the joint challenges of high dimensionlity in states and high sparsity in observations for nonlinear Bayesian filtering. We introduce a coupled Variational Autoencoder (VAE) with two encoders to encode the full states and sparse observations in a consistent way guaranteed by a latent distribution matching and regularization as well as a consistent state reconstruction. With comparison to several methods, we demonstrate the higher accuracy, faster convergence, and higher efficiency of Latent-EnSF for two challenging applications with complex models in shallow water wave propagation and medium-range weather forecasting, for highly sparse observations in both space and time.
Authors
Phillip Si, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., psi6@gatech.edu
Peng Chen, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., pchen402@gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Direct Assimilation of Observables

speakers.name: Dongbin Xiu
speakers.affiliation: Ohio State University, U.S.; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a method for conducting data assimilation for data-driven models of unknown partially observed dynamical and PDE systems. When only a subset of the system’s state variables is observed, memory-dependent models can account for the unobserved dynamics and provide surrogates for the evolution of the observable states. We perform assimilation in this reduced setting by leveraging memory-dependent deep neural networks based in flow-map learning and the Mori–Zwanzig formalism. Given noisy and partial observations of the observable states over an assimilation window, we aim to approximate the corresponding memory trajectory to update the model's prediction. This assimilation enables the model to generate accurate long-time forecasts of the observable states, including for unseen initial conditions. In addition, we investigate model-correction of the surrogate during assimilation to further improve our approximation of the unknown governing dynamics.
Authors
Caroline Tatsouka, Ohio State University, U.S., tatsouka.3@osu.edu
Dongbin Xiu, Ohio State University, U.S., xiu.16@osu.edu
Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., ymarz@mit.edu

sessions.title: Efficient Data-Driven Models for UQ and Optimization - Part II of II

talks.title: Learning Low-Rank Neutron Transport Dynamics on Linear and Nonlinear
talks.abstract:
.
In this presentation, we propose semi-intrusive low-rank model reduction strategies for time-dependent neutron transport problems. Starting from a full-order discretization of the transport equation in space and angle, we reformulate the dynamics in global monolithic form and construct reduced models using both linear subspaces and nonlinear manifolds approaches. The nonlinear framework leverages quadratic lifting maps (element-wise and tensorial) that embed low-dimensional latent variables into higher-dimensional reduced spaces, enhancing the expressive power of the reduced basis. To avoid the prohibitive cost of projecting transport operators, we introduce a data-driven operator inference strategy that directly estimates reduced operators from high-fidelity simulation data. Numerical experiments on a one-dimensional slab benchmark show that tensorial lifting achieves markedly improved accuracy compared to linear and element-wise quadratic approaches while maintaining significant computational speed-ups. The results highlight the potential of nonlinear manifold reduction combined with operator inference to deliver efficient and accurate surrogate models for neutron transport dynamics, with promising applicability to large-scale, multi-dimensional settings.
Authors
Francesco A. B. Silva, Texas A&M University, U.S., francesco.silva@tamu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Scaling Up UQ: From Algorithm Development to Supercomputing
talks.abstract:
.
UQ for compute-heavy simulations requires a combination of advanced UQ algorithms, efficient surrogates and scalable simulators. This talk presents (1) recent advances in algorithms (e.g. parallel prefetching for multilevel UQ), (2) software frameworks allowing even UQ prototypes to transparently control supercomputers (UM-Bridge, Tesseract) and (3) a large-scale UQ application that combines the best of the three worlds to learn earthquake model parameters from full-field seismic simulations and indirect observations.

Linus Seelinger, Pasteur Labs, Germany, mail@linusseelinger.de

Generative Models for Scientific Computing: Trustworthiness, Physical Consistency, Computational Challenges, and Applications - Part I of II

talks.title: Generative Learning for Forward and Inverse Modeling of Complex Spatiotemporal Physics
talks.abstract:
.
Generative models offer a principled way to represent uncertainty in high-dimensional dynamical systems, yet most surrogates in scientific computing remain point predictors tied to a single task or regime. This talk presents a hybrid physics-integrated generative framework that unifies forward forecasting, flow reconstruction/super-resolution, data assimilation, and inverse modeling for complex spatiotemporal PDEs. The core idea is to learn a probabilistic prior over fields/trajectories using diffusion or conditional flow-matching, and to couple that prior with a coarse-grained differentiable solver during inference via solver-conditioned diffusion posterior sampling. Practically, we model either (i) small-scale residuals/SGS stresses conditioned on the coarse state, or (ii) the full field, and enforce incompressibility, energy/enstrophy budgets, spectral targets, and symmetry through architectural and loss constraints. These yield calibrated ensembles that respect physics while accommodating chaotic phase drift and partial/noisy observations. Across homogenous, isotropic to wall-bounded turbulence testbeds, this yields high-k spectral recovery and reduced long-horizon budget drift, delivers well-calibrated posteriors for state and parameter estimation, and enables zero-shot data assimilation and inverse modeling.
Authors
Meet Parikh, Cornell University, U.S., mhp66@cornell.edu
Jian-Xun Wang, Cornell University, U.S., jw2837@cornell.edu

talks.title: Modeling of Spatial Asymmetries in Temperatures in North America with Normalizing Flows

speakers.name: Julie Bessac
speakers.affiliation: National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S.; Mitchell Krock , University of Missouri, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Combining strengths from deep learning and classical statistics can help describe complex relationships between variables that can have significant impacts (e.g., environmental, or financial applications). Neural networks learn complicated nonlinear relationships from large datasets under limited parametric assumptions. By definition, the number of samples of rare events is small, which limits the ability of the data-hungry, nonparametric neural network to describe these events. Inspired by recent cold winter weather events in North America caused by atmospheric blocking, we examine several probabilistic generative models for the entire multivariate probability distribution of daily boreal winter surface air temperature. Compared to vine copulas, the statistical standard for multivariate copula modeling, deep learning methods show improved ability to reproduce complicated asymmetries in the spatial distribution of ERA5 temperature reanalysis, including the spatial extent of in-sample rare events.
Authors
Julie Bessac, National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S., julie.bessac@nrel.gov
Mitchell Krock, University of Missouri, U.S., mk52n@missouri.edu

talks.title: Generative Neural Networks towards Intractable Bayesian Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Johann Rudi
speakers.affiliation: Virginia Tech, U.S.; Deep Ray, University of Maryland, College Park, U.S.; Anwesa Dey, University of Utah, U.S.; Andreas Mang, University of Houston, U.S.; Ionut-Gabriel Farcas, Virginia Tech, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk we consider uncertainty quantification (UQ) for inverse problems applied to physical systems governed by differential equations that additionally have stochasticity.   Each "dimension of complexity" - the forward problem, inverse problem, UQ - dramatically increases the computational challenges. Even simple forward problems become challenging when framed as inverse problems if the data misfit term generates highly nonlinear and/or nondifferentiable mappings. Extending inference to UQ adds another dimension of challenges and leads to computational intractability for complex systems.
This talk investigates whether generative neural networks can break some of the complexity. The goal is for a neural network (NN) to represent an "inverse mapping": from observational data to parameters. State-of-the-art NNs can be trained to predict point estimates or empirical representations of distributions, and as such delivering UQ. Using NNs in the context of inverse mappings is also referred to as amortized inference; and the literature also classifies inverse problems and UQ (with or without NNs) as simulation-based inference.  The talk considers open challenges remaining at the intersection of inverse problems, deep learning, and computational mathematics: Which NN expressivity can approximate solutions of ill-posed inverse problems? What is the impact of finite training data and noise? How do generative NNs scale as sampling generators for Bayesian inverse problems?
Authors
Johann Rudi, Virginia Tech, U.S., jrudi@vt.edu
Deep Ray, University of Maryland, College Park, U.S., deepray@umd.edu
Anwesa Dey, University of Utah, U.S., deyanwesa@gmail.com
Andreas Mang, University of Houston, U.S., andreas@math.uh.edu
Ionut-Gabriel Farcas, Virginia Tech, U.S., farcasi@vt.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Hierarchical Flow Matching with Mini-Batch Couplings
talks.abstract:
.
Flow matching has emerged as a compelling generative modeling approach that is widely used across domains. During training, flow matching learns to model a velocity field. At inference, to generate samples, an ordinary differential equation (ODE) is numerically solved via forward integration of the modeled velocity field.   To better capture the multi-modality that is inherent in typical velocity fields, we introduce hierarchical flow matching. It uses a hierarchy of ODEs that are numerically integrated when generating data.   Each level of the hierarchy of ODEs captures the distribution of the next level, just like vanilla flow matching uses the velocity field to capture a multi-modal data distribution.   While this hierarchy enables modeling multi-modal distributions at any hierarchy level, the complexity of the modeled distributions remains identical across levels of the hierarchy. We therefore further investigate how to progressively adjust the complexity of the distributions across different levels of the hierarchy via mini-batch couplings.   We show the benefits of mini-batch couplings in hierarchical flow matching via compelling results on synthetic and imaging data, and provide theoretical justification.
Authors
Zhizhen Zhao, University of Illinois, U.S., zhizhenz@illinois.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: UQ for Transformers & Transformers for UQ - Part II of II

talks.title: Understanding in-Context Learning on Structured Manifolds: Bridging Attention to Kernel Methods
talks.abstract:
.
While in-context learning (ICL) has achieved remarkable success in natural language and vision domains, its theoretical understanding—particularly in the context of structured geometric data—remains unexplored. This paper initiates a theoretical study of ICL for regression of Holder functions on manifolds. We establish a novel connection between the attention mechanism and classical kernel methods, demonstrating that transformers effectively perform kernel-based prediction at a new query through its interaction with the prompt. This connection is validated by numerical experiments, revealing that the learned query–prompt scores for Holder functions are highly correlated with the Gaussian kernel. Building on this insight, we derive generalization error bounds in terms of the prompt length and the number of training tasks. When a sufficient number of training tasks are observed, transformers give rise to the minimax regression rate of Holder functions on manifolds, which scales exponentially with the intrinsic dimension of the manifold, rather than the ambient space dimension. Our result also characterizes how the generalization error scales with the number of training tasks, shedding light on the complexity of transformers as in-context kernel algorithm learners. Our findings provide foundational insights into the role of geometry in ICL and novels tools to study ICL of nonlinear models.
Authors
Zhaiming Shen, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., zshen49@gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Activation Steering with a Feedback Controller

speakers.name: Dung Nguyen
speakers.affiliation: National University of Singapore, Singapore; Nhi Pham, Vin University, Vietnam; Hieu Vu

talks.abstract:
.
Controlling the behaviors of large language models (LLM) is fundamental to their safety alignment and reliable deployment. Yet, existing steering methods are primarily driven by empirical insights and lack theoretical performance guarantees. In this work, we develop a control-theoretic foundation for activation steering by showing that popular steering methods correspond to the proportional (P) controllers, with the steering vector serving as the feedback signal. Building on this finding, we propose Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID) Steering, a principled framework that leverages the full PID controller for activation steering in LLMs. The proportional (P) term aligns activations with target semantic directions, the integral (I) term accumulates errors to enforce persistent corrections across layers, and the derivative (D) term mitigates overshoot by counteracting rapid activation changes. This closed-loop design yields interpretable error dynamics and connects activation steering to classical stability guarantees in control theory. Moreover, PID Steering is lightweight, modular, and readily integrates with state-of-the-art steering methods. Extensive experiments across multiple LLM families and benchmarks demonstrate that PID Steering consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving more robust and reliable behavioral control.
Authors
Tan Nguyen, National University of Singapore, Singapore, tanmn@nus.edu.sg

talks.title: Transformer As a Nonlinear Predictor
talks.abstract:
.
I will describe a mathematical framework for causal nonlinear prediction in settings where observations are generated from an underlying hidden Markov model (HMM). Both the problem formulation and the proposed solution are motivated by the decoder-only transformer architecture, in which a finite sequence of observations (tokens) is mapped to the conditional probability of the next token. I will describe these correspondences as part of my talk.

talks.title: Transformers Through the Lens of Support-preserving Maps Between Measures
talks.abstract:
.
Transformers are deep architectures that define "in-context maps" which enable predicting new tokens based on a given set of tokens. In this work, we model neural networks as maps on probability measures and study the question "what kind of maps between measures are transformers?" We fully characterize the properties of maps between measures that enable them to be represented in terms of in-context maps via a push forward. On the one hand, these include transformers; on the other hand, transformers universally approximate representations with any continuous in-context map. These properties are preserving the cardinality of support and that the regular part of their Fréchet derivative is uniformly continuous. Moreover, we show that the solution map of the Vlasov equation, which is of nonlocal transport type, for interacting particle systems in the mean-field regime for the Cauchy problem satisfies the conditions on the one hand and, hence, can be approximated by a transformer; on the other hand, we prove that the measure-theoretic self-attention has the properties that ensure that the infinite depth, mean-field measure-theoretic transformer can be identified with a Vlasov flow.

talks.title: The Measure Evolution of Hyperbolic PDEs with Random Data

speakers.name: Amir Sagiv
speakers.affiliation: New Jersey Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We study pointwise statistics in 1D hyperbolic PDEs with random initial data–that is, the distribution of the solution at any given point in space and time. For linear problems, the PDF evolves via the same PDE as the original system. In the nonlinear case, we derive a linear transport equation for the CDF, valid up to shock formation, beyond which a counterexample shows no such equations exist. For systems, we extend this to multi-point statistics. These equations offer a computationally efficient alternative to Monte Carlo methods and surrogate models. They also yield a priori statistical error bounds for standard Monte Carlo methods.

Amir Sagiv, New Jersey Institute of Technology, U.S., amir.sagiv@njit.edu

talks.title: Invariant Measures for Data-Driven Dynamical System Identification

speakers.name: Jonah Botvinick-Greenhouse
speakers.affiliation: Cornell University, U.S.; Yunan Yang, Cornell University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we propose a novel approach for performing dynamical system identification, based upon the comparison of simulated and observed physical invariant measures. While standard methods directly treat time-trajectories as inference data, we instead seek models fitting the observed global time-invariant statistics. With this change in perspective, we gain robustness against pervasive challenges in system identification including noise, chaos, and slow sampling. In the first half of the talk, we pose the system identification task as a partial differential equation (PDE) constrained optimization problem, in which synthetic stationary solutions of the Fokker-Planck equation, obtained as fixed points of a finite-volume discretization, are compared to physical invariant measures extracted from observed trajectory data. In the latter half, we use Takens' embedding theory to introduce a critical data-dependent coordinate transformation which can guarantee unique system identifiability from the invariant measure alone. This contribution resolves a major challenge of system identification through invariant measures, as systems exhibiting distinct transient behaviors may still share the same time-invariant statistics in their state-coordinates. Throughout, we present comprehensive numerical tests which highlight the effectiveness of our approach on a variety of challenging system identification tasks.

Gaussian Processes for Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of II

talks.title: Fast Multitask Gaussian Process Regression

speakers.name: Aleksei Sorokin
speakers.affiliation: Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S.; Pieterjan Robbe, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Fred J. Hickernell, Illinois Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian process (GP) regression is a powerful probabilistic modeling technique with built-in uncertainty quantification. When one has access to multiple correlated simulations (tasks), it is common to fit a multitask GP (MTGP) surrogate which is capable of capturing both inter-task and intra-task correlations. However, with a total of $N$ evaluations across all tasks, fitting an MTGP is often infeasible due to the $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ storage and $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ computations required to store, solve a linear system in, and compute the determinant of the $N \times N$ Gram matrix of pairwise kernel evaluations. In the single-task setting, one may reduce the required storage to $\mathcal{O}(N)$ and computations to $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ by fitting "fast GPs" which pair low-discrepancy design points from quasi-Monte Carlo to special kernel forms which yields nicely structured Gram matrices, e.g., circulant matrices. This article generalizes fast GPs to fast MTGPs which pair low-discrepancy design points for each task to special product kernel forms which yields nicely structured block Gram matrices, e.g., circulant block matrices. An algorithm is presented to efficiently store, invert, and compute the determinant of such Gram matrices with optionally different sampling nodes and different sample sizes for each task. A GPU-compatible, open-source Python implementation is made available in the FastGPs package (https://alegresor.github.io/fastgps/). We validate the efficiency of our algorithm and implementation compared to standard techniques on a range of problems with low numbers of tasks and large sample sizes.

talks.title: Multilevel Lattice-based Kernel Approximation for Random Elliptic PDEs

speakers.name: Alexander D. Gilbert
speakers.affiliation: University of New South Wales, Australia Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Kernel interpolation using lattice rules have shown to be efficient methods for approximating solutions to elliptic PDE with periodic random coefficients. The two key features of kernel methods with lattice rules are that the periodicity gives rise to higher-order convergence and the lattice structure can be exploited for fast computation using the FFT. Building upon the work of Kaarnioja, Kazashi, Kuo, Nobile, Sloan (Numer. Math. 2022), this talk outlines how multilevel methods can be leveraged to further improve the efficiency. I will discuss the complexity analysis of the multilevel kernel approximation, which relies on new mixed regularity bounds for the periodic setting. Finally, I will present numerical results demonstrating the improved efficiency compared so  single level kernel interpolation.

sessions.title: Scientific Machine Learning Methods and Applications for Inverse Problems and Data Assimilation - Part II of III

talks.title: Operator Learning for Surrogate Modeling in Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
Scientific machine learning is transforming the field of data assimilation by providing accurate and cheap-to-evaluate surrogate models for many dynamical systems. In the first part of this talk, I will show that Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs) enjoy polynomial sample complexity for estimating the solution map of a wide class of evolution PDEs. Our theory leverages that these PDEs can be accurately approximated via pseudo-spectral methods, and that these numerical schemes can in turn be efficiently learned using FNOs. In the second part of this talk, I will show that ensemble Kalman filters with machine-learned surrogate models achieve long-time filter accuracy under standard observability assumptions on the true dynamics and observation models.

talks.title: Capturing Closure Terms in Nonlinear Dynamics Using Data Assimilation

speakers.name: Mohammad Khalil
speakers.affiliation: Eric Crislip , Rileigh Bandy, Teresa Portone

talks.abstract:
.
Constitutive models play a crucial role in the analysis of dynamical systems in numerous engineering and scientific disciplines. These models provide a systematic and quantitative framework for (a) understanding the behavior, and (b) predicting the evolution of complex systems. Oftentimes such models, by design, overlook certain aspects of the underlying physics for a variety of reasons, such as the need to reduce computational requirements to simulate those systems or the lack of complete understanding of complex physical phenomenon.
As models continue to be used in exploring new regimes of operation, previously overlooked and unresolved dynamics start to gain significance due to declining predictive accuracies. In order to maintain a target level of predictive accuracy in such settings, it is important to capture closure terms using data-driven approaches. We promote the use of stochastic dynamical models to capture missing closure terms, to be trained within a data assimilation setting. This talk will examine the utility in applying advanced Bayesian model selection techniques to answer questions related to the type of stochastic model needed to capture closure terms. For numerical investigation, we consider a slight modification of the nonlinear Duffing oscillator to examine the efficacy of the proposed framework.

talks.title: On Over-Parametrized Models and Sobolev Training

speakers.name: Katharine Fisher
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Timo Schorlepp, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
With Sobolev training, neural networks are provided data about both the function of interest and its derivatives. This setting is prevalent in scientific machine learning—appearing in molecular dynamics emulators, derivative-informed neural operators, and predictors of summary statistics of chaotic dynamical systems—as well as in traditional machine learning tasks like teacher-student model distillation. However, fundamental questions remain: How does over-parameterization influence performance? What role does the signal-to-noise ratio play? And is additional derivative data always beneficial?
In this work, we study these questions using tools from statistical physics and random matrix theory. In particular, we consider Sobolev training in the proportional asymptotics regime in which the problem dimensionality d, single hidden-layer features p, and training points n grow to infinity at fixed ratios. We focus on target functions modeled as single-index models (i.e., ridge functions with a single intrinsic dimension), providing theoretical insights into the effects of derivative information in high-dimensional learning.

talks.title: An Efficient Bayesian Experimental Design Framework for Active Learning of Model Discrepancy
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian experimental design (BED) offers a principled framework for optimizing data acquisition by leveraging probabilistic inference. However, practical implementations of BED are often compromised by model discrepancy, i.e., the mismatch between predictive models and true physical systems, which can potentially lead to biased parameter estimates. While data-driven approaches have been recently explored to characterize the model discrepancy, the resulting high-dimensional parameter space poses severe challenges for both Bayesian updating and design optimization. In this talk, we will present an efficient Bayesian experimental design framework via ensemble Kalman methods, which enables an active data acquisition for the calibration of model discrepancy. The performance of the proposed method is studied by a classic numerical example of BED governed by a convection-diffusion equation, and the results confirm that the proposed framework efficiently identifies informative data to calibrate the model discrepancy and robustly infers the unknown physical parameters in the modeled system.

talks.title: Generative Prediction-Correction Framework for Long-Time Emulations of Spatially-Resolved Climate Extremes
talks.abstract:
.
Accurately quantifying the increased risks of climate extremes requires generating large ensembles of climate realization across a wide range of emissions scenarios, which is computationally challenging for conventional Earth System Models. We propose GEN2, a generative prediction-correction framework for an efficient and accurate forecast of the extreme event statistics. The prediction step is constructed as a conditional Gaussian emulator, followed by a non-Gaussian machine-learning (ML) correction step. The ML model is trained on pairs of the reference data and the emulated fields nudged towards the reference, to ensure the training is robust to chaos. We first validate the accuracy of our model on historical ERA5 data and then demonstrate the extrapolation capabilities on various future climate change scenarios. When trained on a single realization of one warming scenario, our model accurately predicts the statistics of extreme events in different scenarios, successfully extrapolating beyond the distribution of training data.

talks.title: Scalable Extreme Event Estimation for Stochastic Differential Equations Via Large Deviation Theory and Carleman-Fredholm Determinants

speakers.name: Timo Schorlepp
speakers.affiliation: Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.; Tobias Grafke, University of Warwick, United Kingdom

talks.abstract:
.
Estimating the probability of extreme events is an important problem in many scientific and engineering disciplines, as they are often associated with rare system failures or catastrophes. Asymptotically, rare event probabilities can be estimated with a Laplace approximation, known as (precise) large deviation theory. The approach involves (i) solving an optimization problem to find the most likely realization of the random parameter leading to a prescribed outcome, and (ii) calculating a determinant to account for Gaussian perturbations around the minimizer.
In this talk, I will discuss how to carry out these steps numerically in a scalable way for extreme events in stochastic differential equations (SDEs) and stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) with Brownian noise. In particular, I will highlight the necessity to treat the determinant calculation in step (ii) correctly from an infinite-dimensional point of view to ensure scalability of the numerical method to high dimensions. This leads to either a Fredholm or Carleman-Fredholm determinant computation, depending on whether the second variation of the noise-to-event map is trace-class or only Hilbert-Schmidt.
To illustrate these points, I will consider two examples of extreme event estimates for S(P)DEs with multiplicative noise: a predator-prey SDE as a toy problem, and a 2D random advection-diffusion problem as a nontrivial SPDE.
Presentation based on: arXiv:2502.20114 (joint work with T. Grafke).
Authors
Timo Schorlepp, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S., timo.schorlepp@nyu.edu

talks.title: Extreme Event Aware Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Quantifying and predicting rare and extreme events persists as a crucial yet challenging task in understanding complex dynamical systems. Many practical challenges arise from the infrequency and severity of these events, including the considerable variance of simple sampling methods and the substantial computational cost of high-fidelity numerical simulations. Numerous data-driven methods have recently been developed to tackle these challenges. However, a typical assumption for the success of these methods is the occurrence of multiple extreme events, either within the training dataset or during the sampling process. To overcome this limitation, we introduce the framework of Extreme Event Aware or $\eta$-learning which does not assume the existence of extreme events in the available data. $\eta$-learning reduces the uncertainty even in `unchartered' extreme event regions, by enforcing the extreme event statistics of a few observables during training, which can be available or assumed through qualitative arguments or estimated with unlabeled data. This type of statistical regularization results in models that fit the observed data and align with the prescribed observable statistics, enabling the generation of unprecedented extreme events even when the training data lack extremes therein. Theoretical results based on optimal transport as well as extensive numerical experiments are offered to justify and illustrate the favorable properties of the $\eta$-learning framework.
Authors

talks.title: Posterior Covariance Structures in Gaussian Processes

speakers.name: Difeng Cai
speakers.affiliation: Southern Methodist University, U.S.; Edmond Chow, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Yuanzhe Xi, Emory University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we present a comprehensive analysis of the posterior covariance field in Gaussian processes, with applications to the posterior covariance matrix. Our geometric analysis reveals how the Gaussian kernel’s bandwidth parameter and the spatial distribution of the observations  influence the posterior covariance as well as the corresponding covariance matrix, enabling straightforward identification of areas with high or low covariance in magnitude. Drawing inspiration from the a posteriori error estimation techniques in adaptive finite element methods, we also pro-  pose several estimators to efficiently measure the absolute  posterior covariance field, which can be used for efficient  covariance matrix approximation and preconditioning.

talks.title: Domain-Aware Gaussian Process for Quantitative Learning in Scientific Computing
talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian Process (GP) plays a significant role in the Bayesian statistical learning framework. It is a powerful tool for black-box optimization and for uncertainty quantification in digital twins. We have been developing a number of techniques to increase the usability of GP in large scale applications involving millions of data points. These techniques include fast kernel matrix inversion, new domain-aware kernels, multi-task, multi-objective and multi-fidelity learning. We will demonstrate the versatility of the GP framework through its use in parameter tuning of parallel programs, hardware knobs tuning of scientific instruments, and decision support for solving PDEs.

talks.title: Efficient Optimization of Plasma Radiation Detector Configurations Using Imperfect Inference Models
talks.abstract:
.
The configurations of instruments fielded on an experiment affect the amount of information captured and the quality of subsequent inference. We investigate the problem of optimizing plasma x-ray radiation detectors in a magneto-inertial fusion experiment at Sandia National Laboratories. It is impossible to directly measure properties such as the temperature of the thermonuclear fusion plasma produced in these experiments because of the extreme environment and destructive nature of the experiment. Among other diagnostics, several detectors are placed with significant standoff from the fusion target to capture the x-rays emitted by the fusion plasma, which can be used to infer some of its properties. To optimize the configuration of these detectors, a high-fidelity model (HFM) is used for simulating outputs and a low-fidelity model (LFM) is used for inference. We develop methods based on A- and L-optimality criteria that are efficient to compute while explicitly accounting for the discrepancy between the HFM and the LFM. The method allows us to find detector configurations that perform similarly to or better than the configuration obtained using an existing sampling-based optimization method while decreasing computational time by a factor of 50.

Difan Song, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., dfsong@gatech.edu

talks.title: Uncertainty-Quantified X-Ray Ptychography Via Generative Prediction-Powered Inference
talks.abstract:
.
We propose a novel prediction-powered inference (PPI) framework for generative modeling in X-ray ptychographic imaging. Inverse problems in X-ray imaging are inherently ill-posed and plagued by noise, incomplete measurements, and model mismatch. Instead of relying solely on deterministic reconstructions, our method leverages a generative prior and formulates posterior recovery as an entropic optimal transport problem. By coupling generative sampling with PPI, we obtain calibrated UQ that captures multimodal solution landscapes and produces interpretable posterior diagnostics. This generative PPI approach provides explicit measures of reconstruction reliability, enabling more robust and transparent decision-making in X-ray imaging applications.

talks.title: VPBoost: Accelerating Gradient Boosting with Variable Projection

speakers.name: Elizabeth Newman
speakers.affiliation: Tufts University, U.S.; Deepanshu Verma, Clemson University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Gradient boosting (GB) constructs predictive models by sequentially training weak learners that serve as descent directions in function space. While highly successful for decision trees, particularly through frameworks such as XGBoost, applying GB to neural networks (NNs) remains computationally expensive due to the high-dimensional, non-convex optimization required for each weak learner. Recent advances demonstrate that boosted NNs can outperform decision trees on complex data, yet training efficiency remains a critical bottleneck.
Variable projection (VarPro) has emerged as a powerful optimization strategy for separable NNs, which decompose into nonlinear feature extraction followed by linear mapping. By analytically eliminating the linear weights through partial optimization, VarPro reduces the parameter space and accelerates convergence.
We propose VPBoost, a novel algorithm that combines VarPro with an XGBoost's algorithm for gradient boosting. For each weak learner, VPBoost solves a reduced optimization problem over only the nonlinear parameters while maintaining optimal linear weights analytically. This exploitation of separability yields a bi-level optimization framework where the outer problem minimizes over featurizer parameters and the inner problem provides closed-form solutions for linear weights.
Our methodology establishes convergence guarantees analogous to standard gradient descent while achieving faster training per weak learner and requiring fewer ensemble members. Moreover, gradient boosting integrates naturally with VarPro, reducing the computational and implementation complexity barrier that VarPro traditionally presents. Numerical experiments demonstrate substantial computational savings and improved generalization compared to traditional gradient boosting approaches for NNs, positioning VPBoost as an efficient alternative for scientific machine learning and data-intensive applications.

talks.title: Machine Learning for UQ in Inverse Problems Governed by Non-linear Dynamical Systems

speakers.name: German Villalobos
speakers.affiliation: University of Houston, U.S.; Johann Rudi, Virginia Tech, U.S.; Andreas Mang , University of Houston, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
We study neural networks (NNs) for estimating hidden parameters and quantifying uncertainty from noisy data. Parameter estimation is formulated as a Bayesian inverse problem for parametrized nonlinear systems. The problem poses major challenges for classical methods due to strong nonlinearities, nonconvexity, and sharp gradients. We show that NNs address these challenges by learning reconstruction maps from observational data. Using a single forward pass, the NN infers model parameters, noise parameters for both autocorrelated additive noise and stochastic differential equation–driven noise, and the posterior covariance matrix. We compare different NN architectures, analyze the impact of noise on accuracy, and report training times on specialized hardware. Our results demonstrate that NNs provide an efficient and flexible framework for parameter and uncertainty estimation in dynamical systems.
