sessions.title: Advancing Uncertainty Quantification Through Open-Source Software - Part II of II

talks.title: UncertaintyQuantification.jl: Reliability Analysis and Simulation under Uncertainty in Julia

speakers.name: Ander Gray
speakers.affiliation: UK Atomic Energy Authority, United Kingdom; Jasper Behrensdorf

speakers.name: Lukas Fritsch
speakers.affiliation: Leibniz Universität Hannover, Germany; Matteo Broggi

talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is central to making reliable predictions in science and engineering, particularly when models are exposed to variability in inputs, parameters, or data. This talk presents UncertaintyQuantification.jl, a Julia package designed to streamline uncertainty modelling and reliability analysis for the computational sciences. The package implements some state-of-the-art reliability methods, including Monte Carlo, line sampling, and subset simulation, making it possible to efficiently estimate rare event probabilities and failure domains in high-dimensional systems.
Beyond more typical probabilistic approaches, it offers tools for handling imprecise probabilities, such as interval and set-valued uncertainty models, enabling a conservative quantification even when information is limited or ambiguous. The package integrates easily with external solvers and scales from desktop prototyping to HPC clusters. Numerical examples will illustrate how these methods may be used in challenging risk assessments involving stochastic variability and epistemic uncertainty, including applications to large-scale nuclear fusion models on HPC systems.

talks.title: Operator Learning for Wildfire and Smoke Dynamics

speakers.name: Zachary Morrow
speakers.affiliation: John D. Jakeman, Joseph Hart, Bart van Bloemen Wanders

talks.abstract:
.
High-consequence physical systems often feature quite complex dynamics, which are too computationally burdensome within outer-loop analysis.  Wildfires are a prominent example of such a physical, causing widespread health and financial damage each year. Typically, however, they are simulated with a two-way coupled system of partial differential equations (PDEs) for the fire front and the atmosphere.
Surrogate modeling replaces a complex model with a less accurate but significantly faster approximation, which makes repeated querying tractable. The proper choice of surrogate depends not only on the properties of the complex model but also on the desired outer-loop analysis. In this talk, we discuss two outer-loop analyses in which different surrogate models are necessary to capture the relevant model behavior and ensure computational tractability: ignition-point inversion (approximated with deep flow maps) and smoke impacts (approximated with a basis of polynomial operators). The implementation of both surrogate models is available in open-source software.

Zachary Morrow, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., zbmorro@sandia.gov

talks.title: RobustGaSP, RobustCalibration: Two Packages for Forward Prediction and Inverse Estimation

speakers.name: Clayton Ellis
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Santa Barbara, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we introduce RobustGaSP and RobustCalibration packages for forward prediction and inversion estimation. The first package implements the parallel partial Gaussian process surrogate model for emulating expensive computer simulations with either scalable output or massive coordinates. The tool is implemented in the RobustGaSP package available in R, MATLAB, and Python, for predicting both scalar- and vector-valued outputs with uncertainty assessment through the predictive standard deviation. The second tool is implemented in the RobustCalibration package, which handles Bayesian data inversion or model calibration by one or multiple types of experimental observations. A unique feature of the package is the inclusion of fast surrogate models of both scalar- and vector-valued computer simulations that bypass the expensive simulation in one line of code. Additionally, any difference between the simulation and reality can be incorporated by modeling the discrepancy function as a Gaussian process or scaled Gaussian process model. These tools have been applied in a wide range of science and engineering research problems that include emulating expensive simulation at molecular-, meso- and macro-scales, active learning with error control, and data inversion with satellite interferograms or velocity fields from microscopy data.
Authors
Clayton Ellis, University of California, Santa Barbara, U.S., claytonellis@ucsb.edu

sessions.title: Optimal Experimental Design for Digital Twins - Part II of III

talks.title: Risk-aware Nonlinear Bayesian Experimental Design
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian optimal experimental design typically aims to maximize expected information gain, equivalent to maximizing mutual information. This reflects a risk-neutral perspective, treating all possible outcomes based on their average Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence between prior and posterior. However, in settings where risk aversion is important, expectation may not be the most appropriate objective. We propose a new criterion based on superquantile information gain, which captures the tail behavior of the information distribution and aligns with risk-averse decision-making. We also develop a Monte Carlo estimator tailored for this criterion. The approach is demonstrated on synthetic benchmarks and a sensor placement problem in fluid dynamics.
Authors
Ellen Meyer, University of Michigan, U.S., ecmeyer@umich.edu

talks.title: Adaptive Sensor Optimization with Performance Guarantees for Digital Twins
talks.abstract:
.
The scalable optimization of sparse sensor placement is crucial for prediction, sensing and control of safety-critical engineering systems. Identifying the fewest possible sensor measurements often translates into sparsity penalties on optimization design variables. Current approaches rely on relaxation, heuristics, and trial-and-error selection of hyperparameters, which challenge the interpretability and verification of resulting sensor placements. In this talk, we propose a statistical mechanics approach for robust sparse sensor placement, using free energies to analyze the optimization landscape, optimize hyperparameters and quantify uncertainty with respect to noise and data volume. We illustrate how this approach provides optimization landscapes and critical noise regimes for reconstruction of high-dimensional fields from sparse sensors and data-driven priors. We showcase how these tools can be used for constrained optimization of sensor placement in nuclear energy digital twins.
Authors
Krithika Manohar, University of Washington, U.S., kmanohar@uw.edu

talks.title: Optimal Experimental Design for Digital Twins Using a Network-Flow Approach

speakers.name: Marco Mangano
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Paul Manns, Technische Universität Dortmund, Germany; Sven Leyffer, Carlo Graziani

talks.abstract:
.
Efficient data assimilation is a significant bottleneck towards the development of highly scalable and reliable digital twins. Uncertainty quantification and optimization becomes particularly challenging when either the nature of the system or cost limitations prevent the deployment of a large number of sensors for data collection.  We propose a novel trajectory design approach for the optimal experimental design (OED) of contiguous data acquisition based on a network-flow formulation. In contrast to conventional control strategies, we discretize the spatial domain into a set of network nodes and identify the optimal path between nodes to minimize uncertainty. This approach allows for a more thorough exploration of the design space than what multi-start local optimizations can do.   The network-flow approach we propose translates into a challenging discrete optimization problem, whose objective is expensive to evaluate, highly nonlinear, and can be non-smooth. Moreover, the objective function needs to be evaluated over the entire path instead of being summed edge-by-edge. With an increasing number of edges, the number of possible paths rapidly increases and the combinatorial problem becomes intractable. We showcase a branch-and-bound algorithm we developed, which leverages tailored bounds and constraints to tackle the optimization at a tractable computational cost.

sessions.title: Bayesian Inverse Problems: Methods and Applications - Part I of II

talks.title: Spotlight Inversion by Orthogonal Projections in a Bayesian Framework

speakers.name: Daniela Calvetti
speakers.affiliation: Case Western Reserve University, U.S.; Nuutti Hyvonen, Aalto University, Finland; Ville P. Kolehmainen, University of Eastern Finland, Finland; Erkki Somersalo, Case Western Reserve University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In inverse problems, the goal is to estimate unknown parameters from indirect noisy observations. It is not uncommon that the forward model assigning the observed variables to given values of the unknowns depend on variables that are not of primary interest, often referred to as nuisance parameters. In this article, we consider linear inverse problems, and propose a novel technique based on orthogonal projections to eliminate, or at least mitigate, the contribution of the nuisance parameters on the data. The approach is referred to as spotlight inversion, as it allows to focus on the part of primary interest of the unknown parameter, leaving the uninteresting part in the shadow. Focusing the spotlight on the portion of the solution of interest may lead to very substantial reductions in the computational complexity, as illustrated with computed examples.
Authors
Daniela Calvetti, Case Western Reserve University, U.S., dxc57@case.edu
Nuutti Hyvonen, Aalto University, Finland, nuutti.hyvonen@aalto.fi

talks.title: Variable Projection through a Bayesian Lens
talks.abstract:
.
Many inverse problems, including convolution-based models arising in imaging, involve operators whose kernels depend on unknown parameters. In such settings, inference requires estimating these parameters while accounting for uncertainty. When the forward model is linear in some variables and nonlinear in others, the resulting posterior distribution exhibits exploitable structure. In this presentation, we show that the classical variable projection method admits a natural Bayesian interpretation. By marginalizing over the linear variables, we obtain a reduced posterior over the nonlinear parameters that coincides with the variable projection objective. This perspective enables efficient sampling in the reduced space, improving computational efficiency while retaining principled uncertainty quantification for inverse problems with separable structure.
Authors
Jordan T. Dworaczyk, Arizona State University, U.S., jtdworac@asu.edu
Malena I. Espanol, Arizona State University, U.S., malena.espanol@asu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Efficient Parameter Estimation for Separable Nonlinear Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Good estimates of hyperparameters can be critical for solving inverse problems accurately, but estimating these hyperparameters often requires solving a challenging and expensive optimization problem involving log determinants.  In this work, we consider two optimization approaches, both of which involve stochastic average approximations (SAA) of the objective function.  The first approach uses a Monte Carlo approach to approximate the objective function and gradient. The second approach is a majorization-minimization approach that replaces a challenging optimization problem with a sequence of simpler ones.  Specifically, we use a majorization function for the log-determinant term and combine it with a randomized trace estimator to form a majorant.  Both of these methods can be used within a general framework for hyperparameter estimation that includes both estimating standard hyperparameters (e.g., noise and prior variances) and estimating model parameters (e.g., unknown parameters that define the forward process). That is, we consider separable nonlinear inverse problems, where the forward model assumes a parametric form, and the goal is to improve the model parameters to more accurately solve the resulting linear inverse problem. By treating the model parameters as hyperparameters and using the proposed approaches for hyperparameter estimation, we provide an efficient approach to estimate model parameters in separable nonlinear inverse problems.
Authors
Elle Buser, Emory University, U.S., ebuser@emory.edu
Julianne Chung, Emory University, U.S., julianne.mei-lynn.chung@emory.edu

talks.title: Sample-Based Uncertainty Quantification for Inverse Problems with Poisson Data
talks.abstract:
.
Inverse problems arise when physics-based models are fit to observational data. The unknown parameters in such models are often spatially distributed and become large-scale after discretization. The resulting parameter estimation problems are typically ill-posed, meaning that solutions are unstable with respect to errors in the data. In this talk, I will focus on inverse problems where the data follows a Poisson distribution, a common scenario in imaging applications. I will formulate the Poisson inverse problem within a Bayesian inference framework, allowing for uncertainty quantification through the posterior distribution. I will then present MCMC methods for sampling from the posterior in the Poisson case that incorporate nonnegativity constraints, which are natural in imaging applications.
Authors
Johnathan M. Bardsley, University of Montana, U.S., bardsleyj@mso.umt.edu

sessions.title: UQ for Personalized Cardiac Models - Part II of II

talks.title: Multilevel MCMC for Bayesian Inference of IART Scar Parameters with Uncertainty

speakers.name: Piet Claus
speakers.affiliation: Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium; Hans Dierckx, Leids Universitair Medisch Centrum, Netherlands; Giovanni Samaey, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium

talks.abstract:
.
Cardiac arrhythmia arise from abnormal electrical activity in the heart and are associated with a large number of deaths. Their underlying pathophysiology varies between individuals, necessitating patient-specific treatment. Personalized models or cardiac digital twins offer a promising new approach. However, current imaging techniques limit the fidelity with which individual cardiac anatomy and function can be captured, making uncertainty quantification (UQ) in model predictions essential.
We employ Bayesian inference for parameter estimation in a cardiac electrophysiology model and for UQ that accounts for measurement and discretization errors. In a two-dimensional cardiac tissue slab, we simulate the electrical signal propagation that coordinates heart contraction. From mapping catheter data, we aim to estimate the shape parameters of electrically inactive regions, or scars, which disrupt signal propagation and can lead to arrhythmia.
In our talk, we propose a Markov chain Monte Carlo method that requires a minimal number of samples for posterior estimation by defining a likelihood based on summary statistics and by using an adaptive proposal distribution. To account for the discretization error, we inflate the likelihood variance, and we tailor the meshing strategies used for the forward simulations in the accept-reject step. Further computational efficiency is achieved through a multilevel method that substitutes expensive simulations with cheaper approximations.

Maarten Volkaerts, Katholieke Universiteit Leuven, Belgium, maarten.volkaerts@kuleuven.be

talks.title: Reconstructing Cardiac Dynamics and Model Parameters Using Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
Cardiac ventricular action potentials arise from nonlinear interactions between transmembrane voltage, ionic currents, and intracellular ion concentrations. Current experimental methods cannot simultaneously record membrane potential and multiple currents, limiting understanding of how perturbations such as pacing changes or drug treatments initiate or prevent arrhythmias. Biophysical models simulate these dynamics but rely on averaged parameters, reducing their ability to capture biological heterogeneity. We address this using data assimilation, a computational approach that integrates models with noisy observations to jointly estimate state variables and parameters. A heterogeneous virtual population of guinea pig ventricular cells was created by varying ionic conductances, with Gaussian noise added to simulated voltage and calcium signals. The method reconstructed the full state space with error below noise levels and accurately estimated conductances. Compared with two post hoc optimization methods, data assimilation achieved higher accuracy with less computation. Applied to a virtual population of human iPSC-derived ventricular cells, it successfully reconstructed complex action potential morphologies and key parameters. These results show that data assimilation enables precise state reconstruction and parameter estimation in heterogeneous populations, enhancing the predictive power of biophysical models for studying arrhythmia mechanisms.
Authors
Mario Mendez Perez, Ohio State University, U.S., mendezperez.1@osu.edu
Seth H. Weinberg, Ohio State University, U.S., weinberg.147@osu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Active Learning for Uncertainty Quantification in Personalized Cardiac Models
talks.abstract:
.
Personalized computer simulation models provide physicians with a patient-specific virtual organ to predict therapeutic responses, at minimal to no risk to the patient. However, clinical translation of these models has been slow. A key barrier is the variability in model predictions that, if not quantified, will hamper decision support. Probabilistic inference in this context is challenging, because standard Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling requires repeated model simulations that are computationally infeasible.  Surrogate modeling can be an effective solution by building an efficient approximation of the expensive simulation model.  To build a “globally accurate” approximation of a highly nonlinear model, however, is computationally infeasible without sacrificing local approximation accuracy.
In this talk, we describe our effort in cast surrogate modeling as a machine-learning problem, in which the training data are virtually infinite (in a continuous space) but expensive to obtain (incurring model evaluations). We describe the concept of “active surrogate modeling” where surrogate construction will actively search the optimal training points at which to query the model. This high-dimensional active search is further embedded into a low-dimensional latent space through probabilistic generative modeling, which enables surrogate-accelerated MCMC sampling. We demonstrate the efficacy of these developments in the context of patient-specific cardiac modeling.
Authors
Linwei Wang, Rochester Institute of Technology, U.S., linwei.wang@rit.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Astrodynamics - Part I of III

talks.title: Approximate Bayesian Inference for Cislunar Tracking Using Alternate Coordinate Representations

speakers.name: Kyle DeMars
speakers.affiliation: Purdue University, U.S.; Maaninee Gupta, Texas A&M University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The cislunar dynamical environment is characterized by highly nonlinear and chaotic behavior. Conventional approaches to filtering and tracking, which tend to impose simplifying assumptions on the dynamics and uncertainty realization, are no longer appropriate.
The Modified Generalized Equinoctial Orbital Elements (M-GEqOEs) are a promising technique for high-fidelity propagation of states and uncertainty in cislunar space. These orbital elements may be embedded with any necessary perturbations, conservative or non-conservative, and the element set is defined for the entire cislunar domain. In addition, uncertainty evolved using the M-GEqOEs preserves Gaussian behavior for longer propagation times than conventional Cartesian propagation, yielding improvements in uncertainty realism.
The downside of the M-GEqOEs (or other non-Cartesian coordinates) is that they tend to introduce additional nonlinearities relative to conventional measurement sets, such as range and range-rate measurements or line-of-sight measurements. The added nonlinearity tends to deteriorate the performance of standard filtering and tracking approaches. This work combines the M-GEqOE methodology with recent advances in approximate Bayesian filtering to formulate a parameter flow approach that ameliorates the issues of coordinate system selection in the measurement update.
Simulation results are considered for standard cislunar test cases to demonstrate the efficacy of the developed approach.
Authors
Kyle DeMars, Purdue University, U.S., kdemars@purdue.edu
Maaninee Gupta, Texas A&M University, U.S., maanineegupta@tamu.edu

talks.title: Advances in Kernel-Based Ensemble Gaussian Mixture Filtering with Applications in Astrodynamics

speakers.name: Renato Zanetti
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Andrey A. Popov, University of Hawaii at Manoa, U.S.; Uwe Hanebeck, Karlsruhe Institute of Technology, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
This work advances techniques for onboard nonlinear filtering with applications in cislunar astrodynamics. An effective onboard filter must be accurate, consistent, efficient, and verifiable. Recent work has shown that the kernel-based Ensemble Gaussian Mixture Filter (EnGMF) is an accurate and consistent nonlinear filter capable of handling non-Gaussian probability distributions. However, its fixed number of Gaussian components restricts efficiency and its random resampling step prevents verifiability. To address these issues, the EnGMF has recently been modified such that it adaptively controls its number of Gaussian components (improving efficiency) and it deterministically resamples components in its resampling step (ensuring verifiability). The presented applications revolve around cislunar orbits, demonstrating the need for nonlinear filtering methods and their potential for deep-space operations. Simulation results indicate that the presented advancements of the EnGMF not only maintain the accuracy and consistency of the traditional EnGMF, but also improve its efficiency and ensure its verifiability&#8212a significant step forward in developing onboard nonlinear filters with applications in cislunar astrodynamics.
Authors
Dalton Durant, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., ddurant@utexas.edu

talks.title: Polynomial Updates for the Unscented Kalman Filter
talks.abstract:
.
Common filters are usually based on the linear approximation of the optimal minimum mean square error estimator. The Unscented Kalman Filter handles nonlinearity of the dynamics and of the measurement equation through the unscented transformation, but remains a linear estimator, meaning that the state estimate is a linear function of the measurement. This talk proposes a polynomial approximation of the optimal estimator, improving the update step of the common UKF. The new update retains the structure of the linear counterpart but includes information from the measurement powers to obtain a more accurate estimate. The result is a polynomial update that better approximates the true Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE), drastically improving the accuracy of the filter, and that can be generalized to any arbitrary order. Whenever needed, the prediction of uncertainties in the UKF is enhanced with the conjugate unscented transformation (CUT), to best obtain the high-order central moments predictions required to perform a polynomial update. Numerical results show the benefits in accuracy and robustness of the new technique, which can be generalized to upgrade other linear estimators to their polynomial versions and to work with non-Gaussian noises.
Authors
Simone Servadio, Iowa State University, U.S., servadio@iastate.edu
Chiran Cherian, Iowa State University, U.S., cbckbc@iastate.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Polynomial Chaos-Based Estimation Leveraging Dimension Reduction and the Active Subspace

speakers.name: Zee Toler
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
With increasing mission complexity and space activity prompting the desire for more capable orbit determination methods, uncertainty quantification in astrodynamics has increasingly relied on non-Gaussian estimators and surrogate modeling techniques. One type of surrogate model that has seen increasing usage in this field is polynomial chaos, a response surface method that maps a set of input uncertainties to an output space using orthogonal polynomial basis functions. Here, we present the Intrusive Polynomial Chaos Filter (IPCF), a nonlinear estimator utilizing polynomial chaos expansions (PCEs) to represent the quantities of interest. Like many complex uncertainty quantification methods, PCEs are vulnerable to the curse of dimensionality, where growth in dimension and degree disproportionately increase complexity. In the IPCF’s case, the nonlinear update increases the solution’s degree, and new random inputs are added each time the surrogate is propagated or updated with a measurement. To control the surrogate’s growth, the degree and dimension are reduced to those of the original prior PCE after each update. In this work, we present two methods of input dimension reduction, each with different advantages. The first leverages sensitivity analysis via the Sobol indices to reduce the surrogate cost-effectively. The second identifies and exploits the PCE’s active subspace to preserve more information at a higher computational cost.
Authors
Zee Toler, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., zee.toler@utexas.edu

sessions.title: Learning Dynamics with Evolving Uncertainty: Interplay of Bayesian Modeling, Transformers, and State-Space AI - Part I of III

talks.title: A Natural Primal-Dual Hybrid Gradient Method for Adversarial Neural Network Training on Solving Partial Differential Equations

speakers.name: Shu Liu
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a scalable deep learning–based algorithm for solving PDEs that integrates preconditioned gradient methods with adversarial training. By multiplying the PDE with a test function, we reformulate the problem as an inf–sup saddle-point problem and apply the Primal–Dual Hybrid Gradient (PDHG) algorithm. To enhance efficiency and stability, we introduce appropriate preconditioning operators into the proximal steps of the PDHG algorithm, yielding a natural gradient ascent–descent scheme for updating neural network parameters. The resulting natural gradients are evaluated efficiently using the Krylov subspace method (MINRES) involving iterative matrix–vector products. Convergence guarantees are established for time-continuous algorithms when applied to linear equations, particularly elliptic PDEs in divergence form. We validate the method on a wide range of linear and nonlinear PDEs with dimensions ranging from 1 to 50. Numerical results demonstrate more stable convergence and higher accuracy of our approach than several widely used deep learning–based solvers.
Authors
Shu                    Liu, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S., sl25bn@fsu.edu

talks.title: In-Context Learning of Linear Dynamical Systems with Transformers: Error Bounds and Depth-Separation

speakers.name: Yuxuan Zhao
speakers.affiliation: University of Minnesota, Twin Cities, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This paper investigates approximation-theoretic aspects of the in-context learning capability of the transformers in representing a family of noisy linear dynamical systems. Our first theoretical result establishes an upper bound on the approximation error of multi-layer transformers with respect to an -testing loss uniformly defined across tasks. This result demonstrates that transformers with logarithmic depth can achieve error bounds comparable with those of the least-squares estimator. In contrast, our second result establishes a non-diminishing lower bound on the approximation error for a class of single-layer linear transformers, which suggests a depth-separation phenomenon for transformers in the in-context learning of dynamical systems. Moreover, this second result uncovers a critical distinction in the approximation power of single-layer linear transformers when learning from IID versus non-IID data.
Authors
Yuxuan Zhao, University of Minnesota, Twin Cities, U.S., zhao1895@umn.edu

talks.title: Transformers Learn Variable-Order Markov Chains In-Context
talks.abstract:
.
Large language models show strong in-context learning (ICL), but how transformers implement it in complex settings is unclear. Prior work targets fixed-order Markov chains (FOMC); natural language fits variable-order Markov chains (VOMC), or context trees (CTs). We study VOMC ICL as data compression on small alphabets and low orders, using context-tree weighting (CTW) and prediction by partial matching (PPM) as baselines; CTW is Bayesian-optimal under a class of CTW priors. Empirically, (1) transformers can compress VOMC in context, while PPM degrades; (2) depth matters little—even two layers work well; and (3) models trained/tested on non-CTW priors can beat CTW. To explain this, we inspect attention and extract two mechanisms, yielding two constructions: (1) a D+2-layer transformer that accurately mimics CTW for CTs of maximum order D; and (2) a 2-layer transformer that uses the feed-forward network for probability blending. Unlike the FOMC setting, an explicit counting mechanism is key. Implemented synthetic layers produce hybrid transformers that match or exceed standard transformers’ VOMC ICL while using far fewer parameters.

Chao Tian, Texas A&M University, U.S., chao.tian@ece.tamu.edu

talks.title: Learning Solution Operator of Dynamical Systems with Diffusion Maps Kernel Ridge Regression
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, I will discuss a simple kernel ridge regression (KRR) framework with a dynamic-aware validation strategy for long-term prediction of complex dynamical systems. The proposed approach implicitly adapts to the intrinsic geometry of the system's invariant set, without requiring explicit manifold reconstruction or attractor modeling, procedures that often limit predictive performance. I will demonstrate the robustness of this approach across a broad range of systems, including smooth manifolds, chaotic attractors, and high-dimensional spatiotemporal flows, DM-KRR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art random feature, neural-network and operator-learning methods in both accuracy and data efficiency.
Authors
John Harlim, Pennsylvania State University, U.S., jharlim@psu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: On the Dimensionality of Dynamics on Complex Networks: from Exact Reductions to Approximate Ones
talks.abstract:
.
Behind the blur of high-dimensional nonlinear dynamics and the intricate organization of complex systems lie essential mechanisms that drive the emergence of macroscopic phenomena. Diverse approaches to dimensionality reduction have been developed to uncover these mechanisms. Reducing the dimensionality of dynamical systems on complex networks is challenging, though exact or approximate reductions are sometimes possible. This talk presents concrete examples and their implications. First, we consider the Kuramoto model with heterogeneous phase lags on general weighted, directed, signed graph. Using Koopman theory, we derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of distinct constants of motion in the model, including new ones generated from Lie symmetries. These results reveal a broad class of network motifs that support conserved quantities, thereby enabling exact dimension reductions and providing new insights into synchronization. Second, we show that complex systems are often modeled using dynamics that depend on low-rank matrices describing the networks of interactions. We present three indicators of the low-rank hypothesis and show its ubiquity in random network models. We then verify the hypothesis for empirical networks of various origins and examine its repercussions for nonlinear dynamics. In particular, we demonstrate that networks described by low rank matrices cause the dynamics they support to collapse onto a much lower-dimensional manifold.
Authors
Vincent Thibeault, Université Laval, Canada, vincent.thibeault.1@ulaval.ca

sessions.title: Coffee Break and Poster Presentations

talks.title: Low-Rank All-at-Once Stochastic Galerkin Solvers for Incompressible Flows

speakers.name: Guillaume Ramelet
speakers.affiliation: Michelin, U.S.; Ahmed Shokry, École Polytechnique de Paris, France; Aymeric Dieuleveut, Ecole Polytechnique, France Bayesian Nonparametric Calibration of Climate Models

talks.abstract:
.
We address the challenge of applying conformal prediction (CP) to online soft sensing of product quality in batch processes, where the key assumption of data exchangeability is violated due to temporal dependence and evolving operating conditions.    We propose a mechanism that integrates Split Conformal Prediction (SCP) with Just-in-Time Learning (JITL) in a latent space constructed via self-organizing maps (SOM).    At each prediction time, a buffer of past latent representations is collected based on (i) topological similarity and (ii) distance-based similarity.    A new model is then trained from scratch on a randomly partitioned buffer: one subset for model fitting and one for calibration.    SCP quantiles are derived over multiple splits, yielding distribution-free prediction intervals tailored to the current context.    This procedure is repeated for every batch, resulting in a dynamic, locally calibrated prediction mechanism.    We investigate whether local exchangeability can be formalized in latent spaces with such a selection mechanism and explore conditions under which theoretical coverage guarantees may be derived.    The approach is evaluated on a Michelin rubber mixing process, highlighting its potential to provide reliable uncertainty quantification in non-exchangeable, time-dependent industrial systems.
Authors

talks.title: Bayesian Nonparametric Calibration of Climate Models

speakers.name: Anirban Chakraborty
speakers.affiliation: Texas A&M University, U.S.; Paul Wiemann, Ohio State University, U.S.; Daniel Drennan, Texas A&M University, U.S.; Felix Jiminez, Amazon, U.S.; Matthias Katzfuss, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S. Projected Score Matching for Generative Modeling

talks.abstract:
.
A climate model (CM) is a set of differential equations representing the behavior of the Earth system. For a given input-parameter value, the CM encodes a distribution that is analytically intractable but samples of which can be produced at high computational cost by running the CM with slightly perturbed initial conditions. The goal is to calibrate the CM by finding the parameter value that most closely matches the CM to the true climate distribution. Existing methodologies achieve this calibration task by fitting Gaussian process to the CM samples that only targets the mean and does not address the distributional uncertainty present within the climate model. Here, we propose a calibration algorithm via Bayesian optimization using a flexible probabilistic surrogate model that can learn any arbitrary distribution through a random bijective mapping between the CM samples and standard normal distribution. We further model the bijective maps using autoregressive Gaussian processes whose inputs include the CM input parameters. Our framework is scalable and hence it can be applied to large spatial climate fields. We demonstrate the performance of the approach on numerical examples.
Authors
Anirban Chakraborty, Texas A&M University, U.S., chakraan@musc.edu

Matthias Katzfuss, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., katzfuss@gmail.com

talks.title: Projected Score Matching for Generative Modeling and Inverse Problems in Chaotic Systems

speakers.name: Nisha Chandramoorthy
speakers.affiliation: University of Chicago, U.S. Phase Reduction in Particle Method for Simulation of Stochastic Limit Cycle Oscillators

talks.abstract:
.
Physical measures associated with chaotic systems are often singular but admit a conditional density on the lower-dimensional unstable manifold. In this work, we exploit this lower-dimensional absolutely continuous structure to enable faster generative modeling of such measures. In particular, the learned drift term or vector field of the generative model only uses the well-defined unstable component of the score. To supply the training data for the unstable scores, we use an existing, rigorous algorithm from Chandramoorthy and Wang SIAM J. Appl. Dyn. Sys. 2022. Using these conditional scores, and numerically estimated unstable tangent vectors, we use the learned vector field and a reconstruction scheme to sample from the target measures. We show preliminary numerical results on low-dimensional chaotic systems, wherein we obtain improved reconstructions compared to a vanilla score-based model for the same number of training examples.

talks.title: Phase Reduction in Particle Method for Simulation of Stochastic Limit Cycle Oscillators

speakers.name: Daniel Forger
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S. SIPF-PIC: Efficient Large-Scale Simulation of 3D Chemotactic Systems

talks.abstract:
.
Noisy oscillators arise in the modeling of many scientific problems, particularly in physics, neuroscience, ecology and climate studies. Phase reduction is a traditional approach that simplifies the dynamics. However, the assumption of weak noise in phase reduction limits its application. Particle methods, on the other hand, provide more accurate results, but are often computationally expensive. In this work, we present how phase reduction techniques can be incorporated into particle methods to study population level behaviors of noisy coupled oscillators. We use elliptical Gaussian basis functions to solve the Fokker-Planck equation that describes the evolution of the probability density function. Previous studies have suggested splitting and combining the Gaussian kernels under certain conditions. We propose an original particle shrinking method based on phase reduction. Our method reduces the number of particles and accelerates the particle updating process, thereby achieving higher computational efficiency. At the same time, we preserve the structure of the possibly high-dimensional state space, which avoids oversimplification. In particular, we show that this method is well adapted to systems with relaxation oscillation. As illustration, we apply our method to 4-dimensional and 5-dimensional Hodgkin-Huxley type neuronal models.

Yangyang Du, University of Michigan, U.S., yyadu@umich.edu
Daniel Forger, University of Michigan, U.S., forger@umich.edu

talks.title: SIPF-PIC: Efficient Large-Scale Simulation of 3D Chemotactic Systems

speakers.name: Jingyuan Hu
speakers.affiliation: University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong Total HSIC: A New Index for Global Sensitivity Analysis

talks.abstract:
.
The Keller-Segel system models biological aggregation driven by chemotaxis, such as bacterial pattern formation. Simulating its complex 3D dynamics, especially near blow-up singularities where density diverges, is computationally prohibitive with existing methods. To overcome this, we developed the SIPF-PIC framework, a novel hybrid method combining Lagrangian particle tracking with spectral field solvers using optimized interpolation. Crucially, SIPF-PIC reduces the cost per timestep from $\mathcal{O}(PH^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(P + H^3 \log H)$ while maintaining convergence, where $P$ is the particle count and $H$ the grid resolution per dimension. This enables large-scale simulations capturing elusive blow-up structures like evolving ring singularities, beyond simple point collapse.

talks.title: Total HSIC: A New Index for Global Sensitivity Analysis

speakers.name: Alen Alexanderian
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S. Cancelled Spreader Identification under Temporal Data Uncertainty: A Two-Stage Framework Arash Latifi

speakers.name: Caterina Scoglio
speakers.affiliation: Kansas State University, U.S. Regularity Estimate

speakers.name: Sparse Approximation of Pathwise Robust Duncan-Mortensen-Zakai Equation

talks.abstract:
.
Global sensitivity analysis (GSA) is a fundamental component of uncertainty quantification used to identify which input parameters most influence a model's output. Traditional methods, such as variance-based Sobol' indices, are limited because they only capture influence that manifests through variance and assume statistical independence of inputs. This can lead to inaccurate sensitivity scores, particularly when dealing with complex, non-linear dependencies.
We propose an alternative approach using the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion (HSIC). Unlike variance-based methods, HSIC is a non-parametric measure that captures general statistical dependence, including non-linear and higher-order effects, by embedding probability distributions into Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Spaces (RKHS). Our findings demonstrate that this approach offers a compelling and flexible alternative to traditional GSA methods, capable of detecting a wider range of statistical dependencies.

talks.title: Spreader Identification under Temporal Data Uncertainty: A Two-Stage Framework

speakers.name: Yuhua Meng
speakers.affiliation: University of Hong Kong, China; Zhongjian Wang, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore; Stephen S.T. Yau, Tsinghua University, P. R. China; Zhiwen Zhang, University of Hong Kong, China Uncertainty Quantification in Tracer Transport: Pressure-to-concentration Propagation vs. pCN-DE McMC Inference

talks.abstract:
.
In this paper, we establish
a priori
estimate for arbitrary-order derivatives of solution to pathwise robust Duncan-Mortensen-Zakai equation within the framework of weighted Sobolev space. The weight function, which vanishes on the physical boundary, is crucial in completing the
a priori
estimate proof, but also introduce a loss of regularity estimate near the boundary. Therefore, we employ the Sobolev inequalities and their weighted analogues to sharpen the regularity bound, providing improvements in both classical Sobolev space and H{\"o}lder continuity estimates. The refined regularity estimate reinforces the plausibility of the quantized tensor train (QTT) method and consolidates the conditions under which the method converges. To further enhance the capacity of the method to solve nonlinear filtering problem in real time manner, we refine the method in the case of a functional polyadic drift $f$ and observation $h$. Finally, we perform some numerical simulations to reaffirm our theory. For high-dimensional cubic sensor problems, our method demonstrates superior efficiency and accuracy in comparison to the particle filter (PF) and extended Kalman filter (EKF). Beyond this, for multi-mode problems, while PF exhibits lack of precision due to its stochastic nature and EKF is constrained by the Gaussian assumption, the enhanced method provides exact reconstruction of multi-mode conditional density function.
Authors

talks.abstract:

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Tracer Transport: Pressure-to-concentration Propagation vs. pCN-DE McMC Inference

speakers.name: Marcio Borges
speakers.affiliation: LNCC/MCT, Brazil; Felipe Pereira, University of Texas at Dallas, U.S. Numerical Approximation of Effective Diffusivities in Homogenization of Nondivergence-Form Equations with Large Drift by a Lagrangian Method

talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty quantification in porous media flow remains a major challenge due to the high dimensionality of the parameter space, which strongly limits the efficiency of standard Markov chain Monte Carlo (McMC) methods. Even after dimensionality reduction, achieving robust sampling with fast convergence is far from trivial, and the difficulty becomes even more pronounced in transient problems. In previous work, the authors showed that the Preconditioned Crank–Nicolson Differential Evolution (pCN-DE) algorithm yields satisfactory performance for 2D single-phase flow problems with five-spot well configurations. Building upon these results, we now investigate uncertainty propagation for solute transport by considering concentration fields in a 2D tracer problem. Specifically, we compare two approaches: (i) propagating uncertainty from the pressure solutions to the concentration, and (ii) performing a direct McMC simulation on the concentration data using the pCN-DE method. The comparison highlights the differences between propagated and directly inferred statistics, providing insights into the efficiency and accuracy of each approach.

Michel Tosin, LNCC/MCT, Brazil, michelt@posgrad.lncc.br

talks.title: Numerical Approximation of Effective Diffusivities in Homogenization of Nondivergence-Form Equations with Large Drift by a Lagrangian Method

speakers.name: Han Wu
speakers.affiliation: University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong Activation Level Uncertainty Quantification of Hyperspectral Neural Radiance Fields for High Consequence Remote Sensing Applications

talks.abstract:
.
In this paper, we study numerical methods for the homogenization of linear second-order elliptic equations in nondivergence-form with periodic diffusion coefficients and large drift terms. Upon noting that the effective diffusion matrix can be characterized through the long-time variance of an associated diffusion process, we construct a Lagrangian numerical scheme based on a direct simulation of the underlying stochastic differential equation and utilizing the framework of modified equations, thereby avoiding the need to solve the Fokker–Planck–Kolmogorov equation. Through modified equation analysis, we derive higher-order weak convergence rates for our method. Finally, we conduct numerical experiments to demonstrate the accuracy of the proposed method. The results show that the method efficiently computes effective diffusivities, even in high dimensions.

Han Wu, University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, shivers641@gmail.com

talks.title: Activation Level Uncertainty Quantification of Hyperspectral Neural Radiance Fields for High Consequence Remote Sensing Applications
talks.abstract:
.
Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) is a key capability for detection of targets such as gases, liquids, or solids in remote sensing applications. The detection process becomes challenging when objects in the scene occlude the target. We propose to advance the utility of 3D scene understanding methods, neural radiance fields (NeRFs), to cohesively synthesize multi-observation HSI data into a single scene thereby providing the ability to look around targets or obstructions. NeRFs have shown promising results in addressing this task, specifically for gas plume detection and identification, but the limitations of these newer models have not been fully explored. We leverage Gaussian processes to capture neuron level uncertainty per layer. By quantifying uncertainty at the the activation level, we gain insight into the stability of the learned representation at each stage of the model. The representation level uncertainty highlights where the network is confident in its spectral-spacial encoding versus where it may be under-constrained. For remote sensing applications, decision-makers require detection outputs with measures of confidence in those outputs, especially in complex environments where occlusions, atmospheric effects, or sparse measurements can degrade performance. Understanding and propagating representation uncertainty allows us to flag unreliable predictions in a single off-the-shelf model, prioritize additional observations, and ultimately improve trust and robustness.
Authors
Sophia Zorek, Rice University, U.S., saz2@rice.edu
Zig Hampel-Arias, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., zhampel@lanl.gov
Adra Carr, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., acarr@lanl.gov

sessions.title: Generative Models for Scientific Computing: Trustworthiness, Physical Consistency, Computational Challenges, and Applications - Part II of II

talks.title: Scalable Nonlinear Manifold Reduced Order Models: Domain Decomposition and Cross-PDE Expressivity
talks.abstract:
.
The domain decomposition (DD) nonlinear-manifold reduced-order  model (NM-ROM) represents a computationally efficient strategy that integrates underlying physics principles into a neural network–based, data-  driven framework. Compared to linear subspace methods, NM-ROMs pro-  vide superior expressivity and enhanced reconstruction capabilities, while  the DD strategy enables cost-effective, parallel training of autoencoders by  partitioning the domain into algebraic subdomains. In this work, we de-  velop and assess DD-NM-ROMs for the two-dimensional time-dependent  Burgers’ equation and the diffusion equation, and compare them with  their linear subspace counterparts (DD-LS-ROMs). Both approaches em-  ploy a bottom-up strategy: ROMs are trained on smaller subdomains  and subsequently deployed on larger, composable domains. Remarkably,  the neural network manifold representation demonstrates the ability to  generalize across PDEs: a single network trained jointly on Burgers and  diffusion data can accurately predict unseen solutions of each equation individually. Numerical experiments show that DD-NM-ROMs consistently  outperform DD-LS-ROMs in both accuracy and computational speed-up,  while also enabling stable extrapolation to larger spatial domains. These  findings highlight the potential of DD-NM-ROMs as a scalable and expressive framework for reduced-order modeling of diverse PDE systems.
Authors
Ping-Hsuan Tsai, Virginia Tech, U.S., pinghsuan@vt.edu

talks.title: Operator Learning Approaches for Inverse Problems in Imaging
talks.abstract:
.
Inverse problems in imaging often involve reconstructing unknown quantities from indirect and noisy measurements, and their solution is typically hindered by expensive forward computations and ill-posedness. Recent advances in operator learning provide new perspectives on how to address these challenges. In this talk, we will discuss how neural operator architectures can be used both to accelerate classical Bayesian inference methods and to learn novel operator-to-function mappings arising in imaging applications. In the talk, we will discuss some theoretical approximation guarantees and also illustrate these ideas through examples from medical imaging.
Authors
Anuj Abhishek, Case Western Reserve University, U.S., axa1828@case.edu

talks.title: Generative Ensembles for UQ: A Diffusion Model Approach to Forward and Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Score-based diffusion models offer a unified, probabilistic framework for solving forward and inverse problems in scientific computing (Jacobsen, Zhuang, Duraisamy; SISC, 2025). This presentation demonstrates their power for robust uncertainty quantification (UQ) through contributions in field reconstruction and large- scale weather forecasting. For inverse problems, our spatially-aware diffusion model reconstructs global fields from sparse data by generating an ensemble of plausible realizations (Zhuang, Cheng, Duraisamy; CMAME, 2025). We demonstrate that the ensemble mean converges to the deterministic solution, providing a well-behaved framework for UQ and covariance estimation for data assimilation.  For forward problems, we showcase our LaDCast framework, the first latent diffusion model for global weather forecasting (Zhuang, Cheng, Duraisamy; 2025). It produces a 10-day forecast in under 10 seconds on a single GPU and generates an entire forecast ensemble without artificial noise injections. This provides a computationally efficient and low-bias approach to UQ for a grand-challenge problem.  These examples highlight a pathway toward diffusion-based probabilistic foundation models that are scalable and inherently suited for the UQ demands of modern scientific computing.

Yilin Zhuang, University of Michigan, U.S., ylzhuang@umich.edu

talks.title: Reduced Modeling of Chaotic, Turbulent, and Stochastic Systems via Population Dynamics

speakers.name: Benjamin Peherstorfer
speakers.affiliation: Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Learning models of time-dependent processes that generalize across initial conditions and parameter regimes is a key challenge in machine learning and the computational sciences. For chaotic, turbulent, and stochastic systems, modeling the dynamics of individual trajectories can be exceedingly challenging because trajectories can be erratic and irregular, and in stochastic settings may even be nowhere differentiable. Instead, we propose learning population dynamics, which model how the distribution of the system states evolves over time. By learning population dynamics, we deliberately discard trajectory-specific information to obtain dynamics that are smoother and more well behaved than the underlying sample trajectories. We argue that this loss of information is often an acceptable, principled form of reduced modeling because population-level statistics are maintained, and these are often the quantities that are of interest in scientific and engineering settings.

sessions.title: Does Conditioning Matter? Conditional Diffusion Models for Engineering and Scientific Problems in UQ

talks.title: Conditional Generation of Rare Events with Diffusion Models

speakers.name: Malik Hassanaly
speakers.affiliation: National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S.; Graham Harper, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Zach Grey, National Institute of Standards

speakers.name: Alex Rybchuk
speakers.affiliation: National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S.; Cristian J. Villatoro, University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Andrew Glaws, National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S.; Gianluca Geraci, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Ryan King, National Renewable Energy Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools to generate scenarios design and optimization, offering the ability to generate diverse (high recall) and high-quality (high precision) samples.
For critical energy infrastructures, design constraints are often framed as resilience to rare scenarios, typically expressed as probabilistic constraints. This motivates the need for probability-conditioned scenario generation.
In this work, the problem of generating turbulent fluid flow scenarios conditioned on their probability is explored. This task relies on two separate steps: a) labeling data snapshots with a probability b) conditioning the generation on said probability. For step a), two approaches are proposed: one that relies on normalizing flows to label the data samples; and one that labels data samples from the diffusion process via a score-based likelihood reconstruction. It is shown that while the rarity of the generated scenarios can be controlled, generating events more rare than the ones observed in the dataset is not possible. In view of this result, a multifidelity training approach for the diffusion model is proposed and discussed.

talks.title: A Training-Free Conditional Diffusion Model for Learning Stochastic Dynamical Systems

speakers.name: Yanfang Liu
speakers.affiliation: Middle Tennessee State University, U.S.; Yuan Chen

speakers.name: Dongbin Xiu
speakers.affiliation: Ohio State University, U.S.; Guannan Zhang, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This study introduces a training-free conditional diffusion model for learning unknown stochastic differential equations (SDEs) using data. The proposed approach addresses key challenges in computational efficiency and accuracy for modeling SDEs by utilizing a score-based diffusion model to approximate their stochastic flow map. Unlike the existing methods, this technique is based on an analytically derived closed-form exact score function, which can be efficiently estimated by a Monte Carlo method using trajectory data, and hence eliminates the need for neural network training to learn the score function. By generating labeled data through solving the corresponding reverse ordinary differential equation, the approach enables supervised learning of the flow map. Extensive numerical experiments across various SDE types, including linear, nonlinear, and multi-dimensional systems, demonstrate the versatility and effectiveness of the method. The learned models exhibit significant improvements in predicting both short-term and long-term behaviors of unknown stochastic systems, often surpassing baseline methods like GANs in estimating drift and diffusion coefficients.

talks.title: Constraint-Guided Conditional Diffusion for Power-Grid Generation
talks.abstract:
.
Maps of distribution grids are often incomplete, proprietary, or out of date, yet cities still need to plan new neighborhoods, move lines underground, integrate local energy technologies (rooftop solar, home batteries, EV charging), and rebuild after storms. We frame grid synthesis as conditional generative modeling: a diffusion model that learns a distribution over feasible feeder topologies and samples many plausible designs. Sampling is guided by grid constraints—radial structure, equipment/connection limits, and basic voltage/thermal checks—so the generated networks remain physically plausible while explicitly capturing uncertainty. The result is a practical scenario engine: instead of one “best” network, we produce ensembles ready for standard planning workflows (power flow, hosting-capacity, and resilience studies). For evaluation, we use the generated graphs as inputs to a pipeline that constructs complete, simulation-ready synthetic distribution feeders from map and context data, showing improved realism, diversity, and downstream utility over prior GNN-based topology generators. To our knowledge, this is the first conditional diffusion approach for grid topology generation, offering a data-light, uncertainty-aware path to modern grid planning.

talks.title: Multimodal Data Assimilation Using Score-Based Diffusion Models
talks.abstract:
.
Score-based diffusion modeling is a generative machine learning algorithm that can be used to sample from complex distributions. They achieve this by learning a score function, i.e., the gradient of the log-probability density of the data, and reversing a noising process using the same. Once trained, score-based diffusion models not only generate new samples but also enable zero-shot conditioning of the generated samples on observed data. This promises a novel paradigm for data and model fusion, wherein the implicitly learned distributions of pretrained score-based diffusion models can be updated given the availability of online data in a Bayesian formulation. In this article, we apply such a concept to the super-resolution of a high-dimensional dynamical system, given the real-time availability of low-resolution and experimentally observed sparse sensor measurements from multimodal data. Additional analysis on how score-based sampling can be used for uncertainty estimates is also provided. Our experiments are performed for a super-resolution task that generates the ERA5 atmospheric dataset given sparse observations from a coarse-grained representation of the same and/or from unstructured experimental observations of the IGRA radiosonde dataset. We also demonstrate accurate recovery of the high dimensional state given multiple sources of low-fidelity measurements and AI-based numerical weather prediction models.

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Multifidelity Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of III

talks.title: Uncertainty-aware Mixture of Experts Surrogate Models for Computational Material Science

speakers.name: Cosmin Safta
speakers.affiliation: Pieterjan Robbe, Reese Jones, Christophe Bonneville

speakers.name: Habib N Najm
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Laurent Capolungo, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a framework for uncertainty aware mixture-of-experts surrogate constitutive models with a focus on computational material science. This framework allows for the integration of multiple heterogeneous models, in a multifidelity framework. Each model addresses specific aspects of the material behavior and are combined in a multi-fidelity framework for a more accurate representation of the material response to different physical regimes. We endow this framework with uncertainty via Langevin sampling by introducing a stochastic differential equation with a trainable drift function corresponding to the log-posterior distribution of the surrogate model parameters. We will discuss computational expense tradeoffs between the dimensionality of the surrogate constitutive model and the associated posterior distribution model, and the number of samples generated by the Langevin process, and their impact on model predictions for practical computational material science exemplars.

talks.title: Towards the Construction of Inverse Triangular Transport Maps from Multifidelity Information
talks.abstract:
.
Transport maps provide deterministic couplings between probability distributions and are broadly useful in uncertainty quantification tasks. Triangular transport maps, distinguished by their eponymous triangular structure, possess several desirable numerical and theoretical properties. Map construction can be done using samples from the target distribution in a process called inverse transport, but the accuracy of these maps is often contingent on having many high-fidelity training samples, which can be costly to obtain. To address this, we build maps from multifidelity information, leveraging an ensemble of information sources of varying cost and accuracy. We propose two multifidelity inverse transport methods. The first is a hierarchical approach based on deep transport maps and inspired by [1]. We train maps to move between adjacent distributions within an assumed fidelity hierarchy. The second method trains a single map that incorporates all information sources simultaneously through multifidelity corrections to its parameterization. We compare both approaches across various problems, demonstrating their effectiveness in reducing high-fidelity data requirements in inverse transport.
SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525
[1] Benjamin Peherstorfer and Youssef Marzouk. “A transport-based multifidelity preconditioner for Markov chain Monte Carlo”. In: Advances in Computational Mathematics 45 (2019), pp. 2321–2348.
Authors
Owen N. Davis, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ondavis@sandia.gov
Gianluca Geraci, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ggeraci@sandia.gov

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Transport-Map Augmented MCMC
talks.abstract:
.
Model calibration in computational physics is often hindered by the prohibitive cost of high-fidelity simulations. Traditional approaches mitigate this by replacing expensive solvers with surrogate models, but this substitution can reduce robustness and incur bias. We propose a multi-fidelity Bayesian inference framework that fuses information from models of varying resolution by combining Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) with multilevel Monte Carlo (MLMC) estimators. The key innovation is a transport map-based coupling in a shared reference space, which induces strong correlations among posterior samples across fidelity levels, leading to accurate and low-variance estimators. This construction avoids the need for surrogate replacement, while ensuring that all models contribute coherently to the inference in a physically consistent manner.
We demonstrate our approach in turbulence model calibration, where Reynolds-averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) models require inference of uncertain closure coefficients to improve predictive reliability in nonlinear flow regimes. By leveraging a surrogate and a RANS solver, the framework delivers calibrated posteriors and predictive uncertainty bounds at substantially reduced computational cost compared to single-fidelity methods. These results demonstrate that multi-fidelity calibration achieves significant speedups over traditional Bayesian approaches, making large-scale uncertainty quantification in complex models more practical.

Joaquim Martins, University of Michigan, U.S., jrram@umih.edu
Alex Gorodetsky, University of Michigan, U.S., goroda@umich.edu

talks.title: Estimating Probability Distributions Using Multi-Fidelity Methods

speakers.name: Thomas Dixon
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Geoffrey Bomarito, NASA Langley Research Center, U.S.; Gianluca Geraci, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; James Warner, NASA Langley Research Center, U.S.; Joshua Pribe, Analytical Mechanics Associates, U.S.; Michael S. Eldred, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Alex Gorodetsky, University of Michigan, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Multi-fidelity sampling methods are efficient strategies to perform forward uncertainty quantification in a variety of applications.  These methods are based on the classical Monte Carlo estimator, but fuse predictions from two or more models of varying fidelity to obtain estimators with a greater precision given a prescribed computational budget.  Despite their success, most of these multi-fidelity methods are designed to estimate statistical moments, e.g., mean and variance, while few previous works have focused on estimating probability distributions or quantiles.  In this work, we extend the approximate control variates (ACV) approach to estimate cumulative density functions using multi-output estimation.  A novel interpolation method for smoothing out statistical noise is also introduced to further improve estimator accuracy.  We present results on the evaluation of distributions for additively manufactured Ni-base superalloys.

sessions.title: Surrogates for Uncertainty Quantification with Stochastic Simulators - Part I of II

talks.title: No-Replication Stochastic Emulation for Multi-Output Responses and Application to Seismic Risk Assessment

speakers.name: Sang-ri Yi
speakers.affiliation: Rice University, U.S.; Alexandros A. Taflanidis, University of Notre Dame, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Seismic risk assessment is a promising application area of surrogate modeling, providing computationally efficient approximations of complex structural dynamic behavior. A challenge, however, is the presence of aleatoric uncertainty in surrogate predictions, which arises when embedding high-dimensional stochasticity of earthquake ground motions into the low-dimensional input of the surrogate. The resulting indeterminacy of structural response prompts the application of stochastic emulation, which can predict the entire probability distributions of model responses. However, challenges exist in its application: (i) often the training points are selected from existing ground motion databases, limiting the acquisition of replicated samples and restricting training data size; (ii) structural outputs tend to exhibit significant correlations, requiring a new methodology to faithfully capture the joint statistics of multi-output predictions. In response to these gaps, we propose a Gaussian process (GP)-based multi-output stochastic emulation method capable of predicting the joint probability distribution of outputs, while not relying on sample replications. This is achieved by introducing auxiliary surrogates that predict the second-order statistics of the outputs, leveraging observations of sample deviation statistics. The proposed framework can accommodate Gaussian or non-Gaussian marginal predictions obtained using existing stochastic emulation methods without extra simulations.
Authors
Sang-ri Yi, Rice University, U.S., sangri.yi@rice.edu
Alexandros A. Taflanidis, University of Notre Dame, U.S., a.taflanidis@nd.edu

talks.title: Efficient Reliability Analysis of Stochastic Simulators via Active Learning of Heteroscedastic GP
talks.abstract:
.
Reliability analysis of computationally expensive stochastic simulators is challenged by the need to disentangle epistemic (modeling) uncertainty from aleatoric (intrinsic noise) uncertainty. Standard surrogate models often fail this task, especially under tight computational budgets where simulation replications are infeasible. We introduce an active learning framework, AL-NR-HGP, that efficiently addresses this problem. Our approach is founded on a non-replicated heteroscedastic Gaussian process (NR-HGP), which uses coupled GPs to simultaneously learn a system's mean response and its input-dependent noise from a single set of non-replicated data. The learning process is driven by a novel learning function derived from a first-order sensitivity analysis of the conditional failure probability. This function intelligently guides exploration by decomposing the posterior variance into its epistemic and aleatoric contributions, prioritizing points that most effectively reduce uncertainty about the limit-state boundary. For parallel execution, a weighted k-means batching strategy ensures the selection of diverse and informative points. On benchmark problems, AL-NR-HGP demonstrates superior accuracy and efficiency, providing more robust failure probability estimates than crude Monte Carlo and competing surrogate-based methods.
Authors
Han Peng, Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Hong Kong, hpengak@connect.ust.hk
Jize Zhang, Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Hong Kong, cejize@ust.hk
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Efficient Importance Sampling for Value of Information Analysis in the Presence of Rare Events
talks.abstract:
.
In decision-making situations characterized by uncertainty and risk, there often exist opportunities to gather additional information, contributing to better informed decisions. The so-called Value of Information (VoI) can be evaluated to enhance data acquisition, but its major drawback lies in its high computational complexity. VoI evaluation necessitates integration over potential observation outcomes for each candidate strategy, incorporating optimization over possible actions based on the expected costs for the action-measurement combinations. We focus on efficient estimation of the outermost integration over potential observation outcomes. Estimation of this integral with brute-force Monte Carlo sampling is inefficient in cases with several measurements having more than two possible outcomes. We propose a novel importance sampling scheme, which sequentially approximates the optimal importance sampling density using a Gaussian process model of the logarithmically-transformed failure probability. Thereby, the sequentially drawn training points and their corresponding failure probability estimates serve as both importance samples and updates to the surrogate model, with accurate estimation of low-probability outcomes being essential. Using engineering test cases, we demonstrate the method’s potential to accelerate VoI calculations, with the hope that their implementation can enable more frequent usage in practice.
Authors
Lea Friedli, Technische Universität München, Germany, lea.friedli@hotmail.com
Iason Papaioannou, Technische Universität München, Germany, iason.papaioannou@tum.de
Daniel Straub, Technische Universität München, Germany, straub@tum.de

talks.title: Deep Learning Frameworks for Metamodeling of Nonlinear Stochastic Dynamic Systems under Parametric and Predictive Uncertainty
talks.abstract:
.
Metamodeling of high-dimensional, nonlinear dynamic structural systems subject to natural hazards presents formidable challenges, especially when simultaneously accounting for uncertainties in the external dynamic loads and structural parameters. While uncertainties related to external dynamic loads from natural hazards have been successfully incorporated, few formulations have simultaneously considered loading and parameter uncertainties. To address this gap, three metamodeling frameworks are formulated in this work, each coupling a feature-extraction module—implemented through a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), a message-passing neural network (MPNN), or an autoencoder (AE)—with a long short-term memory (LSTM) network using Monte Carlo dropout and a negative log-likelihood loss. The resulting architectures (MLP-LSTM, MPNN-LSTM, and AE-LSTM) were validated on two case studies: a multi-degree-of-freedom Bouc–Wen system and a 37-story fiber-discretized nonlinear steel moment-resisting frame, both subjected to stochastic seismic excitation and structural parameter uncertainty. All three approaches achieved low errors: MLP–LSTM was most accurate for the lower-dimensional Bouc–Wen system, whereas MPNN–LSTM and AE–LSTM performed best on the more complex steel frame. In addition, predictive variance consistently tracked actual error, supporting active learning and confidence assessment in stochastic response trajectory prediction.
Authors
Haimiti Atila, University of Michigan, U.S., hatila@umich.edu
Seymour Spence, University of Michigan, U.S., smjs@umich.edu

sessions.title: JUQ Invited Papers - Part II of II

talks.title: Localised Surrogate Transition: Fast-mixing MCMC Without Gradients
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, I will revisit Multilevel Delayed Acceptance (MLDA) an efficient Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method for high-dimensional Bayesian inverse problems that does not require gradient information of the underlying posterior distribution. Instead, it uses a hierarchy of surrogates to accelerate convergence, e.g., coarser approximations of the likelihood. In previous works, we have demonstrated the fast convergence and the high effective sample size for MLDA, as well as multilevel variance reduction. In recent theoretical works, it has been shown that including gradient information allows to improve the upper bound on the total-variation mixing-time of MCMC from  for Random Walk Metropolis to  for MALA or  for HMC. Here,  is the  condition number of a -strongly convex and -smooth posterior distribution. We prove that for a suitable hierarchy of surrogates the same speedup as for MALA can be achieved by MLDA without explicit gradient evaluations. This requires two modifications of the surrogate densities in MLDA, akin to Tikhonov regularisation and tempering. Beyond these theoretical results, our numerical experiments indicate that successive states of the chain exhibit substantially reduced autocorrelation, even compared to the original MLDA.  Numerical evidence suggests that incorporating tempering into the surrogate densities also improves robustness with respect to multimodality of the target density.

sessions.title: Scientific Machine Learning Methods and Applications for Inverse Problems and Data Assimilation - Part III of III

talks.title: Harnessing Huge Ensembles for Data-Driven Extreme Weather Prediction

speakers.name: Thorsten Kurth
speakers.affiliation: NVIDIA, U.S.; Ankur Mahesh, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory

speakers.name: University of California Berkeley
speakers.affiliation: U.S.; Mauro Bisson, Marius Koch, Georg Ertl, Dallas Foster, Jean Kossaifi

speakers.name: Karthik Kashinath
speakers.affiliation: NVIDIA, U.S.; Anima Anandkumar, California Institute of Technology & NVIDIA, U.S.; William D. Collins, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, U.S.; Mike Pritchard

talks.abstract:
.
FourCastNet 3 (FCN3) advances global weather modeling by implementing a scalable, geometric machine learning (ML) approach to probabilistic ensemble forecasting. The approach is designed to respect spherical geometry and to accurately model the spatially correlated probabilistic nature of the problem, resulting in stable spectra and realistic dynamics across multiple scales. FCN3 delivers forecasting accuracy that surpasses leading conventional ensemble models and rivals the best diffusion-based methods, while producing forecasts 8 to 60 times faster than these approaches. In contrast to other ML approaches, FCN3 demonstrates excellent probabilistic calibration and retains realistic spectra, even at extended lead times of up to 60 days. All of these advances are realized using a purely convolutional neural network architecture tailored for spherical geometry. Scalable and efficient large-scale training on 1024 GPUs and more is enabled by a novel training paradigm for combined model- and data-parallelism, inspired by domain decomposition methods in classical numerical models. Additionally, FCN3 enables rapid inference on a single GPU, producing a 60-day global forecast at 0.25°, 6-hourly resolution in under 4 minutes. Its computational efficiency, medium-range probabilistic skill, spectral fidelity, and rollout stability at subseasonal timescales make it a strong candidate for improving meteorological forecasting and early warning systems through large ensemble predictions.
