talks.title: Can Transformers Discover Physical Laws from Data? Scaling General-Purpose Models from Molecular Dynamics to 3D Turbulence
talks.abstract:
.
Scientific machine learning often relies on rigid “physics-informed" architectures. However, as the broader deep learning community converges toward domain-agnostic Transformers, a fundamental question arises: must physical inductive biases always be engineered, or can they emerge by learning from large-scale data? We investigate this across two regimes: atomistic modeling and 3D turbulence.
First, we show unmodified Transformers achieve competitive accuracy in molecular prediction tasks without explicit physical priors. Analysis reveals that the models autonomously learn internal representations that align with physical structures like electrostatics. Second, we introduce EddyFormer, integrating spectral-element tokenization with a Transformer backbone for fluid dynamics. EddyFormer achieves a 30x acceleration over Direct Numerical Simulation at $256^3$ resolution. Applied to unseen domains 4x larger than in training, EddyFormer demonstrates domain generalization, preserving physics-invariant metrics such as energy spectra, correlation and structure functions. Together, these results suggest that general-purpose architectures can autonomously discover complex physical structure from data, pointing toward flexible and scalable approaches for multi-scale modeling. We conclude by discussing how this framework can extend to low-data regimes, serving as flexible data-driven priors where physical constraints are strategically applied.
Authors
Aditi Krishnapriyan, University of California, Berkeley, U.S., aditik1@berkeley.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Amortized Sequential Bayesian Inference and Experimental Design with Attention-Based Conditional Normalizing Flow

speakers.name: Lianghao Cao
speakers.affiliation: California Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Sequential Bayesian inference involves recursively updating the probability distribution of the uncertain parameter in a model based on a stream of noisy and sparse observations of the environment. It plays a central role in problems cast as partially-observed Markov decision processes, e.g., digital twins and sequential Bayesian experimental design. In this talk, I introduce a method that uses a single attention-based conditional normalizing flow for the amortized approximation of the sequence of posteriors conditioned on streaming action–data pairs (the observation history). This method has three key features. First, it can learn the evolution of parametric uncertainty by predicting a posterior given any observation history of arbitrary length, extrapolating well beyond the training horizon. Second, it is naturally permutation-invariant to the order of the observation history, while allowing the conditional structure of the likelihood to be encoded via positional embeddings. Third, it enables fast, stable, and explicit density evaluations once trained, suitable for downstream tasks such as estimating incremental expected information gains for sequential experimental design. I show promising numerical results on sequential inference problems such as signal-source localization finding, inference of patient-specific pharmacokinetics/dynamics, and inference of nonlinear viscoelasticity via controlled uniaxial tension.
Authors
Lianghao Cao, California Institute of Technology, U.S., lianghao@caltech.edu

sessions.title: Quantification and Prediction of Rare and Extreme Events - Part II of II

talks.title: Assimilative Causal Inference

speakers.name: Marios Andreou
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Erik Bollt, Clarkson University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Causal inference is fundamental across scientific disciplines, yet existing methods struggle to capture instantaneous, time-evolving causal relationships in complex, high-dimensional systems. In this paper, assimilative causal inference (ACI) is developed, which leverages Bayesian data assimilation to trace causes backward from observed effects. ACI solves the inverse problem rather than quantifying forward influence. It uniquely identifies dynamic causal interactions without requiring observations of candidate causes, accommodates short datasets, and scales efficiently to high dimensions. Crucially, it provides online tracking of causal roles, which may reverse intermittently, and facilitates a mathematically rigorous criterion for the causal influence range, revealing how far effects propagate. ACI opens new avenues for studying complex systems, where transient causal structures are critical.
Authors
Nan Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., chennan@math.wisc.edu

talks.title: Large Deviation Theory-Informed Importance Sampling for Rare Event Estimation and Control
talks.abstract:
.
Rare and extreme events like hurricanes, energy grid blackouts, dam breaks, earthquakes, and pandemics are infrequent but have severe consequences. Because estimating the probability of such events can inform strategies that mitigate their effects, scientists must develop methods to study the distribution tail of these occurrences. However, calculating small probabilities is hard, particularly when involving complex dynamics and high-dimensional random variables. In this talk, I will discuss our proposed method for the accurate estimation of rare event or failure probabilities for expensive-to-evaluate numerical models in high dimensions, and its application to rare event control. The proposed approach combines ideas from large deviation theory and adaptive importance sampling.  The importance sampler uses a cross-entropy method to find an optimal Gaussian biasing distribution, and reuses all samples made throughout the process for both, the target probability estimation and for updating the biasing distributions. Large deviation theory is used to find a good initial biasing distribution through the solution of an optimization problem. Additionally, it is used to identify a low-dimensional subspace that is most informative of the rare event probability. We compare the method with a state-of-the-art cross-entropy-based importance sampling scheme using examples including a tsunami problem.
Authors
Shanyin Tong, University of Pennsylvania, U.S., tong3@sas.upenn.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Data-Driven Dynamical Precursors of Extreme Events in High-Dimensional Chaotic Dynamics

speakers.name: Eirini Katsidoniotaki
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Extreme events in high-dimensional chaotic dynamical systems often arise from rare, transient instabilities that are difficult to anticipate from limited observations. As a result, purely statistical predictors trained on historical time series frequently fail to generalize in the tail regimes that dominate risk. We present a fully data-driven framework for long-lead prediction of intermittent extremes that constructs interpretable dynamical precursors by tracking internal transient instabilities of the system. Instability is quantified using finite-time Lyapunov exponent–like indicators evaluated in a low-dimensional, adaptively evolving subspace spanned by Optimal Time-Dependent modes, avoiding the prohibitive cost of full tangent-space computations. These instability-informed precursors are integrated into a Transformer-based forecaster to produce probabilistic long-horizon predictions of energy dissipation and threshold exceedances. Using two-dimensional Kolmogorov flow as a canonical model of intermittent turbulence, we demonstrate that dynamical precursors substantially improve extreme-event predictability and extend practical lead times relative to observable-based indicators, providing a scalable and uncertainty-aware approach for forecasting rare events in complex systems.
Authors
Eirini Katsidoniotaki, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., eirka289@mit.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Efficient and Scalable Algorithms for Uncertainty Quantification in Scientific Applications - Part II of II

talks.title: Physics-Informed Gaussian Process with Applications in ODE/PDE Parameter Estimation
talks.abstract:
.
Parameter estimation for nonlinear dynamic system models, represented by ordinary differential equations (ODEs) or partial differential equations (PDEs), using noisy and sparse experimental data is a vital task in many fields. We propose a fast and accurate method, physics-informed Gaussian process, for this task. Our method uses a Gaussian process model over system components, explicitly conditioned on the physics information that gradients of the Gaussian process must satisfy the ODE/PDE system. By doing so, we completely bypass the need for traditional numerical integration and achieve substantial savings in computational time. Our method is also suitable for inference with unobserved system components and provides uncertainty quantification. Our method is distinct from existing approaches as we provide a principled statistical construction under a Bayesian framework, which rigorously incorporates the ODE/PDE system through conditioning.
Authors
Shihao Yang, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., shihao.yang@isye.gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Uncertainty-Aware Ptychographic Reconstruction: A Robust, Cost-Aware Inference Framework
talks.abstract:
.
We build on normalizing-flow surrogates of the ptychographic posterior to deliver calibrated uncertainty in both magnitude and phase while capturing multi-modal solutions. Using an amplitude-based likelihood with TV regularization, flows match the reconstruction quality of advanced iterative solvers and expose where uncertainty concentrates (e.g., reduced scan overlap and phase–magnitude crosstalk). We further develop a robust version at substantially lower cost, making flow-based UQ practical for large ptychography workloads.
Authors
Zichao Di, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., wendydi@mcs.anl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Fast Transforms for Spectral Modeling of Gaussian Random Fields

speakers.name: Paul Beckman
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The specification of a covariance function is of paramount importance when employing Gaussian process models, but the requirement of positive definiteness severely limits those used in practice. Designing flexible stationary covariance functions is, however, straightforward in the spectral domain, where one needs only to supply a positive and symmetric spectral density. We introduce an adaptive integration framework for efficiently and accurately evaluating covariance functions and their derivatives at irregular locations directly from any continuous, integrable spectral density. We show that this framework can be made highly scalable by leveraging the nonuniform fast Fourier transform (NUFFT) in one dimension, and a novel nonuniform fast Hankel transform (NUFHT) in higher dimensions. As a result, we demonstrate several orders of magnitude speedup compared to naive uniform quadrature approaches, allowing us to evaluate covariance functions from slowly decaying, singular spectral densities at millions of locations to a user-specified tolerance in seconds on a laptop.
Authors
Paul Beckman, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., paul.beckman@austin.utexas.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Exact Gaussian Processes for Massive Datasets Via Non-Stationary Sparsity-Discovering Kernels
talks.abstract:
.
A Gaussian Process (GP) is a prominent mathematical framework for stochastic function approximation in science and engineering applications. Its success is primarily attributed to the GP's analytical tractability, robustness, and natural inclusion of uncertainty quantification. Unfortunately, the use of exact GPs is prohibitively expensive for large datasets due to their unfavorable numerical complexity of $O(N^3)$   in computation and $O(N^2)$ in storage, where $N$ is the size of the dataset. All existing methods addressing this issue utilize some form of approximation, which can lead to inaccuracies in function approximations and often limit the user's flexibility in designing expressive kernels. Instead of inducing sparsity via data-point geometry and structure, we propose to take advantage of naturally-occurring sparsity by allowing the kernel to discover—instead of induce—sparse structure. The premise of this work is that the data sets and physical processes modeled by GPs often exhibit natural or implicit sparsities, but commonly used kernels do not allow us to exploit such sparsity. The core concept of exact, and at the same time sparse GPs, relies on kernel definitions that provide enough flexibility to learn and encode not only non-zero but also zero covariances. This principle of ultra-flexible, compactly supported, and non-stationary kernels, combined with HPC, lets us scale exact GPs well beyond 5 million data points.
Authors
Marcus M. Noack, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, U.S., MarcusNoack@lbl.gov

sessions.title: Structure-Exploiting Techniques for UQ and ML - Part II of II

talks.title: Autoencoders for Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
In this work, we describe a new data-driven approach for inverse problems that exploits technologies from machine learning, in particular autoencoder network structures. We consider a paired autoencoder framework, where two autoencoders are used to efficiently represent the input and target spaces separately and optimal mappings are learned between latent spaces, thus enabling forward and inverse surrogate mappings. We focus on interpretations using Bayes risk and empirical Bayes risk minimization, and we provide various theoretical results and connections to existing works on low-rank matrix approximations. Similar to end-to-end approaches, our paired approach creates a surrogate model for forward propagation and regularized inversion. However, our approach outperforms existing approaches in scenarios where training data for unsupervised learning are readily available but training pairs for supervised learning are scarce. Furthermore, we show that cheaply computable evaluation metrics are available through this framework and can be used to predict whether the solution for a new sample should be predicted well.
Authors
Emma Hart, Emory University, U.S., ehart5@emory.edu

talks.title: A Latent-Consistent Variational Autoencoder for Diversifying Generation

speakers.name: Yeonjong Shin
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S.; Ichiro Takeuchi, Shotaro Tsutsui

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we present a structured variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture designed to preserve latent consistency, namely, the latent-consistent variational autoencoder (LCVAE). Latent consistency is considered a core property of generative models for better generation diversity. While existing works have attempted to enforce it through soft constraints (i.e., introducing a penalty term in loss functions), the proposed model, LCVAE, exactly satisfies latent consistency through hard constraints by construction. LCVAE generalizes a popular generative model, Glow, and utilizes the matrix pseudoinverse. Additional reparameterization techniques stabilize the training process. The effectiveness of LCVAE is demonstrated on the MNIST dataset with the task of generating images of the number 5 without including them in training.

talks.title: Training Data Distribution Optimization for Scientific Machine Learning
talks.abstract:
.
In scientific machine learning, models are often deployed with parameter values or boundary conditions that differ from those used in training. This talk presents the learning where to learn problem, which asks how to design a training data distribution that minimizes average prediction error across a family of deployment regimes. I will outline a theoretical analysis that explains how the training distribution influences deployment accuracy and motivates two adaptive algorithms based on bilevel optimization and alternating optimization in the space of probability measures. I will also describe discretized implementations that use either parametric distribution classes or nonparametric particle based gradient flows. Numerical results on learning functions and solution operators of partial differential equations show that the optimized training distributions outperform nonadaptive designs and improve robustness to distribution shift.
Authors
Nicolas Guerra, Cornell University, U.S., nmg64@cornell.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: An Occam's Razor Approach to Uncovering and Encoding Multivariable Structure in Kernel Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Emulating the input-output behavior of black-box models demands approximation methods that are both interpretable and expressive. Existing approaches involve tradeoffs: kernels and orthogonal bases offer interpretability but limited expressivity or scalability, while deep neural networks achieve expressiveness at the cost of interpretability. We aim to bridge this gap through total sensitivity kernels, a parameterized kernel family where each parameter is linked to the total sensitivity of a corresponding input. Automatic tuning of these parameters adapts the kernel to the multivariable structure of the target function, discovering which inputs matter and how they interact. Numerical results demonstrate that the method drives more efficient and accurate approximations while revealing interpretable structure.
Authors
John Darges, North Carolina State University, U.S., jdarges@emory.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Advances in MCMC Sampling Methods - Part I of III

talks.title: Mixing Time of the Proximal Sampler Via Strong Data Processing Inequality
talks.abstract:
.
Sampling is a fundamental algorithmic task in many applications. Sampling has intricate connections to optimization, for example via the perspective of sampling as optimization in the space of probability distributions. Many sampling algorithms are based on discretizing the Langevin dynamics, and they have mixing time guarantees which parallel the convergence guarantees from optimization. In this talk, we study the Proximal Sampler algorithm, which is an approximate proximal discretization of the Langevin dynamics. We show that the Proximal Sampler algorithm has mixing time guarantees that match the Langevin dynamics in continuous time, and parallel the convergence guarantees of the proximal gradient method from optimization. We highlight our proof technique which proceeds by establishing a strong data processing inequality for the algorithm in various statistical divergences.
Authors
Andre Wibisono, Yale University, U.S., andre.wibisono@yale.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Recent Progress in Sampling Via Diffusion Model, Fine Tuning and Stochastic Optimal Control

speakers.name: Molei Tao
speakers.affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
The algorithmic task of sampling from unnormalized density (or probability mass function if it is a discrete setup) has been studied for decades due to its importance. Diffusion model on the other hand is a much more recent technology that powers generative AI. Even more recently, stochastic optimal control started to provide an effective way for fine-tuning a generative model such as diffusion model. Can these contemporary technologies help sampling? This talk will briefly report several recent advances in this regard, including both learning-based and non-learning-based methods, and illustrate a pleasant possibility of alleviating metastability when sampling from multimodal distribution.
Authors
Molei Tao, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., mtao@gatech.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Optimal Experimental Design for Digital Twins - Part III of III

talks.title: Goal Oriented Optimal Design of Infinite-dimensional Bayesian Inverse Problems Using Quadratic Approximations

speakers.name: Bart G. Van Bloemen Waanders
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Alen Alexanderian

talks.abstract:
.
We consider goal-oriented optimal experimental design (OED) for Bayesian linear inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) with infinite-dimensional inversion parameters. In particular, we seek sensor placement strategies that minimize the posterior variance of a goal quantity of interest. The target quantity is assumed to be a nonlinear functional of the inversion parameters. We propose an OED approach that uses a quadratic approximation of the goal-functional to define our experimental design criterion which is obtained by integrating the quadratic approximation over the data. Under the assumption of Gaussian prior and noise models, we derive a closed-form expression for this criterion. Subsequently, we propose efficient and accurate computational methods and use a greedy approach to obtain our sensor placement strategy. We illustrate the proposed approach for two model inverse problems governed by PDEs. Our numerical results demonstrate the proposed approach outperforms non-goal-oriented (A-optimal) and linearization-based (c-optimal) approaches.
Authors

talks.title: Derivative-Informed Recurrent Neural Operator for Infinite-Dimensional Sequential Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Peng Chen
speakers.affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Jinwoo Go, Brookhaven National Laboratory, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present a computational framework for sequential Bayesian optimal experimental design (SBOED) for PDE-constrained systems with infinite-dimensional uncertainties. We introduce an adaptive terminal optimality criterion that enables adaptive global optimality, and an equivalent, computation-friendly reformulation using Laplace and low-rank posterior approximations. To accelerate SBOED, we develop the derivative-informed latent attention neural operator (LANO), which combines (i) derivative-guided dimensionality reduction, (ii) attention to capture latent dynamics, and (iii) efficient latent-space training with projected Jacobians. LANO provides fast, accurate surrogates for parameter-to-observable maps and their Jacobians, and supports reduced-space computation of MAP points, eigenpairs, and posterior sampling. In a tumor-growth application optimizing MRI acquisition times, LANO outperforms two alternative neural surrogates and matches FEM accuracy for MAP and eigenvalues, while delivering an amortized 180× speed-up.

talks.title: A Control-Oriented Approach to Optimal Sensor Placement

speakers.name: Arvind Saibaba
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S.; Bart G. Van Bloemen Waanders

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a control-oriented optimal experimental design (cOED) approach for linear PDE-constrained Bayesian inverse problems. In particular, we consider optimal control problems with uncertain parameters that need to be estimated by solving an inverse problem, which in turn requires measurement data. We consider the case where data is collected at a set of sensors. While classical Bayesian OED techniques provide experimental designs (sensor placements) that minimize the posterior uncertainty in the inversion parameter, these designs are not tailored to the demands of the optimal control problem. In the present control-oriented setting, we prioritize the designs that minimize the uncertainty in the state variable being controlled or the control objective. We propose a mathematical framework for uncertainty quantification and cOED for parameterized PDE-constrained optimal control problems with linear dependence to the control variable and the inversion parameter. We also present scalable computational methods for computing control-oriented sensor placements and for quantifying the uncertainty in the control objective. Additionally, we present illustrative numerical results in the context of a model problem motivated by heat transfer applications.

sessions.title: Bayesian Inverse Problems: Methods and Applications - Part II of III

talks.title: Learning Paths for Dynamic Measure Transport: A Regularity Control Perspective

speakers.name: Aimee Maurais
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Bamdad Hosseini, University of Washington, U.S.; Youssef M. Marzouk, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Sampling from a target probability distribution is a fundamental task in data assimilation and inverse problems, wherein the target distribution of interest is a Bayesian posterior. Many recent sampling approaches make use of dynamics, such as ordinary or stochastic differential equations, to transport samples from a tractable reference distribution (such as the prior) to the target distribution. This dynamic measure transport (DMT) approach induces a path of distributions, corresponding to the distributions of the intermediate states of the dynamics, between the reference and the target, and in some DMT approaches it is possible to specify this path explicitly. Understanding of how to choose paths well, however, is lacking, and some paths, including the geometric annealing path commonly employed in Bayesian applications, can produce undesirable “teleportation behavior.” In this talk we bring a control perspective to the problem, posing a flexible family of optimization problems for identifying paths of distributions for DMT and advocating for the use of objective terms which encourage smoothness of the corresponding velocity fields. We present a numerical algorithm for solving these problems based on recent Gaussian process methods for solution of partial differential equations and demonstrate the ability of our method to recover more efficient and smooth transport models compared to those which use the geometric annealing path.

talks.title: Amortized Inference and Dimensionality Reduction for Bayesian Inverse Problems on Function Spaces

speakers.name: Bamdad Hosseini
speakers.affiliation: University of Washington, U.S.; Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada

talks.abstract:
.
We present algorithms and analysis for Bayesian inverse problems on function spaces via prior-preserving truncations. Given a posterior distribution $d\nu = \exp(-\Phi(u)) d\mu$ on a Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$, we consider approximate distributions of the form $d\hat{\nu} = \exp(-\phi \circ \psi(u)) d\mu$, where $\psi : \mathcal{H} \mapsto \mathbb{R}^d$. We study its approximation properties of this class of measures and present efficient algorithms for computation.

talks.title: Bayesian Calibration and Optimization for Pulsed Power
talks.abstract:
.
Traditionally, there are two pillars of science: theory and experimentation. These two inform one another and lead scientists to make educated guesses and decisions toward advancing science. More recently, the driving force behind scientific advancement has not just focused on how much information can be learned, but how quickly. Additionally, experimental data can be costly and difficult to obtain. With these motivations in mind, the field of experimental design aims to maximize the information gained from as few experimental data points as possible. Computation, as the third pillar of science, has been used to facilitate optimal experimental design.
Sandia’s Z machine is the world’s most powerful and efficient laboratory radiation source. A principal uncertainty in Z experiments is how effectively current can be delivered. Power flow simulations are very intensive, making them infeasible to use in critical design and optimization studies. Developing a consistent picture of how losses develop and evolve would improve understanding of present-day experiments and better constrain circuit model representations, providing a basis for quantifying uncertainties in circuit models applied to Z and improve confidence in predictions of target performance. This presentation details the implementation of a Bayesian optimization study to maximize the information gained from Z experimental data and design.

talks.title: Optimal Sensor Placement for Gaussian Processes Using Column Subset Selection

speakers.name: Jessie Chen
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S.; Hangjie Ji, ; Arvind Saibaba, North Carolina State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian processes use data measured at a finite number of sensor locations to reconstruct a spatially dependent function with quantified uncertainty. However, since one can only deploy a finite number of sensors, it is important to determine how to optimally place the sensors to minimize a measure of the uncertainty in the reconstruction. We consider the Bayesian D-optimality criterion to determine the optimal sensor locations by choosing k sensors from a candidate set of m sensors. Since this is an NP-hard problem, our approach uses sensor placement as a column subset selection problem (CSSP) on the covariance matrix, computed using the kernel function on the candidate sensor points. We propose an algorithm that uses the Golub-Klema-Stewart framework (GKS) to select sensors and provide an analysis of lower bounds on the D-optimality of these sensor placements. To reduce the computational cost in the GKS step, we propose and analyze randomized Nyström approximations on the kernel matrix. We demonstrate the performance of our method on two applications: fiber coating dynamics and sea surface temperature.

sessions.title: Verification, Validation, and Uncertainty Quantification for Engineering Applications - Part I of III

talks.title: Scalable Given-Data Sobol’ Index Estimators for Very High-Dimensional Sensitivity Analysis in Neural Networks

speakers.name: Teresa Portone
speakers.affiliation: Bert J. Debusschere , Samantha Yang, Emiliano Islas-Quinones

talks.abstract:
.
Commonly used methods for Global Sensitivity Analysis (GSA), such as the pick-freeze Saltelli approach for computing Sobol' indices, require a number of samples that scales linearly with the number of parameter inputs, and a forward model that can evaluate samples at arbitrary locations.   In this work, we make use of given-data methods for GSA and extend them to scale up to problems with extremely high-dimensional input spaces.  Our extensions include a general definition of the given-data Sobol’ index estimator with arbitrary partition, a streaming algorithm to process input-output samples in batches, and a heuristic to filter out small indices that are indistinguishable from zero indices due to statistical sampling noise.  We apply our approach to compute the sensitivity of neural network outputs with respect to the neural network weights, and demonstrate scaling up to hundreds of thousands of inputs.

talks.title: Specifying Prior Distributions in Reliability Applications

speakers.name: Qinglong Tian
speakers.affiliation: University of Waterloo, Canada; Colin Lewis-Beck, Jarad Niemi

talks.abstract:
.
In reliability analysis, data are often sparse due to limited failure events or complex censoring mechanisms, making traditional inference challenging. Bayesian methods offer a principled framework for incorporating prior knowledge—such as physics-of-failure models or historical data—into the analysis, enabling more robust inference under data scarcity. This paper explores strategies for specifying prior distributions in reliability contexts, emphasizing both informative and default priors. It reviews existing approaches that yield desirable frequentist properties, including coverage accuracy, and introduces extensions tailored to the practical realities of reliability data. Through theoretical discussion and applied examples, the paper illustrates how carefully chosen priors can enhance inference quality, support decision-making, and address challenges posed by censoring and limited failure observations.

talks.title: Diffusion Models for Bayesian Inverse Problems in Low Temperature Plasmas
talks.abstract:
.
A common task across all fields of science and engineering is to predict the inputs of a model or simulation given partial observations of the model’s outputs. These "inverse problems" are notoriously difficult to solve.  Classical optimization methods are commonly used but only provide one answer and thus cannot quantify the uncertainty in that answer. Bayesian inference methods, such as Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), address this need by generating samples from the posterior distribution of the inputs, specified by a likelihood function. The generated samples can be analyzed to quantify uncertainty in model predictions. Such methods are robust and widely used, but are difficult to parallelize and have challenges scaling to high dimensions.
To this end, we propose and demonstrate the use of a diffusion model for solving inverse problems in the field of low temperature plasmas. These models have become extremely widely-used for generative modeling tasks, particularly image generation. They have also been applied more recently to text generation and the solution of inverse problems in science and engineering. Specifically, we apply a diffusion model to the task of learning unknown scalar input fields given partial observation of the results of a 1-D fluid simulation of a Hall thruster. We show that these methods provide a powerful, robust, and fast alternative to traditional Bayesian inference methods.
Authors
Thomas Marks, University of Michigan, U.S., marksta@umich.edu
Alex Gorodetsky, University of Michigan, U.S., goroda@umich.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Diffusion Non-Additive Model for Multi-Fidelity Simulations with Tunable Precision
talks.abstract:
.
Computer simulations are indispensable for analyzing complex systems, yet high-fidelity models often incur prohibitive computational costs. Multi-fidelity frameworks address this challenge by combining inexpensive low-fidelity simulations with costly high-fidelity simulations to improve both accuracy and efficiency. However, certain scientific problems demand even more accurate results than the highest-fidelity simulations available, particularly when a tuning parameter controlling simulation accuracy is available, but the exact solution corresponding to a zero-valued parameter remains out of reach. In this paper, we introduce the Diffusion Non-Additive (DNA) model, inspired by generative diffusion models, which captures nonlinear dependencies across fidelity levels using Gaussian process priors and extrapolates to the exact solution. The DNA model: (i) accommodates complex, non-additive relationships across fidelity levels; (ii) employs a nonseparable covariance kernel to model interactions between the tuning parameter and input variables, improving both predictive performance and physical interpretability; and (iii) provides closed-form expressions for the posterior predictive mean and variance, allowing efficient inference and  uncertainty quantification. The methodology is validated on a suite of numerical studies and real-world case studies. An R package implementing the proposed methodology is available to support practical applications.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Astrodynamics - Part II of III

talks.title: Uncertainty Propagation in Orbital Dynamics with Arbitrary Probability Distribution and Quantified Error Bounds
talks.abstract:
.
The Fokker–Planck partial differential equation (FP-PDE) governs the time evolution of uncertainty in stochastic dynamical systems. In orbital dynamics, solving the FP-PDE is challenging due to nonlinear motion, high-dimensional states, and large space–time domain. We present a physics-informed neural network (PINN) approach that approximates the FP-PDE solution—the probability density over continuous space–time $p(x,t)$. This approach is agnostic to coordinates and neural network designs. We outline relevant coordinate choices and introduce architectures that enforce probability-density structure, supporting efficient sampling or analytic marginalization. We analyze approximation error and derive upper bounds on average and worst-case deviations—total variation and $L_{\infty}$, respectively—in terms of the training loss. These certificates support tasks such as bounding conjunction probabilities and performing statistical orbit determination from ensembles of probability densities. Numerical studies on two-body dynamics show accurate propagation of non-Gaussian uncertainty, tight error bounds, and practical training. Although evaluated on the two-body problem, the framework generalizes to more complex regimes, including cislunar space, where orbital uncertainty propagation is critical. More broadly, it applies to well-posed FP-PDE problems and can benefit from advances in neural architectures (e.g., transformers) while retaining error certificates.
Authors
Chun-Wei Kong, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., chun-wei.kong@colorado.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Preliminary Phase Space Decomposition of the Three-Body Model for Long-Time Uncertainty Propagation
talks.abstract:
.
Long-time uncertainty propagation methods for unperturbed and weakly perturbed two-body models have been developed for the purpose of predicting the debris environment at near-Earth orbits over decade long time spans. These methods often leverage averaging techniques, ergodicity theory, and a dissipative factor due to the presence of the Earth's upper atmosphere. Equivalent long duration models for a three-body environment, such as the cislunar domain, do not yet exist.
In this talk we introduce a possible path forward to achieve analogous long-time uncertainty propagation in the three-body model using the fact that periodic orbits are dense to generate a finite covering and hence decomposition of the phase space. From this preliminary phase space decomposition we present initial investigations on uncertainty representation and transfer from localized neighborhoods of the model to globalization. Techniques from normal form theory and Markov processes are used in the definition of the decomposition and modeling external as well as truncation terms for uncertainty propagation.
Authors
Jannik Graebner, Princeton University, U.S., jg3607@princeton.edu
Jack Schwartz, Princeton University, U.S., js1765@princeton.edu

talks.title: Nonlinearity and Uncertainty Informed Gaussian Mixture Splitting for Astrodynamics Uncertainty Propagation

speakers.name: Keith LeGrand
speakers.affiliation: Purdue University, U.S.; Jackson Kulik, Utah State University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
As strong space domain awareness capabilities become increasingly important, accurate representations of uncertainty propagated through nonlinear astrodynamical systems are essential. Gaussian mixtures can characterize the resulting non-Gaussian uncertainty while retaining the computationally convenient properties of Gaussians. Adaptive Gaussian mixture approaches increase resolution by splitting to account for dynamical nonlinearities that can induce significant non-Gaussianity upon the uncertainty distribution. To obtain an accurate yet tractable representation of uncertainty, the splitting direction should be chosen to best approximate the true propagated density with a limited number of mixands. This talk discusses optimal Gaussian splitting using higher-order measures of nonlinearity that also account for the underlying distribution through input whitening, uncertainty scaling, and spherical averaging. These heuristics utilize higher-order tensor approximations of the nonlinear solution flow, which are additionally exploited opportunistically to obtain higher-order approximations of the propagated moments. A deferred splitting technique is presented that reduces the required number of numerical integration operations by leveraging the cocycle conditions of state transition tensors. These splitting heuristics are evaluated over three orbits and shown to accurately capture the transformed uncertainty distributions, with deferred splitting significantly improving runtimes.
Authors
Gerard A. Siciliano, Purdue University, U.S., gsicilia@purdue.edu

Jackson Kulik, Utah State University, U.S., jackson.kulik@usu.edu

talks.title: Regularized Wasserstein Proximal Algorithms for Nonsmooth Sampling Problems

speakers.name: Fuqun Han
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we introduce regularized Wasserstein proximal algorithms for nonsmooth sampling problems. Our approach is based on a splitting scheme for the time-implicit discretization of the probability flow ODE. In this framework, the score function—defined as the gradient of the log-density of the current distribution—is approximated via the regularized Wasserstein proximal. We establish convergence to the target distribution under suitable conditions and demonstrate the effectiveness of the method through numerical experiments on high-dimensional nonsmooth sampling tasks. We further highlight its application to generative modeling, including the design of a specialized class of transformers.
Authors
Fuqun Han, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S., fqhan@math.ucla.edu

talks.title: A Meta-Learning Perspective on Transformer-Based Language Models and Their Learning Dynamics
talks.abstract:
.
The Transformer architecture has become prominent in developing large causal language models. However, mechanisms to explain its capabilities are not well understood. Focused on the training process, here we establish a meta-learning view of the Transformer architecture when trained for the causal language modeling task, by explicating an inner optimization process within the Transformer. Further, within the inner optimization, we discover and theoretically analyze a special characteristic of the norms of learned token representations within Transformer-based causal language models. Our analysis is supported by experiments in various settings.

talks.title: Continuous-Depth Graph Neural Networks in the Infinite-Node Limit

speakers.name: Mingsong Yan
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Santa Barbara, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Continuous-depth graph neural networks, also known as Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs), extend Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with the continuous-depth architecture of Neural ODEs. What is the behavior of GNDEs as graph size grows? In this talk, we introduce Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) as the infinite-node limit of GNDEs and establish their well-posedness. We present trajectory-wise convergence of GNDEs to Graphon-NDEs as the number of nodes tends to infinity, and we provide explicit convergence rates for GNDEs on both deterministic and random graphs. These results offer theoretical justification for a practical and computationally efficient strategy: deploying GNDE models trained on smaller graphs to much larger ones. Numerical experiments further validate our theoretical findings.

talks.title: Data-Driven Enhanced Variational Modeling of Flocking Dynamics

speakers.name: Yiwei Wang
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Riverside, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Variational models play a central role in physics, materials science, biology, and data science. Such models are governed by a free energy functional and rate of energy dissipation. A key challenge in building variational models lies in determining these two quantities from data. In this talk, we present a PDE-free learning framework that separately identifies the free energy and the dissipation mobility directly from simulation data. The method is based on the minimizing movement scheme, which naturally encodes the energy–dissipation law in a discrete-time formulation. As a case study, we demonstrate the framework on several illustrative examples, including flocking dynamics.

sessions.title: Numerical Methods for PDEs under Uncertainty: Propagation, Inference, Optimal Control, and Optimization - Part III of III

talks.title: Uncertainty Reduction in Digital Twins Simulations
talks.abstract:
.
Creating reliable digital twins is often challenged by significant model uncertainty, including unknown governing equations and internal parameters, unobserved states, and even unspecified dimensionality. This lack of knowledge presents a fundamental barrier to traditional modeling and parameter inference techniques, making robust prediction a major challenge.     We introduce a Flow Map Learning (FML) framework that bypasses these challenges by constructing a predictive model directly from observational data. Our approach uses a deep neural network to learn the evolution operator, i.e., flow map, which maps a finite history of observed states to the system's future state. The core insight is that the observational history itself implicitly encodes the effects of the unknown parameters and unobserved dynamics. By conditioning on this history, the model can accurately determine the system's trajectory, thereby resolving the core challenge of forecasting under uncertainty.     The trained FML model provides a compact and efficient surrogate representation of the unknown system, enabling accurate long-horizon predictions of quantities of interest (QoI) while naturally accommodating various uncertainty sources. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through several numerical examples, showcasing its capability to produce reliable predictions and reduce uncertainty in digital twin applications.
Authors
Dongbin Xiu, Ohio State University, U.S., xiu.16@osu.edu

talks.title: Rockafellian Relaxation for PDE Constrained Optimization with Distributional Ambiguity.
talks.abstract:
.
Stochastic optimization problems are generally known to be ill-conditioned to the form of the underlying uncertainty. A framework is introduced for optimal control problems with partial differential equations as constraints that is robust to inaccuracies in the precise form of the  problem uncertainty. The framework is based on problem relaxation and involves optimizing a bi-variate, “Rockafellian" objective functional that features both a standard control variable and an  additional perturbation variable that handles the distributional ambiguity. In the presence of dis-  tributional corruption, the Rockafellian objective functionals are shown in the appropriate settings  to $\Gamma$-converge to uncorrupted objective functionals in the limit of vanishing corruption. Numerical examples illustrate the framework’s utility for outlier detection and removal and for variance reduction.
Authors
Harbir Antil, George Mason University, U.S., hantil@gmu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Novel Method for Uncertainty Quantification in Hyperbolic Problems Using Linear Programming
talks.abstract:
.
We present an alternative approach to treat random hyperbolic equations numerically. This approach does not suffer from the loss of hyperbolicity and it allows to incorporate existing methods, like stochastic collocation methods. The approach relies on the usage of Young measures that introduce an inherent linear structure on the problem. The latter is then discretized  and it is included in a classical high-order central Upwind scheme. Numerical results are presented.

talks.title: On Invariant-Domain Preserving Uncertainty Quantification for Conservation Laws

speakers.name: Jake J. Harmon
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Jerett Cherry, Colorado State University

talks.abstract:
.
Conservation or balance laws, partial differential equations for which the evolved state quantities are conserved, model physical phenomena spanning orders of magnitude in spatial and temporal scales. For these problems, numerical and physical complexity mandate intricate schemes to obtain satisfactory capability. Though challenging even in a deterministic setting, as a result of their practical significance, uncertainties permeate these studies: initial conditions, boundary conditions, material characteristics, and equations of state, among other properties and conditions, all contribute sources of uncertainty that preclude mere point estimates in robust, predictive computations. In this talk, we consider invariant-domain preserving uncertainty quantification based on a performant extension of recent advancements in stochastic active discretizations and stochastic finite volume methods for conservation laws. To demonstrate how these advancements enable enhanced propagation of uncertainty, we consider a series of benchmark examples based on Euler's equations.

talks.title: Unsupervised In-context Operator Learning on the Probability Measure Space
talks.abstract:
.
Many fundamental problems on probability measure spaces, such as optimal transport, mean field games, and Wasserstein gradient flows, are computationally demanding, while existing learning-based methods often rely on single instance solvers and require costly retraining for each new instance. In context learning with transformer models offers a new paradigm for approximating families of operators from a few context examples without task specific retraining. I will discuss our recent work on an unsupervised in context operator learning framework for learning solution operators on probability measure spaces. The method is discretization free, making it effective for high dimensional measure transport problems, and does not require supervised solution labels, substantially reducing the cost of data generation. If time permits, I will also discuss a generalization error analysis of the proposed transformer-based model, connecting it to emerging theory on in context learning and highlighting broader theoretical implications.

talks.title: Quantifying Epistemic Uncertainty in Diffusion Models
talks.abstract:
.
Diffusion models generate diverse samples by design, but that diversity is not the same as model uncertainty. In practice, many uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for diffusion models conflate aleatoric variability from the reverse diffusion noise with epistemic uncertainty arising from uncertain parameters. In this talk, we present a new methodology for estimating epistemic uncertainty in diffusion models (see https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.09170). Our approach is based on a Fisher–Laplace formulation that propagates parameter uncertainty through the reverse diffusion process. To make our approach scalable, we introduce FLARE (Fisher–Laplace Randomized Estimator), which approximates the Fisher information by uniformly subsampling parameters across all layers. Across synthetic time-series generation benchmarks designed to probe multimodality, mode switching, and low-density regions, FLARE produces more faithful epistemic uncertainty scores and enables more reliable filtering of low-confidence samples than BayesDiff and common Laplace-based baselines.
Authors
Adityi Gupta, University of California, Berkeley, U.S., agupta@icsi.berkeley.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Conditional Flow Matching for Data Assimilation in Chaotic and Turbulent Systems

speakers.name: Meet Parikh
speakers.affiliation: Cornell University, U.S.; Yaqin Chen, Notre Dame University, U.S.; Jianxun Wang, Cornell University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Flow-based generative models trained via flow matching are emerging as expressive priors for scientific inverse problems. This talk studies training-free conditional generation, guiding a pretrained flow model at inference time to assimilate sparse, noisy observations without any retraining. We develop a simple taxonomy and benchmark of guidance schemes that (i) modify the source term of the learned flow with likelihood or physics gradients (observation and PDE-residual guidance) and (ii) apply iterative corrections during integration (predictor–corrector, score fusion, and hybrid schedules). On canonical chaotic and turbulent testbeds, we evaluate accuracy and efficiency using RMSE, calibration, spectral recovery, energy-budget drift, and wall-time. Our analysis provides a comprehensive comparison of their accuracy and computational efficiency, offering practical insights for applying conditional generative models to challenging scientific inverse problems. The practical recipes (guidance strength and scheduling vs. noise level, sensor layout, and solver discretization) distilled from these analyses will let practitioners turn any pretrained flow-matching model into a plug-and-play data assimilation engine for complex spatiotemporal physics.
Authors
Meet Parikh, Cornell University, U.S., mhp66@cornell.edu
Yaqin Chen, Notre Dame University, U.S., ychen234@nd.edu
Jianxun Wang, Cornell University, U.S., jw2837@cornell.edu

talks.title: Temporal Generative Modeling for Weather and Climate Prediction

speakers.name: Salva Cachay
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Accurate and reliable weather and climate prediction is vital for societal planning, disaster mitigation, and understanding climate change. The increasing frequency of extreme weather events demands robust probabilistic forecasts that effectively quantify uncertainty. While generative models, particularly diffusion models, offer a promising path, methodological innovations are needed to overcome crucial limitations in their computational and data efficiency for these large-scale problems. In this talk, I will discuss methodological advancements that address these efficiency bottlenecks by explicitly leveraging the temporal structure of the data to design more efficient generative models for forecasting and emulation problems.
Authors
Salva Cachay, University of California, San Diego, U.S., salvaruehling@gmail.com

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Multifidelity Uncertainty Quantification - Part III of III

talks.title: Quantifying Resource Allocation Uncertainty for Multi-Fidelity Estimation
talks.abstract:
.
This talk is about estimating a parameter $\theta$ from models of different fidelities.  Each model has an evaluation cost.  Our goal is to allocate a budget to model evaluations in order to construct an unbiased estimator of $\theta$ having minimal variance.  Approximate control variates and the multilevel best linear unbiased estimator are frameworks that address this problem, but they assume the covariance between models is known.  In practice, the covariance must be estimated empirically.  We quantify and mitigate the effect of this estimation on the allocation of budget to model evaluations.
Authors
Aurya Javeed, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., asjavee@sandia.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Bi-Fidelity Diffusion Modeling for Rare-Event Probability Estimation
talks.abstract:
.
Estimating the probability of rare but critical system events is challenging when input dimensionality is high and high-fidelity simulations are costly. We present a bi-fidelity framework that directly models the low-dimensional quantity of interest (QoI) to compute probability of failure, without constructing a full input-to-output surrogate. Low-fidelity simulations provide a computationally inexpensive source of data for training a score-based diffusion model, which is augmented with sparse high-fidelity evaluations to accurately capture the QoI distribution. High-fidelity data are incorporated via an energy network that biases the diffusion model toward high-fidelity behavior. Energy-guided sampling efficiently explores rare-event regions, and tail-focused optimization ensures an accurate description of extreme events. This approach bypasses the curse of dimensionality inherent in traditional surrogates, efficiently capturing heavy tails and multi-modal features in high-dimensional systems, with applications in safety-critical engineering scenarios.
Authors
Audrey Gaymann, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., audrey.gaymann@colorado.edu
Alireza Doostan, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., Alireza.Doostan@Colorado.EDU

talks.title: Enhancing Supervised Dimension Reduction via Multi-Model Data Fusion

speakers.name: Owen N. Davis
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Daniele E. Schiavazzi, University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Andrea Zanoni, Scuola Normale Superiore, Pisa, Italy

talks.abstract:
.
Quantities of interest (QoIs) in large scientific and engineering applications are typically not independent, and can therefore be represented on a manifold, i.e. with fewer dimensions than in the original ambient space. In this context, strategies for linear dimensionality reduction (DR), like Principal Component Analysis (PCA), are commonly employed to identify linear transformations of the input parameters into a more parsimonious coordinate system, still accurately capturing most of the variability in the quantities of interest (QoIs). Alternatively, strategies for nonlinear DR (like diffusion maps and autoencoders) represent high-dimensional data on nonlinear low-dimensional manifolds. In a recent contribution [1], we introduced NeurAM, a data-driven paradigm where a nonlinear reduced-order manifold is discovered that preserves the input-to-output map. In this talk, we extend this idea by formulating a supervised loss in terms of a multi-fidelity surrogate model, as opposed to a single-fidelity surrogate as in NeurAM. On multiple test problems with varying complexity, we demonstrate that this extension leads to estimators of reduced variance, while also reducing the amount of required high-fidelity training data.
SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525
[1] Zanoni, et al., NeurAM: nonlinear dimensionality reduction for uncertainty quantification through neural active manifolds, arXiv:2408.03534, 2024.

talks.title: Multi-fidelity Ensembles for UQ in Monte Carlo Transport Applications

speakers.name: Aaron Olson
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Daniel M. Tartakovsky, Stanford University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty propagation is a key component of predictive science. Algorithms that scale efficiently for large dimensions have been proposed, however, dealing high-fidelity applications still remains challenging due to their computational cost, which limits the available number of realizations. For instance, Monte Carlo radiation transport solvers are notoriously expensive to converge to satisfactory statistical levels with limited number of particles.  Multi-fidelity strategies were introduced to lower the computational burden of uncertainty analysis: the idea is to combine limited high-fidelity realizations with a larger set of realizations obtained from lower fidelity models. Sampling methods exploit correlation among models to reduce the estimator variance. Unfortunately, limited studies exist on the effect that the stochasticity intrinsic in solvers or physical processes, e.g., random media, exercise on the correlation/cost trade-off among models.  Building on our recent modeling efforts in representing stochastic media for radiation transport applications, we explore the cost/correlation trade-off that models, their numerical approximations, and their implementations can provide to multi-fidelity studies in the context of radiation transport applications when stochastic media effects are important.
SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525. This work is supported by SNL LDRD Project 237058.
Authors

Aaron Olson, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., aolson@sandia.gov
Daniel M. Tartakovsky, Stanford University, U.S., tartakovsky@stanford.edu

sessions.title: Surrogates for Uncertainty Quantification with Stochastic Simulators - Part II of II

talks.title: Dimensionality Reduction-Based Stochastic Simulator for Seismic Risk Analysis of Bridge Structures

speakers.name: Jungho Kim
speakers.affiliation: Sejong University, Korea; Ziqi Wang, University of California, Berkeley, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Seismic risk analysis of bridge structures often requires high-dimensional stochastic simulations that couple structural models with ground motion uncertainties. However, the associated computational cost can be prohibitive, especially when quantifying low-probability responses or conducting parametric studies. This study introduces a dimensionality reduction-based stochastic simulator that efficiently captures the essential variability of high-dimensional input spaces while preserving the underlying stochastic structure of seismic excitations. The proposed framework combines dimensionality reduction techniques with a probabilistic sampling strategy to generate surrogate stochastic responses, thereby alleviating the need for repeated full-scale simulations. Application to a benchmark bridge model demonstrates that the proposed simulator can accurately reproduce key seismic risk metrics, including response distributions, tail characteristics, and sensitivity indices, with significantly reduced computational effort. These results highlight the potential of the proposed stochastic simulator as a practical tool for seismic risk assessment of complex bridge structures, enabling more efficient integration of structural reliability and resilience considerations in performance-based design.
Authors
Jungho Kim, Sejong University, Korea, junghokim@sejong.ac.kr
Ziqi Wang, University of California, Berkeley, U.S., ziqiwang@berkeley.edu

talks.title: Introducing Bayesian Inference for Stochastic Polynomial Chaos Expansions
talks.abstract:
.
Stochastic simulators arise in engineering and the sciences whenever a model produces different outputs for the same inputs, due to intrinsic randomness. Their analysis is computationally demanding, as their output is a random variable requiring characterization of its conditional distribution. Surrogate models have thus been developed to emulate such simulators efficiently. Among them, stochastic polynomial chaos expansions (SPCE) stand out by relying on minimal distributional assumptions. SPCE introduces a latent variable to capture intrinsic randomness and builds a polynomial chaos expansion in the joint input–latent space. However, its current training relies on multi-step sparse regression and cross-validation, which can be computationally expensive and prone to suboptimal models.  We propose a Bayesian framework for SPCE that overcomes these limitations. Sparsity-inducing priors on the expansion coefficients enable principled basis selection, while priors on the noise variance provide regularization and remove the need for cross-validation. Posterior inference, performed via sampling methods such as Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, further yields predictive uncertainty estimates, a feature particularly valuable for active learning.
Authors
Arthur Hatstatt, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, arthur.hatstatt@ibk.baug.ethz.ch
Maliki Moustapha, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, moustapha@ibk.baug.ethz.ch

Bruno Sudret, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, sudret@ibk.baug.ethz.ch

talks.title: Surrogate-Based Probabilistic Fatigue Assessment of Transmission Lines with Gaussian Process Regression: From Damage Estimation to Inspection Planning
talks.abstract:
.
Transmission line towers are repeatedly subjected to fluctuating wind loads that drive fatigue damage in bolted connections, threatening long-term reliability. This study develops a surrogate-based probabilistic framework to assess fatigue damage and guide inspection planning. High-fidelity finite element simulations are performed to capture joint-level responses under both preloaded and loosened bolt conditions, with fatigue quantified using rain-flow cycle counting and Miner’s rule. The resulting dataset is used to train Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) surrogates, which provide rapid and accurate predictions of fatigue accumulation across diverse wind speeds and directions. By coupling these surrogates with joint probability distributions of typical winds and a Poisson-based extreme-event model, the framework synthesizes annual damage and propagates uncertainty through sampling-based simulations. Results highlight fatigue-critical connections, reveal the relative roles of typical versus extreme winds, and quantify the influence of stress concentration factors and bolt preloading states on vulnerability. Building on these insights, inspection strategies are optimized by incorporating detection probabilities, repair resets, and cost–risk trade-offs. The proposed methodology delivers a computationally efficient, reliability-informed tool for fatigue risk assessment and inspection scheduling of transmission line systems.
Authors
Pooria Mazaheri, Iowa State University, U.S., mazaheri@iastate.edu
Alice Alipour, Iowa State University, U.S., alipour@iastate.edu

talks.title: Uncertainty-Aware Fragility Modeling of Urban Building Exteriors Subjected to Hurricane-Induced Windborne Debris with Conditional Generative Adversarial Nets

speakers.name: Gaofeng Jia
speakers.affiliation: Colorado State University, U.S.; Yue Dong, Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong; Yanlin Guo, Colorado State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Building exteriors are highly susceptible to windborne debris damage during hurricanes. Fragility functions that estimate the probability of exterior failure as a function of hurricane intensity are commonly used to assess this risk. However, developing the fragility function usually requires numerous expensive physics-based simulations. To address this challenge, this study proposes a new machine learning-based surrogate model that leverages conditional generative adversarial networks (cGAN) to model the distribution of the stochastic output, i.e., the maximum impact of windborne debris on the building exterior, conditioned on hurricane intensity. To ensure the accuracy and reliability of cGAN, a stochastic simulation-guided training strategy is proposed that treats the associated data preparation process as an augmented stochastic simulation problem. This approach enhances exploration of the input space and enables the formulation of a novel cGAN training metric tailored toward fragility modeling. The trained cGAN model can rapidly generate extensive damage realizations under various hurricane scenarios, which facilitates the construction of fragility functions. Further, Monte Carlo dropout is introduced to quantify the uncertainty of the fragility function established using cGAN. Such uncertainty provides end users with a measure of confidence in the derived fragility estimates. An illustrative example is presented to validate the efficacy of the proposed approach.
Authors
Ziluo Xiong, Colorado State University, U.S., ziluo.xiong@colostate.edu

Yanlin Guo, Colorado State University, U.S., yanlin.guo@colostate.edu

sessions.title: Data Driven Inference, Reduction, and Estimation using Dynamical Models - Part I of III

talks.title: A Labeled Random Finite Set Approach to Dynamical Model Identification

speakers.name: Andrei A. Klishin
speakers.affiliation: University of Hawaii at Manoa, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Traditional model and system identification techniques do not distinguish between uncertainty in parameters and uncertainty in model form. We aim to make use of the labeled random finite set framework (traditionally used for quantifying uncertainty about space objects) in order to decouple these two notions from each other. This allows us to model uncertainty about terms that might or might not exist but would otherwise have large parameters. We pose this as a new type of Bayesian parameter-and-model-form Bayesian estimation problem and extend canonical ensemble Kalman filtering techniques to this new approach.

talks.title: Data-Driven Representations of Model-Form Error in Oscillatory Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Many physical systems can be modeled as forced oscillators, such as the vertical motion of bridges or driving cars, or the strings of a musical instrument. In particular, we consider a critical oscillation in the field of space weather, i.e., fluctuations of the total electron content of the ionosphere under solar forcing. However, this system poses many modeling challenges, including (i) large observational errors; (ii) significant model form error (MFE) in reduced models; and (iii) the sheer scale of the computation. Therefore, we propose a data assimilation method that estimates the state and the MFE simultaneously. Given that there is large error in both observations and the model, we must take advantage of some known or assumed error structure in either or both of these sources. We demonstrate the method with the Duffing oscillator and show some preliminary results using real data from the upper atmosphere.
Authors
Noah Peterson, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., noah.peterson-2@colorado.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Lagrangian Conditional Gaussian Koopman Network for Data Assimilation and Prediction
talks.abstract:
.
Lagrangian data assimilation seeks to recover hidden Eulerian flow fields from sparse and indirect observations of moving tracers. This problem is fundamentally challenging because of the nonlinear coupling between tracer trajectories and the underlying flow, rendering posterior inference computationally intractable for realistic, high-dimensional systems. In this work, we develop a Lagrangian conditional Gaussian Koopman network (LaCGKN), a structure-preserving and data-driven framework for joint data assimilation and prediction from Lagrangian observations. LaCGKN embeds the Eulerian flow dynamics into a low-dimensional latent space governed by a nonlinear stochastic system with conditional Gaussian structures, enabling analytic posterior updates without ensemble forecasting. Different from existing conditional Gaussian Koopman formulations that rely on direct Eulerian observations, the Lagrangian setting introduces additional constraints on the latent representation, which must simultaneously encode the flow dynamics and mediate nonlinear tracer-flow interactions. To address these challenges, the LaCGKN incorporates three key architectural components: (i) tracer homogenization to enforce permutation equivariance and enable generalization across varying numbers of tracers; (ii) Fourier-based positional encoding to capture spatial dependence and reconstruct local flow features at moving tracer locations; and (iii) an SVD-inspired low-rank parameterization of the latent transition operator, which reduces parameter complexity while preserving expressive capacity. An application to a two-layer quasi-geostrophic flow with surface tracer observations demonstrates that LaCGKN achieves accurate and efficient Lagrangian data assimilation and prediction, without reliance on ensemble methods or the governing physical model. These results establish the LaCGKN as a unified and computationally tractable alternative to both traditional model-based approaches and purely black-box data-driven methods.
Authors
Zhongrui Wang, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., wang3262@wisc.edu
Nan Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., chennan@math.wisc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Simulation and Data Assimilation in An Idealized Coupled Atmosphere-Ocean-Sea Ice Floe Model with Cloud Effects
talks.abstract:
.
Sea ice plays a crucial role in the climate system, particularly in the Marginal Ice Zone (MIZ), a transitional area consisting of fragmented ice between the open ocean and consolidated pack ice. As the MIZ expands, understanding its dynamics becomes essential for predicting climate change impacts. However, the role of clouds in these processes has been largely overlooked. This paper addresses that gap by developing an idealized coupled atmosphere-ocean-ice model incorporating cloud and precipitation effects, tackling both forward (simulation) and inverse (data assimilation) problems. Sea ice dynamics are modeled using the discrete element method, which simulates floes driven by atmospheric and oceanic forces. The ocean is represented by a two-layer quasi-geostrophic (QG) model, capturing mesoscale eddies and ice-ocean drag. The atmosphere is modeled using a two-layer saturated precipitating QG system, accounting for variable evaporation over sea surfaces and ice. Cloud cover affects radiation, influencing ice melting. The idealized coupled modeling framework allows us to study the interactions between atmosphere, ocean, and sea ice floes. Specifically, it focuses on how clouds and precipitation affect energy balance, melting, and freezing processes. It also serves as a testbed for data assimilation, which allows the recovery of unobserved floe trajectories and ocean fields in cloud-induced uncertainties.
Authors
Changhong Mou, Utah State University, U.S., changhong.mou@usu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Assessing and Quantifying Model-form Uncertainty and Model-form Error - Part I of III

talks.title: A Tractable Nonparametric Probabilistic Framework for Model-Form Uncertainty in Turbulent CFD
talks.abstract:
.
We present a computationally tractable approach for modeling and quantifying model-form uncertainty in viscous CFD. The method distinguishes between two sources: turbulence-modeling uncertainty and all other sources (e.g., boundary conditions). Accordingly, it combines two complementary strategies. The first targets turbulence modeling by decomposing the Reynolds stress tensor into deviatoric and spherical parts; constructing a hyperparameterized probabilistic model of the eigenvalues of the deviatoric component based on its spectral properties; and extending this to a complete probabilistic representation of the Reynolds stress. Each realization corresponds to an admissible turbulence model within a family. The second strategy adapts a recently developed nonparametric probabilistic method to this context. Grounded in stochastic, projection-based model order reduction (PMOR), it ensures computational tractability while capturing additional model-form and parametric uncertainties. Hyperparameters of both components are identified by minimizing a data-driven stochastic loss function, with the framework also accounting for the uncertainty introduced by PMOR itself to enable efficient Monte Carlo simulation. The approach is demonstrated on RANS-based aerodynamic analysis of the NASA Common Research Model in transonic flow, where wind tunnel data is available, highlighting its potential for large-scale CFD uncertainty quantification with turbulence modeling.
Authors
Charbel Farhat, Stanford University, U.S., cfarhat@stanford.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Embedded Model Error Using Orthogonal Gaussian Processes

speakers.name: Mridula Kuppa
speakers.affiliation: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; Khachik Sargsyan, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Marco Panesi, University of California, Irvine, U.S.; Habib N. Najm, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this work, we extend the embedded model error framework to incorporate flexible model error representations that distinguish uncertainy due to model error from that resulting from measurement noise. In an inverse modeling context, we calibrate a simplified model using data from a more detailed predictive model treated as the truth in the presence of measurement noise. Specifically, we embed a Gaussian process (GP) in the model-to-be-calibrated, enabling flexible model corrections. For tractability, we adopt the weight-space GP perspective, and we examine its equivalence to the function-space view in both interpolative and extrapolative scenarios through numerical experiments.     To separate the predictions of the model from those of the embedded GP, we extend the orthogonal GP method, originally developed for external GPs, to the embedded setting. Specifically, we propose two strategies: (i) linearizing the embedded GP model, and (ii) introducing regularization in the GP prior to enforce orthogonality between the GP and the model gradients in the parameter space. Tests on synthetic linear and nonlinear examples demonstrate that the framework provides flexible corrections, with orthogonality constraints effectively decorrelating model parameters and GP weights, thereby enabling efficient posterior sampling of the model and its correction.
Authors

talks.title: Analytic Solutions to Nonlinear Odes Via Spectral Power Series and Why Highly Reduced Models Can Work
talks.abstract:
.
A typical question in the field of MFE is: how can we improve a reduced set of ODEs or PDEs to better approximate a high-fidelity one? But we could also ask: how come these reduced models work as well as they do in the first place? In this talk, I'll present analytic solutions to nonlinear ODEs to explain why highly reduced models may work well, even for extrapolative scenarios. These solutions are found through a new method called spectral power series (SPS), which relies on eigenvalues of the linearized system around equilibrium. The exact coefficients of the series are found with linear algebra and some combinatorics. Finally, to ground SPS with existing dynamical systems theory, I'll show SPS as an explicit construction of the Koopman expansion and the Hartman-Grobman transformation.
Authors
Rebecca Morrison, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., rebeccam@colorado.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Stochastic and Non-Local Closure Modeling for Nonlinear Dynamical Systems Via Latent Generative AI Methods
talks.abstract:
.
Closure models are widely used in simulating complex multiscale dynamical systems such as turbulence and Earth’s climate, for which direct numerical simulation that resolves all scales is often too expensive. For those systems without a clear scale separation, deterministic and local closure models often lack enough generalization capability, which limits their performance in many real-world applications. In this talk, we present a data-driven modeling framework for constructing stochastic and nonlocal closure models via latent conditional diffusion model and neural operator. We also compare the implicit regularization of the latent space offered by a joint training scheme against two explicit approaches that incorporate the latent space regularization, via metric-preserving (MP) or geometry-aware (GA) constraints. The results show that the proposed methodology provides a systematic approach via latent generative AI methods to constructing efficient and generalizable data-driven stochastic closure models for multiscale dynamical systems with continuous spatiotemporal fields.
Authors
