sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Propagation in Transport Dominated PDEs - Part I of II

talks.title: A General Framework for Convergence of Statistical Solutions and Applications
talks.abstract:
.
A trajectory statistical solution of a given evolution equation is a probability measure on the space of trajectories whose support is contained in the corresponding set of solutions, in addition to obeying a suitable regularity condition. I will present a general result yielding a set of verifiable assumptions that yield convergence of statistical solutions of a given parametrized family of evolution equations towards a statistical solution of a limiting system. As illustration, I will apply this result to the inviscid limit of the Navier-Stokes equations towards the Euler equations, and to Galerkin approximations of the Navier-Stokes equations.
Authors
Cecilia Mondaini, Drexel University, U.S., cf823@drexel.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Structure-Aware Warm-Starts for Uncertainty Propagation in Transport-Dominated PDEs

speakers.name: Daniel M. Tartakovsky
speakers.affiliation: Stanford University, U.S.; Hongli Zhao , University of Chicago, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Transport-dominated phenomena pose difficulties for physics-informed machine learning (PIML), because training of PIML models can become unstable or inaccurate due to stiffness in the optimization landscape, spectral bias toward smooth solutions, and strong sensitivity to initialization and loss balancing. These challenges impact uncertainty quantification (UQ) as it demands high-fidelity forward solves across a high-dimensional uncertainty space, while surrogate models can be brittle; especially as advection speed increases and the problem becomes more ill-conditioned for gradient-based training. We present a structure-aware warm-start framework for efficient UQ in transport-dominated PDEs based on numerical continuation and transfer learning to stabilize and accelerate training of surrogate models. The core idea is to construct a sequence of regularized problems (e.g., reduced advection strength, increased regularization, coarser discretization) and to warm start each stage using the solution from the previous stage. This continuation path is combined with transfer across related geometries or dimensions (e.g., from 1D to 2D advection) to reduce the marginal cost of additional samples and enable rapid exploration of parameter regimes. We demonstrate that the proposed warm-start strategy improves robustness and reduces training time for uncertainty propagation compared with naive random-start baselines, while maintaining accuracy in solution profiles. The framework is compatible with Monte Carlo and collocation-style sampling, and it provides a practical route to scalable UQ when high-fidelity solves of transport-dominated PDEs are the computational bottleneck.
Authors
Daniel M. Tartakovsky, Stanford University, U.S., tartakovsky@stanford.edu
Hongli Zhao, University of Chicago, U.S., honglizhaobob@uchicago.edu

sessions.title: Recent Developments in Statistical Methods for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of III

talks.title: Validation of UQ Methods for AI Surrogate Models

speakers.name: Boya Zhang
speakers.affiliation: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S.; Amanda Muyskens, Real Good AI, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
While it is essential to characterize the trustability of AI models, uncertainty quantification remains an open question for this field.  Even for the narrow scope of AI surrogates with numeric inputs and outputs, there are many different approaches to quantifying uncertainty, but these methods do not agree.  In this talk, we discuss our efforts to evaluate the performance of these differing UQ methods.           LLNL-ABS-2011553. talks.title: Quantifying Uncertainty in Grid Topology Mappings: A Distribution Free Risk Control Approach

speakers.name: Jayanth Jagalur
speakers.affiliation: Jaffe Luke, Juliette Franzman, Christina Mao, Mason Blake

talks.abstract:
.
Accurate recovery of grid topology from overhead geo-spatial imagery underpins simulation, reliability, and resilience analysis of the electricity grid infrastructure. Errors in computer-vision outputs – missed towers, spurious detections – propagate to downstream tasks and can misinform planning decisions. We develop a distribution-free risk-control framework for uncertainty quantification of grid-topology extraction that allows us to separate existence from geometric quality of model predictions. Specifically we define existence-centric risks (recall shortfall and false-discovery proportion) for detection that go beyond IoU based metrics, and use Conformal Risk Control (CRC) and Learn-then-Test (LTT) frameworks to provide explicit, finite-sample statistical guarantees. Our procedure is detector-agnostic, yields interpretable per-detection probabilities for downstream tasks,  and can be merged into existing pipelines. It directly supports risk-aware decisions in resilience, reliability, and investment planning for power-grid infrastructure. This work was performed under the auspices of the U.S. Department of Energy by Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory under Contract DE-AC52-07NA27344. Document release number: LLNL-ABS-2011544.

talks.title: Deep Gaussian Process Regression for Shock Modeling
talks.abstract:
.
Accurate modeling of shock phenomena is critical in the field of materials science, where predicting responses under extreme conditions is essential. Statistical emulators of high fidelity simulations make it possible to perform the large number of evaluations necessary for uncertainty quantification. However, traditional Gaussian process (GP) approaches often struggle with the inherent nonstationarity of shock data and other ML methods lack probabilistic interpretability. Instead, Deep Gaussian Process Regression (DGPR) is a powerful, flexible framework for emulation that extends standard Gaussian processes to accommodate complex response behavior by stacking multiple layers. We use a DGP to emulate a shock response and calibrate to real-world shock data, comparing performance to a standard GP.
Authors
Laura Wendelberger, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S., wendelberger1@llnl.gov

talks.title: Scalable Transport Maps to Enable Large-Scale Uncertainty Quantification for NASA Remote Sensing Missions

speakers.name: Jonathan Hobbs
speakers.affiliation: NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S.; Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada; Gregory Halverson, Kerry Cawse-Nicholson

speakers.name: Otto Lamminpää
speakers.affiliation: NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S.; Meredith Franklin, University of Toronto, Canada

talks.abstract:
.
Earth-observing satellite missions are capturing information at increasingly fine spatial and temporal scales, providing great potential for science, applications, and informed decision making. However, the corresponding massive data volumes place increasing demands on computational resources and limit feasible implementation of standard uncertainty quantification (UQ) practices, e.g. Monte Carlo. Additionally, the retrieval of high priority geophysical variables, like evapotranspiration (ET), relies on algorithms that ingest variables from multiple sources with varying fidelity of UQ. These inputs are typically spatial fields with uncertainties that exhibit complex spatial structure that must be propagated to ensure reliable decision-making from field-level to global scales. In this talk, we present a novel implementation of transport maps to enable computationally feasible, forward UQ for ET products from NASA’s ECOSTRESS mission. We propose a hierarchical transport map that is flexible to capture arbitrary input uncertainty distributions varying across space and time. The resulting map amortizes the cost of performing UQ across a range of geophysical conditions (e.g. multiple ecosystems), thereby enabling scalable UQ for operational satellite data pipelines. We demonstrate the methodology over agricultural regions with complex space-time dynamics, and we discuss ongoing work to extend the methods to capture spatially correlated uncertainty information.

sessions.title: Advances in MCMC Sampling Methods - Part II of III

talks.title: Preconditioning for Mcmc: Concepts, Practices, and Challenges
talks.abstract:
.
Motivated by the improvement of sampling algorithms, the constant creation of preconditioners leaves a slew of new techniques in the wake of each passing year [Titsias 2023, Cui et al. 2024, Cai et al. 2024]. Despite this, the notion of preconditioning in the minds of practitioners and theoreticians is vague and incomplete. Each new preconditioner comes with its own justification, particular to each individual problem setting.  	In the talk I will formalise the notion of preconditioning, unifying existing and future preconditioners under a single conceptual framework. I will then lead the audience through a catalogue of potential hurdles to the good operation of a sampler. In each case I will explain why the hurdle obstructs the sampling operation and I will introduce and test the preconditioners that have been posed to surmount them. I’ll provide either theory or empirical examples to verify claims as to the malignance of the obstacle and the effectiveness and behaviour of the preconditioner. Then I will introduce a taxonomy for preconditioners: those that modify the target distribution, and those that modify the algorithm. I will describe the natural capabilities of the preconditioners that fall into these classes. Lastly I will introduce an important concept informing the construction of preconditioners, which we call `Gaussian Equivalence’.
Joint work with Sam Power
Authors
Max Hird, University of Waterloo, Canada, max@hirds.co.uk

talks.title: Diffusion Annealed Langevin Monte Carlo for Generative Modelling and Sampling

speakers.name: Deniz Akyildiz
speakers.affiliation: Imperial College London, United Kingdom; Sebastian Reich, Universität Potsdam, Germany; Andrew Duncan, Imperial College London, United Kingdom

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we study the theoretical properties of general diffusion paths and their Langevin Monte Carlo implementation, termed diffusion annealed Langevin Monte Carlo (DALMC), under weak assumptions on the data distribution. We derive non-asymptotic error bounds for annealed Langevin dynamics where the path of distributions is given by Gaussian convolutions of the data, as in diffusion models. We then extend the analysis to heavy-tailed (Student’s t) diffusion paths, establishing—for the first time—their theoretical validity for heavy-tailed data. These results provide rigorous guarantees for a class of score-based generative models that interpolate between a simple distribution (Gaussian or Student’s t) and the data in finite time, offering a broader perspective than standard diffusion approaches based on a forward Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process.
Building on this framework, we introduce new methods for efficient sampling from complex, unnormalised targets via multiscale dynamics. By exploiting stochastic averaging in a slow–fast system of stochastic differential equations, we estimate intermediate scores along the diffusion path without training or inner-loop MCMC. We present two algorithms: MultALMC, based on multiscale annealed Langevin dynamics, and MultCDiff, which uses multiscale controlled diffusions for the reverse-time OU process. Both overdamped and underdamped variants are developed, with theoretical convergence guarantees to the target diffusion path.

talks.title: Optimizing Irreversible Perturbations of the Unadjusted Langevin Algorithm

speakers.name: Youssef M. Marzouk
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Konstantinos Spiliopoulos, Boston University, U.S.; Benjamin Zhang, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we investigate irreversible perturbations of overdamped Langevin dynamics as a strategy to accelerate sampling. These perturbations add skew-symmetric drift terms that preserve the target distribution while inducing rotational flows, thereby improving exploration. While their advantages are well understood in continuous time, the role of discretization and the design of optimal perturbations for discrete-time algorithms remain largely unexplored. Focusing on the Unadjusted Langevin Algorithm (ULA), we develop a framework for optimizing constant irreversible perturbations that balances two competing objectives: maximizing the spectral gap of the continuous dynamics to speed convergence, and minimizing discretization error that induces bias. We introduce new criteria that jointly quantify bias and efficiency, and show how they identify perturbations that improve sampling performance beyond existing constructions.

sessions.title: Scientific Machine Learning Using Software-informed Priors - Part I of II

talks.title: Shock-Capturing Data Assimilation Via Hierarchical Bayes, EnKF, and Discontinuous Galerkin
talks.abstract:
.
Data assimilation methods are currently used to tackle many practical, applied problems throughout computational science. At their heart, they gauge the state of a targeted dynamical system, and many workhorse methods (EnKF, ETKF, etc.) use ensembles to represent the uncertainty over this target state. We approach these methods with a particular focus toward hyperbolic conservation laws, giving piecewise-smooth states with possibly large discontinuities. While many works focus on representing the uncertainty over the dynamical system state more rigorously by using nonlinear transformations, they are often agnostic of the knowledge over the dynamical system they quantify uncertainty for. Contrasting with the (localized) ensemble Kalman filter, we demonstrate the ability to ensure that each
sample
drawn from our data assimilation method is a realistic realization of the underlying partial differential equations (PDEs), and further extend our method to take advantage of underlying structure in discontinuous Galerkin formulations of the PDEs.
Authors
Daniel Sharp, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., dannys4@mit.edu
Jan Glaubitz, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., Glaubitz@mit.edu
Mathieu Le Provost, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., n/a

talks.title: Hierarchical Prior-Informed Dictionary Learning Methods
talks.abstract:
.
Dictionary matching methods seek to match a given data vector to measured or synthetically generated dictionary templates that can be interpreted to constitute software-informed priors. The problem can be recast as a sparse coding problem. To facilitate the process, it is often useful to parcel the dictionary into subdictionaries using some coherence criterion, and then identify the relevant subdictionaries leading to a deflated coding problem. In this talk, we review Bayesian methods that improve the identifiability of the relevant subdictionaries and lead to improved performance and interpretability, and lower computational cost than solving the original matching problem. The methodology is demonstrated with applications from different areas, e.g., brain imaging.
Authors
Erkki Somersalo, Case Western Reserve University, U.S., ejs49@case.edu

talks.title: Using Sciml to Learn a Prior from Population Data
talks.abstract:
.
Stochastic inverse problems have been attracting increasing attention in recent years due to advances in data acquisition techniques which enable utilizing tremendous amounts of data to construct data-informed physics-based computational models. In particular, a recently developed approach, called data-consistent inversion (DCI), solves a specific class of stochastic inverse problems where one seeks an aleatoric characterization of model inputs over a population of assets/individuals.  Moreover, this approach has been recently utilized to construct population-informed priors to enhance the information gained in performing asset-specific Bayesian inference for digital twins. At the same time, scientific machine learning methods have also become much more prevalent in computational science, largely due to their ability to learn and exploit low-dimensional structure in high-dimensional data.  The subject of utilizing approximate models/maps in the context of DCI has been studied in extensive detail.  In this presentation, we will describe some of our recent work on using data-driven (SciML) surrogate models to expedite the solution to both Bayesian and data-consistent inverse problems.
Authors
Tim Wildey, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., tmwilde@sandia.gov

talks.title: Extreme Scale Bayesian Inference and Optimal Experimental Design for Wave Propagation-based Source Inversion

speakers.name: Stefan Henneking
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Alice Gabriel, University of California, San Diego, U.S.; Omar Ghattas , University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We consider Bayesian source inversion problems and associated optimal experimental design governed by wave propagation in very high dimensional parameter space. This class of problems is fundamentally challenging since it does not lend itself to low dimensional approximation. We achieve a real-time Bayesian inversion capability by exploiting the time-shift invariance of the wave propagation problem and resulting parameter-to-observable map, an offline–online decomposition with a modest number of offline (adjoint) wave propagation solutions along with a wave propagation-free online component, and novel parallel algorithms for the online component that map well onto GPU clusters. Capitalizing on this capability, we design novel greedy algorithms for solving sensor selection OED problems with the goal of maximizing expected information gain for both the parameter field as well as posterior predictive quantities of interest. The real-time Bayesian inversion and OED framework is demonstrated for acoustic–gravity wave propagation-based tsunami early warning in several high-risk subduction zones settings. Bayesian inverse problems with up to 1 billion inversion parameters representing spatiotemporal seafloor motion are solved in less than a second on 512 GPUs, and OED sensor selection problems for tsunami early warning with hundreds of acoustic pressure sensors are solved to near optimality in a few hours.

sessions.title: Bayesian Inverse Problems: Methods and Applications- Part III of III

talks.title: Probabilistic Imaging Using Diffusion Image Priors
talks.abstract:
.
Probabilistic imaging frames reconstruction as posterior inference, leveraging a physical forward model (likelihood) and an expressive image prior for both high-quality reconstructions and uncertainty quantification. In this talk, we extend diffusion image priors beyond the standard “known-operator” setting to blind inverse problems, where key parts of the measurement model are unknown and must be inferred jointly with the image. We present PRISM, a diffusion-based Bayesian framework that places diffusion priors on the image and on the unknown operator parameters, and performs posterior sampling via a principled alternating (Gibbs-style) scheme. A central ingredient is a measurement-conditioned diffusion model for the operator, which injects observation information to improve robustness under poor initialization and model mismatch. We demonstrate PRISM on blind motion deblurring, showing improved recovery and credible uncertainty estimates over recent diffusion baselines.
Authors
Yu Sun, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., ysun214@jh.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Efficient Simulation of High-Resolution Turbulent Systems Using Diffusion Models
talks.abstract:
.
Simulating large and high-resolution turbulent systems can be computationally prohibitive. While modern machine learning models such as diffusion models offer promising generative capabilities, generating high-resolution turbulent fields remains challenging due to GPU memory limitations arising from the large input size. In this work, we address this challenge by proposing a hierarchical strategy based on diffusion models. We first train a low-resolution model to generate the global dynamics of turbulence. In parallel, we train patch-wise diffusion models to predict high-resolution local patches, conditioned on previous high-resolution patches and the current low-resolution global field. These patches are then combined using a partition of unity to produce a seamless high-resolution solution. In our framework, each model only processes a small portion of the domain—either the low-resolution global field or localized high-resolution patches—significantly reducing memory requirements compared to end-to-end training at full resolution. Moreover, the method is naturally parallelizable by design. Numerical experiments demonstrate the performance and efficiency of the proposed approach.
Authors
Zezhong Zhang, Auburn University, U.S., zez0002@auburn.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Priorconditioned Sparsity-Promoting Projection Methods for Deterministic and Bayesian Linear Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Rapidly-growing fields like data science and machine learning require fast, accurate methods for inverse problems. Key challenges include ill-posedness, high dimensionality, and complex model constraints. Sparsity-promoting techniques for deterministic and Bayesian regularization address these by solving sequences of high-dimensional iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS) problems on lower-dimensional subspaces.   Generalized Krylov subspace (GKS) methods efficiently solve these IRLS sequences by projecting large-scale problems into a small, successively enlarged subspace. However, these sparsity-promoting GKS (S-GKS) methods converge slowly. We address this by combining S-GKS with priorconditioning (PS-GKS). Integrated into the iterative alternating sequential (IAS) algorithm, PS-GKS enables automatic selection of the generalized gamma hyper-prior's shape/rate parameter. We also explore restarting and recycling variants that handle memory constraints. Numerical experiments confirm that PS-GKS and its variants are competitive with or outperform existing hybrid Krylov methods.
Authors
Mirjeta Pasha, Virginia Tech, U.S., mpasha@vt.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Marginalization-Based Regularization in Separable Nonlinear Bayesian Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Separable nonlinear inverse problems arise in many applications where a forward model depends linearly on some unknowns and nonlinearly on others, with semi-blind deconvolution as a motivating example. In this talk, we adopt a Bayesian perspective with Gaussian observation noise and Gaussian priors on the linear variables, and show that common choices of priors for the nonlinear parameters lead to maximum a posteriori estimates equivalent to regularized separable nonlinear least-squares problems, which can be solved efficiently via variable projection. We then show that analytically marginalizing the linear variables yields a reduced posterior for the nonlinear parameters whose objective augments the usual variable-projection residual with a principled log-determinant term: this term encodes uncertainty in the eliminated variables, discourages degenerate “no-blur” solutions, and admits scalable evaluation for structured forward models. Numerical results in semi-blind deconvolution illustrate improved stability and reconstruction quality of the proposed marginalization-based regularization.
Authors
Malena I. Espanol, Arizona State University, U.S., malena.espanol@asu.edu
Jordan T. Dworaczyk, Arizona State University, U.S., jtdworac@asu.edu

sessions.title: Verification, Validation, and Uncertainty Quantification for Engineering Applications - Part II of III

talks.title: VVUQ for Reduced Models of Edge Transport Barriers in Tokamak Fusion Devices

speakers.name: Cole Stephens
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Pieterjan Robbe

speakers.name: Bert J. Debusschere
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Ping-Yu Li, Joseph Schmidt, Saeid Houshmandyar

speakers.name: Norman M. Cao
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Caitlin Curry, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; David Hatch

speakers.name: Todd Oliver
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The performance of tokamak fusion devices in high confinement mode depends on an edge transport barrier which increases the achievable plasma pressure. However, predictions of plasma temperature and density in the transport barrier remain limited by gaps in our understanding of turbulent transport. While high-fidelity nonlinear simulations are computationally expensive and impractical for routine use, existing reduced models rely on transport constraints that limit their applicability in relevant plasma scenarios.   This work addresses these limitations by developing fast, validated quasilinear transport models for the pedestal. The models are based on a mixing-length approach, utilizing linear gyrokinetic simulations performed with GENE. A Bayesian uncertainty quantification framework is employed to calibrate and validate the models against experimental pedestal profiles from the DIII-D tokamak, incorporating experimental uncertainties via Gaussian process regression. The forward propagation of uncertainties is implemented using the integrated modeling framework ASTRA. Initial validation results are presented for two transport models: one based on electron temperature gradient (ETG) modes [Hatch et al., Nucl. Fusion, 2024] and another targeting electromagnetic modes.

talks.title: MaLT: Machine-Learning-Guided Test Case Design and Fault Localization of Complex Software Systems

speakers.name: Irene Ji
speakers.affiliation: JMP Statistical Discovery LLC, U.S.; Simon Mak, Duke University, U.S.; Ryan Lekivetz

talks.abstract:
.
Software testing is essential for the reliable and robust development of complex software systems. However, testing and fault localization of such systems can be very costly. To mitigate this cost, we outline in this work a holistic machine-learning-guided test case design and fault localization (MaLT) framework, which leverages recent probabilistic machine learning methods to accelerate the testing of complex systems. MaLT consists of three steps: (i) the construction of a suite of test cases using a covering array for initial testing, (ii) the investigation of posterior root cause probabilities via a Bayesian fault localization procedure, then (iii) the use of such Bayesian analysis to guide selection of subsequent test cases via active learning. The proposed MaLT framework can thus facilitate efficient identification and subsequent diagnosis of software faults with limited test runs.
Authors
Irene Ji, JMP Statistical Discovery LLC, U.S., irene.ji@jmp.com

talks.title: Evidence Aggregation from VVUQ Activities Towards Quantitative Model Credibility Assessment
talks.abstract:
.
A computational approach is developed to aggregate information from a variety of model assessment activities and provide the decision-maker with quantitative information relating to model credibility. Model credibility is defined here as the degree to which the decision-maker believes that using the model prediction is acceptable for its intended application, based on the collected evidence. The proposed credibility measure makes use of six assessment activities considered in the Predictive Capability Maturity Model (PCMM) developed at Sandia National Laboratories. Based on the maturity, strength and relevance of evidence provided by the six PCMM elements, Dempster-Shafer theory (DST) is employed to combine the assessment scores and provide a three-dimensional metric of model credibility: a [0,1] measure of the degree of evidentiary support for the model's acceptability for its intended use, a [0,1] measure of the degree of evidentiary support against the model's acceptability for its intended use, and a [0,1] measure of the uncertainty about a model's acceptability due to lack of mature and/or relevant evidence. Extension of this credibility metric to assess model risk, i.e., risk of making wrong decisions, is discussed. This work was funded by Sandia National Laboratories.
Authors

Joshua Mullins, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., jmullin@sandia.gov

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Astrodynamics - Part III of III

talks.title: A Parallel Grid-based Algorithm for Orbital Uncertainty Propagation

speakers.name: Benjamin L. Hanson
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.; Carlos Rubio

speakers.name: Adrián García-Gutiérrez
speakers.affiliation: University of León, Spain; Thomas Bewley

speakers.name: Aaron Rosengren
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Eulerian nonlinear uncertainty propagation methods often suffer from finite domain limitations and computational inefficiencies. A recent approach to this class of algorithm, Grid-based Bayesian Estimation Exploiting Sparsity, addresses the first challenge by dynamically allocating a discretized grid in regions of phase space where probability is non-negligible. However, the design of the original algorithm causes the second challenge to persist in high-dimensional systems. This paper presents an architectural optimization of the algorithm for CPU implementation, followed by its adaptation to the CUDA framework for single GPU execution. The algorithm is validated for accuracy and convergence, with performance evaluated across distinct GPUs. Tests include propagating a three-dimensional probability distribution subject to the Lorenz '63 model and a six-dimensional probability distribution subject to the Lorenz '96 model. The results imply that the improvements made result in a speedup of over 1000 times compared to the original implementation. We leverage this speedup to feasibly propagate the six-dimensional state uncertainty of a highly inclined Saturn-Enceladus halo orbit in the Circular Restricted Three-Body Problem.
Authors
Benjamin L. Hanson, University of California, San Diego, U.S., blhanson@ucsd.edu

Aaron Rosengren, University of California, San Diego, U.S., arosengren@ucsd.edu

talks.title: Taylor Series Methods for Aerocapture Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Grace Calkins
speakers.affiliation: University of Colorado Boulder, U.S.; David Woffinden, NASA Johnson Space Center, U.S.; Jay McMahon, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
This paper presents an analytical approach for uncertainty quantification in aerocapture scenarios using higher-order augmented directional state transition tensors with Gaussian mixture models to efficiently propagate uncertainties and represent nonlinear state distributions. State transition tensors (STTs), higher-order Taylor series expansions of the solution flow, have been widely used as a computationally efficient analytical uncertainty quantification method for orbital scenarios, but have not previously been applied to aerocapture. However, obtaining higher-order STTs requires integrating exponentially more equations. Directional state transition tensors (DSTTs) mitigate this cost by projecting the state into a reduced-dimension basis. This work develops new dynamics analysis techniques to identify effective bases for this reduction, including decomposed and higher-order Cauchy Green tensors and augmented formulations tailored to quantities of interest such as apoapsis radius. These dynamics-informed directions are used for state transition tensor basis reduction and Gaussian mixture model mixand splitting. The proposed method is demonstrated in a Uranus aerocapture scenario, with results compared against Monte Carlo simulations and the Unscented Transform.
Authors
Grace Calkins, University of Colorado Boulder, U.S., grace.calkins@colorado.edu

sessions.title: Learning Dynamics with Evolving Uncertainty: Interplay of Bayesian Modeling, Transformers, and State-Space AI - Part III of III

talks.title: From Single to Multiple Kernels: Learning Interacting Particle Systems on Networks
talks.abstract:
.
We present a framework for jointly learning the network structure, interaction kernels, and type assignments in heterogeneous interacting particle systems (IPS) on networks. The agents follow first-order stochastic dynamics, with interactions governed by unknown kernel functions depending on discrete type pairs. Since species information is not observed a priori, the learning problem is inherently a non-convex mixed-integer optimization. To address this challenge, we decouple the task into a low-rank matrix sensing stage and a clustering stage, followed by decomposition and post-processing to refine estimates. The matrix sensing step leverages shared kernel structure to recover a low-rank embedding of network and kernel parameters, while the clustering step infers discrete types via angular separation of normalized embeddings. We establish theoretical guarantees under restricted isometry properties (RIP), and validate the method on synthetic examples, including heterogeneous predator–prey systems. Our approach generalizes to a broad class of multitype IPS models and provides a scalable, robust solution for learning structured particle interactions from trajectory data.
Authors
Quanjun Lang, Duke University, U.S., quanjun.lang@duke.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Reduction Modeling and Computation for Multiscale Problems - Part I of III

talks.title: Differentiable Dynamic Mode Decomposition
talks.abstract:
.
We propose Differentiable Dynamic Mode Decomposition (Diff-DMD), a framework that enables  gradient-based design and analysis of nonlinear dynamical systems directly through functions of DMD eigenvalues and modes. By embedding an adjoint-based sensitivity formulation into the  standard DMD pipeline, Diff-DMD computes exact derivatives of modal quantities with respect to system parameters and data at the cost of a single forward factorization plus one adjoint  solve—independent of the number of parameters. We derive sensitivities for both independently distributed and sequential snapshot constructions and further obtain data sensitivities to support robust design and adversarial data analysis. The approach yields orders-of-magnitude speedups over finite-difference approximations while maintaining accuracy. We demonstrate the method on (i) stabilizing a linear system by shifting the real parts of its eigenvalues, (ii) suppressing chaos in the Lorenz system by steering parameters until all continuous-time DMD eigenvalues have negative real parts, and (iii) increasing the oscillation frequency of a seven-dimensional yeast glycolysis model under stability and slow-decay regularization. Across these cases, Diff-DMD produces stable, accurate gradients that agree with finite differences and enable efficient optimization in high-dimensional settings, offering a scalable surrogate  for design, control, and system identification based on Koopman/DMD modal structure.

talks.title: Structure-preserving Lift and Learn: Scientific Machine Learning for Nonlinear Conservative Partial Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
This talk presents structure-preserving Lift & Learn, a scientific machine learning method that employs lifting variable transformations to learn structure-preserving reduced-order models for nonlinear partial differential equations (PDEs) with conservation laws. We propose a hybrid learning approach based on a recently developed energy-quadratization strategy that uses knowledge of the nonlinearity at the PDE level to derive an equivalent quadratic lifted system with quadratic system energy. The lifted dynamics obtained via energy quadratization are linear in the old variables, making model learning very effective in the lifted setting. Based on the lifted quadratic PDE model form, the proposed method derives quadratic reduced terms analytically and then uses those derived terms to formulate a constrained optimization problem to learn the remaining linear reduced operators in a structure-preserving way. We demonstrate the generalizability of quadratic models learned via the proposed structure-preserving Lift & Learn method through three numerical examples: the one-dimensional wave equation with exponential nonlinearity, the two-dimensional sine-Gordon equation, and the two-dimensional Klein-Gordon-Zakharov equations. The numerical results show that the proposed learning approach is competitive with the state-of-the-art structure-preserving data-driven model reduction method in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency.

Harsh Sharma, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., hsharma33@wisc.edu

talks.title: Data-Driven Mori–Zwanzig with Variable Projection Operators

speakers.name: Yen Ting Lin
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
The Mori–Zwanzig (MZ) formalism decomposes an observable into resolved and unresolved components through a projection operator, and the accuracy of the resulting reduced-order model is critically dependent on this choice. Traditionally, the projection operator is fixed and applied recursively to derive the memory terms, which capture the intrinsic non-Markovian character of the MZ formalism. In earlier work, we demonstrated that the projection operator can be defined implicitly as a learning problem, specified by a model class and an empirical risk function to be minimized. However, fixing the same projection operator imposes a constraint: both the model class and cost function must remain identical across the Markov term and all memory operators.
In this work, we relax this restriction by allowing variable projection operators within the MZ framework. This generalization enables a data-driven approach in which different components of the MZ expansion can be modeled flexibly with mixed models. We present theoretical insights into this extended formulation and provide numerical results illustrating that the variable-projection MZ framework yields more accurate and adaptable reduced-order models compared to the conventional fixed-operator approach.

Yen Ting Lin, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., yentingl@lanl.gov

talks.title: Active Learning for Neural PDE Solvers

speakers.name: Marimuthu Kalimuthu
speakers.affiliation: Universität Stuttgart, Germany; David Holzmüller, Inria, France; Makoto Takamoto, NEC Corporation, Japan; Mathias Niepert, Universität Stuttgart, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
Efficiently solving partial differential equations (PDEs) is critical across science and engineering. Neural PDE solvers have emerged as a promising alternative to established numerical solvers, offering fast and differentiable surrogate models. However, a main obstacle for neural PDE solvers is the high initial cost of obtaining the training dataset.   In this talk, we will discuss active learning (AL) as a method to reduce data generation costs. By iteratively selecting only the most informative inputs to the simulator, AL can reach the same level of accuracy with fewer training samples. Yet, PDEs also pose a significant challenge for deep AL, as they involve an autoregressive, high-dimensional regression problem that differs fundamentally from the standard applications, such as image classification.  To evaluate common deep AL strategies in the context of neural PDE solvers, we recently introduced the AL4PDE benchmark, which we describe in the second part of the talk. AL4PDE is a modular framework designed to accelerate the development of new AL strategies for neural PDE solvers. It contains ready-to-use neural PDE solvers, AL strategies, and PDE tasks. Based on AL4PDE, we present a benchmark study showing that deep AL methods can already reduce the average error compared to a randomly sampled dataset. In particular, AL achieves lower worst-case errors, making the final surrogate model more robust. Finally, we discuss recent advances in AL for PDEs.

talks.title: Information Theory Based Active Learning For High Dimensional Regression and Operator Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Deep learning has found extensive success for generative and classification tasks. This success is partially due to the large amounts of data available for training large neural network models. In scientific computing, however, training sets can be scarce and new data expensive to collected, often requiring long numerical simulations or even physical experiments that cannot be done in-silico. Efficient data collection has the potential to help increase accuracy and model robustness in this setting. Active learning takes into consideration model (or epistemic) uncertainty in order to sample data points that have the largest potential of decreasing model uncertainty. In this project, we present a framework for active learning in regression and operator learning, practically extending the popular acquisition function BALD to high-dimensional regression. Our methodology is defined using epistemic neural networks, which provide a general framework that applies to Bayesian Neural Networks, Deep Ensembles, MC Dropout and more. By running numerical experiments, we find a clear advantage to using our active learning methods over variance sampling.

talks.title: Active Operator Learning over Shapes and Other Function Spaces
talks.abstract:
.
Active learning has proven successful for robust training of surrogates in settings where data generation can be controlled. Such methods typically rely on uncertainty-aware surrogates to drive efficient dataset generation, and as a result are most established for learning functions that map between vector spaces. In recent years, there has been increasing interest in non-vector structured data, like graph- and field-structured data, as they are common in the scientific domain (e.g. spatio-temporal processes).
To that end, we explore ways to quantify the uncertainty of such operator approximations, particularly for operators that map shapes to quantities of interest. Using functional or graph shape representations, we develop such operators for closed surfaces with UQ. Secondly, we explore ways to generate a pool of valid candidates (e.g. shapes) for active learning, which is not as trivial as generating candidates in a vector space. We show how these two ingredients can be coupled to yield an effective active learning recipe for operators.

talks.title: Continuum Transformers Perform In-Context Learning by Operator Gradient Descent
talks.abstract:
.
Transformers robustly exhibit the ability to perform in-context learning, whereby their predictive accuracy on a task can increase not by parameter updates but merely with the placement of training samples in their context windows. Recent works have shown that transformers achieve this by implementing gradient descent in their forward passes. Such results, however, are restricted to standard transformer architectures, which handle finite-dimensional inputs. In the space of PDE surrogate modeling, a generalization of transformers to handle infinite-dimensional function inputs, known as "continuum transformers,' has been proposed and similarly observed to exhibit in-context learning. Despite impressive empirical performance, such in-context learning has yet to be theoretically characterized. We herein demonstrate that continuum transformers perform in-context operator learning by performing gradient descent in an operator RKHS. We demonstrate this using novel proof strategies that leverage a generalized Representer Theorem for Hilbert spaces and gradient flows over the space of functionals on a Hilbert space. We further show the operator learned in context is the Bayes Optimal Predictor in the infinite depth limit of the transformer. We then provide empirical validations of this result and demonstrate that the parameters under which such gradient descent is performed are recovered through pre-training.

talks.title: Generative PDE Solving from Partial Observation and Optimization-free PDE Constraints Enforcement
talks.abstract:
.
We present a unified framework for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) using video-inpainting diffusion transformer models. Unlike existing methods that devise specialized strategies for either forward or inverse problems under full or partial observation, our approach unifies these tasks under a single, flexible generative framework. Specifically, we recast PDE-solving as a generalized inpainting problem, e.g., treating forward prediction as inferring missing spatiotemporal information of future states from initial conditions. To this end, we design a transformer-based architecture that conditions on arbitrary patterns of known data to infer missing values across time and space. Our method proposes pixel-space video diffusion models for fine-grained, high-fidelity inpainting and conditioning, while enhancing computational efficiency through hierarchical modeling. Extensive experiments show that our video inpainting-based diffusion model offers an accurate and versatile solution across a wide range of PDEs and problem setups, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Moreover, we further develop a novel technology to ensure that the output solutions follow the underlying PDEs. We make an algorithmic improvement over the popular diffusion posterior sampling approach by adopting a learned gradient descent approach for robust inference time constraints enforcement.
Authors
Jeong Joon Park, University of Michigan, U.S., jjparkcv@umich.edu

talks.title: Constrained Flow Matching for Scientific Applications
talks.abstract:
.
Generative models that satisfy hard constraints are essential in real-world applications, where physical laws or task requirements must be strictly enforced. Some approaches exist, but they struggle to guarantee feasibility. While repeated projection enforces feasibility, it distorts the learned distribution, leading to a mismatch with the data manifold. To mitigate this issue, existing methods introduce complex multi-stage procedures that apply projection to clean samples during sampling. However, these strategies typically increase complexity and suffer from error accumulation across steps. We seek to address these challenges by proposing a novel training-free method, Chance-constrained Flow Matching (CCFM). CCFM integrates stochastic optimization into the sampling process, enabling efficient and concise enforcement of hard constraints while maintaining high sample quality. Importantly, CCFM guarantees feasibility in the same manner as conventional repeated projection and, despite operating directly on noisy intermediate samples, is theoretically equivalent to projecting onto the feasible set defined by clean samples. Numerical experiments show that CCFM achieves superior performance in PDE solution generation and molecular docking.
Authors
Jinhao Liang, University of Virginia, U.S., jliang@email.virginia.edu

talks.title: Physics-Informed Posterior Sampling in Function Spaces
talks.abstract:
.
Inverse problems in scientific computing are foundational yet challenging due to high dimensionality, ill-posedness, and uncertainty. Traditional methods like optimization or MCMC often struggle with computational expense and robustness to noise. While generative models have emerged for PDE solving, they often lack flexibility or data efficiency. In this talk, we explore physics-informed posterior sampling using flow-based models in function spaces, enabling resolution-agnostic, physics-consistent inverse solvers. We introduce Function-space Diffusion Posterior Sampling (FunDPS), the first infinite-dimensional diffusion framework for PDE inverses. By training unconditional denoisers on functions and guiding via sparse measurements with theoretical guarantees, FunDPS achieves up to 32% accuracy gains and 4x computational efficiency over grid-based baselines. The architecture also unlocks powerful multi-resolution strategies for further speedups. Building on this, we present the Decoupled Diffusion Inverse Solver (DDIS), which decouples prior learning from physics-informed sampling. This design mitigates over-smoothing in diffusion posterior sampling and enables the incorporation of advanced operator learning for inverse solving. We hope to provide new insights for infinite-dimensional generalization of posterior sampling, fostering scalable physics-informed tools for science.
Authors
Jiachen Yao, California Institute of Technology, U.S., jiachen.yao@caltech.edu

talks.title: Towards a Multimodal Foundation Model for Heterogeneous Catalysis

speakers.name: Daniel Israel
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The discovery of new catalysts is a major challenge, because it is limited by iterative, trial-and-error experimentation and computationally intensive simulations. Machine learning has shown promise to accelerate catalysis, but existing models are often restricted to a single data modality, unable to bridge the gap between vast textual knowledge in scientific literature and the explicit 3D atomic geometries of materials. To address this challenge, we introduce Alchemic, a natively multimodal foundation model that unifies natural language and 3D atomic structures. Our approach utilizes a Transformer backbone to learn the joint distribution over both data types. We achieve this through training a continuous tokenizer to embed structures in latent space; we then apply a flow matching objective to generate latent structures conditioned on the text input. Training with multimodality remains a significant challenge in AI-driven materials discovery and AI for science in general.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Digital Twins for Earth Science - Part I of III

talks.title: Decision Making and Control with Digital Twins for Underground Energy Storage
talks.abstract:
.
Decision making for underground energy storage must balance operational performance with safety under sparse observations and substantial subsurface uncertainty. We present a digital twin for uncertainty quantification (UQ) and closed-loop operations optimization—e.g., selecting injection schedules, pressure-management strategies, and monitoring actions—driven by probabilistic state estimates from time-lapse geophysical data.  The digital twin couples physics-based simulators with amortized Bayesian inference to learn a conditional posterior over reservoir states (e.g., pressure and saturation) given seismic monitoring data. These posterior distributions quantify uncertainty and propagate it into downstream control decisions. We then formulate risk-aware objectives that optimize operations subject to explicit safety criteria, using probability-of-failure (PoF) constraints and conditional value-at-risk (CVaR) penalties to manage low-probability, high-impact events such as reservoir overpressure that could compromise storage integrity.

talks.title: Scientific Machine Learning Enhanced Forecasting in Subsurface Flows

speakers.name: Hannah Lu
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Accurate forecasting of subsurface flow migration and trapping remains a central challenge in geoscience. Despite well-established physical understanding and advanced numerical simulators, predictive uncertainty persists due to geological heterogeneity, data scarcity, and the prohibitive cost of high-fidelity simulations. This talk presents a scientific machine learning  framework to enhance forecasting capabilities for subsurface flows across scales. Starting from lab-scale experiments, we develop digital twins that combine experimental data, physics-based models, and machine learning surrogates to reproduce and predict multiphase flow dynamics under both pressure-driven and capillary–buoyancy–dominated flow regimes. The framework enables quantitative studies of energy storage retention and flow transport, while identifying key geological and barrier properties that control storage/operation performance. Through collaborations, this research aims to establish a scalable and interpretable SciML foundation for digital twins of subsurface energy storage, bridging experimental observables with predictive modeling of subsurface flow in realistic heterogeneous formations.

speakers.name: Haizhao Yang
speakers.affiliation: University of Maryland, College Park, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Learning operators between infinitely dimensional spaces is an important learning task arising in wide applications in machine learning, data science, mathematical modeling and simulations, etc. This talk introduces a new discretization-invariant operator learning approach based on data-driven kernels via deep learning. Compared to existing methods, our approach achieves attractive accuracy in solving forward and inverse problems, prediction problems, and signal processing problems with discretization invariant, i.e., networks trained with a fixed data structure can be applied to heterogeneous data structures without expensive re-training. Under mild conditions, quantitative generalization error will be provided to understand operator learning in the sense of non-parametric estimation.

talks.title: Kernel Methods for Operator Learning
talks.abstract:
.
We present a kernel-based framework for learning operators between Banach  spaces. We show that even with simple kernels, our approach is competitive in terms of  cost-accuracy trade-off and either matches or beats the performance of Neural Network  methods on a majority of PDE-based benchmarks. Additionally, our framework offers  several advantages inherited from kernel methods: simplicity, convergence guarantees, a  priori error estimates, and Bayesian UQ. We also present recent work on high-accuracy operator learning for the emulation of numerical solvers. We show that by emulating the Newton updates of a numerical solver, we can achieve machine precision on PDE forward and inverse problems using operator learning.

talks.title: Data-driven Probabilistic Medium-Range Weather Forecasting
talks.abstract:
.
The recent revolution in data-driven methods for weather forecasting has lead to a fragmented landscape of architectures and training strategies, obscuring the drivers of forecast accuracy. Here, we demonstrate that state-of-the-art probabilistic skill requires neither architectural constraints nor specialized training heuristics. We introduce a scalable framework for learning atmospheric dynamics by combining a downsampled latent space with a history-conditioned local projector that resolves high-resolution physics. We find that our framework is robust to the choice of probabilistic estimator, supporting stochastic interpolants, diffusion models, and CRPS-based ensemble training. Validated against the Integrated Forecasting System and the deep learning probabilistic model GenCast, our framework achieves statistically significant improvements on most variables. These results suggest scaling a general-purpose model is sufficient for medium-range prediction, eliminating the need for tailored training recipes and proving effective across the spectrum of probabilistic frameworks. This is a joint work with Jean Kossaifi, Nikola Kovachki, Morteza Mardani, Daniel Leibovici, Suman Ravuri, Ira Shokar Edoardo Calvello, Mohammad Shoaib Abbas, Peter Harrington, Ashay Subramaniam, Noah Brenowitz, Boris Bonev, Wonmin Byeon, Karsten Kreis, Dale Durran, Arash Vahdat, Mike Pritchard and Jan Kautz.

talks.title: Training Distribution Design in the Space of Probability Measures
talks.abstract:
.
What probability distribution should training data be sampled from to best approximate a target function or operator?  This talk provides an answer in the setting that “best” refers to out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization accuracy with respect to a family of downstream tasks. The talk proposes to minimize average-case OOD accuracy, or its upper bound, over the space of probability measures. The minimizing data distribution depends not just on the model class (e.g., kernel regressors, neural networks) and the accuracy metric, but also on the target map itself. This leads to two implementable adaptive and target-dependent data selection algorithms based on either bilevel or alternating optimization. The new approach produces trained surrogate models that display increased robustness when evaluated on test data from shifted and unseen distributions. These models further empirically outperform others trained on traditional nonadaptive or target-independent data distributions in several function approximation, operator learning, and inverse problem tasks.

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Double Descent in Sparse Sensing

speakers.name: Andrei A. Klishin
speakers.affiliation: University of Hawaii at Manoa, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Sparse sensing is the paradigm of reconstructing full state of a complex system using few localized measurements and taking advantage of training data to propose a tailored basis specific to the system. The question of where to place sensors has been previously addressed with efficient techniques like QR pivoting and its extensions that account for engineering constraints. In this talk we turn to the question of the reconstruction algorithm and demonstrate that naive pseudoinverse leads to the phenomenon of double descent, or the instability of reconstruction the sensor and mode numbers match. We argue that optimal data-driven regularization of the reconstruction reduces the reconstruction variance, suppresses the double descent instability, and provides both an integral prediction of the error and a detailed spatial heatmap of how the error is distributed.

talks.title: An Online Variational Inference Framework for Joint Estimation of State and System Parameters: Theory and Methodology
talks.abstract:
.
We propose a factorization-based online variational inference approach that incrementally assimilates data and constructs an approximate joint posterior distribution of unknown system parameters and states. The approximation is factorized into a marginal distribution over the system parameters and a conditional distribution of the states given the parameters. This factorization enables recursive updates through a two-stage procedure. First, the parameter posterior is approximated via variational inference. Second, the conditional state distribution is computed using existing filtering methods based on the estimated parameter posterior. The algorithmic design is guided by a theoretical result that establishes upper bounds on the joint posterior approximation error. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable performance in both low- and high-dimensional settings and remains robust under noisy and partial observations, as well as model misspecification.

sessions.title: Assessing and Quantifying Model-form Uncertainty and Model-form Error - Part II of III

talks.title: Informing Predictive Uncertainties in the Presence of Model Misspecification: Generalized Bayesian Inference Approaches
talks.abstract:
.
In real-world engineering applications, computational models play a crucial role in guiding decision-making by providing predictions in regimes where observational data is unavailable. However, assessing the reliability of extrapolative predictions requires meaningful uncertainty quantification in calibration scenarios. While Bayesian inference provides a unifying framework for characterizing and extrapolating uncertainties, standard approaches can underpredict uncertainties when model misspecification is present. Even with the integration of physics-based or data-driven enhancements to reduce model discrepancy, the risk of misspecification remains. Advances in more generalized Bayesian inference techniques have demonstrated the potential to improve robustness to misspecification by employing more flexible loss function characterizations compared to conventional likelihood-based approaches. This work investigates novel formulations of such generalized losses that directly penalize poor uncertainty characterizations. We demonstrate this approach on an application problem in contaminant transport through heterogeneous porous media.
Authors
Rebekah White, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., rebwhit@sandia.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Detecting Model Form Error in Dynamical Systems Using Information Field Theory
talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty in the governing equations is a major source of error when conducting inference in dynamical systems. Incorrect model forms cannot typically be compensated for by parameter calibration, and neglecting this issue risks biased estimates and misleading conclusions. While existing model selection techniques provide ways to choose among candidate models, they are often limited by a narrow pool of options that may not be representative of the unmodeled dynamics. An alternative line of work introduces correction terms into the governing equations, but most approaches rely on general forms, e.g., Gaussian processes or neural networks, that do not necessarily preserve physical constraints and symmetries. The goal of this presentation is to identify model correction terms from observations while ensuring a thermodynamically consistent parametrization. Our methodology builds on information field theory, a scalable Bayesian framework that enables us to embed the governing physics as prior knowledge over the field with a learnable trust. We then update the trust through a pretrained joint distribution of data and governing fields, using a diffusion-based model. We showcase the capability of our approach through synthetic examples spanning dynamical systems of different complexity, including Hamiltonian systems and those with energy dissipation. The results illustrate how the approach can recover missing physics while providing a principled quantification of model-form error.
Authors
Motahareh Mirfarah, Purdue University, U.S., mmirfara@purdue.edu
Alex Alberts, Purdue University, U.S., albert31@purdue.edu
Ilias Bilionis, Purdue University, U.S., ibilion@purdue.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Hyper-Differential Sensitivity Analysis with Respect to Model Discrepancy
talks.abstract:
.
Computational models have made significant progress in simulating complex physical phenomena. However, this has led to a substantial increase in computational costs, making high-fidelity models impractical for analyses such as optimization. To address this challenge, low-fidelity models can be constructed, which are more amenable to optimization. Examples of low-fidelity modeling approaches include averaging small length scales, omitting physical processes, and employing data-driven model approximations. However, modeling errors propagate through the optimization process, resulting in suboptimal solutions. This work considers the use of hyper-differential sensitivity analysis to learn improved optimization solutions from limited high-fidelity model evaluations. We introduce a representation of model discrepancy—the difference between high- and low-fidelity models—and calibrate it in a Bayesian framework. The model discrepancy posterior is propagated through the optimization problem using post-optimality sensitivity analysis, yielding a posterior distribution for the optimization solution. We present an interpretable and efficient approach to generate optimization solution posterior samples. We demonstrate that as little as one high-fidelity solve can provide a significant improvement in the optimization solution. Furthermore, we leverage Bayesian optimal experimental design to guide high-fidelity model evaluations.

talks.title: Rl-Daunce: Reinforcement Learning-Driven Data Assimilation with Uncertainty-Aware Constrained Ensembles
talks.abstract:
.
Machine learning is increasingly used to enhance data assimilation. While supervised learning has become the standard approach, reinforcement learning (RL) with its sequential decision-making structure, aligns with the iterative nature of assimilation by dynamically balancing forecasts and observations. We present RL-DAUNCE (Reinforcement Learning–Driven Data Assimilation with Uncertainty-Aware Constrained Ensembles) built on three core ideas: (i) RL-DAUNCE naturally inherits the computational efficiency of machine learning, while it uniquely structures its agents to mirror ensemble members in conventional data assimilation methods; (ii) multiple ensemble trajectories are advanced to quantify uncertainty beyond mean-state estimates; and (iii) constraints are enforced through a primal–dual optimization strategy and action-space restrictions, ensuring physical consistency throughout the assimilation process.
We apply RL-DAUNCE to the Madden–Julian Oscillation, a strongly intermittent atmospheric phenomenon with non-Gaussian behavior and multiple physical constraints. The method outperforms the standard ensemble Kalman filter, and matches the performance of constrained EnKF while requiring substantially less computation. RL-DAUNCE demonstrates strong skill in recovering intermittent signals, capturing extremes, and quantifying uncertainties, highlighting the potential of reinforcement learning as a flexible and efficient tool for constrained data assimilation.

talks.title: Multiproposal Elliptical Slice Sampling
talks.abstract:
.
We present Multiproposal Elliptical Slice Sampling (MESS), a multiproposal extension of Elliptical Slice Sampling (ESS) that allows multiple candidate states to be sampled in parallel at each iteration and favors accepting a candidate far from the current state. This can result in substantial reduction in wall clock time per independent sample, especially for expensive likelihoods. We also provide theoretical justification and experimental evidence for dimension-free mixing, showing that MESS is adequate for Bayesian PDE inverse problems. We demonstrate the algorithm on a toy model for solute transport. The model, strongly inspired by real physics,   considers an implied infinite-dimensional parameter and a non-linear parameter-to-observation map that generates highly non-Gaussian Bayesian posteriors with complex correlation structures.
Authors
Guillermina Senn , Norwegian University of Science and Technology, Norway and Indiana University, Bloomington, U.S., guillermina.senn@ntnu.no

sessions.title: Recent Developments in Statistical Methods for Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of III

talks.title: Scalable Non-Gaussian Variational Inference for Spatial Fields Using Sparse Autoregressive Normalizing Flows

speakers.name: Paul Wiemann
speakers.affiliation: Ohio State University, U.S.; Matthias Katzfuss , University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce a novel framework for scalable and flexible variational inference targeting the non-Gaussian posterior of a latent continuous function or field. For both the prior and variational family, we consider sparse autoregressive structures corresponding to nearest-neighbor directed acyclic graphs. Within the variational family, conditional distributions are modeled with highly flexible normalizing flows. We provide an algorithm for doubly stochastic variational optimization, achieving polylogarithmic time complexity per iteration. Empirical evaluations show that our method offers improved accuracy compared to existing techniques.
Authors
Paul Wiemann, Ohio State University, U.S., wiemann.3@osu.edu
Matthias Katzfuss, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., katzfuss@gmail.com

talks.title: Gaussian Processes for Uncertainty Quantification with Big Data
talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty quantification is critical in machine learning for workflows such as error propagation, Bayesian optimization, and decision-making under uncertainty. While neural networks have predictive utility, they lack a simple and general approach to modeling uncertainty. In contrast, Gaussian processes (GPs) provide native uncertainties and allow explicit testing of model assumptions. However, standard GP methods face severe computational and memory bottlenecks with large datasets or high-dimensional inputs, and common isotropic or stationary covariance models are often too restrictive. We address the GP computational challenges in two stages. First, we introduce MuyGPs, a leave-one-out cross-validation framework that induces sparsity to enable scalable GP inference with a large number of observations. Second, we present a variable selection procedure and model designed for high-dimensional problems that mitigates the curse of dimensionality and can be combined with MuyGPs for computational efficiency. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this combined method in a few surrogate model cases, highlighting both their practical advantages and their limitations.
Authors
Amanda Muyskens, Real Good AI, U.S., almuyskens@realgoodai.org
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Elastic Bayesian Model Calibration
talks.abstract:
.
Functional data are ubiquitous in scientific modeling. For instance, quantities of interest are modeled as functions of time, space, energy, density, etc. Uncertainty quantification methods for computer models with functional response have resulted in tools for emulation, sensitivity analysis, and calibration that are widely used. However, many of these tools do not perform well when the computer model’s parameters control both the amplitude variation of the functional output and its alignment (or phase variation). This paper introduces a framework for Bayesian model calibration when the model responses are misaligned functional data. The approach generates two types of data out of the misaligned functional responses: (1) aligned functions so that the amplitude variation is isolated and (2) warping functions that isolate the phase variation. These two types of data are created for the computer simulation data (both of which may be emulated) and the experimental data. The calibration approach uses both types so that it seeks to match both the amplitude and phase of the experimental data. The framework is careful to respect constraints that arise especially when modeling phase variation, and is framed in a way that it can be done with readily available calibration software.
Authors
Devin Francom, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., dfrancom@lanl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Spatial Warping Error Models for Addressing Structural Biases in Aerosol Climate Simulations
talks.abstract:
.
Aerosols influence the global climate by affecting cloud formation and altering the scattering and absorption of radiation. To reduce aerosol-related climate uncertainties, relevant climate model parameters are constrained by comparing the simulator outputs to observations of atmospheric conditions. Recent advances improve these comparisons by addressing the systematic model discrepancy between the simulator and atmospheric observations using a data-driven additive model discrepancy error. However, this treatment is not necessarily representative of typical structural errors in climate model outputs. In particular, misspecification in atmospheric dynamics can lead to spatial warping errors between the simulation and observations where the simulated structure of an atmospheric feature may be correct overall but it is displaced in space or distorted in shape. To address this, we propose a novel spatiotemporal modeling framework that models the spatial warping errors as random transport maps that capture these spatial distortions. We generate plausible transport maps using convex Gaussian processes and describe how simulation-based inference enables us to address the resulting likelihood-free inference problem for constrained spatial processes. When applied to the UKESM1 climate model and remotely sensed aerosol observations, our approach yields significantly higher likelihoods and stronger parameter constraints in comparison to models that rely solely on additive errors.
Authors
Erik Bensen, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., ebensen@andrew.cmu.edu
Victor Sanchez, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., vsanchez@andrew.cmu.edu
Mikael Kuusela, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., mkuusela@andrew.cmu.edu
Hamish Gordon, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., hamishg@andrew.cmu.edu

sessions.title: Advances in MCMC Sampling Methods - Part III of III

talks.title: Improved Energies and Wave Function Accuracy with Weighted Variational Monte Carlo
talks.abstract:
.
Neural network parametrizations have increasingly been used to represent the ground and excited states in variational Monte Carlo~(VMC) with promising results.  However, traditional VMC methods only optimize the wave function in regions of peak probability. The wave function is uncontrolled in the tails of the probability distribution, which can limit the  accuracy of the trained wavefunction approximation. To improve the approximation accuracy in the probability tails, this paper interprets VMC as a gradient flow in the space of wave functions, followed by a projection step. From this perspective, arbitrary probability distributions can be used in the projection step, allowing the user to prioritize accuracy in different regions of state space. Motivated by this theoretical perspective, the paper tests a new weighted VMC method on the antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model for a periodic spin chain. Compared to traditional VMC, weighted VMC reduces the error in the ground state energy by a factor of $2$ and it reduces the errors in the local energies away from the mode by large factors of $10^2$–$10^4$.
Authors
Huan Zhang, École normale supérieure – PSL, huan.zhang@lmd.ipsl.fr

talks.title: Scalable Langevin Monte Carlo Methods for Bayesian Inference
talks.abstract:
.
We introduce Langevin Monte Carlo methods for estimating expectations of observables under high-dimensional probability measures. We discuss discretization strategies and Metropolization methods for removing bias due to discretization error.  We then present a new unbiased method for Bayesian posterior means based on kinetic Langevin dynamics that combines advanced splitting methods with enhanced gradient approximations. Our approach avoids Metropolis correction by coupling Markov chains at different discretization levels in a multilevel Monte Carlo approach. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that our proposed estimator is unbiased, attains finite variance, and satisfies a central limit theorem. We prove similar results using both approximate and stochastic gradients and show that our method’s computational cost scales independently of the size of the dataset. Our numerical experiments demonstrate that our unbiased algorithm outperforms the “gold-standard” randomized Hamiltonian Monte Carlo.  We then discuss whether it is always necessary to remove discretization bias in high-dimensional models and applications of unadjusted MCMC algorithms in Bayesian Neural Network models.
Authors
Peter Whalley, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, peter.whalley@math.ethz.ch
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Monte Carlo Framework for Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation in Sequence Prediction

speakers.name: Qidong Yang
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Weicheng Zhu, Laure Zanna, Tim Rudner

talks.abstract:
.
Probabilistic prediction of sequences from images and other high-dimensional data remains a key challenge, particularly in safety-critical domains. In these settings, it is often desirable to quantify the uncertainty associated with the prediction (instead of just determining the most likely sequence, as in language modeling). In this paper, we consider a Monte Carlo framework to estimate probabilities and confidence intervals associated with sequences. The framework uses a Monte Carlo simulator, implemented as an autoregressively trained neural network, to sample sequences conditioned on an image input. We then use these samples to estimate  probabilities and confidence intervals. Experiments on synthetic and real data show that the framework produces accurate discriminative predictions, but can suffer from miscalibration. To address this shortcoming, we propose a time-dependent regularization method, which produces calibrated predictions.

sessions.title: Scientific Machine Learning Using Software-informed Priors - Part II of II

talks.title: Robust Optimal Experimental Design for Inverse Problems: Theory and Software Implementation
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, I present a family of probabilistic methods for solving challenging model-based optimal experimental design (OED) problems. This includes robust and constrained binary OED problems with black-box objectives for batch sensor placement, and trajectory optimization for sequential decision making in large-scale inverse problems. In the probabilistic approach, the optimization variable is viewed as a random variable and is associated with a parametric conditional probability model. A stochastic optimization problem is then formulated and efficiently solved by following a policy gradient approach. I will also give a high-level overview of PyOED, a highly extensible scientific package that enables rapid development, testing, and benchmarking of model-based OED methods for inverse problems. The presented probabilistic approaches to OED are implemented and are publicly available through PyOED.
Authors
Ahmed Attia, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., aattia@anl.gov

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Approaches to Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Aniket Jivani
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Tommie Catanach

speakers.name: Ruben Villarreal
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Xun Huan, University of Michigan, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Experimental data is often vital but expensive to collect, making it important to find experimental design conditions that maximize the value of these experiments. Bayesian optimal experimental design (OED) provides a framework for identifying the ideal design by leveraging a mathematical model that simulates the experimental outcomes. The practicality of OED is often limited by the
fidelity
of the model used. High-fidelity models are computationally expensive, making OED intractable, while low-fidelity models can lead to suboptimal designs. Furthermore, current OED methods often overlook the computational cost of the model evaluations themselves.
This work introduces two multi-fidelity approaches to overcome these limitations. The first approach uses approximate control variate estimators to expedite the estimation of the expected information gain (EIG), providing EIG estimates that are unbiased with respect to the high-fidelity model, but with significant computational speedups.
The second approach treats the multi-fidelity model ensemble as a set of numerical experiments that can also be planned via OED. This method uses a sequential and goal-oriented strategy to jointly learn the quantity of interest as well as the relationships between numerical and physical experiments in a cost-effective way.
We demonstrate the value of these multi-fidelity approaches on a set of practical engineering applications benchmark problems.
Authors
