talks.title: Power Flow Informed Priors for Energy Market Applications

speakers.name: Michael Brennan
speakers.affiliation: Salvo Energy, U.S.; Matthew Parno, Solea Energy, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Power flow models are central to energy market operations, forming the foundation of software tools ranging from simple line flow calculations to security-constrained optimal power flow. These tools underpin how Independent System Operators determine prices and how market participants engage in the energy markets. Yet the inherent uncertainty in inputs—load forecasts, renewable generation, and system topology—poses major challenges for both operational reliability and trading strategies.
In this work, we propose constructing prior distributions over power flow quantities of interest, grounded in the empirical marginal distributions of relevant inputs pushed through power flow simulations. We study the resulting priors, with a particular focus on how their structure depends on both the data and the software implementation of the underlying physical models. This perspective highlights how software-informed priors can guide scientific machine learning in energy systems, enabling more robust decision-making under uncertainty.

sessions.title: Data-driven Inverse Problem Methods with Statistical and Operator Learning

talks.title: Identifying Informative Designs for Constraining Viscosity in MgO under Shocked Conditions

speakers.name: Eric Johnsen
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Sonya Dick, South Dakota School of Mines & Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
When modeling materials with extremely high strain rates, such as shock compression experiments, or materials with extremely low strain rates, such as rheological flows, solids are often modeled as a fluid. For example, Earth’s mantle, though mostly solid, is treated as a viscous fluid over geological timescales with resistance to flow characterized by an effective viscosity (Colvin et al., 2003; Mikaelian, 2013).  However, experimental constraints on mantle-relevant materials, such as MgO, at appropriate pressures and temperatures remain limited (Foley, 2019; Foley et al., 2012; Karato, 2011).   Prior work has explored using Richtmyer-Meshkov instability (RMI) growth rates to infer the strength of MgO under shock, comparing experimental results to theory to help constrain viscosity (Perez et al., 2025). We extend this by introducing optimal experimental design (OED) aimed at identifying experimental conditions that best constrain MgO viscosity under shock loading. Our setup considers a shock incident on a corrugated interface between epoxy and MgO, with design variables including shock strength, initial amplitude, and wavelength. We define the experimental objective in terms of maximizing expected information gain on viscosity.

talks.title: Data Assimilation with Normalizing Flow Applied to Seismic Images
talks.abstract:
.
Seismic measurements governed by the wave equation provide an indirect means of estimating subsurface properties such as density and seismic velocity. In practice, uncertainty in active sources and environmental noise, together with the strong nonlinearity of wave propagation, render the associated inverse problems ill-posed. Moreover, inversion approaches often depend heavily on the choice of initial subsurface models, limiting their reliability for monitoring applications.
We focus on the problem of monitoring injected subsurface CO2 to verify containment for long-term geological storage. The migration of CO2 in the subsurface is described by multiphase flow physics and depends on heterogeneous permeability and porosity fields. We apply two ensemble-based data assimilation approaches, comparing the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) to conditional normalizing flow filters (CNFFs). We first demonstrate a failure for each method on a toy Gaussian problem and the large seismic-CO2 system before directly comparing their accuracy and uncertainty estimates on the large system.
Authors
Grant Bruer, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., gbruer@gatech.edu
Abhinav Gahlot, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., agahlot8@gatech.edu
Felix Herrmann, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., felix.herrmann@gatech.edu
Edmond Chow, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., echow@cc.gatech.edu

talks.title: Weakly-Constrained 4D Var for Downscaling with Uncertainty using Data-Driven Surrogate Models

speakers.name: Philip Dinenis
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Vishwas Rao

talks.abstract:
.
Dynamic downscaling typically involves using numerical weather prediction (NWP) solvers to refine coarse data to higher spatial resolutions. Data-driven models such as FourCastNet have emerged as a promising alternative to the traditional NWP models for forecasting. Once these models are trained, they are capable of delivering forecasts in a few seconds, thousands of times faster compared to classical NWP models. However, as the lead times, and, therefore, their forecast window, increase, these models show instability in that they tend to diverge from reality. In this work, we propose to use data assimilation approaches to stabilize them when used for downscaling tasks. Data assimilation uses information from three different sources, namely an imperfect computational model based on partial differential equations (PDE), from noisy observations, and from an uncertainty-reflecting prior. In this work, when carrying out dynamic downscaling, we replace the computationally expensive PDE-based NWP models with FourCastNet in a “weak-constrained 4DVar framework" that accounts for the implied model errors. We demonstrate the efficacy of this approach for a hurricane-tracking problem; moreover, the 4DVar framework naturally allows the expression and quantification of uncertainty. We demonstrate, using ERA5 data, that our approach performs better than the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF) and the unstabilized FourCastNet model, both in terms of forecast accuracy and forecast uncertainty.

Philip Dinenis, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., pdinenis@anl.gov

sessions.title: Verification, Validation, and Uncertainty Quantification for Engineering Applications - Part III of III

talks.title: Deep Gaussian Process Poisson Modeling of Large-Scale Satellite Count Data for Computer Model Calibration

speakers.name: Dave Osthus
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Yifan Huang, University of Alabama, Huntsville, U.S.; Fan Guo

talks.abstract:
.
The National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) launched the  Interstellar Boundary Explorer (IBEX) satellite in 2008 to learn more about  the region at the boundary between our solar system and interstellar space,  called the heliopause. IBEX detects energetic neutral atoms (ENAs) originating from the heliopause and enables space scientists to estimate their rate of emission throughout the sky. Space scientists have developed a variety of competing theories (with corresponding computer simulations) to explain ENA generation and propagation and hope to use data collected by IBEX to evaluate the relative strength of evidence for each proposed model. Statistical computer model calibration allows data from IBEX to be paired with computer simulations to accomplish this task. However, the count observations, limited but data-intense simulation runs and field measurements, and nonstationary response surface present some unique challenges. We propose a Vecchia-approximated deep Gaussian process Poisson model for the rate of ENAs. Our model has shown a greater ability to learn the complex, nonstationary mean response surface, and provide appropriate uncertainty quantification in different regions of the input space, while maintaining a simpler covariance function. We integrate this model into a novel Markov chain Monte Carlo computer model calibration framework to understand the distribution of the parameters that govern ENA generation.

talks.title: Neural Bayesian Methods for Spatio-Temporal Data Fusion of Remote Sensing Observations

speakers.name: Joaquim Teixeira
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.; Amy Braverman, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology; Maggie Johnson, NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S.; Sudipto Banerjee, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Hyperspectral and multispectral imaging satellites provide complementary remote sensing data across diverse temporal, spatial, and spectral resolutions. However, these resolutions are often misaligned: satellites with high temporal resolution typically have low spatial resolution, and vice versa. Statistical fusion of multi-sensor data offers the potential to generate high-resolution images across space, time, and spectra at near-global scales. Here, we present a highly scalable Neural Bayesian framework for fusing massive hyperspectral datasets from instruments with varying temporal, spatial, and spectral support. Our approach leverages a Gaussian process formulation in which 3-D Convolutional Neural Networks with Residual Blocks estimate posterior distributions of kernel parameters, while sparse matrix techniques enable efficient approximate Bayesian inference. By combining deep learning, sparse methods modeling, and high-performance computing, our method delivers well-calibrated uncertainties with orders-of-magnitude speedups over existing approaches, enabling rapid, high-fidelity spatio-temporal data fusion at remarkable scales.

talks.title: Functional Data Analysis and Deep Learning for Accelerated Statistical Model Calibration

speakers.name: Kwesi A. Ohene-Obeng
speakers.affiliation: Luis Damiano, Khachik Sargsyan, Bert J. Debusschere

talks.abstract:
.
We present a unified framework for the robust calibration of complex    physical and engineering models under uncertainty. Our methodology integrates    functional data analysis, deep learning surrogates, and optimization-based    uncertainty calibration in function space. Data are represented via wavelet    expansions, preserving the temporal structure of high-dimensional data    throughout the surrogate construction and calibration processes. A Monte Carlo    dropout neural network is trained to map model input parameters to functional    response coefficients, providing accurate surrogate predictions with epistemic    uncertainty bands. Calibration is performed through deterministic optimization    of model parameters against data profiles, enabling parameter estimation    informed by entire functional curves rather than isolated pointwise    measurements. By coupling concepts from functional data analysis and deep    learning, our framework establishes a scalable foundation for future    wavelet-enabled statistical calibration.

sessions.title: Hybrid AI-Statistical Approaches for Environmental Spatio-Temporal Processes - Part I of II

talks.title: An Overview of Hybrid Statistical and Deep Neural Models for Spatial and Spatio-Temporal Processes
talks.abstract:
.
Environmental processes include complex dependencies across time, space, and sub-process. These processes are inherently dynamic, where spatial processes evolve with time.  It has been a long-standing problem in spatio-temporal statistics to develop efficient, flexible, and realistic data-driven models for such processes in a way that appropriately accounts for uncertainty in data, process, and parameters.  With the popularization of deep neural models in machine learning, a large collection of new methods and computational approaches are available for statisticians and applied mathematicians to use to address these challenges. In this overview lecture, I will present some hybrid deep neural/statistical approaches that have been used in environmental statistics in recent years.  These include methods in which statistical models are embedded within deep learning paradigms and methods in which deep neural models are embedded in statistical models.
Authors
Christopher Wikle, University of Missouri, U.S., wiklec@missouri.edu

talks.title: Improved Subseasonal Forecasting of Extreme Weather Using Statistical Machine Learning

speakers.name: Maike Holthuijzen
speakers.affiliation: Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.; Jacob Johnson, Sandia National Laboratories

talks.abstract:
.
Extreme temperatures cause more than \$100 billion in damages annually within the United States alone. This means it is of great societal importance to improve predictive skill of these temperature extremes on subseasonal timescales (2-6 weeks) beyond that of the traditional physics-based model. In this study, we evaluate the performance of statistical-machine learning (ML) methods, including Echo State Networks (ESN) and Random Forests (RF), for forecasting weekly average temperatures across five U.S. regions, with forecast horizons ranging from one to four weeks. In addition to point forecasts, we assess each model's ability to predict extremes, defined as weekly temperature anomalies exceeding one standard deviation from climatology. All ML models are benchmarked against two standard baselines: a persistence model and a climatology-plus-trend model. We additionally compare feature importance between models. Our results provide a comprehensive comparison of ML methods for subseasonal temperature forecasting and offer new insights into the complexity of feature relevance across lead times and regions. talks.title: Bayesian Closure Modeling for Dynamic Systems

speakers.name: Dan Drennan
speakers.affiliation: Texas A&M University, U.S.; Kyle Neal, Rileigh Bandy, Moe Khalil

talks.abstract:
.
Computational simulations of dynamical systems often require constitutive or closure models to represent unresolved phenomena, enhance computational efficiency, or correct model form error in the simulation. Data-driven closure models (DDCMs) employ statistical machine learning (ML) to learn the closure term and uncertainty as a function of dynamic system states and have the potential to increase the predictive capability of computational simulations. Our approach addresses learning dynamics in data sparsity regimes by augmenting the observed measurements with dense state and closure terms estimated from experimental data using a particle filter. For trustworthiness, accurate uncertainty quantification (UQ) for DDCM predictions is essential. Uncertainties that arise from sparse or noisy experimental data are mapped to the estimated closure term using the particle filter, and then probabilistic statistical models propagate uncertainties to the predicted quantity. We demonstrate our methodology on an exemplar from dynamic ecological modeling and provide preliminary results using Bayesian hierarchical models with regularization for the DDCM.  SNL is managed and operated by NTESS under DOE NNSA contract DE-NA0003525.

talks.title: Locally Adaptive Conformal Inference for Operator Models
talks.abstract:
.
Operator models are regression algorithms between Banach spaces of functions. They have become an increasingly critical tool for spatiotemporal forecasting and physics emulation, especially in high-stakes scenarios where robust, calibrated uncertainty quantification is required. We introduce Local Sliced Conformal Inference (LSCI), a distribution-free framework for generating function-valued, locally adaptive prediction sets for operator models. We prove finite-sample validity and derive a data-dependent upper bound on the coverage gap under local exchangeability. On synthetic Gaussian-process tasks and real applications (air quality monitoring, energy demand forecasting, and weather prediction), LSCI yields tighter sets with stronger adaptivity compared to conformal baselines. We also empirically demonstrate robustness against biased predictions and certain out-of-distribution noise regimes.
Authors
Trevor Harris, University of Connecticut, U.S., zft24002@uconn.edu

sessions.title: Generative and Data-Driven Approaches for Uncertainty Quantification in Complex Dynamical Systems

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Prediction of Two-Dimensional Geophysical Turbulence with Generative Models
talks.abstract:
.
Two-dimensional geophysical turbulence exhibits strong intermittency, non-Gaussian variability, and finite-memory effects that challenge deterministic reduced-order closures and yield over-confident forecasts. This talk presents a generative-AI framework for probabilistic prediction and uncertainty quantification (UQ) in 2D geophysical flows (e.g., quasi-geostrophic turbulence). We combine conditional diffusion models with physics-aware embeddings to produce calibrated ensemble forecasts directly in vorticity/streamfunction space. The models are trained to approximate the conditional law of subgrid tendencies given resolved states (and short histories), with soft constraints enforcing incompressibility and energy/enstrophy budgets. We quantify both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty via likelihood/score objectives and Bayesian weight regularization and validate with a-posteriori rollouts against high-resolution simulations. Diagnostics include spectra and fluxes, PDFs of vorticity and gradients, extreme-event exceedance probabilities, and long-horizon stability.
Authors
Xuping Xie, Old Dominion University, U.S., xxie@odu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Learning Gradient Flow Dynamics from Particle Data

speakers.name: Qihao Ye
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We propose an interpretable learning framework for identifying the gradient flow dynamics of particle-based evolution systems. The objective is to recover the underlying energy functional, which naturally decomposes into internal, drift, and interaction terms. The key idea of our approach is to design a learning algorithm that preserves the structure of the energy. The inferred model can then be used to generate more particle data over longer time horizons and under various initial conditions. This approach yields a physically grounded model that not only captures observed dynamics but also generalizes to predict behaviors in complex particle systems.
Authors
Qihao Ye, University of California, San Diego, U.S., q8ye@ucsd.edu

talks.title: Diffusion-Based Stochastic Operator Networks for Uncertainty Quantification in Stochastic Partial Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
We propose a novel framework for effectively quantifying the uncertainty in the solutions of stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs) using stochastic operator learning. SPDEs has been used intensively to model complicated physical systems under uncertainty, however, while accurate, classical solvers require problem-specific discretizations, adaptive meshing near singular features, which are often tedious and costly. To avoid such difficulties, we develop the Stochastic Operator Network (SON) by combining the stochastic optimal control mechanism of the Stochastic Neural Network (SNN) with the architecture of the DeepONet to learn the noisy solution operators of SPDEs directly from the available data. By formulating the training procedure as a stochastic optimal control problem, the SON is able to learn the uncertainty presented in the solution operators through the diffusion coefficients appearing in the SNN. Numerical experiments on benchmark SPDEs demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of the proposed method in replicating several noisy solution operators.
Authors
Phuoc Toan Huynh, Florida State University, U.S., th24ba@fsu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Numerical Analysis of Discontinuous Galerkin Method for Nonlinear Stochastic Convection-Diffusion Equation
talks.abstract:
.
There has been a growing interest for designing efficient high order numerical algorithms to approximate solutions of stochastic partial differential equations (SPDEs). In this talk, we develop fully-discrete high-moment stability analysis and error estimates for nonlinear stochastic convection-diffusion equations in one and higher dimensions, adopting local discontinuous Galerkin (LDG) method in space and implicit-explicit Euler-Maruyama scheme in time. Existing work on DG methods for SPDEs mostly focus on linear equations and semi-discrete analysis. This is because solutions of SPDEs are generally not time differentiable and are not bounded in the path variable. In this work, we prove high-moment stability analysis and (path-wise) error estimates for fully nonlinear equation on subsets whose probability converge to one. Numerical experiments are provided to verify the strong order of accuracy of our numerical schemes.
Authors
Yiming Chen, Ohio State University, U.S., chen.11042@osu.edu

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Reduction Modeling and Computation for Multiscale Problems - Part II of III

talks.title: Microstructural Models for Coarsening Dynamics in Polycrystalline Materials
talks.abstract:
.
Polycrystalline materials consist of small grains separated by boundaries with different lattice orientations, and these structures strongly affect material properties. A method by which the grain structure can be engineered in polycrystalline materials is through grain growth (coarsening) of a starting structure. The evolution of the grain/grain boundary structure and associated grain growth is a very complex, multiscale, and multiphysics process. It involves the dynamics of grain boundaries, triple junctions in 2D (triple curves/lines and quadruple points in 3D), and the dynamics of lattice misorientations. So, grain growth can be regarded as the anisotropic evolution of a large cellular network and can be described by a set of deterministic local evolution laws for the growth of individual grains combined with stochastic models for the interaction between them.
In this talk, I will present recent perspectives on mathematical modeling, simulation and analysis of the evolution of the grain boundary network in polycrystalline microstructures. Relevant experiments and future research will also be discussed.
This work is collaborated with Katayun Barmak, Batuhan Bayir, William M Feldman, David Kinderlehrer, Chang (Kamala) Liu, Chun Liu, Masashi Mizuno, Thuong Nguyen, Jeffrey Rickman, and Matthew Patrick, and is supported by the DMREF program(DMS-2118172) and the Simons Foundation Fellowship Award SFI-MPS-SFM-00010667.
Authors
Yekaterina Epshteyn, University of Utah, U.S., epshteyn@math.utah.edu

talks.title: PDE Foundation Model: Generalization, Learning to Learn and More
talks.abstract:
.
This talk explores the path toward foundation models for scientific computing, focusing on learning solution operators for parametric partial differential equations (PDEs). The core of the presentation showcases our contributions to overcoming these limitations: (1) A multi-modal transformer framework (PROSE) that achieves zero-shot generalization to new PDE families by jointly processing input functions and symbolic equations; and (2) A meta-learning strategy (LeMON) that enables a single pre-trained neural operator to rapidly adapt to new physical systems with minimal data. Together, these advances bridge the gap between specialized, single-task solvers and a flexible, multi-operator foundation model capable of real-time simulation and design.
Authors
Jingmin Sun, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., jsun143@jhu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Model-free Method for Discovering Symmetry in Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
Symmetry in differential equations reveals invariances and offers a powerful means to reduce model complexity. Lie group analysis characterizes these symmetries through infinitesimal generators, which provide a local, linear criterion for invariance. However, identifying Lie symmetries directly from scattered data, without explicit knowledge of the governing equations, remains a significant challenge. In this talk, I will introduce a numerical scheme that approximates infinitesimal generators from data sampled on an unknown smooth manifold, enabling the recovery of continuous symmetries without requiring the analytical form of the differential equations. We employ a manifold learning technique, Generalized Moving Least Squares, to prolongate the data, from which a linear system is constructed whose null space encodes the infinitesimal generators representing the symmetries. Convergence bounds for the proposed approach are derived. Several numerical experiments, including ordinary and partial differential equations, demonstrate the method’s accuracy, robustness, and convergence, highlighting its potential for data-driven discovery of symmetries in dynamical systems.
Authors
Max Kreider, Pennsylvania State University, U.S., mbk6295@psu.edu

sessions.title: Machine Learning and UQ - 1

talks.title: Adversarial Calibration of Ipcw-Weighted Bayesian Neural Mixtures for Right-Censored Survival Prediction

speakers.name: Holighi Khalil Shafie
speakers.affiliation: University of Northern Colorado, Greeley, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Right-censored survival data are ubiquitous in clinical studies and pose two intertwined challenges: censoring bias–skewing risk estimates when events go unobserved–and model overconfidence–where deep survival networks underestimate uncertainty. To tackle both, we propose an adversarial calibration framework that (i) applies inverse-probability-of-censoring weights to correct partial-likelihood bias, (ii) replaces the standard DeepSurv output with a Bayesian mixture of parametric hazard components to capture parameter and structural uncertainty, and (iii) trains a generative “uncertainty critic” to distinguish true event-time samples from model-drawn ones and thereby correct overconfident predictions.
We evaluate on two large clinical cohorts (METABRIC breast cancer and SEER-Medicare lung cancer), comparing against standard DeepSurv+IPCW, DeepSurv+Bayesian mixtures (no adversary), and common post-hoc calibration methods (temperature scaling, isotonic regression). Our approach achieves up to a 15% reduction in integrated Brier score, calibration slopes closer to 1.0, and 2𔃁-point gains in concordance index—especially in low-incidence subgroups (<10% event rate). Ablation studies confirm the critic uncovers latent miscalibration patterns, yielding robust, uncertainty-aware risk estimates suitable for personalized decision support.
Authors
Sunday O. Aghamie, University of Northern Colorado, Greeley, U.S., aghamie@gmail.com
Holighi Khalil Shafie, University of Northern Colorado, Greeley, U.S., khalil.shafie@unco.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Latent Generative Modeling of Random Fields from Limited Data
talks.abstract:
.
The ability to accurately model random fields plays a critical role in uncertainty quantification (UQ) analyses involving uncertain functional quantities (e.g., heterogeneous material properties, turbulent flows). Deep generative models offer a powerful tool for sampling high- or infinite-dimensional uncertainties like random fields, but their reliance on large, complete training datasets limits their applicability in science and engineering contexts, where data are typically sparse or indirect.
In this work, we propose a novel framework for latent generative modeling of random fields that incorporates domain knowledge to supplement limited training data. A variational autoencoder (VAE) is first used to learn compressed, latent representations of functions that adhere to known physical or statistical constraints incorporated into the VAE loss function. Generative modeling is then performed in the latent space to yield higher quality random field samples, in particular for complex (e.g., multimodal, heavy-tailed) function distributions. Efficacy is demonstrated on two challenging applications: wind velocity field reconstruction from sparse sensors and material property inference from a limited number of indirect measurements. Results show the effectiveness of incorporating domain knowledge constraints for data-limited problems and the improved performance of the latent generative modeling approach versus directly sampling the pre-trained VAE as in existing work.
Authors
James Warner, NASA Langley Research Center, U.S., james.e.warner@nasa.gov

talks.title: Conformalized Ensemble of Neural Networks for Blast Wave Propagation

speakers.name: Vincent Souveton
speakers.affiliation: CEA, DAM, DIF, France; Pierre Sochala, ASNR (French Authority for Nuclear Safety

speakers.name: Radiation)
speakers.affiliation: France; Sébastien Terrana, CEA, DAM, DIF, France

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we propose the use of a neural network ensemble to enhance learning and obtain robust confidence intervals on a vector-to-vector regression problem. Relying on the predictive uncertainty decomposition provided by an ensemble of models, we improve training in sparse data regimes through a variance-based active learning strategy. In order to build and calibrate accurate predictive confidence intervals, we apply adaptive conformal prediction based on the ensemble’s mean and variance. Our approach is evaluated on a challenging nonlinear high-dimensional blast-wave propagation problem, where the goal is to predict the peak overpressure from multi-block input data. Numerical experiments show that a variance-based active learning strategy is particularly well-suited to this setting when the training set is small. By selecting the most informative examples to label, it improves training efficiency and achieves superior performance compared to direct training. Finally, our method produces calibrated intervals that are significantly narrower than those obtained by applying conformal prediction to a single neural network.
Authors
Vincent Souveton, CEA, DAM, DIF, France, vincent.souveton@cea.fr
Pierre Sochala, ASNR (French Authority for Nuclear Safety and Radiation), France, pierre.sochala@asnr.fr

talks.title: Generative Transport for Uncertainty Quantification from Statistical Observations

speakers.name: Anubhav Dwivedi
speakers.affiliation: University of Minnesota, U.S.; Gs Sidharth, Iowa State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In many chaotic and high-dimensional dynamical systems, the available data consists only of statistics such as mean, spectra, and higher order moments, providing indirect evidence of the underlying state. Because these statistics do not uniquely determine the underlying dynamics, reconstructing states is inherently uncertain and naturally calls for statistical data assimilation. We address this by introducing a statistic-conditioned generative transport for uncertainty quantification, which models the distribution of plausible short-horizon trajectories while enforcing consistency with observed statistics at every step. At inference, the transport is guided by observed statistics through a gradient signal estimated with Monte Carlo samples of the next-state bridge. This preserves the familiar predict–nudge loop but replaces pointwise likelihoods with statistic likelihoods, producing ensembles that remain consistent with observations and yield calibrated measures of predictive uncertainty without retraining. The method combines statistic-conditioned guidance with higher-order stochastic integration and is evaluated with standard calibration and distance metrics such as coverage, continuous ranked probability score (CRPS), and Wasserstein distance. We demonstrate the approach on Lorenz-63, Lorenz-96, and compressible boundary-layer turbulence, highlighting how different statistic choices affect both parameter/state identifiability and the reliability of uncertainty estimates.
Authors
Anubhav Dwivedi, University of Minnesota, U.S., anubhavd91@gmail.com
Gs Sidharth, Iowa State University, U.S., sidgs@iastate.edu

sessions.title: Operator Learning and UQ

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Deep Learning Framework for Parameters Estimations in Hydrodynamics
talks.abstract:
.
Solving parametric Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) is a very challenging task, especially in very high-dimensional cases. In recent years, the advancements in deep learning methods have led the scientific community gradually to more and more complex, sophisticated, and computationally expensive model architectures for capturing the input-output map. These data-driven methods employ architectures that are mostly inspired by successful image-to-image methods. Such models always fail in clearly out-of-distribution scenarios, because they contain no physics-related features in their architecture. We focus on the problem domain of high-dimensional random heterogeneous media, and we propose a lightweight probabilistic generative model that incorporates physics in its architecture in the form of a vectorized, auto-differentiable coarse-grained solver. We demonstrate, clearly, its superior performance against state-of-the-art models with orders of magnitude more parameters in challenging out-of-distribution forward and inverse problems with extremely sparse and noisy data.
Authors
Matthaios Chatzopoulos, Technische Universität München, Germany, matthaios.chatzopoulos@tum.de
Phaedon-Stelios Koutsourelakis, Technische Universität München, Germany, p.s.koutsourelakis@tum.de

talks.title: Generative Physics-Aware Neural Implicit Solvers for Multiscale, Parametric Pdes with Applications in Heterogeneous Media. GenPANIS
talks.abstract:
.
Solving parametric Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) is a very challenging task, especially in very high-dimensional cases. In recent years, the advancements in deep learning methods have led the scientific community gradually to more and more complex, sophisticated, and computationally expensive model architectures for capturing the input-output map. These data-driven methods employ architectures that are mostly inspired by successful image-to-image methods. Such models always fail in clearly out-of-distribution scenarios, because they contain no physics-related features in their architecture. We focus on the problem domain of high-dimensional random heterogeneous media, and we propose a lightweight probabilistic generative model that incorporates physics in its architecture in the form of a vectorized, auto-differentiable coarse-grained solver. We demonstrate, clearly, its superior performance against state-of-the-art models with orders of magnitude more parameters in challenging out-of-distribution forward and inverse problems with extremely sparse and noisy data.

talks.title: Dgenno: A Novel Physics-Aware Neural Operator for Solving Forward and Inverse PDE Problems Based on Deep, Generative Probabilistic Modeling
talks.abstract:
.
Reliable long-term forecasting of dynamical systems remains a key challenge for neural operators, which often degrade when extrapolating beyond their training horizon. Standard Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets) either disregard temporal causality through fixed-horizon rollouts or accumulate errors in autoregressive schemes, limiting robustness under uncertainty. We introduce TI-DeepONet, a physics-aware framework that integrates neural operators with adaptive numerical time-stepping. By reformulating the learning task as the approximation of instantaneous time-derivative fields, our method preserves the Markovian structure of dynamical systems, supports continuous-time prediction, and enables higher-order time integrators at inference—balancing efficiency and accuracy. We further propose TI(L)-DeepONet, which employs learnable coefficients in multi-stage integration schemes to adapt to system-specific variability and enhance predictive fidelity. Across five benchmark PDEs spanning chaotic, dissipative, dispersive, and high-dimensional dynamics, our approach achieves stable extrapolation errors extending to nearly twice the training interval: about 81% lower than autoregressive methods and 70% lower than fixed-horizon rollouts. This research establishes a physics-aware operator learning paradigm that bridges neural approximation with numerical analysis, preserving the causal structure while addressing a critical gap in long-term forecasting of complex physical systems.
Authors
Dibyajyoti Nayak, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., dnayak2@jh.edu
Somdatta Goswami, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., somdatta@jhu.edu

sessions.title: Machine Learning and UQ - 2

talks.title: Intelligent Feature Selection in Machine Learning: A Hybrid Optimization Framework
talks.abstract:
.
In high-dimensional datasets, feature selection is particularly important for enhancing the computational efficiency, interpretability, and accuracy of machine learning models. The present study delineates the design and development of a feature selection framework that is optimization-based and flexible. This framework incorporates metaheuristic algorithms, including Improved JAYA, PSO, and GA, to identify the most informative feature subsets. The framework is assessed using publicly accessible datasets that encompass behavioural sciences and health informatics. A hybrid objective function that promotes model generalisability is emphasized in our methodology, which balances classification accuracy with feature subset minimization. The proposed approach outperforms traditional filter and wrapper methods in terms of accuracy and reduction ratio when coupled with standard classifiers such as Support Vector Machines (SVM) and Random Forests (RF), as evidenced by experimental results. The proposed framework provides a modular and scalable solution that allows researchers and practitioners to integrate a variety of optimizers based on the nature of their data and problem domain. This work is a valuable contribution to the advancement of machine learning systems that are interpretable, resource-efficient, and high-performing. These systems have a wide range of applications, including medicine diagnosis, stress detection, and human behaviour modelling.
Authors
Ramandeep Kumar, Lovely Professional University, India, raman.pb777@gmail.com

talks.title: Characterizing Collisional Chemistry Uncertainty for Low Temperature Plasma Simulations with Bayesian Inference and Support Vector Machines

speakers.name: Robert Moser
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Numerically predicting the properties of low-temperature plasma devices, such as torches, relies on characterization of the reaction rates of the constituent plasma species, which depend on the values of electron-impact collision cross sections. These cross sections are epistemically uncertain in two ways. First, the data obtained for common species transitions through individual experiments and quantum calculations often systematically disagree with each other beyond their measurement errors. Second, most transitions involving higher-lying excited states have no experimental data. To produce reasonable plasma simulation input models to represent these chemistry uncertainties in forward propagation experiments, we use a Bayesian inference model to determine probabilistic models of cross sections for transitions with data, using knowledge of the quantum calculation of electron-argon collisions to enforce appropriate uncertainty bounds through the prior distribution. We then use support vector machines (SVM) to extrapolate these priors to transitions with no data. Using both chemistry-intrinsic properties like the transition threshold energy and extrinsic factors such as the total number of experiments as features, the SVM approach obtains distributions for cross sections of higher-lying state transitions. Given the low amount of data used to train the SVM, we validate these distributions within uncertainty bounds known through domain-specific knowledge.
Authors
Garo Bedonian, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., garo.bedonian@gmail.com
Todd Oliver, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., oliver@oden.utexas.edu
Robert Moser, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., rmoser@oden.utexas.edu

sessions.title: Gaussian Processes

talks.title: Modular Jump Gaussian Processes

speakers.name: Christopher Franck
speakers.affiliation: Virginia Tech, U.S.; Mickaël Binois, Inria d'Université Côte d'Azur, France; Chiwoo Park, University of Washington, U.S.; Robert Gramacy, Virginia Tech, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian processes (GPs) furnish accurate nonlinear predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty. However, the typical GP setup has a built-in stationarity assumption, making it ill-suited for modeling data from processes with sudden changes, or "jumps" in the output variable. The "jump GP" (JGP) was developed for modeling data from such processes, combining local GPs and latent "level" variables under a joint inferential framework.  But joint modeling can be fraught with difficulty. We aim to simplify by suggesting a more modular setup, eschewing joint inference but retaining the main JGP themes: (a) learning optimal neighborhood sizes that locally respect manifolds of discontinuity; and (b) a new cluster-based (latent) feature to capture regions of distinct output levels on both sides of the manifold. We show that each of (a) and (b) separately leads to dramatic improvements when modeling processes with jumps. In tandem (but without requiring joint inference) that benefit is compounded, as illustrated on real and synthetic benchmark examples from the recent literature.
Authors
Anna Flowers, Virginia Tech, U.S., arflowers@vt.edu
Christopher Franck, Virginia Tech, U.S., chfranck@vt.edu
Mickaël Binois, Inria d'Université Côte d'Azur, France, mickael.binois@inria.fr

Robert Gramacy, Virginia Tech, U.S., rbg@vt.edu

talks.title: Bayesian Vecchia Gassian Process Tree Model for High-Dimensional Data
talks.abstract:
.
In many real-world applications, Gaussian process (GP) has been widely used for nonparametric regression due to its ability of spatial smoothing via covariance modeling; however, GP suffers from computational challenges and is not well-suited for modeling heterogeneous structures in the data. To address this issue, we develop a Bayesian treed Vecchia GP model by exploiting the ideas in Vecchia’s approximation and the treed GP model. The proposed model can not only allow efficient computation for large datasets but also allow spatial nonstationarity modeling. To enable fast posterior inference, we also design a sequential Monte Carlo algorithm to generate posterior samples from the tree efficiently, thus avoiding slow-mixing issues of using reversible-jump Markov chain Monte Carlo algorithms. We demonstrate its superior performance in both simulation examples and a real-world application.
Authors
Cong Ye, Iowa State University, U.S., congye@iastate.edu
Pulong Ma, Iowa State University, U.S., plma@iastate.edu

sessions.title: Surrogate Models

talks.title: Vector-Valued Surrogate Models Combining Linear Dimension Reduction and Neural Networks
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we introduce a framework to solve complex multi-block vector-to-vector regression problems. The proposed approach combines supervised or unsupervised linear dimension reduction with nonlinear mapping in latent space using neural networks. A subspace error metric is employed to determine the optimal reduced space dimensionality given the training set size. To learn the mapping in the reduced space, we propose two novel statistically-informed strategies: a variance-informed method that trains a single neural network, and a dependency-informed method that constructs a sequence of neural networks tailored to each output coordinate. Our methods are applied to a blast wave propagation problem which consists in predicting the maximum overpressure with two sources of uncertainty, namely the height of burst and the elevation field both scaled by the explosion yield. Numerical experiments across various training set sizes and reduced space dimensionalities demonstrate the accuracy and robustness of the proposed methods. Compared to high-dimensional mapping with a large neural network, our approach achieves superior performance with significantly reduced computational cost. Furthermore, the resulting surrogate model enables efficient statistical analysis of the predictions, including conditional probability distributions and global sensitivity fields.
Authors
Pierre Sochala, ASNR (French Authority for Nuclear Safety and Radiation), France, pierre.sochala@asnr.fr
Vincent Souveton, CEA, DAM, DIF, France, vincent.souveton@cea.fr
Sébastien Terrana, CEA, DAM, DIF, France, sebastien.terrana@cea.fr

talks.title: Surrogate-Aided Bayesian Calibration with Adaptive Learning Strategies

speakers.name: Alexandros A. Taflanidis
speakers.affiliation: University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Bangalore Satish Aakash, University of California, Berkeley, U.S.; Sang-ri Yi, Rice University, U.S.; Joel Conte, University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This work investigates an adaptive surrogate model implementation for accelerating Bayesian inference of computationally expensive engineering models.  Gaussian Process (GP) regression is adopted as surrogate modelling technique, while the formulation is embedded within a sequential Monte Carlo approach. The adaptive training of the GP is established by iteratively adding training points to inform the target posterior density approximation. At each iteration, a GP model is constructed using the current set of training points to approximate the system response and, consequently, the posterior distribution. This approximation is then used to facilitate sampling from the posterior. The iterative procedure terminates once the posterior density approximations from successive iterations exhibit sufficient similarity. Convergence is assessed using the similarity of the approximated posterior densities. If convergence is not established, the surrogate model is refined through an adaptive DoE that uses the weighted integrated mean squared error as acquisition function. Through proper selection of the weights, exploitation and exploration strategies are promoted to improve, respectively, efficiency and robustness for the GP approximation. Strategies are examined for improving computational efficiency for the DoE optimization and for the sequential Monte Carlo sampling once the surrogate model has been updated.

Alexandros A. Taflanidis, University of Notre Dame, U.S., a.taflanidis@nd.edu

talks.title: Tensor Decompositions for Interpretable Low-Rank Representation of Atmospheric Data

speakers.name: Riti Bahl
speakers.affiliation: Emory University, U.S.; Luis Damiano, Bert J. Debusschere, Khachik Sargsyan

talks.abstract:
.
The Quasi-Biennial Oscillation (QBO) is a quasi periodic wind pattern in the equatorial stratosphere that plays an important role in large-scale variability of the atmosphere. Its structure is high-dimensional, involving dependencies across latitude, longitude, time, and pressure levels. Accurately modeling patterns from such data is challenging due to its scale and complexity. In this work, we investigate tensor decompositions, specifically the Canonical Polyadic Decomposition, as a framework for revealing interpretable low-rank structure in QBO datasets. These methods provide a systematic way to separate modes of variability, quantify contributions across different dimensions, and highlight underlying dynamical processes. We discuss how tensor-based approaches can serve as an effective tool for calibration and uncertainty quantification by enabling reduced-order representations that capture essential variability.

Riti Bahl, Emory University, U.S., riti.bahl@emory.edu

talks.title: A Global–Local Multiscale Markov Chain Monte Carlo Method for Domain-Decomposed Bayesian Inference

speakers.name: Felipe Pereira
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Dallas, U.S.; Marcio Borges, LNCC/MCTIC, Brazil

talks.abstract:
.
Uncertainty quantification in PDE-constrained inverse problems is vital to engineering and physical science applications. Yet Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) remains computationally demanding due to high dimensionality and slow multi-chain convergence. The multiscale sampling method (MSM), inspired by domain decomposition for PDEs, accelerates MCMC by exploiting spatial structure. We introduce a global–local MSM that tackles the curse of dimensionality at two levels: the fine-grid problem is first approximated by a truncated Karhunen–Loève expansion (KLE); then the global KLE is reduced via local, low-dimensional KLEs over non-overlapping subdomains. This yields faster convergence, typical of lower-dimensional systems. Each iteration combines one global update with parallel local updates, creating a multi-chain scheme well suited to modern HPC. To assess cost–accuracy trade-offs, we study the gap between the true posterior and approximate posteriors from the new approach. Results show improved convergence and scalable uncertainty quantification for PDE models.
Authors
Aidan R. Gettemy, University of Texas at Dallas, U.S., arg210006@utdallas.edu
Felipe Pereira, University of Texas at Dallas, U.S., felipepereira62@gmail.com
Marcio Borges, LNCC/MCTIC, Brazil, marcio.rentes.borges@gmail.com

sessions.title: Mixed Topics - I

talks.title: Latent Score-Based Diffusion Model for Data Assimilation in Subsurface Flow
talks.abstract:
.
Data assimilation, using multimodal monitoring data, plays a critical role in forecasting and managing large-scale subsurface flow systems. Traditional inversion methods for inferring geological parameters (e.g., permeability) and predicting state variables (e.g., pressure, saturation) often face challenges due to the high-dimensional, nonlinear nature of the problem and the computational cost of ensemble simulations. In this work, we develop a novel data assimilation framework using a latent score-based diffusion model for joint inference of model parameters and state variables. Autoencoders are used to transform both geological models and dynamic state fields into low-dimensional latent space. A score-based diffusion model is then applied in the latent space as a conditional sampler for the joint posterior distribution, enabling efficient assimilation of multi-source observations without retraining the score estimator. We demonstrate this framework on a synthetic 3D heterogeneous geological carbon storage model, incorporating both in-situ monitoring well data and interpreted 4D seismic observations. The posterior results show that the method significantly reduces uncertainty in model parameters and predictions of pressure and saturation. We also evaluate the impact of observation type on posterior uncertainty, presenting the potential of this approach for integrating multi-source observations and real-time monitoring planning in subsurface applications.

Su Jiang, Carnegie Mellon University, U.S., sujiang@andrew.cmu.edu

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Forecasting Effective Motion Models
talks.abstract:
.
A central problem in the study of stochastic dynamical systems is forecasting effective motions when the underlying microscopic dynamics are both high-dimensional and computationally expensive.   A classic example is molecular dynamics, which generates realistic models of molecular systems but requires simulating the full motion of all atoms, with dimensionality proportional to the number of tracked atoms and often prohibitive cost.   Yet, meaningful analysis is usually performed in reduced representations such as principal components or reaction coordinates. We investigate probabilistic time-series forecasting (TSF) methods as a route to obtain reduced dynamical models for such systems.   Specifically, probabilistic TSF yields not only point predictions but also full predictive distributions, thereby quantifying uncertainty in the forecast. The resulting distributions approximate effective dynamical models in lower-dimensional coordinates while capturing both deterministic trends and stochasticity.   In this talk, I will investigate the application of deep learning tools for probabilistic TSF in molecular dynamics and show how these methods can guide analysis and enable practical applications. By emphasizing predictive distributions, the formulation ensures consistency with the variability structure of the original dynamics and points toward new strategies for efficient analysis of complex stochastic systems.
Authors
Pedro Pessoa, Arizona State University, U.S., ppessoa@asu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Flow Matching for Probabilistic Learning of Dynamical Systems from Missing Or Noisy Data
talks.abstract:
.
Learning dynamical systems is crucial across many fields, yet applying machine learning techniques remains challenging due to missing variables and noisy data. Classical mathematical models often struggle in these scenarios due to the arose ill-posedness of the physical systems. Stochastic machine learning techniques address this challenge by enabling the modeling of such ill-posed problems. Thus, a single known input to the trained machine learning model may yield multiple plausible outputs, and all of the outputs are correct. In such scenarios, probabilistic forecasting is inherently meaningful. In this study, we introduce a variant of flow matching for probabilistic forecasting which estimates possible future states as a distribution over possible outcomes rather than a single-point prediction.  Perturbation of complex dynamical states is not trivial. Community uses typical Gaussian or uniform perturbations to crucial variables to model uncertainty. However, not all variables behave in a Gaussian fashion. So, we also propose a generative machine learning approach to physically and logically perturb the states of complex high-dimensional dynamical systems. Finally, we establish the mathematical foundations of our method and demonstrate its effectiveness on several challenging dynamical systems, including a variant of the high-dimensional WeatherBench dataset, which models the global weather at a 5.625° meridional resolution.

talks.title: Movement-Derived Contact Networks and Latent Embeddings for Wildlife Health Modeling and Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Md Mahedi Hasan
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Daryl DeFord, Vassar College, U.S.; Ian McGahan

speakers.name: Juan Francisco Reyes
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Daniel Storm, Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources, U.S.; JUN Zhu, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Daniel Walsh, University of Montana, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Latent embeddings map individual animals, represented as nodes of a graph, to unique vectors containing rich contextual information (e.g., behaviors). These vectors enable us to model the probability of a connection between two nodes as a function of their latent positions in time. In this presentation, we apply a random dot product graph (RDPG) framework to estimate latent positions by exploiting the graph's spectral structure using adjacency spectral embedding. For spatio-temporal data, we extend this idea to dynamic RDPG and temporal graph neural networks to learn time-varying interaction embeddings from evolving networks.      In a case study of chronic wasting disease, pathogen transmission occurs through direct and indirect environmental exposure. The challenges from uncertain population sizes, spatial dependence, and changing landscapes complicate the statistical inference of animal movement and contact rates. Using GPS collar data consisting of recorded animal locations at specified intervals in time, we compute the trajectory embeddings and fuse them with interaction embeddings for predicting when, where, and which animals will interact. These embeddings allow us to quantify indirect exposure via visits within a threshold distance to contaminated sites and are critical for downstream tasks such as estimating pairwise contact probabilities and node-level contact rates, while explicitly accommodating spatial structures and environmental dynamics.

sessions.title: Score-Based Methods

talks.title: Model Error Covariance Estimation for Weak Constraint Variational Data Assimilation
talks.abstract:
.
The accuracy of state estimates from weak-constraint four-dimensional variational (4D-Var) data assimilation depend strongly on data and model error covariances. While data error is assumed known, we focus on estimating the model error covariance by framing weak-constraint 4D-Var as a regularized inverse problem, where the inverse model error covariance acts as the regularization matrix. We examine isotropic and non-isotropic forms with hyperparameters including variance, spatial correlation length, and temporal correlation scale. Using the representer method, we reduce the problem from state space to data space, enabling efficient application of regularization parameter selection techniques to estimate covariance hyperparameters. The representer approach also provides analytic expressions for the state estimate, allowing matrix forms for the L-curve, generalized cross-validation (GCV), and Chi-square methods. We validate the approach by assimilating simulated data into a 1D transport equation for wildfire smoke transport under varying noise and model perturbations. Results show the methods successfully estimate hyperparameters for both isotropic and non-isotropic covariances, indicating isotropic forms suffice when the first guess is more accurate than the data, while non-isotropic covariances are preferred when data is more reliable.

talks.title: Multiple Data Assimilation As An Approximate Maximum A Posteriori Estimator

speakers.name: Renato Zanetti
speakers.affiliation: University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Andrey A. Popov, University of Hawaii at Manoa, U.S.; Kristen Michealson, University of Texas at Austin, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This work reinterprets Multiple Data Assimilation (MDA), a recursive update widely used in ensemble Kalman filtering and smoothing, through the lens of maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation. For linear-Gaussian systems, MAP and minimum mean-square error estimates coincide, but for nonlinear observation models they diverge. Although MDA has been empirically successful, its theoretical connection to MAP has remained unclear. We show that MDA can be derived as a homotopy-based numerical approximation to an ordinary differential equation (ODE) that transports the prior mean to the MAP estimate. This perspective reveals MDA not as an optimization algorithm, but as a first-order ODE solver. Building on this framework, we propose a Runge–Kutta inspired adaptive-step MDA algorithm with error control. Numerical experiments, including assimilation in the chaotic Lorenz ’96 system with nonlinear observations, demonstrate that the new adaptive approach achieves improved accuracy and efficiency compared to standard MDA variants. While MDA is not an exact MAP estimator—since it omits higher-order terms—it nonetheless provides a practical and effective approximation when observation models exhibit sufficient local linearity. This homotopy formulation clarifies MDA’s theoretical foundation and motivates extensions toward more accurate and robust ensemble-based filtering methods.
Authors

talks.title: Error Analysis of the Projected Po Method with Additive Inflation for the Partially Observed Lorenz 96 Model
talks.abstract:
.
We consider the filtering problem with the partially observed Lorenz 96 model. Although the accuracy of the 3DVar filter applied to this problem has been established, that of the EnKF has not yet been. This study aims to establish the error bound of a variant of the EnKF, known as the PO method. By introducing the additive inflation and a projection of the background covariance to the observation space, we establish the error bound of the PO method. A numerical example validates theoretical findings and shows the potential to extend the analysis. We also discuss the relationships between the analyses of the EnKF and those of other data assimilation algorithms.

talks.title: A Nitsche Approach for Coupled Multiphysics Problems

speakers.name: Aparna Bansal
speakers.affiliation: Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, India; Nicolas A. Barnafi, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Chile; Dwijendra Narain Pandey, Indian Institute of Technology Roorkee, India; Ricardo Ruiz-Baier, Monash University, Australia

talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we present a mathematical and numerical model for the interaction between free fluid flow and a poroelastic medium. The Brinkmann equation is used to describe the flow within the porous domain, allowing for the inclusion of inertial effects. A thermodynamically consistent framework is proposed for modeling soft tissue perfusion. To approximate the solution, we develop a mixed finite element method based on Nitsche’s approach and establish the well-posedness of the discrete formulation. Furthermore, we derive a priori error estimates in the energy norm. Numerical experiments are provided to confirm the theoretical convergence rates and to demonstrate the method’s capability in accurately capturing the underlying physical behavior.

talks.title: A High-Dimensional Density Estimation Method and Its Application for Solving PDEs
talks.abstract:
.
Probability density estimation remains an open challenging problem in computational science and engineering. By coupling the Knothe-Rosenblatt (KR) rearrangement and the flow-based generative model, we developed an invertible transport map, called KRnet, for high-dimensional density estimation. In this talk, we give an overview of KRnet and discuss its adaptive version for the Fokker-Planck equations and stochastic dynamic systems. KRnet for solving Bayesian inverse problems is also studied. This is joint work with Yani Feng, Junjie He, Kejun Tang and Xiaoliang Wan.
Authors
Qifeng Liao, ShanghaiTech University, China, liaoqf@shanghaitech.edu.cn

talks.title: Preconditioned Crank–Nicolson Differential Evolution Markov Chain Monte Carlo Method Applied to Stochastic Two-Phase Flow Problem in Porous Media

speakers.name: Marcio Borges
speakers.affiliation: LNCC/MCTIC, Brazil; Felipe Pereira, University of Texas at Dallas, U.S.; Michel Tosin, LNCC/MCT, Brazil

talks.abstract:
.
The shape and size of the proposal distribution are known to be very crucial for the convergence of the Markov chains, mainly in porous media fluid flows, where the stochastic dimension can be high. In this work, we use the new Preconditioned Crank–Nicolson Differential Evolution Markov Chain Method (pCN-DE) in a two-phase flow problem. The pCN-DE method is a population-based approach that combines the classical Preconditioned Crank–Nicolson (pCN) and Differential Evolution (DE) Metropolis algorithms, allowing for the exchange of information between the parallel chains and providing information about the scale and orientation of the jumping distribution with fewer limitations with respect to the number of parallel chains needed. On the other hand, the pCN contribution guarantees the invariance of the proposals. The new method yielded significantly better results compared to the classical pCN.

talks.title: Implicit Time Integration of High-Dimensional Tensor Differential Equations on Tensor Train Manifolds Based on a Cross Algorithm
talks.abstract:
.
This work presents an algorithm for the implicit time integration of high-dimensional tensor differential equations (TDEs) on tensor train manifolds. In addition to time-dependent problems, the framework also applies to linear tensor equations. The method offers several advantages: (i) it achieves substantial savings in memory and floating-point operations by employing a cross-approximation strategy based on CUR decomposition combined with the discrete empirical interpolation method, (ii) it handles TDEs with general nonlinear terms, regardless of their specific form, and (iii) it incorporates rank adaptivity, enabling the tensor rank to adjust automatically to meet a desired accuracy. The algorithm’s performance is demonstrated through a range of numerical examples.

Behzad Ghahremani, University of Pittsburgh, U.S., beg66@pitt.edu

sessions.title: UQ for PDEs - I

talks.title: Exact Uncertainty Propagation Through Solvers for Linear Elliptic Partial Differential Equations
talks.abstract:
.
We present a novel framework for deriving push-forward measures for stationary, multigrid, and domain-decomposed solvers for linear elliptic partial differential equations of the form $$\nabla_x \cdot (a(x) \nabla_x u(x)) = f(x,\omega),$$ where $f$ is a random field. By exploiting the linearity of these solvers and the closure of Gaussian random variables under linear transforms, our method efficiently propagates uncertainties. We relax Gaussianity assumptions using the denseness of Gaussian mixture models in the space of continuous probability measures, allowing arbitrarily accurate approximations of complex distributions via variational inference. Benchmarks validate our approach, comparing uncertainty propagation in diverse linear systems against Monte Carlo simulations. Our framework extends beyond elliptic boundary value problems, enabling robust and precise uncertainty quantification for a wide range of linear algebraic systems. These results underscore its potential for impactful applications in computational science and engineering.
Authors
Rahul Manavalan, Lund University, Sweden, rahul.manavalan@matstat.lu.se

talks.title: Bayesian Calibration with Functional Outputs Using Elastic Partial Matching

speakers.name: Paul Casteras
speakers.affiliation: CEA, DAM, DIF, France; Josselin Garnier, Ecole Polytechnique, France; Julien Bect, CNRS, CentraleSupelec, Université Paris-Saclay, France; Gwenael Salin, CEA, DAM, DIF, France

talks.abstract:
.
Calibrating a simulation model involves estimating model parameters by comparing its outputs with experiences to ensure that simulation results accurately reflect these experiences. However, when outputs are functions of time, there are multiple ways to quantify the difference between experimental and simulated outputs. It has recently been proposed to use elastic functional data analysis, which makes it possible to decompose a functional output into two new functions: a function temporally aligned to a template and the corresponding warping function. This approach splits the problem into two independent calibration tasks to address functional misalignment. The first task is based on the comparison of the aligned functions and the second one is based on the comparison of the warping functions. In this work, we extend this framework with elastic partial alignment and with the use of Gaussian process regression. We apply the method to a test case and compare it to a Bayesian calibration method that does not use functional alignment.

talks.title: Information Geometry of Uncertainty Quantification for Downstream Decision Making: Resource Theory Perspective
talks.abstract:
.
Consider Decision Maker (DM) attempting to maximize utility $u(x,y)$ over decision $x$, where random variable y represents uncertain State of Environment (SoE). Bayesian DM maximizes the expected utility assuming known probability distribution $Q_(y)$. Robust Bayesian DM assumes that distribution of y, Q(y) lies within certain Ambiguity Set (AS) P and maximizes the expected utility against the worst-case scenario distribution Q within this set. If DM is capable of decision randomization, this optimization defines Phi-equilibria in zero-sum game between DM and Adversarial Environment (AE) where P represents the AE's resources. We propose Resource Theory based Uncertainty Quantification with respect to the ability of the AE's resources to cause loss in the expected DM utility by manipulating AS P. Given prior $Q_(y)$, we consider three types of AE's uncertainty: Agnostic, Distributional, and Entanglement, with ASs P(H), P(H,D), and P(H,D,I) respectively. P(H) is comprised of distributions $Q(y)~exp[(lnQ_(y))/T], T > 0$ with entropy not exceeding H. P(H,D), allowing AE to benefit from knowledge of the DM utility, is comprised of distributions Q with entropy not exceeding H and Kullback-Leibler deviation from P(H) not exceeding D. Finally, P(H,D,I), allowing AE's  strategies correlated with DM’s decisions, is comprised of conditional distributions $Q(y \vert x)$ for which mutual information between $y$ and $x$ does not exceed I and joint distribution Q(x,y) satisfies the above constraints.
Authors
Vladimir Marbukh, National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S., marbukh@nist.gov

sessions.title: UQ for PDEs - II

talks.title: A Cur Krylov Solver for Large-Scale Generalized Sylvester and Non-Sylvester Linear Matrix Equations

speakers.name: Hessam Babaee
speakers.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh, U.S.; Damiano Lombardi, Inria Paris-Rocquencourt, France

talks.abstract:
.
This paper introduces a novel methodology leveraging CUR decomposition for solving large-scale generalized Sylvester as well as non-Sylvester equations on low-rank matrix manifolds. The approach decomposes the original equations into two smaller subproblems, each characterized by substantially reduced coefficient matrices. This decomposition facilitates the application of existing efficient solvers specifically designed for the structures where the unknown matrix is large from one side and small from the other side. By selectively solving the equation at strategically chosen rows and columns instead of the full matrix, the proposed method significantly reduces computational overhead. Additionally, the method incorporates rank adaptivity, dynamically adjusting computational rank to reach the desired accuracy.  A Krylov-based scheme is presented to solve the resulting subproblems, which enables solving large-scale problems and does not require Sylvester structure. The methodology is demonstrated in three settings: (i) implicit time integration of matrix differential equations on low-rank manifolds, (ii) steady-state Lyapunov equations, and (iii) non-Sylvester linear matrix equations, such as those arising in nonlinear matrix problems.

talks.title: Likelihood-Tempering Sequential Monte Carlo Method and Its Applications to Chemical Engineering Problems
talks.abstract:
.
In this study, we propose and demonstrate a sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) method incorporating likelihood tempering for non–time-series data, which accelerates uncertainty quantification when the model is expensive to evaluate. Unlike Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods that rely on sequential steps, or conventional SMC methods where time-series measurements are assimilated sequentially, the proposed likelihood-tempered SMC modifies the likelihood function sequentially and approximates it using scattered particles. The algorithm can be parallelized, as the computation of particles can be distributed across multiple processors.
The advantages of the likelihood-tempered SMC are demonstrated with multiple examples. Its performance over conventional MCMC methods is quantified in several benchmark problems. In addition, the approach is applied to a real chemical engineering problem, where parameters in a complex differential equation model are estimated and their uncertainties quantified. Furthermore, computational time is significantly reduced through parallel implementation.
Authors
Tatsuki Maruchi, Nagoya University, Japan, maruchi.tatsuki.t7@s.mail.nagoya-u.ac.jp
Tomoyuki Yajima, Nagoya University, Japan, yajima.tomoyuki@material.nagoya-u.ac.jp
Yoshiaki Kawajiri, Nagoya University, Japan, kawajiri@nagoya-u.jp

talks.title: Efficient and Scalable Method for Sparse Rayleigh Quotient Maximization
talks.abstract:
.
Sparse Rayleigh Quotient Maximization (sparse RQM) is a cardinality-constrained optimization problem that arises in many contexts, including machine learning, statistical analysis, dimensionality reduction, and dynamical systems analysis.  The cardinality constraint in sparse RQM turns the standard RQM into an NP-hard problem, requiring a combinatorial search to find globally optimal solutions.  Here, we introduce an efficient iterative method that - in contrast with L1-regularization - provides both a priori guarantees on the degree of solution sparsity and performance bounds on the optimization objective, computing (sub)optimal solutions to the sparse RQM problem.  We use several benchmark problems to demonstrate the method's cubic convergence and scalability to problem-instances with thousands of decision variables.  We then apply our method in the context of dynamical systems analysis and reduced-order modeling of a fluid flow, wherein our approach successfully identifies sparse and spatially localized instability mechanisms that drive energy amplification in a compressible turbulent boundary layer.
Authors
Jonathan Smith, University of Minnesota, U.S., smi01444@umn.edu

talks.title: Nomad for Stochastic Simulator and One Shot Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Akshay Thakur
speakers.affiliation: University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Souvik Chakraborty, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, India

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a neural operator framework, termed mixture density nonlinear manifold decoder (MD-NOMAD), for emulating the conditional response distribution of stochastic simulators. Our framework leverages an amalgamation of the pointwise operator learning neural architecture, nonlinear manifold decoder (NOMAD), with versatile mixture density-based methods for approximating the conditional probability distributions of stochastic output functions. Furthermore, MD-NOMAD harnesses the ability of probabilistic mixture models to estimate complex probability distributions and inherits the high-dimensional scalability of NOMAD. A key feature of MD-NOMAD is its ability to perform one-shot uncertainty propagation, eliminating the need for Monte Carlo methods in statistical computations for uncertainty propagation tasks.  We conduct empirical assessments on a wide array of stochastic ordinary and partial differential equations and present the corresponding results, which highlight the performance of the proposed framework.
Authors
Akshay Thakur, University of Notre Dame, U.S., athakur3@nd.edu
Souvik Chakraborty, Indian Institute of Technology, Delhi, India, souvik@am.iitd.ac.in

sessions.title: Mixed Topics - II

talks.title: Rényi Extropy for Uncertainty Quantification: Theory and Applications

speakers.name: Luckshay Batra
speakers.affiliation: BML Munjal University Gurgaon, India; Ruchika Lochab, International Management Institute, India

talks.abstract:
.
This paper revisits the framework of Rényi extropy and extends its mathematical properties for uncertainty quantification. New results include its convergence to information extropy at limiting values and its ability to assume both positive and negative measures, providing deeper insight into uncertainty. We also unify Rényi extropy with information and Tsallis extropies. To illustrate practical relevance, we apply Rényi extropy to cryptocurrency markets, showing its advantage in capturing non-Gaussian dynamics and quantifying risk more effectively than traditional entropy-based measures. Further, we integrate Rényi extropy with machine learning models for price prediction, highlighting its role as a versatile tool for uncertainty quantification in complex systems.

talks.title: Comparative Analysis of Algorithmic Performance and Bias Metrics for Bias Detection: A Pre and Post-Fairness Intervention Study
talks.abstract:
.
Fairness in machine learning systems is a major concern in sensitive AI domains. Biased outcomes generally emanate from imbalanced data that affect protected groups. This study assesses two approaches in detecting bias, algorithm performance and  fairness specific bias metrics to determine the most effective approach in detecting and quantifying bias, and to assess the bias mitigation strategy. The proposed bias mitigation strategy targets the root causes of data imbalance that mainly contribute to such disparate outcomes. Quantifying bias involves the use of detection metrics like Disparate Impact, Demographic Parity, and Equal Opportunity Difference, and also involving algorithmic performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. This allows for a comparison across different algorithmic architectures with respect to fairness. Results showed that 1) the fairness intervention reduced bias, as shown by a significant decrease in bias metrics, 2) the gained fairness had no cost on algorithm performance, and 3) the comparative analysis showed that bias metrics are more sensitive to disparities detection, while algorithmic performance provides insights into the overall reliability of the models. We may conclude that this work stresses the importance of including both fairness-specific and traditional performance metrics in bias detection toward a comprehensive assessment of the effect of fairness interventions
Authors
Muna Abdelrahim, Jackson State University, U.S., muna.abdelrahim@students.jsums.edu

talks.title: Ellipsoidal State Estimation for Quadratic Systems with Unknown But Bounded Noise
talks.abstract:
.
This talk addresses state estimation for quadratic dynamical systems with linear measurements in the presence of unknown but bounded process disturbances and sensor noise. We use Lyapunov theory and Petersen’s lemma from the robust controls literature to establish convex semi-definite programming conditions that yield an estimator with guaranteed ellipsoidal error bounds. In contrast to Kalman and particle filtering approaches, the ellipsoidal viewpoint delivers worst-case, certificate-backed assurances without relying on noise distributions, making it suitable when distributions are unavailable or unreliable. We demonstrate our approach on a series of examples, including inertial navigation, aerodynamic flow reconstruction, and joint state-parameter estimation.
Authors
Amir Enayati Kafshgarkolaei, University of Minnesota, U.S., Enaya004@umn.edu
Maziar S. Hemati, University of Minnesota, U.S., mhemati@umn.edu
