sessions.title: Optimal Experimental Design - I

talks.title: Maximum Entropy Sequential Design for Bayesian Hierarchical Gaussian Processes: Application to Building Physicochemical Surrogate Models
talks.abstract:
.
We present an information-theoretic sampling framework for surrogate modeling in hybrid settings, where known physics combines with data-driven inference of a latent function. We place a Gaussian process (GP) prior over the latent function and place priors on the GP hyperparameters to encode structural information from practitioner knowledge. To make inference and acquisition tractable, we approximate the GP predictive posterior with a finite, uniformly weighted mixture of GPs.
Our main contribution is the derivation of entropy-based acquisition functions for the mixture representation. We provide a closed-form expression for the differential entropy of the approximate posterior and establish a lower bound using a Taylor expansion around the mean function of each GP component. This contribution yields a principled criterion for selecting input locations that maximize expected information gain.
Numerical experiments show that entropy-guided sampling improves sample efficiency by concentrating queries in regions of high predictive uncertainty and information content. The resulting surrogates converge more quickly, achieve higher predictive accuracy, and remain consistent with the structural priors encoded in the GP hierarchy. More broadly, the framework demonstrates how entropy approximations for Gaussian mixture posteriors enable uncertainty-aware acquisition in Bayesian nonparametric modeling.
Authors
Kyla D. Jones, University of Notre Dame, U.S., kjones29@nd.edu
Alexander Dowling, University of Notre Dame, U.S., adowling@nd.edu

talks.title: Practical Aspects of the Virtual Noise Convex Optimum Design Approach for Correlated Responses

speakers.name: Werner Müller
speakers.affiliation: Johannes Kepler Universität, Linz, Austria; Andrej Pázman, Comenius University, Bratislava, Slovakia

talks.abstract:
.
We present several practically-oriented extensions and considerations for the virtual noise method in optimal design under correlation. First we introduce a slightly modified virtual noise representation which further illuminates the parallels to the classical design approach for uncorrelated observations. We suggest more efficient algorithms to obtain the design  measures. Furthermore, we show that various convex relaxation methods used for sensor selection are special cases of our approach and can be solved within our framework. Finally, we provide practical guidelines on how to generally approach a design problem with correlated observations and demonstrate how to utilize the virtual noise method in this context in a meaningful way.

talks.title: Pac-Bayes Certificates for Bayesian Inverse Problems: A Case Study on the Heat Equation
talks.abstract:
.
Although Bayesian inverse problems for partial differential equations (PDEs) yield posterior distributions and credible intervals, these can be overconfident in sparse or noisy sensing regimes. We use the one-dimensional heat equation as a test case to introduce PAC-Bayes certificates into the Bayesian inversion framework. Our approach connects tempered (Gibbs) posteriors with PAC-Bayes theory to obtain finite-sample, distribution-free guarantees, extended with a mesh-robust decomposition that separates discretization from generalization.
The reliability of PAC-Bayes certificates in detecting overfitting is shown by experiments with varying sensor counts, noise levels, and discretizations. Gaps between the certified bound $B_\lambda$ and the empirical risk $\widehat{L}$ identify high-complexity cases ($m=5$, small $n$) and weak regularization (e.g., $\lambda=0.5$). Interpretability is improved by the discretization penalty $\eta_h$, which isolates solver error.
A grid of 1,728 experiments was completed in four days on a standard laptop, with each run taking 3-5 minutes, showing computational feasibility. Adaptive MCMC halved sampling while maintaining effective sample size, and certificate computation added under 5% overhead. The method scales with problem size and sensor count, extending beyond the 1D case. PAC-Bayes certification advances reliable, reproducible uncertainty quantification while adding a conservative, auditable layer at negligible cost.
Authors
Tanisha Gupta, University of Delhi, India, tanisha.gupta@allpay.net
Ritu Arora, University of Delhi, India, ritu@jdm.du.ac.in

sessions.title: Optimal Experimental Design - II

talks.title: Bayesian Optimal Experimental Prediction Design for Physical Experiments Informed by Computer Experiments
talks.abstract:
.
We consider Bayesian optimal experimental design for prediction at specified design configurations of physical systems. A computer model describing the system is assumed to be available, providing predictions of the output of interest as a function of the design variables and calibration parameters. Since the computer model does not perfectly represent the system, model discrepancy must be taken into account. Moreover, the calibration parameters that best align the model with reality are unknown. To address these uncertainties, we adopt the framework of Kennedy and O’Hagan (2001), which models both computer experiments and model discrepancy using Gaussian processes.
As a design criterion, we use the mutual information between observations at design points and predictions at target points, thereby incorporating parameter uncertainty. We further introduce a new criterion that combines mutual information with a complexity penalty, guiding the choice of design points and reducing oversampling in regions with high predictive uncertainty. We compare this approach with other criteria such as integrated mean squared prediction error and designs that ignore parameter uncertainty. Our examples include a numerical case study and a biochemical dataset from Swameye et al. (2003), considering both sequential offline and adaptive online designs. Finally, we plan to leverage modern machine learning methods to develop non-myopic adaptive designs for this framework.
Authors
Hao Zhu, Johannes Kepler Universität, Linz, Austria, hao.zhu@jku.at
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Intrusive Uncertainty Quantification and Optimal Experiment Design in the Open-Source Pyomo Ecosystem

speakers.name: Shuvashish Mondal
speakers.affiliation: University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Stephen Cini, Notre Dame University, U.S.; Daniel J. Laky

talks.abstract:
.
This contribution describes ParmEst [1] and Pyomo.DoE [2], two pillars of the open-source Python-based Pyomo [3] ecosystem for computational optimization with (partial differential) algebraic equation mathematical models. Specifically, ParmEst facilitates intrusive frequentist parameter estimation (PE) and uncertainty quantification (UQ) through built-in features, such as covariance matrix estimation, bootstrapping, and likelihood ratio tests. Complementarily, Pyomo.DoE enables optimal experiment design by maximizing various metrics of the Fisher information matrix, such as A-optimality (trace), D-optimality (determinant), E-optimality (minimum eigenvalue), and ME-optimality (condition number). ParmEst and Pyomo.DoE can solve high-dimensional optimization problems by leveraging the model structure and exact derivative information. Finally, we will discuss future opportunities to integrate PE and UQ capabilities with optimization under uncertainty, including robust optimization (RO) with non-convex models via PyROS [4], a Pyomo-based RO package.
References:  1. Klise, K. A.; Nicholson, B. L.; Staid, A.; Woodruff, D. L. Comput. Aided Chem. Eng. 2019, 47, 41–46. Elsevier.  2. Wang, J.; Dowling, A. W. AIChE J. 2022, 68 (12), e17813.  3. Hart, W. E.; Watson, J.-P.; Woodruff, D. L. Math. Program. Comput. 2011, 3 (3), 219–260.  4. Isenberg, N. M.; Akula, P.; Eslick, J. C.; Bhattacharyya, D.; Miller, D. C.; Gounaris, C. E. AIChE J. 2021, 67 (5), e17175.

talks.title: Bayesian Experimental Design for History Matching
talks.abstract:
.
History matching (HM) is a type of computer model calibration that rules out input parameter settings that are inconsistent between observations and computer model output according to a distance-based implausibility function and uncertainty specifications.  The remaining parameter space is termed as Not Ruled Out Yet (NROY). History matching has proven to be more effective when performed in waves. At each wave of HM, a new ensemble is obtained to update an emulator (surrogate model) before finding an NROY space.   In this talk, we propose a Bayesian experimental design to optimise the data collection process using a loss function that compares the volume of the NROY space obtained with an updated emulator to the volume of the “true” NROY space obtained using a “perfect” emulator. The intuition behind the proposed loss function corresponds to the aim of history matching. In particular, by performing history matching, we want to ensure that we are not going to incorrectly rule out points that are in fact close to observations, or leave regions of space that give output far from the observations. We compare the proposed methodology to space-filling design approaches using toy models as well as applications in climate and healthcare models.
Authors
Victoria Volodina, University of Exeter, United Kingdom, v.volodina@exeter.ac.uk

talks.title: Intrusive Uncertainty Quantification and Optimal Experiment Design to Characterize Membrane Separation Systems

speakers.name: Keshav Kasturi Rangan
speakers.affiliation: Molly Dougher, Laurianne Estrada, Shammah Lilonfe, Pratham Singh, William Phillip

talks.abstract:
.
Membrane separation systems are crucial to sustainable water treatment, chemical processing, and the recovery of critical minerals. However, their design is hindered by uncertainty in transport models and parameters. This study introduces an intrusive uncertainty quantification (UQ) and model-based design of experiments (MBDoE) framework to characterize neutral and charged nanofiltration membranes. By leveraging advances in dynamic diafiltration apparatuses, model-based data analytics, and Pyomo.DOE, this framework simultaneously discriminates between transport models, parameter estimates, and selects operating conditions that maximize information content while minimizing experimental effort. Intrusive approaches leverage Fisher information matrix criteria (A-, D-, E-, and ME-optimality) and weighted regression for heterogeneous data, enabling robust parameter estimation and model discrimination in multi-salt, charged transport systems. Finally, the study explores opportunities to create self-driving laboratories (SDLs) and digital twins that autonomously design, execute, and refine experiments. This work envisions establishing a scalable, predictive foundation for uncertainty-aware membrane characterization and inverse design of separation processes in the domains of water, energy, and critical materials.

sessions.title: Applications - I

talks.title: Mixed-Order Adsorption Kinetics under Uncertainty

speakers.name: Juan Carlos Cortés
speakers.affiliation: Universidad Politecnica de Valencia, Spain; Oksana A. Chkrebtii, Ohio State University, U.S.; Ana Navarro Quiles, Universidad Politecnica de Valencia, Spain

talks.abstract:
.
Adsorption is a fundamental process with wide-ranging applications in catalysis, separations, and environmental protection. While pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models are commonly used to describe adsorption kinetics, complex systems often involve competing mechanisms. The mixed-order model provides a more flexible and accurate description by combining both driving forces. Traditional parameter estimation methods for this model frequently yield biased or unreliable results. To address this, we propose a probabilistic framework that explicitly accounts for uncertainty arising both from the underlying dynamical system and from experimental measurement errors. In particular, we treat the model parameters as continuous random variables and apply the Random Variable Transformation technique to derive the first probability density function of the solution. This approach not only yields distributions for the model parameters but also provides a complete probabilistic characterization of the solution itself, offering a more robust and informative representation of adsorption dynamics. We also highlight ongoing work exploring alternative formulations, such as embedding the deterministic model into a Gaussian Process framework combined with particle filtering.

talks.title: Bayesian Optimization of Plasma Kinetic Reaction Conditions
talks.abstract:
.
Low-temperature plasma processes are increasingly used in chemical synthesis, energy conversion, and environmental applications. Yet, their systematic study remains difficult because of nonlinear pathways, high-dimensional parameter spaces, and the prohibitive cost of kinetic simulations. To address these challenges, we present a data-driven framework that integrates Gaussian Process (GP) regression with Bayesian Optimization (BO) to efficiently explore and optimize plasma kinetic systems.
Initial data from ZDPlasKin, a widely used high-fidelity plasma kinetics simulator, is employed to train GP surrogates that capture complex input–output relationships while providing quantified predictive uncertainty. We compare multiple GP modeling choices, including kernel families and hyperparameter learning strategies, and evaluate their effect on predictive accuracy and uncertainty calibration. Building on these surrogates, BO is used to adaptively select new simulation points, and we examine the impact of different acquisition functions (e.g., Expected Improvement, Upper Confidence Bound, Probability of Improvement) on optimization efficiency.
This iterative, UQ-driven design reduces the number of simulations needed to map the design space, accelerates identification of high-yield regimes compared to random or space-filling approaches, and demonstrates how uncertainty-aware surrogate modeling enables more systematic and efficient exploration of complex reacting systems.

talks.title: Surrogate-Based Probabilistic Fatigue Assessment of Transmission Lines with GPR: From Damage Estimation to Inspection Planning
talks.abstract:
.
Transmission line towers are repeatedly subjected to fluctuating wind loads that drive fatigue damage in bolted connections, threatening long-term reliability. This study develops a surrogate-based probabilistic framework to assess fatigue damage and guide inspection planning. High-fidelity finite element simulations are performed to capture joint-level responses under both preloaded and loosened bolt conditions, with fatigue quantified using rain-flow cycle counting and Miner's rule. The resulting dataset is used to train Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) surrogates, which provide rapid and accurate predictions of fatigue accumulation across diverse wind speeds and directions. By coupling these surrogates with joint probability distributions of typical winds and a Poisson-based extreme-event model, the framework synthesizes annual damage and propagates uncertainty through sampling-based simulations. Results highlight fatigue-critical connections, reveal the relative roles of typical versus extreme winds, and quantify the influence of stress concentration factors and bolt preloading states on vulnerability. Building on these insights, inspection strategies are optimized by incorporating detection probabilities, repair resets, and cost–risk trade-offs. The proposed methodology delivers a computationally efficient, reliability-informed tool for fatigue risk assessment and inspection scheduling of transmission line systems.
Authors
Pooria Mazaheri, Iowa State University, U.S., mazaheri@iastate.edu

talks.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Approach for Quantification of Fabrication Uncertainties in Integrated Photonics
talks.abstract:
.
Photonic integrated circuits enable high-bandwidth signal processing through carefully engineered interference of optical waves. However, as a consequence, these devices are exceptionally sensitive to even small stochastic variations in the fabrication process; nanometer-scale deviations in width, height, sidewall angle, and roughness of waveguides perturb effective indices and coupling coefficients, resulting in phase errors and spectral shifts that accumulate throughout the circuit.
We will discuss the use of hierarchical Bayesian modeling and inference as a principled way to quantify the distributions of process-level fabrication uncertainties in integrated photonics. Accurate electromagnetic forward models are used to map fabrication parameters to device behavior; by inverting the measurements of an ensemble of fabricated devices, we then disentangle approximations of process-level uncertainty distributions from measurement noise and model errors. We contend that such an explicit quantification of fabrication variations is a key step to herald the next generation of robust photonic fabrication; it feeds back naturally into the engineering iteration cycle by informing fabrication process modifications that reduce variations or by suggesting device designs that increase resilience to irreducible fabrication variations.

sessions.title: Applications - II

talks.title: Advancing Storm Surge Hazard Emulation Through the Implementation of Graph Neural Networks

speakers.name: Alexandros A. Taflanidis
speakers.affiliation: University of Notre Dame, U.S.; Norberto Nadal-Caraballo, Madison Yawn

speakers.name: Luce Aucoin
speakers.affiliation: US Army Corps of Engineers, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Probabilistic storm surge hazard estimation plays a key role in coastal resilience planning. Uncertainty quantification is achieved by generating an ensemble of storm scenarios based on local climatology, using a high-fidelity hydrodynamic model to compute surge responses for each scenario, and finally obtaining the statistics for the peak surge across different nodes within the geographic domain of interest. To reduce the burden in this hazard estimation (related to the high computational cost of the high-fidelity models) surrogate modeling is used. The choice of surrogate model is important because storm surge is governed by highly nonlinear relationship between storm characteristics and surge response patterns, compounded by complex spatial dependencies. This study investigates sparse graph-based surrogates, specifically graph neural networks (GNNs), for peak storm surge emulation. Sparsity is embedded into the graph connectivity, enabling the efficient application of GNNs to high-dimensional coastal domains while alleviating memory and scalability challenges. In addition, a response-based similarity metric is introduced to define graph edges, ensuring that the surrogate reflects the existing complex dependencies in surge responses across geo-locations. The results are evaluated in terms of predictive accuracy, computational efficiency, and memory requirements, providing a comprehensive assessment of the suitability of the proposed framework for storm surge emulation.

talks.title: Stochastic Optimal Control for Trajectory Optimization in Mine Counter Measure Simulations
talks.abstract:
.
Simulating mine counter measure (MCM) search missions at sea performed by autonomous vehicles presents a complex challenge. The goal of MCM simulations consists of computing trajectories for autonomous vehicles surveying a domain where sea mines are suspected. These trajectories have to be such that the residual risk of not detected the sea mines after survey is below a certain threshold. In [1], we model the MCM problem as a stochastic optimal control problem. Mathematically, this translates as minimizing the total mission time needed to survey a domain $\Omega$ for a given residual risk, i.e.,   \begin{equation}  \text{min}\, T_F,  \label{eq:min}  \end{equation}  subjected to  \begin{equation}   \mathbb{E}[q\left(T_F\right)] :=  \int_\Omega \text{e}^{-\int_0^{T_F} \gamma\left(x\left(\tau\right),\omega\right)\, d\,\tau}f_X\left(\omega\right) d\,\omega \leq \text{Residual Risk}.  \label{eq:exp}  \end{equation}  In [1], we present a relaxation strategy combined with a quasi-Monte Carlo scheme for solving the integral, which shows a speedup of a factor 2 with respect to Monte Carlo. We also expand our implementation to accommodate generalized non-convex domains, e.g., a hexagon.
[1] Blondeel, P., Van Utterbeeck, F., Lauwens, B. (2025).  Application of quasi-Monte Carlo in Mine Countermeasure Simulations with a Stochastic Optimal Control Framework,  in 16th International Conference on Monte Carlo and Quasi-Monte Carlo Methods in Scientific Computing, Waterloo, Canada, 2024.
Authors
Philippe Blondeel, Belgian Royal Military Academy, Belgium, philippe.blondeel@mil.be

Ben Lauwens, Belgian Royal Military Academy, Belgium, ben.lauwens@mil.be

talks.title: Performance of Ellipsoidal Estimation for Low-Earth Orbit Determination under Bounded Parametric Uncertainty

speakers.name: Diganta Bhattacharjee
speakers.affiliation: University of Minnesota, Twin Cities, U.S.; Maziar S. Hemati, University of Minnesota, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
This work focuses on an important problem in relation to space situational awareness: determining orbits of objects in low-Earth orbit (LEO) with imprecise knowledge of parameters. State-of-the-art nonlinear estimators like the extended and unscented Kalman filters (EKF and UKF, respectively) have traditionally been utilized for orbit determination; however, accommodating parametric uncertainty poses a non-trivial challenge. To this end, we employ an ellipsoidal state estimation framework (Bhattacharjee and Subbarao, IEEE Trans. Automat. Contr. 67 (2021), pp. 894-901) that considers ellipsoidal characterizations of the process, measurement, and initial condition uncertainties. The framework relies upon pseudo-linear descriptions of the nonlinearity in dynamical and observation models, and it inherits the recursive “predictor-corrector” structure of the standard Kalman filter variants. At every prediction and correction step, optimal ellipsoids are computed by solving convex semi-definite programming optimization problems that can be reliably solved using standard off-the-shelf solvers. Numerical experiments are performed to quantify nominal robustness of the EKF and UKF against parameter mismatch for orbit determination of a LEO satellite. The ellipsoidal framework exhibits a higher degree of nominal robustness compared to EKF and UKF. The joint parameter and state estimation problem is also studied for the same orbit, and performance of the three methods are compared.
Authors

talks.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Soft Biological Tissue Growth and Remodeling

speakers.name: Jay Humphrey
speakers.affiliation: Yale University, U.S.; Alison Marsden, Stanford University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Cardiovascular biomechanics often involves variations in tissue mass (growth) and microstructure (remodeling), driven by mechanical or chemical stimuli. The classical constrained mixture theory describes this growth and remodeling (G&R) process by modeling the synthesis and degradation of key extracellular matrix constituents: elastin, smooth muscle cells, and collagen fibers. Accurately characterizing this complex mechanobiological phenomenon is essential for the prevention and diagnosis of vascular diseases, yet it remains challenging due to the large number of model parameters, high computational demands, and potential discrepancies with experimental observations. In this work, we investigate uncertainties in the mechanobiologically equilibrated and time-dependent G&R process, focusing on solution behavior classification and parameter sensitivity analysis. In particular, we propose a framework to efficiently classify the outcomes of G&R simulations under perturbations to material integrity and mechanosensitive parameters. This includes leveraging a flexible neural network for fast evaluation of classification probabilities and extraction of decision boundaries, combined with a variance-based global sensitivity analysis to identify the key parameter components driving transitions in simulation outcomes. Our study utilizes both reduced-order and three-dimensional finite element G&R models, offering novel insights into the understanding of mechanobiological equilibrium, numerical stability and the progression of thoracic aortic aneurysms (TAA).

sessions.title: Data Assimilation

talks.title: Conditional Sensitivity and Relative Uncertainty in Stochastic Oscillators Via Adjoint Methods
talks.abstract:
.
Quantifying the effect of stochastic perturbations on high-dimensional dynamical systems is a central challenge in uncertainty quantification. Oscillatory systems, such as neuronal networks or biochemical clocks, exhibit both intrinsic fluctuations and responses to external inputs, making it difficult to disentangle sources of variability. In this work, we show that the gradient of the stochastic asymptotic phase naturally provides a local, state-dependent sensitivity measure of phase to small perturbations.  We interpret this gradient as a measure of relative uncertainty that separates the contribution of intrinsic noise from that of extrinsic inputs. This approach bridges classical adjoint sensitivity analysis, stochastic phase reduction, and conditional uncertainty quantification. Using examples of noisy limit-cycle systems, we demonstrate how this framework efficiently predicts phase diffusion, synchronization, and entrainment under additional perturbations, reducing high-dimensional stochastic dynamics to a low-dimensional phase description while preserving key uncertainty measures.  Our results suggest that conditional adjoint sensitivities can serve as a general tool in UQ for characterizing the impact of perturbations relative to intrinsic stochastic variability, with potential applications in neuroscience, chemical oscillators, and networked stochastic systems.
Authors
Yangyang Du, University of Michigan, U.S., yyadu@umich.edu
Daniel Forger, University of Michigan, U.S., forger@umich.edu

sessions.title: Applied Statistics & Computer Experiments

talks.title: Bayesian Uncertainty Analysis of Prior Beliefs in History Matching Climate Models
talks.abstract:
.
Computer models are vital in climate science to study drivers of climate change; quantify uncertainties in future climate predictions; and to guide policy decisions. However, their direct use is inhibited by: their complex structure; high-dimensional input and output, including spatial-temporal fields; and their long evaluation times taking months on HPCs.
Earth System Models (ESMs) integrate atmosphere, ocean, land, ice, and biosphere. ESMs are used to investigate natural and anthropogenic aerosol emission interactions with clouds yielding large Aerosol Radiative Forcing (ARF; temporal change in Earth’s energy balance due to aerosols) induced uncertainty in historical climate change. ARF is unobservable and key to predicting future climate yet research has resulted in little uncertainty reduction in 30-years of IPCC reports.
Bayesian emulators are fast statistical approximations to model outputs. These are embedded in an uncertainty quantification framework incorporating structural model discrepancy linking ESM and the real-world, as well as representation and observation errors. This enables an efficient global parameter search for model-observation comparison via history matching to resolve parametric uncertainty and robustly constrain ARF for the UK Met Office’s UKESM1 model. This requires many subjective choices: the output set used in history matching; the prior uncertainty specification; and the beliefs represented. We investigate the effect on ARF uncertainty.
Authors
Jonathan Owen, University of Sheffield, United Kingdom, jonathan.owen@sheffield.ac.uk
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Bayesian Variable Screening Via Joint Credible Region for Universal Kriging in Computer Experiments

speakers.name: Fan Zhang
speakers.affiliation: Boise State University, U.S.; Ray-Bing Chen, National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan; Xinwei Deng, Virginia Tech, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Computer experiments using Gaussian process models are widely employed in scientific and engineering applications for emulating complex systems. Much of the existing work on variable selection for such models has focused on either mean function effects or covariance structure effects separately.   This paper proposes a Bayesian variable screening approach that simultaneously evaluates variable importance in both components of Gaussian process models. A new joint credible region method is introduced to identify active variables. The key idea of the proposed method is to construct a two-dimensional credible region using posterior medians of an inert variable's regression coefficient and correlation parameter. This enables a unified decision framework of determining variable to be active or inactive based on whether their median posterior parameter pair lies inside or outside this joint region. The performance of the proposed method is evaluated through comprehensive simulation studies and real applications including the Borehole example and heat exchanger example.

talks.title: Probabilistic Learning of the Purkinje Network from the 12 Lead Electrocardiogram

speakers.name: Francisco Sahli Costabal
speakers.affiliation: Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Chile

talks.abstract:
.
The identification of the Purkinje conduction system in the heart is a challenging task, yet essential for a correct definition of cardiac digital twins for precision cardiology. Here, we propose a probabilistic approach for identifying the Purkinje network from non-invasive clinical data such as the standard electrocardiogram (ECG). We use cardiac imaging to build an anatomically accurate model of the ventricles; we algorithmically generate a rule-based Purkinje network tailored to the anatomy; we simulate physiological electrocardiograms with a fast model; we identify the geometrical and electrical parameters of the Purkinje-ECG model with Bayesian optimization and approximate Bayesian computation. The proposed approach is inherently probabilistic and generates a population of plausible Purkinje networks, all fitting the ECG within a given tolerance. In this way, we can estimate the uncertainty of the parameters, thus providing reliable predictions. We test our methodology in physiological and pathological scenarios, showing that we are able to accurately recover the ECG with our model. We propagate the uncertainty in the Purkinje network parameters in a simulation of conduction system pacing therapy. Our methodology is a step forward in creation of digital twins from non-invasive data in precision medicine.
Authors
Francisco Sahli Costabal, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Chile, fsahlic@uc.cl
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Scaling Bayesian Optimization of Composite Functions for High-Dimensional Inverse Problems: Application to Electron Microscopy

speakers.name: David A. Muller
speakers.affiliation: Cornell University, U.S.; Peter I. Frazier, Cornell University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We study high-dimensional inverse problems of the form:  $$\min_{x\in\mathcal{X}}g(h(x);y),$$  where $h(x)$ is a $d$-dimensional output of an expensive derivative-free simulation under parameter $x$, $y$ is the ground truth vector, and $g$ is a known function that measures discrepancy between simulated and the true outputs. The objective is to efficiently find parameter values $x$ that minimize this error. Bayesian Optimization of Composite Functions (BOCF) tackles such problems by modeling $h(x)$ with a multi-output Gaussian process (GP) surrogate and leveraging the known structure $g$, improving query efficiency. However, when the dimensionality $d$ grows large, inference for multi-output GPs becomes computationally expensive and makes BOCF infeasible.
In this work, we propose a scalable BOCF framework that aggregates components of $h(x)$ to create lower-dimensional outputs, reducing computational cost while preserving the advantage in query efficiency offered by the composite structure. We demonstrate the approach on electron microscopy, where $h(x)$ represents simulated images under parameters $x$ (specimen thickness and misorientation) and $y$ denotes experimental images. Numerical results show that our method recovers the parameters $x$ more accurately and with fewer simulations than standard Bayesian optimization and at a fraction of the runtime required by the original BOCF.
Authors

Peter I. Frazier, Cornell University, U.S., pf98@cornell.edu

talks.title: Steering Generative Models with Experimental Data for Protein Fitness Optimization

speakers.name: Raul Astudillo
speakers.affiliation: Zayed University, Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates

talks.abstract:
.
Designing proteins with desired quantitative properties requires searching an astronomically large sequence space while collecting only limited experimental data. Recent protein generative models—such as diffusion and language models—offer promising ways to guide this search. Yet most existing methods rely on surrogate rewards or large labeled datasets, leaving open how well they perform when only a few hundred wet-lab measurements are available.
In this talk, I will present a systematic study of protein fitness optimization in this data-scarce regime and introduce Steered Generation for Protein Optimization (SGPO), a framework that couples generative protein models with small amounts of experimental data to efficiently discover high-fitness protein variants. SGPO encompasses multiple types of generative models and steering mechanisms, enabling us to identify the most effective design choices for practical protein engineering campaigns.

talks.title: IAGO: Implicit Adaptive Generative Design Optimization, a Data-Scarce Optimization Framework for Physics-Based, Automatically Manufacturable Designs

speakers.name: Orazio Pinti
speakers.affiliation: Atomic Machines, U.S.; Leonardo Ferreira Guilhoto, University of Pennsylvania, U.S.; Simone Venturi, Atomic Machines, U.S.; Paris Perdikaris, University of Pennsylvania, U.S.; Marta D'Elia, Pasteur Labs

talks.abstract:
.
We present IAGO, a framework for design optimization. The method uses latent conditional neural fields trained to represent shapes as signed distance functions (SDFs) that that satisfy manufacturability and geometry constraints. After training, Bayesian Optimization (BO) in the latent space identifies high- and low-performing designs by calling a physics solver to compute domain-specific metrics. Thereafter, a refined model is trained with the additional objectives of generating solutions that resemble high-performing designs, while discouraging recurrence of low-performing ones. This two-steps cycle of model training and BO is repeated until performance requirements are met. Building on prior work "[Berzins et al., Geometry-Informed Neural Networks]", IAGO integrates knowledge of prior designs and is efficient in data-scarce regimes. Further, in contrast to other solver-in-the-loop methods ("[Radler et al., Diverse Topology Optimization using Modulated Neural Fields]", "[Hoyer et al., Neural reparameterization improves structural optimization]"), IAGO does not rely on gradients of the cost function, as common in topology optimization. Our experimental results in the context of structural mechanics show that IAGO produces high-performing and manufacturable designs after a few cycles. In this talk, we will provide a few examples of IAGO's application to structural mechanics problems.
Authors
Orazio Pinti, Atomic Machines, U.S., orazio.pinti@atomicmachines.com
Leonardo Ferreira Guilhoto, University of Pennsylvania, U.S., guilhoto@sas.upenn.edu

sessions.title: Data Efficient Learning - Part I of II

talks.title: Understanding The Effectiveness of Lossy Compression in Machine Learning and AI Training Sets

speakers.name: Robert R. Underwood
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Jon Calhoun, Clemson University, U.S.; Sheng Di

talks.abstract:
.
Often datasets for AI used in scientific applications can be large extreemly large making them difficult to move and store.  One approach to dealing with this are data reduction techniques including sampling (that reduces the number of elements of data) and dimensionality reduction (that reduces the number of features that each element has) but also lossy data compression which reduces the footprint of data by reducing precision of each feature.  In this work, we explore how to most effectively use modern error bounded lossy compressors to reduce the footprint of these datasets to improve the storage and transfer of such datasets.
Authors
Robert R. Underwood, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., runderwood@anl.gov
Jon Calhoun, Clemson University, U.S., jonccal@clemson.edu

talks.title: Multi-Stage Latent Space Dynamics Identification

speakers.name: William Anderson
speakers.affiliation: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
Numerical solutions of partial differential equations (PDEs) are often computationally expensive, driving the need for efficient reduced-order models (ROMs). Gaussian Process-based Latent Space Dynamics Identification (GPLaSDI) is a recently proposed data-driven, non-intrusive ROM framework that compresses simulation data using an autoencoder and learns a system of user-chosen ordinary differential equations (ODEs) governing the latent space dynamics. This allows for rapid predictions by interpolating and evolving the low-dimensional ODEs in the latent space. Additionally, using GPs to interpolate ODEs in the latent space allows for efficient uncertainty-based adaptive sampling of the parameter space. While GPLaSDI has produced effective ROMs for numerous problems, the autoencoder may have difficulty accurately reconstructing training data while also satisfying the imposed dynamics in the latent space, particularly in complex or high-frequency regimes. To address this, we propose multi-stage Latent Space Dynamics Identification (mLaSDI), which trains GPLaSDI models in sequential stages. Each stage learns a new decoder to correct the residual error from previous stages, improving prediction accuracy and reducing sensitivity to hyperparameter choices. We demonstrate mLaSDI on a multiscale oscillatory problem, 2D vortex shedding, and the 1D-1V Vlasov equation, achieving high prediction accuracy for all problems. LLNL-CONF-2006046
Authors
William Anderson, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S., anderson316@llnl.gov

sessions.title: Theory, Computational Methods, and Applications of Measure Transport for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of II

talks.title: Epistemic Uncertainty Quantification with Applications in Machine Learning

speakers.name: Krikamol Muandet
speakers.affiliation: CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
Epistemic uncertainty arises from limited knowledge about the underlying data-generating process and plays a central role in the reliability, robustness, and safety of machine learning systems. In this talk, I will discuss recent advances in quantifying epistemic uncertainty, with a focus on approaches grounded in imprecise probability and related frameworks. I will highlight how these methods go beyond conventional probabilistic modelling, capturing ambiguity and ignorance in a principled way. Applications in machine learning including out-of-distribution generalisation, compositional learning, and AI safety will illustrate the potential of epistemic uncertainty quantification to enable more trustworthy and rational decision-making.
Authors
Krikamol Muandet, CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security, Germany, muandet@cispa.de

talks.title: Proximal Transport Divergences for Robust Generative Flows and Bayesian Inference

speakers.name: Benjamin Zhang
speakers.affiliation: University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Proximal optimal transport (OT) divergences are a novel class of discrepancy measures that interpolate between information divergences and OT distances through infimal convolution. These divergences provide a principled foundation for the proximal optimization methods commonly used in generative modeling. We establish that these divergences possess key mathematical properties: smoothness, boundedness, and computational tractability. We then demonstrate that generative flows trained with these divergences are well-posed with desirable regularity properties, making them particularly effective for learning distributions on low-dimensional manifolds while maintaining stable and robust training dynamics. Finally, we apply these robust generative models to likelihood-free inference for generating samples from Bayesian posteriors supported on low-dimensional manifolds.
Authors
Benjamin Zhang, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S., bjz@unc.edu

talks.title: Transport Vs. MCMC for Conditional Sampling

speakers.name: Bamdad Hosseini
speakers.affiliation: University of Washington, U.S.; Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
A common task in uncertainty quantification is modeling a conditional distribution for parameters of interest given data. To compute descriptive statistics, one often requires independent samples from this distribution. The standard tools are Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods, which provide strong theoretical guarantees but often suffer from slow convergence and require re-initialization for each new conditioning variable. Measure transport methods, by contrast, construct deterministic or stochastic maps between reference and target measures, offering amortized sampling and scalability. In this talk, we present a systematic comparison of measure transport and MCMC across a suite of benchmark UQ problems. Specifically, we construct triangular maps via flow-based and stationary transport models to demonstrate the efficacy of such a conditional sampling approach. Through our experiments, we highlight measure transport as a practical and powerful alternative to MCMC.
Authors
Alex Johnson-Vázquez, University of Washington, U.S., verano13@uw.edu
Bamdad Hosseini, University of Washington, U.S., bamdadh@uw.edu
Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada,  r.baptista@utoronto.ca

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Digital Twins for Earth Science - Part II of III

talks.title: Data Driven Modeling Methods to Enable Real-Time Digital Twins
talks.abstract:
.
We present a numerical framework for constructing a targeted digital twin (tDT) that directly models the dynamics of quantities of interest (QoIs) in a full digital twin. The proposed approach employs memory-based flow map learning (FML) to develop a data-driven model of the QoIs. The trained FML model provides a compact and efficient surrogate representation of the unknown system, enabling the real-time prediction and control for the full DT. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through examples in computational fluid dynamics.
Authors
Qifan Chen, Ohio State University, U.S., chen.11010@osu.edu

sessions.title: Recent Advancements in Operator Estimation and Learning - Part II of III

talks.title: Theory-to-Practice Gap for Operator Learning

speakers.name: Samuel Lanthaler
speakers.affiliation: California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Phillip Grohs, University of Vienna, Austria

talks.abstract:
.
The theory-to-practice gap describes the discrepancy between the parametric complexity and the sampling complexity of learning. For a set of functions with a fixed parametric complexity, or the best possible approximation error rate with respect to the number of model parameters, we examine the corresponding sampling complexity, or the best error rate with respect to the number of data samples given a reconstruction algorithm. In some cases, such as polynomial or trigonometric polynomial approximation, these two rates coincide: determining N degrees of freedom requires N point samples. However, for approximation spaces of neural networks, this intuition does not hold; indeed, the sampling complexity rate is fundamentally limited. As the parametric complexity rate becomes arbitrarily fast, the optimal sampling complexity rate stays uniformly bounded. In this work, we extend the theory-to-practice gap result for learning in finite dimensions to the infinite-dimensional setting of operator learning.
Authors
Margaret K. Trautner, California Institute of Technology, U.S., mtrautne@caltech.edu
Samuel Lanthaler, California Institute of Technology, U.S., slanth@caltech.edu
Phillip Grohs, University of Vienna, Austria, philipp.grohs@univie.ac.at;
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Scaling Scientific Machine Learning: Integrating Theory and Numerics in Both Training and Inference
talks.abstract:
.
Scaling scientific machine learning (SciML) requires overcoming bottlenecks at both training and inference. On the training side, we study the statistical convergence rate and limits of deep learning for solving elliptic PDEs from random samples. While our theory predicts optimal polynomial convergence for PINNs, optimization becomes prohibitively ill-conditioned as networks widen. By adapting descent strategies to the optimization geometry, we obtain scale-invariant training dynamics that translate polynomial convergence into concrete compute and yield compute-optimal configurations. On the inference side, I will introduce Simulation-Calibrated SciML (SCaSML), a physics-informed post-processing framework that improves surrogate models without retraining or fine-tuning. By enforcing physical laws, SCaSML delivers trustworthy corrections (via Feymann-Kac simulation) with approximate confidence intervals, achieves faster and near-optimal convergence rates, and supports online updates for digital twins. Together, these results integrate theory and numerics to enable predictable, reliable scaling of SciML in both training and inference.
Authors
Yiping Lu, Courant Institute, New York University and Northwestern University, U.S., yiping.lu@northwestern.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: On the Benefits of Active Data Collection in Operator Learning
talks.abstract:
.
Pseudo-differential operators (PDOs) are a broad and fundamental class of operators in analysis and partial differential equations, with wide-ranging applications in mathematical and computational sciences. In this talk, I will present our recent work on learning elliptic PDOs from noisy data. Within a wavelet–Galerkin framework, we formulate this task as a structured infinite-dimensional regression problem with multiscale sparsity. Building on this structure, we propose a sparse, data- and computation-efficient estimator that leverages a novel matrix compression scheme tailored to the learning task and a nested-support strategy to balance approximation and estimation errors. In addition to establishing convergence rates for the estimator, we show that the learned operator induces an efficient and stable Galerkin solver whose numerical error matches its statistical accuracy. Our results therefore contribute to bringing together operator learning, data-driven solvers, and wavelet methods in scientific computing.
Authors
Jiaheng Chen, University of Chicago, U.S., jiaheng@uchicago.edu
Daniel Sanz-Alonso, University of Chicago, U.S., sanzalonso@uchicago.edu

talks.title: The RTO Map for Hierarchical Sampling

speakers.name: Tiangang Cui
speakers.affiliation: University of Sydney, Australia; Johnathan M. Bardsley, University of Montana, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In many hierarchical inverse problems, not only do we want to estimate high- or infinite-dimensional model parameters in the parameter-to-observable maps, but we also have to estimate hyperparameters that represent critical assumptions in the statistical and mathematical modelling processes. As a joint effect of high-dimensionality, nonlinear dependence, and nonconcave structures in the joint posterior distribution over model parameters and hyperparameters, solving inverse problems in the hierarchical Bayesian setting poses a significant computational challenge. In this talk, we will present scalable optimization-based Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) methods for solving hierarchical Bayesian inverse problems with nonlinear parameter-to-observable maps and a broader class of hyperparameters. Our algorithmic development is based on the recently developed scalable randomize-then-optimize (RTO) method for exploring the high- or infinite-dimensional parameter space. By using RTO either as a proposal distribution in a Metropolis-within-Gibbs update or as a biasing distribution in the pseudo marginal MCMC, we present efficient sampling tools for hierarchical Bayesian inversion. In particular, the integration of RTO and the pseudo marginal MCMC has sampling performance robust to model parameter dimensions. With minor modifications, the presented methods can also be applied to problems with the Poisson likelihood and other non-Gaussian observation processes.
Authors
Tiangang Cui, University of Sydney, Australia, tiangang.cui@sydney.edu.au

talks.title: Excursions in Bayesian Treatments of Model Error
talks.abstract:
.
Berliner et al. (2023) discusses some challenges and strategies for treating model error in a Bayesian context. They distinguish between two classes of models. The first targets physically-based statistical models that are sufficiently manageable to allow incorporation of a stochastic “model error process'. The second addresses large-scale models in which incorporation of a model error process and updating its distribution is impractical. Current work extends some of the ideas brought forth in Berliner et al. (2023). Under the hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework, we discuss avenues for learning and quantifying the uncertainty in the dimension-reduction operator and for addressing the process model / model error confounding issues. We illustrate the ideas using an application inspired by a classical oceanographic inverse problem.
Authors
Radu Herbei, Ohio State University, U.S., herbei@stat.osu.edu

sessions.title: Data Driven Inference, Reduction, and Estimation using Dynamical Models - Part III of III

talks.title: Uncertainty-Aware Sensor Optimization for Safety-Critical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Sensor placement within safety-critical systems such as nuclear reactors and aerospace applications is highly constrained by limited capacity, extreme operating conditions, high costs, restricted accessibility, and stringent safety regulations. Real-time monitoring of temperature, pressure, velocity, and power requires accurate field reconstruction from sparse measurements while quantifying noise-induced uncertainties. This work develops an uncertainty-aware framework for optimizing constrained sensor locations and field reconstruction through reduced-order models, D-optimal design criteria, followed by QR pivoting. Reconstruction results from QR-optimized sensors demonstrate high accuracy, reduced noise-induced uncertainties, and reconstruction error reduction with minimal sensor requirements across both experimental and simulation datasets. While this approach achieves high reconstruction accuracy for linear systems, we further develop a mutual information-based sensor selection pipeline to address nonlinear reconstruction challenges. This approach demonstrated accurate field reconstruction by selecting sensors that maximize mutual information with the full-field, applied to a boundary shock mixing layer configuration.
Authors
Niharika Karnik, Idaho National Laboratory, U.S., niharika.karnik@inl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Machine Learning–Based Uncertainty Quantification for Condition Monitoring in Nuclear Power Plant Generation Risk Analysis
talks.abstract:
.
This work presents a machine learning–based uncertainty quantification framework for condition monitoring and generation risk analysis in nuclear power plants. The framework integrates machine learning models that estimate equipment reliability from condition monitoring data—while also explicitly accounting for associated uncertainties—in combination with techniques for synthetically generating data on electricity prices and plant output such that the statistical characteristics (i.e., data uncertainty) of the original data are preserved. These uncertainty sources are then propagated through a dynamic nuclear power plant generation risk model to quantify their combined impact on generation risk. The proposed approach improves predictions of future lost generation and supports cost-effective, risk-informed decision-making for project prioritization and long-term equipment reliability planning.
Authors
Congjian Wang, Idaho National Laboratory, U.S., congjian.wang@inl.gov

sessions.title: Assessing and Quantifying Model-form Uncertainty and Model-form Error - Part III of III

talks.title: Unpacking Model Inadequacy: Applications to Nuclear Fuels and Materials

speakers.name: Som L. Dhulipala
speakers.affiliation: Parikshit Bajpai, Pierre-Clément Simon, Jacob Hirschhorn, Benjamin Spencer

talks.abstract:
.
We develop a Bayesian calibration framework for nuclear fuels and materials applications that explicitly represents model&#8211data mismatch via a Gaussian-process inadequacy. The approach propagates uncertainty from parameters, experimental noise, and model form, with a hierarchical variance structure to capture group and cross-group noise. For a nuclear graphite property prediction application, where we are interested in predicting the irradiation-induced dimension change, creep, Young's modulus change ratio, and coefficient of thermal expansion change ratio, we obtain average predictive-error reductions of 54%, 65%, 17%, and 17% when inadequacy is included. We illustrate engineering impact with a multiphysics model of a very-high-temperature reactor prismatic reflector brick, analyzing stresses under high fluence and temperature. Accounting for model inadequacy markedly improves predictive accuracy and provides a robust basis for reliable graphite component design in advanced reactors. For a tristructural isotropic (TRISO) particle application, we compare traditional Arrhenius equation fitted to experimental data and the more advanced lower-length-scale (LLS)-informed model, which considers microstructure information. Applying the inverse UQ process on the experimental datasets revealed modeling inadequacy to be the most dominant source of uncertainty. Experimental noise uncertainty is also significant; however, model parameter uncertainty can be considered negligible. Interestingly, both the Arrhenius equation and the LLS-informed model demonstrated similar levels of modeling inadequacy. For the forward predictive UQ on test datasets, the KOH framework improved both the accuracy and quality of quantified uncertainties in comparison to the standard Bayesian framework. This is true for both the Arrhenius equation and the LLS-informed model. In comparing these modeling approaches, both demonstrated similar performance at the engineering scale, while the LLS-informed model expectedly outperformed the Arrhenius equation at the mesoscale. Overall, across both the applications, it is important to explicitly account for modeling inadequacy in the UQ process, and reinforce the need for continuous refinement of physics-based models in order to address the modeling inadequacy.
Authors
Som L. Dhulipala, Idaho National Laboratory, U.S., Som.Dhulipala@inl.gov
Parikshit Bajpai, Idaho National Laboratory, U.S., parikshit.bajpai@inl.gov

talks.title: Model-Form Uncertainty Quantification and Management in Hierarchical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Many scientific and engineering problems involve predictions that depend on uncertain information—from noisy data to incomplete physical models. Characterizing, propagating, and predicting the performance of complex systems with hierarchical structure is particularly challenging in the presence of uncertainties and modeling errors. In this talk, I will present the Extended Polynomial Chaos Expansion (EPCE) framework, a robust and efficient paradigm that simultaneously propagate all sources of uncertainties and errors in order to provide a probabilistic representation of specific quantities of interest (QoI). We characterize the full probability distribution of the QoI and the uncertainty in the failure probability pertaining to its tails. By combining EPCE with kernel density estimation (KDE) and directional derivatives, we construct sensitivity measures that connect these statistical metrics of QoI to the model-form error and data error to assess their individual and combined impacts. We demonstrate the presented approach in several engineering systems with hierarchical interactions.
Authors
Zhiheng Wang, Texas Tech University, U.S., zhiheng.wang@ttu.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Data Assimilation and Discrepancy Modeling with Shallow Recurrent Decoders
talks.abstract:
.
Complex physical systems are often modeled through simulations to approximate their dominant dynamics. However, these simulation models could inevitably neglect small-scale or hidden processes or oversimplify parameter correlations, leading to reconstructions that can diverge from reality. This creates a pressing need for data assimilation, the process of integrating simulation data with predictive models to produce coherent and accurate estimates of the full state of complex physical systems. In this paper, we propose a framework for Data Assimilation with SHallow REcurrent Decoder (DA-SHRED) which bridges the sim-to-real gap between simulations and sensor data for real physics. For real-world systems on high-dimensional spatiotemporal fields, where the full state cannot be directly observed and must be inferred from sparse sensor measurements, we leverage the latent space learned from a reduced simulation model via SHRED networks, and update these latent variables using real sensor data to accurately reconstruct the full system state. Furthermore, our algorithm incorporates a SINDy-based regression model in the latent space to identify functionals corresponding to missing dynamics in the reduced simulation model. In presented examples, DA-SHRED successfully recovers missing dynamics in highly volatile systems, demonstrating that the combination of efficient temporal encoding and physics-informed correction enables robust data assimilation under sparse sensing constraints.
Authors
Yuxuan Bao, University of Washington, U.S., baoyx@uw.edu
J. Nathan Kutz, University of Washington, U.S., kutz@uw.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Complex Physical Systems and High-Dimensional Data - Part I of II

talks.title: Quantifying Uncertainty in a Poorly Specified Methane Emissions Inverse Problem

speakers.name: Dorit Hammerling
speakers.affiliation: Colorado School of Mines, U.S.; William Daniels, Johns Hopkins University, U.S.; Cal Okenberg , Colorado School of Mines, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Methane, the main component of natural gas, is the second-largest contributor to climate change after carbon dioxide. Methane has a higher heat-trapping potential but shorter lifetime than carbon dioxide, and therefore, rapid reduction of methane emission can have quick and large climate change mitigation impacts. Reducing emissions from oil and gas production facilities, turns out to be a particular promising avenue due to technological advances in continuous emission monitoring technology. We present a Bayesian framework for quick emission detection and localization using continuous methane concentration data measured by multiple monitoring sensors on oil and gas production facilities, demonstrate its effectiveness under real-world conditions and discuss ideas for comprehensive uncertainty quantification that incorporates source localization uncertainty.
Authors
Dorit Hammerling, Colorado School of Mines, U.S., hammerling@mines.edu
William Daniels, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., wdanie16@jh.edu
Cal Okenberg, Colorado School of Mines, U.S., @tbd

talks.title: Generative Modeling for Bayesian Inverse Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Operational data processing pipelines in applications like atmospheric remote sensing  are based on repeatedly solving Bayesian inverse problems involving computationally expensive forward models. In addition to computational costs, the resulting posterior distribution is usually non-Gaussian even when using normal priors, due to moderately non-linear forward models. The reported uncertainty is still based on a Laplace approximation of the posterior covariance, which is known to be prone to underestimating uncertainties. In this work, we apply generative modeling, specifically diffusion based models, to learn the joint distribution of model inputs and outputs, and use this technique to solve the underlying inverse problem fast while providing comprehensive description of the posterior, thanks to the conditional inference provided by inverting the diffusion flow.
Authors
Otto Lamminpaeae, NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S., otto.m.lamminpaa@jpl.nasa.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Probabilistic Token Attribution Score For Large Language Models

speakers.name: Bethany Lusch
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Michael E. Papka, Argonne National Laboratory

speakers.name: University of Chicago
speakers.affiliation: U.S.; Venkatram Vishwanath, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
The generative nature of current Large Language Models (LLMs) is expressed by the conditional probabilities that LLMs compute to sample each response token conditioned on the previous tokens. These probabilities encode the distributional structure that the model learns in training and exploits in inference. We have designed a model-agnostic probabilistic token attribution measure using Bayes rule to invert the logits so as to capture the model's internal representation of the distribution over token sequences. The representation is independent of details of model computational structure. This representation yields the conditional probability of the response given the prompt, and of the response given the prompt with a token marginalized away. Our attribution score is the log of the ratio of these probabilities. We further compute entropy of token distributions, conditioned on the entire remaining context. The interplay between entropy and attribution score sheds light on LLM behavior. We exhibit the behavior of 7 models on 6 prompts and identify token sensitivity and response stability, thereby improving interpretability and guiding users to focus on uncertain or unstable parts of the generation.
Authors
Carlo Graziani, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., cgraziani@anl.gov
Shilpika, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., shilpika@anl.gov
Bethany Lusch, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., blusch@anl.gov
Michael E. Papka, Argonne National Laboratory and University of Chicago, U.S., papka@anl.gov
Venkatram Vishwanath, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., venkatv@mcs.anl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Recent Developments in Statistical Methods for Uncertainty Quantification - Part III of III

talks.title: Addressing Parameter Non-Identifiability Using Bayesian Calibration on the Active Subspace

speakers.name: Allison L. Lewis
speakers.affiliation: Lafayette College, U.S.; Rebecca Everett, Haverford College, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The inference of model parameters for moderate- to high-dimensional model systems can be challenging, since the full set of parameters is often unidentifiable in the sense that they are not uniquely determined by the available data, particularly for applications with restrictive data constraints. Additionally, the computational cost of performing Bayesian inference in a high-dimensional space may not allow for adequate exploration of the entire parameter domain. These issues may be mitigated by using active subspace techniques to identify the subspace of identifiable inputs, then exploiting this subspace to explore only those directions in the input space that will actively inform parameter estimates. Furthermore, such an approach does not require one to determine which subsets of parameters will be fixed versus estimated prior to the inference procedure. In this talk, we illustrate how the use of such a subspace-based approach enables us to inform the maximum number of identifiable parameters (often a larger number than the number of dimensions utilized for inference), while still maintaining convergence properties of MCMC chains.  Additionally, we demonstrate the use of this methodology on a model of tumor growth dynamics for which the original parameter set is unidentifiable.
Authors
Allison L. Lewis, Lafayette College, U.S., lewisall@lafayette.edu
Rebecca Everett, Haverford College, U.S., reverett@haverford.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: All Emulators are Wrong, Many are Useful, and Some are More Useful Than Others
talks.abstract:
.
Accurate and efficient surrogate modeling is essential for modern computational science, and there are a staggering number of emulation methods to choose from. With new methods being developed all the time, comparing the relative strengths and weaknesses of different methods remains a challenge due to inconsistent benchmarking practices and (sometimes) limited reproducibility and transparency. In this work, we present a large-scale, fully reproducible comparison of $29$ distinct emulators across $60$ canonical test functions and $40$ real emulation datasets. To facilitate rigorous, apples-to-apples comparisons, we introduce the R package
duqling
, which streamlines reproducible simulation studies using a consistent, simple syntax, and automatic internal scaling of inputs. This framework allows researchers to compare emulators in a unified environment and makes it possible to replicate or extend previous studies with minimal effort, even across different publications. Our results provide detailed empirical insight into the strengths and weaknesses of state-of-the-art emulators and offer guidance for both method developers and practitioners selecting a surrogate for new data. We discuss best practices for emulator comparison and highlight how
duqling
can accelerate research in emulator design and application.

talks.title: Statistical Emulation with Input Dimension Reduction for Complex Forward Models
talks.abstract:
.
Gaussian process (GP) modeling in high-dimensional input space can result in inefficient emulators and is computationally challenging for a given budget. To tackle these issues, we introduce a fully Bayesian framework that seamlessly integrates dimensionality reduction with GP modeling and inference. Our approach, built on a hierarchical Bayesian model with priors on the Stiefel manifold. Additionally, we introduce a Deep Gaussian Processes (DGP) with built-in dimension reduction, providing a more flexible and powerful tool for complex datasets. We demonstrate that the proposed method improves predictive performance and uncertainty quantification, providing a principled and robust alternative to existing methods.

Alex Konomi, University of Cincinnati, U.S., alex.konomi@uc.edu

Emily L. Kang, University of Cincinnati, U.S., kangel@ucmail.uc.edu

talks.title: Improving Scalability of Multilevel Markov Chain Monte Carlo Using Multiple Proposals

speakers.name: Hillary Fairbanks
speakers.affiliation: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S.; Panayot Vassilevski, Portland State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Model-based simulations are an important tool for predicting physical phenomena. In the presence of uncertain physical parameters, which describe the system of interest, accurately assessing confidence in these results, e.g., performing Bayesian inference, can be computationally expensive, particularly when the uncertain physical parameters are spatially varying quantities. Not only are the corresponding high-fidelity (fine grid) simulations expensive, but commonly used statistical approaches, such as Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), require an exceedingly high number of simulations.
In this work, we develop a new scalable sampling method which complements the algorithmic framework of multilevel MCMC, where coarse grid simulations are used to inform the fine level proposal distribution, thereby accelerating MCMC. Specifically, using tools from algebraic multigrid, we form a (scalable) fine grid Gaussian random field realization from a fine grid proposal by combining Gaussian random fields sampled across multiple levels of discretization. To address the deterioration of coarse level scaling (which occurs in such settings), we leverage a combination of level-dependent redistribution and MCMC proposals performed in parallel. In this talk, we describe this new approach, corresponding theory, and numerical results when applied to a 3D subsurface flow application.
Authors
Hillary Fairbanks, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S., fairbanks5@llnl.gov
Panayot Vassilevski, Portland State University, U.S., panayot@pdx.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Software for Uncertainty Quantification - Part I of II

talks.title: UQLab - Recent Updates and a Rich Upcoming Roadmap
talks.abstract:
.
UQLab is a general purpose uncertainty quantification framework that has recently turned 10 since its first public beta release in July 2015. With multiple state-of-the-art modules ranging from surrogate modeling to advanced statistical inference, from reliability to sensitivity analysis, it is an ever evolving, actively developed software tool that has been adopted by thousands of users worldwide.
In this talk, we want to showcase some of the recent and upcoming additions to the software package, which include:
A powerful surrogate modeling benchmarking infrastructure, that allows researchers to compare their algorithms against the state-of-the-art on different case studies in a fair and standardized way;
A wide selection of new surrogate models, including multiple machine-learning methods such as neural networks and foundation models;
The ability of handling time-series data and models, including advanced surrogate models for dynamic data;
A connection to UM-Bridge, a powerful interface to highly increase interoperability between computational models and uncertainty quantification tools.
Authors
Stefano Marelli, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, marelli@ibk.baug.ethz.ch
Adéla Hlobilová, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, adela.hlobilova@ibk.baug.ethz.ch
Bruno Sudret, ETH Zurich, Switzerland, sudret@ibk.baug.ethz.ch
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: PyTUQ: UQ Tools and Workflows for Computational Models
talks.abstract:
.
The Python Toolkit for Uncertainty Quantification (PyTUQ, https://github.com/sandialabs/pytuq) is a lightweight Python library for a range of uncertainty quantification tasks and workflows. Features include conventional tools such as polynomial chaos machinery with mixed bases, global sensitivity analysis, quadrature point generation, linear regression, Bayesian inference with various flavors of Markov chain Monte Carlo. PyTUQ also includes advanced methods such as Bayesian compressed sensing, sampling-based Rosenblatt transformation and embedded model error calibration.
In this work, we will present the structure of the library, provide several educational examples, and demonstrate PyTUQ's application to various scientific problems, ranging from Earth system modeling to chemistry and materials science.
Authors
Khachik Sargsyan, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ksargsy@sandia.gov

Bert J. Debusschere, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., bjdebus@sandia.gov

talks.title: Variational Bayesian Inference in UncertaintyQuantification.jl

speakers.name: Jasper Behrensdorf
speakers.affiliation: Leibniz Universität Hannover, Germany; Ander Gray, UK Atomic Energy Authority, United Kingdom; Matteo Broggi

talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian updating plays a pivotal role in engineering disciplines such as structural health monitoring, geotechnical engineering, and system identification to update probabilistic models based on collected data.  The posterior distribution describing the updated knowledge on the uncertainties is typically analytically intractable and characterised only through sampling methods based on Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can be computationally prohibitive.
Stochastic transport maps for Bayesian inference offer a compelling alternative, overcoming this limitation by reformulating the Bayesian updating problem as a deterministic, invertible mapping between a simple reference distribution and the target posterior. This variational problem is then solved through sparse integration techniques to efficiently approximate the full posterior probability density function with significantly reduced likelihood evaluations compared to traditional MCMC.
This work presents the latest developments on variational Bayesian inference in the freely available Julia package UncertaintyQuantification.jl. Based on the strong foundation of the framework the implemented algorithms enable the parameter updating of complex high fidelity models. We present applications to two engineering problems, one involving interfacing with a third party solver, and  compare the accuracy and computational demand to classic MCMC methods.
Authors

Matteo Broggi, Leibniz University Hannover, Germany, broggi@irz.uni-hannover.de
Michael Beer, Leibniz University Hannover, Germany, beer@irz.uni-hannover.de

sessions.title: Robust and Scalable Approaches to Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design - Part I of II

talks.title: Nonlinear Oed with Column Subset Selection

speakers.name: Vishwas Rao
speakers.affiliation: Argonne National Laboratory, U.S.; Arvind Saibaba, North Carolina State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We address optimal sensor placement for Bayesian nonlinear inverse problems by formulating the task as a matrix column subset selection problem. The design matrix is derived from the expected information gain criterion. Although the resulting solutions are not necessarily globally optimal, the approach presents a rapid time to solution. The effectiveness of the method is demonstrated on nonlinear model problems.

talks.title: Model-Misspecification-Aware Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Andrea Scarinci
speakers.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S.; Ricardo Babtista, University of Toronto, Canada; Han Cheng Lie, Universität Potsdam, Germany

talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design (BOED) seeks to select an experimental design that provides the most informative data for estimating unknown parameters in the context of Bayesian inverse problems. All frameworks of BOED, however, rely on the assumption of a well-specified model i.e. the model used for inference is capable of capturing the true distributions of the data and its relationship with the parameters of interest. In real-world applications this is however rarely the case: errors or approximations are a natural part of the process. For BOED specifically, this means optimizing (or over-optimizing) experiment design based on wrong assumptions. In this paper we discuss a setting in which several linear Gaussian models are available to represent a certain phenomenon of interest and it is not possible to assess with certainty which is the best one to use. For inversion, a single model choice will have to be made (due to computational cost, for example). However, for the optimal sensor choice, we propose a methodology that can take this uncertainty among the input models into account and lead to a "misspecification-aware" sensor choice. We show how this sensor choice produces, on average, posterior distributions with less bias and variance than those obtained with a classic, well-specified, BOED formulation.
Authors
Andrea Scarinci, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, U.S., scarinci@mit.edu
Ricardo Babtista, University of Toronto, Canada, r.baptista@utoronto.ca
Han Cheng Lie, Universität Potsdam, Germany, hanlie@uni-potsdam.de
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: A Bilinear Inverse Problem with Forward Operator Inaccuracy Applied to Optical Imaging
talks.abstract:
.
Linear inverse problems are common in real-world applications from industry to medical imaging, but handling inaccuracies in the associated forward operators is a relatively unstudied problem. In this work, we assume a set of candidate forward operators and suggest principal component analysis for modeling their variation from the mean, which leads to a bilinear tensor inverse problem. We present two optimization algorithms and Gibbs sampling for approximately solving the problem. As a real-world test case, we apply the algorithms to account for the anatomical inaccuracy that is present in the sensitivity profiles or Jacobian matrices in diffuse optical tomography when an atlas-based model of the head geometry is used instead of the subject's own anatomical model. We demonstrate improvements in the spatial localization and contrast-to-noise-ratio in example reconstructions of simulated brain activity. In addition, Bayesian optimal experimental design that is robust to anatomical variations is discussed for optode placement in diffuse optical tomography.
Authors
Aada Hakula, Aalto University, Finland, aada.hakula@aalto.fi
Pauliina Hirvi, Aalto University, Finland, pauliina.hirvi@aalto.fi
Nuutti Hyvonen, Aalto University, Finland, nuutti.hyvonen@aalto.fi

talks.title: Multi-Fidelity Budget Constrained Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Xun Huan
speakers.affiliation: University of Michigan, U.S.; Tommie Catanach, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
In many scientific and engineering domains, data collection can be expensive and time-consuming, particularly when high-fidelity simulations or experiments are involved. Multifidelity modeling offers a powerful solution by combining data from multiple sources of varying accuracy and cost, potentially enabling more efficient learning. This work introduces a multi-fidelity Gaussian Process framework to jointly model many fidelities of data for optimal experimental design under strict budget constraints. The focus is on developing dynamic, cost-aware policies that guide the sequential selection of experiments to maximize an information-based utility. A central challenge addressed in this work is how to effectively represent the state of the design process in settings where decision sequences and resource usage evolve dynamically. These contributions are broadly applicable and provide a foundation for future extensions to more complex, constrained decision-making settings.
