sessions.title: Recent Advances in Inverse Methods for Complex Systems under Uncertainty - Part I of II

talks.title: An Adaptive Ensemble Smoother – Multiple Data Assimilation (ES-MDA) Method for Carbon Capture Storage Reservoirs

speakers.name: Matthias Morzfeld
speakers.affiliation: Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S.; Chaoyi Wang, Joshua A. White, Chris Sherman

speakers.name: Christina Morency
speakers.affiliation: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, U.S.; Robert Mellors, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We present an adaptive Ensemble Smoother – Multiple Data Assimilation (ES-MDA) strategy within the context of carbon capture and storage (CCS) reservoirs, where the goal is characterization of subsurface field properties. In this study, we compare our findings to state-of-the-art approaches for iterating the ES to convergence. Our results include inversion performance metrics of the proposed adaptive scheme and existing strategies on a 2D field-scale reservoir injection setup. In geologic carbon sequestration applications, observations can include field measurements such as well pressure history as well as derived products from seismic acquisitions and cross-well electrical resistivity measurements. A variety of these data can then be combined to infer reservoir features such as the evolution of CO 2 gas-plume spread through time. Informed risk assessment is critical to the success of proposed reservoir development projects. Ensemble techniques address this issue in providing access to the approximate posterior probability distribution function (pdf), enabling sampling of matched history models, and evaluation of uncertainty. Our primary contribution is an adaptive formulation of ES-MDA that reduces the number of required data assimilation steps and corresponding forward model evaluations, while still obtaining a meaningful UQ.

talks.title: Control-Oriented Optimal Sensor Placement for Infinite-Dimensional Bayesian Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Arvind Saibaba
speakers.affiliation: North Carolina State University, U.S.; Bart G. Van Bloemen Waanders

talks.abstract:
.
We propose a control-oriented optimal experimental design (cOED) framework for infinite-dimensional Bayesian linear inverse problems. The focus is on designing sensor placements in the service of optimal control problems where uncertain parameters must be inferred from sensor data. Conventional Bayesian OED methods typically generate sensor placements that minimize the uncertainty in the inferred parameter. However, such designs are often suboptimal for demands of the optimal control problem. In contrast, our cOED approach seeks experimental designs that minimize the uncertainty in either the controlled state variable or the control objective. We establish a mathematical framework for uncertainty quantification and cOED applicable to parameterized PDE-constrained control problems with linear dependencies on the control and inversion parameters. Furthermore, we develop scalable computational methods for determining these control-oriented sensor placements and for assessing the resulting uncertainty in the control objective. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in the context of a model problem motivated by heat transfer applications.

Rebekah White, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., rebwhit@sandia.gov

talks.title: Solving the Stochastic Inverse Problem As Optimization Over the Probability Measure Space

speakers.name: Qin Li
speakers.affiliation: University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S.; Maria Oprea, Cornell University, U.S.; Li Wang, University of Minnesota, U.S.; Yunan Yang , Cornell University, U.S. Cancelled

talks.abstract:
.
We investigate the stochastic inverse problem: Given $\rho_y$, how to find $\rho_x$ such that $\rho_y= G_\#\rho_x$, where probability distributions $\rho_x$ and $\rho_y$ are connected via the forward map $G$. Depending on $G$'s property, the solution can have drastically different behavior. In the overdetermined case, we formulate a variational problem $\min_{\rho_x}  D(G_\#\rho_x, \rho_y)$, and find that different choices of the metric $D$ significantly affect the quality of the reconstruction. When $D$ is set to be the Wasserstein distance, the reconstruction is the marginal distribution, while setting $D$ to be a $\phi$-divergence reconstructs the conditional distribution. In the underdetermined case, we formulate the constrained optimization $\min_{\{G_\#\rho_x=\rho_y\}}E[\rho_x]$. The choice of $ E$ also significantly impacts the construction: setting $E$ to be the entropy gives us the piecewise constant reconstruction, while setting $E$ to be the second moment, we recover the classical least-norm solution. We also study the case with regularization: $\min_{\rho_x} D(G_\#\rho_x, \rho_y) + \alpha \mathsf R[\rho_x]$, and find that the entropy-entropy pair leads to a regularized solution that is defined in a piecewise manner, whereas the $W_2$-$W_2$ pair leads to a least-norm solution where $W_2$ is the 2-Wasserstein metric.
Authors
Qin Li, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., qinli@math.wisc.edu
Maria Oprea, Cornell University, U.S., mao237@cornell.edu
Li Wang, University of Minnesota, U.S., wang8818@umn.edu
Yunan Yang, Cornell University, U.S., yunan.yang@cornell.edu

sessions.title: Inference and Uncertainty Quantification in Mathematical Physiology and Medicine - Part I of II

talks.title: Parameter Identifiability of a Hemodynamics Model Using Dimension Reduction and Emulation
talks.abstract:
.
Biophysical models have immense potential in providing new tools for the diagnosis and management of disease. This includes cardiovascular monitoring. However, cardiovascular simulations typically require numerous parameters and suffer from limited data. This leads to issues in parameter identifiability, which renders parameters uninterpretable. Assessing identifiability is difficult due to expensive forward solves from the model. Here, we overcome this limitation by using dimension reduction and emulation to expedite computation. We use polynomial chaos expansions (PCEs) on the principal components of a blood flow PDE model to provide a succinct platform for forward model solutions. We use this framework to assess model sensitivity, and follow with a formal identifiability analysis using the profile-likelihood. We show how experimental design choice drastically changes identifiability in the context of blood flow monitoring.
Authors
Mitchel J. Colebank, University of South Carolina, U.S., mjcolebank@sc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Bayesian Information-Theoretic Approach to Determine Effective Scanning Protocols of Cancer Patients

speakers.name: Heyrim Cho
speakers.affiliation: Arizona State University, U.S.; Tin Phan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Tingting Tang, San Diego State University, U.S.; Kathleen Storey

talks.abstract:
.
The aspect of limited temporal data is one of the many challenges when dealing with clinical data. The amount of data that can be practically collected in everyday patients during the therapy is very limited due to the financial cost and the patient’s burden. This motivates us to transfer the mathematical and computational models to meet the challenges in clinical data, before we use them to guide patient therapy via prediction. In this talk, I will discuss modeling approaches to tackle this problem. For instance, I will discuss a Bayesian information-theoretic approach to determine effective scanning protocols of cancer patients. We propose a modified mutual information function with a temporal penalty term to account for the loss of temporal data. The effectiveness of our framework is demonstrated in determining scanning scheduling for radiotherapy and androgen suppression therapy patients.

talks.title: InVAErt Networks: Model Synthesis and Identifiability Analysis for Scientific Agents

speakers.name: Guoxiang Tong
speakers.affiliation: Stanford University, U.S.; Carlos Sing Long, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Chile; Daniele E. Schiavazzi , University of Notre Dame, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Applications of generative modeling and deep learning in physics-based systems have traditionally focused on building emulators - computationally inexpensive approximations of input-to-output maps. However, the remarkable flexibility of data-driven architectures opens opportunities to broaden their scope to include model inversion and identifiability analysis.
We present InVAErt networks, a framework for data-driven analysis and synthesis of parametric physical systems. Through numerical experiments, we demonstrate the framework's versatility across a wide range of problems, including linear systems of equations, spatio-temporal PDEs, and lumped-parameter physiological models. We further introduce an extension for systems with observational noise, enabling the separation of structural from practical identifiability in complex ill-posed inverse problems.
Finally, we discuss recent efforts to integrate InVAErt networks with large language model agents for applications in cardiovascular health.
Authors
Guoxiang Tong, Stanford University, U.S., gxus34@stanford.edu
Carlos Sing Long, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Chile, casinglo@uc.cl
Daniele E. Schiavazzi, University of Notre Dame, U.S., dschiavazzi@nd.edu

sessions.title: Hybrid AI-Statistical Approaches for Environmental Spatio-Temporal Processes

talks.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Model with a Physics Informed Neural Process for Wind Assimilation

speakers.name: Joshua S. North
speakers.affiliation: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, U.S.; Myungsoo Yoo, University of Texas at Austin, U.S.; Giri Gopalan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Christopher Wikle, University of Missouri, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Recently, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have gained popularity due to their ability to represent complex physical phenomena parameterized using partial differential equations (PDEs). This is particularly useful in scientific applications where there is a priori knowledge, and incorporating process dynamics provide physically realistic constraints to the solution. However, PINN-based methods rarely account for data or process uncertainty, limiting their use for real-world applications. The notion of including physical information in data-driven methods is well established within the field of statistics, with multiple distinct paradigms that can be used to account for mechanistic information and realistically quantify uncertainty from multiple sources. However, the statistical methods are limited in that realistic dynamics can be computationally difficult to implement. This talk presents a flexible Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework with a neural dynamic process that is able to account for stochastic process discrepancies, observation uncertainty, and parameter uncertainty. The framework is applied to sparsely observed wind data from multiple sources over the Mediterranean Sea, e.g. in situ and satellite measurements, to assimilate the data sources and produce accurate wind field estimates.
Authors
Joshua S. North, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, U.S., jsnorth@lbl.gov
Myungsoo Yoo, University of Texas at Austin, U.S., myungsoo.yoo@austin.utexas.edu
Giri Gopalan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., ggopalan@lanl.gov
Christopher Wikle, University of Missouri, U.S., wiklec@missouri.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Characterizing Extreme Weather in a Huge Ensemble of Machine Learning Weather Forecasts
talks.abstract:
.
Ensemble weather forecasts offer a promising avenue to calculate the statistics and drivers of high-impact extreme weather events. Current ensemble systems presently use up to 100 members, but larger ensembles could enrich the sampling of internal variability. Due to computational constraints, it is infeasible to generate huge ensembles (comprised of 1,000-10,000 members) with traditional, physics-based numerical models. We replace traditional numerical simulations with machine learning to generate hindcasts of huge ensembles. We construct a calibrated ensemble weather system that represents model uncertainty through perturbed-parameter techniques, and it represents initial condition uncertainty through bred vectors, which sample the fastest growing modes of the forecast.
Using the Spherical Fourier Neural Operator architecture, we generate a huge ensemble (HENS), with 7,424 members initialized each day of summer 2023, the second hottest summer in at least the last 2000 years. HENS samples events 4s away from the ensemble mean. We use HENS to characterize the spread in atmospheric river  intensity and impacts in the Southern Hemisphere. We also apply HENS to ascertain whether tropical cyclones obey conventional or anomalous diffusion subject to large-scale eddies in the general circulation. We use HENS to characterize near-miss counterfactual heatwaves that were plausible in summer 2023.
Authors
Ankur Mahesh, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory and University of California Berkeley, U.S., amahesh@lbl.gov
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Fast Global-Local Reduced Order Models Using Neural Galerkin-Projections for Improved Tsunami Forecasting

speakers.name: Arvind T. Mohan
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Darren Engwirda, CSIRO, Australia

talks.abstract:
.
Computationally expensive numerical solutions of partial difference equation models (PDE)s are critical in understanding complex real-world phenomena. However, the utility of these numeric simulations in many applications is limited by the computational cost of running these models over a breadth of initial conditions necessary to characterize the space of feasible solutions. We propose a novel surrogate framework RANDPROM that combines cutting-edge reduced-order approximations of PDEs within a Bayesian hierarchical model that enables accurate and precise predictions of the PDE solutions at initial conditions which are not included in training data. Using simulations of tsunami wave height as an example dataset, we demonstrate the potential for RANDPROM to produce near real-time predictions of wave height which demonstrates the potential of the RANDPROM surrogate framework to contribute to real-world decision making.
Authors
John Tipton, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., jtipton@lanl.gov
Shane Coffing, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., sxc@lanl.gov
Arvind T. Mohan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., arvindm@lanl.gov
Darren Engwirda, CSIRO, Australia, d.engwirda@gmail.com

talks.title: Efficient Cut-Bayes Computation for Uncertainty Quantification

speakers.name: Giri Gopalan
speakers.affiliation: Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S.; Joseph Mathews, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Many complex physical systems, including those from the environment, can be modeled as a collection of interconnected modules where each one represents a crucial piece of physics that drives the entire system. For instance, these modules could correspond to atmospheric, ocean, and wave components in an earth system model. One way to enable uncertainty quantification in such multiphysics models is with a cut-Bayesian approach (or more generally, a modular or generalized Bayesian approach). This can help address identifiability and mitigate the possibility of misspecification corrupting inferences. Nonetheless, computation is a challenge for cut Bayesian inference, and in this talk we will discuss a recently published sequential Monte Carlo method that comes with finite sample guarantees. We also discuss connections to inference with AI (e.g., deep learning) models.
Authors
Giri Gopalan, Los Alamos National Laboratory, U.S., ggopalan@lanl.gov
Joseph Mathews, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, U.S., jrm133@unc.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Advances in Sequential Inference for Dynamical Systems - Part I of II

talks.title: The Landscape of Sequential Inference for Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Sequential inference underlies the analysis of stochastic dynamical systems in many areas of applied mathematics and data science. In state-space models, latent states evolve in time and are observed through noisy and partial measurements, which leads to recursive Bayesian inference under modeling and computational constraints. This lecture surveys the principal approaches to sequential inference from a unified mathematical perspective. The presentation begins with the linear–Gaussian case, where the Kalman filter provides an exact and tractable solution that serves as a reference point. It then examines nonlinear and non-Gaussian models and the approximations they require. Gaussian filters, sequential Monte Carlo methods, and structured variants that exploit conditional independence, Rao–Blackwellization, and low-dimensional structure are compared in terms of accuracy, stability, and computational complexity. Deterministic alternatives based on variational approximations and message-passing formulations are also discussed. The lecture concludes with a discussion of fundamental limitations, including dimensional scaling and filter stability, and emphasizes the role of model structure to enable tractable inference. The goal is to clarify the relationships among methods and to identify open challenges in sequential inference for complex dynamical systems.
Authors
Petar Djuric, Stony Brook University, U.S., petar.djuric@stonybrook.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Fast and Reliable Variational Inference for Uncovering Latent Dynamical Systems
talks.abstract:
.
Latent dynamical systems provide a powerful framework for uncovering hidden structure in complex time series, with applications spanning engineering, neuroscience, and beyond. However, inference in these models is often slow and numerically unstable, limiting their scalability in real-world settings. In this talk, I will introduce SING, a variational inference algorithm that leverages natural gradient updates to exploit the geometric structure of latent stochastic differential equation models, enabling fast, stable, and parallelizable inference. I will demonstrate how SING produces accurate estimates of latent trajectories and nonlinear drift functions, including those modeled with interpretable Gaussian process priors. Across synthetic and real datasets, SING consistently improves both convergence speed and inference accuracy, advancing our ability to recover meaningful dynamical structure from high-dimensional, noisy sequential data.
Authors
Amber Hu, Stanford University, U.S., amberhu@stanford.edu

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Reduction Modeling and Computation for Multiscale Problems - Part III of III

talks.title: Relative Entropy Methods for Computing Committor Functions
talks.abstract:
.
Motivated by challenges arising in molecular simulation, we analyze and develop methods of computing reactive trajectories and committor functions for systems described by the overdamped Langevin dynamics. Our main technical advance is a new loss function that measures the accuracy of approximations to the committor function related to a given chemical reaction or other rare transition event. Our loss admits a simple interpretation in terms of the distribution of reactive trajectories, and it can be computed in practice to compare the accuracies of different approximations of the committor. We also derive a method of calculating committors by direct minimization of the loss via stochastic gradient descent.
Authors
Brian Van Koten, University of Massachusetts, Amherst, U.S., bvankoten@umass.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Mean Square Error Analysis of Stochastic Gradient and Variance-Reduced Sampling Algorithms
talks.abstract:
.
This talk presents a rigorous analysis of the mean square error (MSE) for stochastic gradient sampling algorithms applied to underdamped Langevin dynamics under a global convexity assumption. We introduce a novel discrete Poisson equation framework to systematically decompose and bound the time-averaged sampling error. For the primary algorithm, Stochastic Gradient UBU (SG-UBU), we establish an explicit MSE bound and demonstrate that its numerical bias exhibits first-order convergence with respect to the step size h. The leading error coefficient is shown to be directly proportional to the variance of the stochastic gradient. The analysis is extended to variance-reduced algorithms for finite-sum potentials, specifically SVRG-UBU and SAGA-UBU. For these methods, we uncover a distinctive phase transition phenomenon: the convergence rate of the numerical bias shifts from first-order to second-order as the step size decreases below a critical threshold. Numerical experiments validate these theoretical findings. Finally, our analysis provides a practical empirical criterion for algorithm selection between mini-batch SG-UBU and mini-batch SVRG-UBU to optimize computational cost.
Authors
Xuda Ye, Purdue University, U.S., ye481@purdue.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Physiology-Constrained Uncertainty Quantification for Glucose Dynamics in Type 1 Diabetes
talks.abstract:
.
Glucose–insulin regulation in Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is characterized by nonlinear dynamics, substantial individual variability, and pervasive uncertainty in both physiological processes and sensor measurements. Developing reliable predictive models and decision-support tools requires mathematical frameworks that can rigorously quantify, propagate, and interpret uncertainty while remaining consistent with known biological constraints. This project advances uncertainty quantification (UQ) for nonlinear biological systems by formulating biologically plausible uncertainty representations, establishing constraints that preserve physiological realism, and developing methods for stable uncertainty propagation through dynamical models. The approach integrates traditional differential-equation models with modern computational tools to produce probabilistic forecasts and risk metrics that remain faithful to underlying physiology. The resulting framework provides broadly applicable UQ methodologies for complex biological systems, with T1D serving as a motivating and clinically relevant application.
Authors
Yixiang Deng, University of Delaware, U.S., yixiangd@udel.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Dynamically Adapted Regularization for Autoencoders with Observed SDE dynamics

speakers.name: Felix Ye
speakers.affiliation: State University of New York, Albany, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce two dynamics-adapted regularizers that improve the accuracy and out-of-manifold generalization of autoencoders trained on manifold-valued stochastic dynamics. Assuming access to an SDE solver and to extrinsic estimates—sample points on the manifold together with their drift vectors and local covariance matrices—we augment the autoencoder objective with (i) an Itô-consistency term that enforces agreement between the decoder’s infinitesimal predictions and the first- and second-order moments prescribed by Itô's formula, and (ii) a geometry-consistency term that aligns the learned drift with the manifold’s tangent space while penalizing normal components and mismatches between decoded covariances and the induced metric. Together these terms couple stochastic moment constraints with manifold geometry, yielding representations whose learned dynamics transfer more reliably across benchmarks. We demonstrate improved generalization on standard examples of manifold SDEs.
Authors
Felix Ye, State University of New York, Albany, U.S., xye2@albany.edu

talks.title: Bias and Pitfalls in Aligning Large Language Models: Optimization and Game-Theoretic Perspectives
talks.abstract:
.
Large language models (LLMs) are aligned with human preferences through reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). However, alignment is empirically observed to introduce bias, degrade calibration, and only imperfectly reflect diverse human preferences. In this talk, I will present recent progress toward a theoretical foundation of LLM alignment. First, I will show how the current form of RLHF induces a bias we call preference collapse, and how this can be mitigated through a novel regularization term added to the reward function. Second, I will revisit RLHF through the lens of social choice theory and present a game-theoretic perspective, highlighting its violations of classical axiomatic consistency. This talk is based on joint work in arXiv:2405.16455, 2503.10990, 2505.20627, and 2506.12350.
Authors
Weijie Su, University of Pennsylvania, U.S., suw@wharton.upenn.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards: Grpo's Effective Loss, Fixed Point Iteration and Success Amplification
talks.abstract:
.
Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) was introduced recently and used successfully to train DeepSeek-R1 models for promoting reasoning capabilities of LLMs using verifiable or binary rewards. We show in this paper that GRPO with verifiable rewards can be written as a Kullback–Leibler (KL) regularized contrastive loss, where the contrastive samples are synthetic data sampled from the old policy. The optimal GRPO policy at time n can be expressed explicitly in terms of the binary reward, as well as the first- and second-order statistics of the old policy (at time n-1) and the reference policy . Iterating this scheme, we obtain a sequence of policies for which we can quantify the probability of success as function of n. We show that the probability of success of the policy satisfies a recurrence that converges to a fixed point of a function that depends on the initial probability of success and the regularization parameter beta of the KL regularizer. We show that the success rate corresponding to this fixed point is guaranteed to be larger than the reference policy's success rate, thereby demonstrating that GRPO effectively amplifies the probability of success of the policy.
Authors
Youssef Mroueh, IBM Research, U.S., mroueh@us.ibm.com
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Simple and Effective Diffusion Language Models
talks.abstract:
.
We are at a pivotal moment in language modeling, as autoregressive (AR) LLMs face growing competition from diffusion-based approaches. I will present the Masked Diffusion Language Model (MDLM), which introduces a new training strategy and a simplified Rao-Blackwellized objective that significantly improves discrete diffusion performance, bringing it close to AR models on standard log-likelihood benchmarks. Unlike AR methods, diffusion models offer advantages in long-term planning, controllable generation, and sampling efficiency. Recent releases such as Google’s Gemini Diffusion, Inception Labs’ Mercury Coder, and ByteDance’s Seed Diffusion—which is built on the MDLM framework—underscore this paradigm shift, signaling that diffusion may soon overtake AR as the dominant framework for language generation.
Authors
Subham Sahoo, Cornell Tech University, U.S., ssahoo@cs.cornell.edu

sessions.title: Data Efficient Learning - Part II of II

talks.title: Belted and Ensembled Neural Network for Linear and Nonlinear Sufficient Dimension Reduction

speakers.name: Yin Tang
speakers.affiliation: University of Kentucky, U.S.; Bing Li, Pennsylvania State University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We introduce a unified, flexible, and easy-to-implement framework of sufficient dimension reduction that can accommodate both linear and nonlinear dimension reduction, and both the conditional distribution and the conditional mean as the targets of estimation. This unified framework is achieved by a specially structured neural network – the Belted and Ensembled Neural Network (BENN) – that consists of a narrow latent layer, which we call the belt, and a family of transformations of the response, which we call the ensemble. By strategically placing the belt at different layers of the neural network, we can achieve linear or nonlinear sufficient dimension reduction, and by choosing the appropriate transformation families, we can achieve dimension reduction for the conditional distribution or the conditional mean. Moreover, thanks to the advantage of the neural network, the method is very fast to compute, overcoming a computation bottleneck of the traditional sufficient dimension reduction estimators, which involves the inversion of a matrix of dimension either $p$ or $n$. We develop the algorithm and convergence rate of our method, compare it with existing sufficient dimension reduction methods, and apply it to two data examples.
Authors
Yin Tang, University of Kentucky, U.S., yin.tang@uky.edu
Bing Li, Pennsylvania State University, U.S., bxl9@psu.edu

talks.title: Efficient Estimation of Information Theoretic Quantities and Their Application in Generative Models
talks.abstract:
.
Information theoretic quantities play a central role in machine learning. The recent surge in the complexity of data and models has increased the demand for accurate estimation of these quantities. However, as the dimension grows the estimation presents significant challenges, with existing methods struggling already in relatively low dimensions. In this talk, we introduce REMEDI for efficient and accurate estimation of differential entropy, a fundamental information theoretic quantity. The approach combines the minimization of the cross-entropy for simple, adaptive base models and the estimation of their deviation, in terms of the relative entropy, from the data density. Our approach demonstrates improvement across a broad spectrum of entropy estimation tasks. We illustrate how the framework can be naturally extended to information theoretic supervised learning models, with a specific focus on the Information Bottleneck approach. In addition, we explore a natural connection between REMEDI and generative modeling using rejection sampling and Langevin dynamics.
Authors
Anirban Samaddar, Argonne National Laboratory, U.S., asamaddar@anl.gov

sessions.title: Theory, Computational Methods, and Applications of Measure Transport for Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of II

talks.title: Error Analysis of Triangular Optimal Transport Maps for Nonlinear Filtering

speakers.name: Michele Martino
speakers.affiliation: University of Washington, U.S.; Amirhossein Taghvaei, University of Washington, Seattle, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The stochastic filtering problem seeks to recover the state of a dynamical system from sequential, noisy observations. Classical approaches such as the Kalman and particle filters often suffer from instability, sampling inefficiency, or intractability in non-linear, non-Gaussian regimes. The Optimal Transport Filter (OTF) circumvents these issues by reformulating the Bayesian update as a conditional optimal transport (COT) problem: the posterior distribution at each step is obtained by pushing forward prior samples through a learned approximation of the COT map. In this paper, we: 1) Extend recent optimal transport map estimation rates to the conditional setting of COT. 2) Introduce a conditional extension tailored to Bayesian inversion in sequential settings to provide the first statistically complete error analysis of the OTF. 3) Investigate and derive sufficient regularity assumptions on the dynamics and observation models defining the filtering problem to obtain quantitative guarantees on the fidelity of posterior approximation, thereby placing the OTF within a principled theoretical framework. 4) Introduce regularization techniques to close the gap between the theoretical and numerical algorithms. These results sharpen the understanding of transport-based inference, bridge connections between stochastic filtering and conditional optimal transport, and inform the design of provably reliable algorithms for high-dimensional Bayesian estimation.
Authors
Mohammad Al-Jarrah, University of Washington, U.S., mohd9485@uw.edu

Niyizhen Jin, University of Washington, U.S., njin2@uw.edu
Michele Martino, University of Washington, U.S., michem29@uw.edu
Amirhossein Taghvaei, University of Washington, Seattle, U.S., amirtag@uw.edu

talks.title: On Some Foundational Issues in Uncertainty Control

speakers.name: Mahmoud Abdelgalil
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The remarkable success of closed-loop control in mitigating the effect of uncertainty on a system’s performance has undoubtedly enabled much of the technological world around us. While the mitigation of uncertainty has been at the heart of control theory since its inception, explicit control of uncertainty is a relatively recent development that has garnered much attention. In this, a main object of study is the Liouville (continuity) equation – the PDE governing the evolution of the probability distribution of the state of a dynamical system. While it was widely believed that the basic question of controllability of the Liouville equation had been resolved, it escaped the community’s attention for almost two decades that early investigations on the subject came short of providing a satisfactory answer, even for linear systems. In this talk, we revisit and address this topic and develop a theory for Collective Steering, the endeavor to shepherd an ensemble of dynamical systems between desired configurations using a common feedback law. Our investigation sheds light on a topological obstruction at the heart of the issue that limits the ability to design feedback control laws that are globally continuous with respect to the specifications. Along the way, we touch upon an elegant geometric framework at the intersection of optimal transport, geometric hydrodynamics, and quantum mechanics.
Authors
Mahmoud Abdelgalil, University of California, San Diego, U.S., mabdelgalil@ucsd.edu

talks.title: Wasserstein Least Squares Regression
talks.abstract:
.
We present Wasserstein Least Squares Regression (WLSR), a model that canonically extends least squares regression in the presence of vector-valued covariates and distribution-valued responses. Unlike competing proposals, which focus on the linear structure in the  space of probability measures, ours works directly with the functional form of linear regression. In this talk, we will delve into the geometry of WLSR and draw connections with the theory of Mixed linear models.
Authors
Uriel Martinez Leon, New York University, U.S., um2065@nyu.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification and Digital Twins for Earth Science - Part III of III

talks.title: Boosting the Representation Accuracy of Neural Networks for Multiscale Problems
talks.abstract:
.
Deep learning techniques are increasingly applied to scientific problems where the precision of networks is crucial. Despite being deemed as universal function approximators, neural networks, in practice, struggle to reduce prediction errors below a certain threshold even with large network sizes and extended training iterations. We developed an algorithm to tackle this issue and demonstrate that the prediction error from multi-stage training can nearly reach the machine precision of double-floating point. This mitigates the longstanding accuracy limitations of neural network training and can be used to address the spectral bias in multiscale problems.  I will discuss an application where neural networks are used to solve geophysical inverse problems, leveraging large-scale Earth observations to uncover constitutive laws for polar ice.
Authors
Ching-Yao Lai, Stanford University, U.S., cyaolai@stanford.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Modeling Partially Observed Nonlinear Dynamical Systems Via Conditional Gaussian Koopman Network
talks.abstract:
.
Deep learning has been widely applied to model and predict complex dynamical systems across various scientific and engineering domains. However, the black-box nature of these models poses significant challenges for outer-loop applications such as data assimilation (DA). Integrating ensemble DA methods with nonlinear deep learning models is often computationally expensive and prone to large sampling errors. To address these challenges, we propose a novel deep learning framework for simultaneous state forecasting and efficient data assimilation. The framework, called Conditional Gaussian Koopman Network (CGKN), transforms general nonlinear systems into partially linear systems with conditional Gaussian structures through data-driven Koopman operator. This conditional Gaussian structure provides analytical DA formulas that significantly accelerate DA and integrate it into model training. Overall, CGKN serves as a digital twin for nonlinear partially observed systems, and its capabilities are demonstrated on a variety of complex systems (including nonlinear PDEs) exhibiting chaos, multiscale features and intermittency.
Authors
Chuanqi Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., cchen656@wisc.edu
Zhongrui Wang, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., wang3262@wisc.edu
Nan Chen, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., chennan@math.wisc.edu
Jinlong Wu, University of Wisconsin-Madison, U.S., jinlong.wu@wisc.edu

talks.title: Advances in Surrogate-Accelerated Calibration for Quasi-Periodic Data
talks.abstract:
.
Simulating climate phenomena like the quasi-biennial oscillation (QBO) is challenging for Earth system models due to uncertain subgrid-scale processes like convective gravity waves. We present a novel workflow that improves the QBO’s representation in the Energy Exascale Earth System Model (E3SM) by using machine learning (ML) to systematically calibrate unresolved physics, replacing subjective tuning. Our methodology begins with the fundamental frequency model (FFM), a domain-informed statistical method we developed to compress high-dimensional, spatio-temporal wind field data. The FFM isolates the QBO signal into two physically interpretable quantities of interest: period and amplitude. We then develop an efficient Gaussian process surrogate, a probabilistic approximation of the full physics model that rigorously links E3SM to ML. This surrogate enables rapid, low-cost exploration of the parameter space. Our framework also facilitates model-predictive decision-making. By employing multi-objective optimization, we navigate the fundamental trade-off between achieving the correct QBO period and amplitude, quantifying a Pareto frontier of optimal solutions instead of a single parameter set. This integrated workflow has improved QBO simulation fidelity in E3SMv3 and provides a transferable template for incorporating ML to model quasi-periodic processes in complex Earth system models.
Authors
Luis Damiano, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ladamia@sandia.gov
Khachik Sargsyan, Sandia National Laboratories, U.S., ksargsy@sandia.gov

sessions.title: Recent Advancements in Operator Estimation and Learning - Part III of III

talks.title: Automatic RKHS for Learning Convolution Kernels

speakers.name: Fei Lu
speakers.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, U.S.; Haibo Li, University of Melbourne, Australia

talks.abstract:
.
Learning convolution kernels in operators from data arises in numerous applications and represents an ill-posed inverse problem of broad interest. With scant prior information, kernel methods offer a natural nonparametric approach with regularization. However, a major challenge is to select a proper reproducing kernel, especially as operators and data vary. We show that the input data and convolution operator themselves induce an automatic, data-adaptive RKHS (DA-RKHS), obviating manual kernel selection. In particular, when the observation data is discrete and finite, there is a finite set of automatic basis functions sufficient to represent the estimators in the DA-RKHS, including the minimal-norm least-squares, Tikhonov, and conjugate-gradient estimators. We develop both Tikhonov and scalable iterative, hybrid, and divide-and-conquer algorithms using the automatic basis functions. Numerical experiments on integral, nonlocal, and aggregation operators confirm that our automatic RKHS regularization consistently outperforms standard ridge regression and Gaussian process methods with preselected kernels.
Authors
Fei Lu, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., flu15@jh.edu
Haibo Li, University of Melbourne, Australia, haibo.li@unimelb.edu.au
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Randomized Methods for Operator Learning

speakers.name: Tracy Yu
speakers.affiliation: University of California, Los Angeles, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Operator learning is a technique for approximating operators between two infinite dimensional Banach spaces of functions using machine learning. One approach is a kernel-based method, which involves the use of a kernel function between pairs of data points. However, for large datasets, this becomes quite computationally expensive. We propose a regularized random Fourier feature method to learn operators in the regime of noisy data. The random feature method is an approximation to a kernel method that uses randomly sampled feature maps and produces comparable results with similar test errors but with significantly lower training times. We provide a detailed study of our proposed method with comprehensive numerical results.
Authors
Tracy Yu, University of California, Los Angeles, U.S., tracy@math.ucla.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: The Measure Theoretic Koopman Operator
talks.abstract:
.
The Stochastic Koopman (SKO) operator has been associated in literature with dynamical systems that exhibit stochastic behaviour. However, the SKO can only recover the expectation of an observable along the trajectory and requires tracking data for its computation. In this work, the Distributional Koopman Operator (DKO) is introduced as a novel framework for analyzing stochastic dynamical systems where only aggregate distribution data is available, avoiding the need for particle tracking. Our DKO framework extends the Koopman operator to act on observables of probability distributions, leveraging the transfer operator to propagate these distributions forward in time. We analyse the properties of this new operator, and show how one can recover information about higher order moments from this formulation. Lastly, we design an algorithm to compute the DKO from data.
Authors
Maria Oprea, Cornell University, U.S., mao237@cornell.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Approximation of the Electrical Impedance Tomography Inversion Operator by Neural Operators
talks.abstract:
.
We present the noise-robust neural operator approximation of the solution map for Calderón's inverse conductivity problem. In this continuum model of electrical impedance tomography (EIT), the boundary measurements are realized as a noisy perturbation of the Neumann-to-Dirichlet map's integral kernel. The theoretical analysis proceeds by extending the domain of the inversion operator to a Hilbert space of kernel functions making use of the Benyamin-Lindenstrauss theorem. The resulting extension shares the same stability properties as the original inverse map, but is now amenable to neural operator approximation. The methodology for EIT exemplifies a broader framework for addressing nonlinear inverse problems with a noise-aware operator learning framework. We give a universal approximation theorem with certain neural operators with explicit expression rates accounting for the modulus of continuity of the (inversion) operator to be approximated.
Joint research with N. Kovachki, M. Lassas and N. Nelsen.
Authors
Maarten de Hoop, Rice University, U.S., mvd2@rice.edu

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification in Data Assimilation Methods

talks.title: Neural Ensemble Kalman Filter: Data Assimilation for Compressible Flows with Shocks

speakers.name: Lorenzo Beronilla
speakers.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, U.S.; Michael Sleeman, California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Matthias Morzfeld, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, U.S.; Andrew Stuart, California Institute of Technology, U.S.; Tamer Zaki, Johns Hopkins University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Data assimilation (DA) in compressible flows with shocks is challenging. Standard DA methods, such as the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF), often generate oscillatory and nonphysical solutions near shocks. We introduce a latent-space formulation of the EnKF, termed the "neural EnKF," which enables robust DA in the presence of shocks. The neural EnKF operates on an ensemble of deep neural networks, each parameterizing a realization of the shocked flow field. The networks share the same architecture and are trained successively using a nearest-neighbor chain, which ensures that shocks in physical space correspond to smooth variations in neural parameter space. Observations in physical space (e.g., pressure) are then assimilated to update the network weights, yielding an analysis ensemble that represents physically consistent shocked flows informed by the data. We present systematic numerical DA experiments with the inviscid Burgers' equation and Sod's shock tube to demonstrate the robustness, efficiency, and accuracy of the neural EnKF, highlighting its ability to preserve sharp shock structures while avoiding spurious oscillations.
Authors

talks.title: Ensemble Score Filter for Data Assimilation of Two-Phase Flow Models in Porous Media
talks.abstract:
.
Numerical modeling and simulation of two-phase flow in porous media is challenging due to the uncertainties in key parameters, such as permeability. To address these challenges, we propose a computational framework by utilizing the novel Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to enhance the accuracy of state estimation for two-phase flow systems in porous  media. The forward simulation of the two-phase flow model is implemented using a mixed finite element method, which ensures accurate approximation of the pressure, the velocity, and the saturation. The EnSF leverages score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions efficiently, avoiding the computational expense of neural network-based methods. By incorporating a closed-form score approximation and an analytical update mechanism, the EnSF overcomes degeneracy issues and handles high-dimensional nonlinear filtering with minimal computational  overhead. Numerical experiments demonstrate the capabilities of EnSF in scenarios with uncertain permeability and incomplete observational data.
Authors
Ruoyu Hu, Florida State University, U.S., rhu2@fsu.edu

talks.title: Ensemble Kalman-Bucy Smoother and Applications
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk, we will present the mathematical formula for the ensemble Kalman-Bucy smoother, a continuous-time data assimilation method. We will show the stochastic version of the smoother. Then we will apply this method to various problems, including causal inference via uncertainty quantification and data assimilation, model identification and parameter estimation from partial observations, and state estimation for extreme events.

talks.title: Operator-Free Data Assimilation for Complex Measurement Processes
talks.abstract:
.
Data assimilation traditionally relies on a measurement operator, or forward model, to link model states to observations. In many applications, the measurement process is occluded by bias or is imperfectly known. We present a suite of operator-free strategies for estimating latent states directly from observations when the forward model is unknown. First, we describe deterministic surrogate observation models in which neural networks are trained to approximate the forward mapping from state space to observation space, providing flexible—but not necessarily operator-free—estimates. Second, we introduce a strategy that leverages kernel mean embeddings to represent conditional distributions in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, enabling nonparametric inference without explicit forward operators. Third, we explore probabilistic generative methods based on variational autoencoders, which learn latent representations that capture the joint structure of states and observations and can be constructed to accommodate measurement uncertainty. To demonstrate the scalability of these approaches, we show how they can be integrated with current filtering algorithms for high-dimensional geophysical systems, including numerical weather prediction, sea ice state estimation, and paleoclimate reconstruction. Results highlight trade-offs in accuracy, cost, and UQ across the three strategies, illustrating a path toward data assimilation for next-generation Earth System models.
Authors
Jonathan Poterjoy, University of Maryland, U.S., poterjoy@umd.edu
Raelynn Wonnacott, University of Maryland, U.S., raelynnw@umd.edu

Michael Evans, University of Maryland, U.S., mnevans@umd.edu

sessions.title: Dynamic Decisions under Uncertainty: Formulations, Algorithms, and Analysis

talks.title: New Optimality Conditions and an Indirect Shooting Method for Stochastic Optimal Control
talks.abstract:
.
Efficiently solving nonlinear stochastic optimal control problems remains a challenge with many applications. Existing optimality conditions typically rely on forward-backward stochastic differential equations (FBSDEs), which are difficult to use in algorithms. In this talk, I will present new optimality conditions for stochastic optimal control, derived using rough path theory. This Pontryagin Maximum Principle uses the same Hamiltonian as in the deterministic setting, while avoiding FBSDEs. This result unlocks the first indirect shooting method for stochastic optimal control, which only searches over the initial adjoint state and converges an order of magnitude faster than a direct method in numerical experiments. I will conclude with applications to driving at the limits of handling and future directions towards robust and efficient uncertainty-aware control.
Authors
Thomas Lew, Toyota Research Institute, U.S.	, thomas.lew@tri.global
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Neural Network Approaches for High-Dimensional Optimal Control and Transport Problems
talks.abstract:
.
We present neural network approaches for high dimensional optimal control and optimal transport problems. The Neural-HJB method approximates optimal policies, enabling real-time control while alleviating the computational burden of traditional approaches. Similarly, the COT-Flow method applies DNNs to solve dynamic conditional optimal transport problems, which is critical for applications like sampling and density estimation in Bayesian inference. Empirical results highlight the effectiveness of both methods, showcasing their performance compared to state-of-the-art alternatives.
Authors
Deepanshu Verma, Clemson University, U.S., dverma@clemson.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Approximation of Risk-Averse Optimal Feedback Control

speakers.name: Philipp A. Guth
speakers.affiliation: RICAM, Austrian Academy of Sciences, Austria; Karl Kunisch, University of Graz

talks.abstract:
.
The challenge of constructing feedback control laws for risk-averse optimal control of partial differential equations with random coefficients is addressed. The control objective composes a tracking-type cost with the nonlinear entropic risk measure. A sequential quadratic programming scheme is derived that iteratively solves linear quadratic subproblems obtained through second-order Taylor expansions of the objective functional, with each subproblem re-centered at the previous iterate. It is shown that this method converges locally quadratically to the unique risk-averse optimal control.
Authors
Philipp A. Guth, RICAM, Austrian Academy of Sciences, Austria, philipp.guth@ricam.oeaw.ac.at

talks.title: Central Limit Theorems for Sample Average Approximations in Stochastic Optimal Control

speakers.name: Johannes Milz
speakers.affiliation: Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.; Alexander Shapiro, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
We establish central limit theorems for the Sample Average Approximation (SAA) method in discrete-time, finite-horizon Stochastic Optimal Control. Using the dynamic programming principle and backward induction, we characterize the limiting distributions of the SAA value functions. The asymptotic variance at each stage decomposes into two components: a current-stage variance arising from immediate randomness, and a propagated future variance accumulated from subsequent stages. This decomposition clarifies how statistical uncertainty propagates backward through time. Our derivation relies on a stochastic equicontinuity condition, for which we provide sufficient conditions. We illustrate the variance decomposition using the classical Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) problem. Although its unbounded state and control spaces violate the compactness assumptions of our framework, the LQR setting enables explicit computation and visualization of both variance components.

sessions.title: Uncertainty Quantification for Complex Physical Systems and High-Dimensional Data - Part II of II

talks.title: Transport Maps for Scalable Uncertainty Quantification of Fine Resolution Remote-Sensing Data Products

speakers.name: Maggie Johnson
speakers.affiliation: NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S.; Ricardo Baptista, University of Toronto, Canada; Olga Kalashnikova, NASA, Jet Propulsion Laboratory, U.S.; Meredith Franklin, University of Toronto, Canada; Gregory Halverson

talks.abstract:
.
Remote sensing data are providing valuable information for science and applications at fine spatial and temporal resolution. In environmental applications, these utility of these science data products is often aided with complementary information from multiple instruments, as well as from geophysical models. This added utility can come at added computational cost with additional complexity for uncertainty quantification (UQ) due to variable fidelity of input uncertainty and spatio-temporal correlation. We present a framework for UQ for these derived products that combines generative flexibility of transport maps (TMs) with scalable spatial statistical methods for dense geophysical fields. The presentation will highlight the implementation for estimates of atmospheric aerosols arising from a remote sensing inverse problem for NASA’s Multi-Angle Imager for Aerosols mission. The mission will deliver multivariate aerosol products for 1-km footprints over a collection of target areas across global land areas.

talks.title: A Bayesian Point Process Tree Model for High-Dimensional Data
talks.abstract:
.
Spatial point patterns arise in many scientific applications from sociology to climate science and to biology. Statistical analysis of spatial point patterns often needs to deal with heterogeneous structures in intensity function estimation. Likelihood based inference for spatial point process models often needs to deal with computational and statistical challenges due to the evaluation of multidimensional integral of intensity function and nonstationarity modeling of intensity functions over possibly irregular domain. In this talk, I will introduce a Bayesian Poisson process tree (PPT) model that allows exact Bayesian inference without relying on integral approximation. In particular, the intensity function model is modeled via a tree structure that is endowed with adaptive shrinkage prior to local features. To allow efficient Bayesian inference, we develop a sequential Monte Carlo algorithm to allow efficient tree learning. The proposed method is demonstrated with various simulation studies and a real application in high-dimensional covariance space.
Authors
Pulong Ma, Iowa State University, U.S., plma@iastate.edu

sessions.title: Software for Uncertainty Quantification - Part II of II

talks.title: Uqpy Version 4.2: A Modular Python Framework for Uncertainty Quantification and Scientific Machine Learning
talks.abstract:
.
UQpy is an open-source Python library for conducting a wide range of uncertainty-quantification (UQ) studies on physical and mathematical systems. In its previous release, it comprises nine modules covering complementary aspects of UQ—from probability distributions and sampling to surrogate modeling of complex systems and state-of-the-art dimension-reduction methods such as Grassmann manifold projections. It adopts an enhanced object-oriented architecture for a simpler user/developer experience, while continuous integration enforces code quality and automated tests validate scientific results. System-agnostic packaging via PyPI, conda-forge, and Docker removes platform constraints and eases dissemination. UQpy Version 4.2 introduces a new Scientific Machine Learning Module (SciML) module that extends UQpy into machine-learning workflows. Built on PyTorch, it provides custom layers, neural networks, and trainers compatible with?torch 2.2.2, designed for “plug-and-play” use within existing PyTorch code. The module effectively extends PyTorch with UQ capabilities for both neural networks and?neural operators, including Bayesian implementations of common layers and the associated training procedures to learn their parameters. A flexible, object-oriented interface lets users define?Fourier Neural Operators (FNOs),?Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets), and?U-shaped neural operators, enabling robust UQ in modern scientific ML models.
Authors
Dimitris Giovanis, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., dgiovan1@jhu.edu
Ponkrshnan Thiagarajan, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., ponkrshnan@jhu.edu
Michael D. Shields, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., michael.shields@jhu.edu

talks.title: Dakota: Software for Black-box UQ and Optimization
talks.abstract:
.
Dakota is an open-source and widely used software package for performing uncertainty quantification, optimization, sensitivity analysis, and model calibration on black box computational simulations. Begun as an internal research project at Sandia National Laboratories almost 30 years ago, we estimate it now has thousands of users worldwide in academia, government, and industry.
This talk will provide a brief introduction to and review of Dakota’s history and capabilities. Particular attention is given to recent developments in algorithms and in Dakota’s general usability, especially in multifidelity and multilevel sampling methods.
In addition, nascent efforts to refresh Dakota’s architecture and user interface will be described.

sessions.title: Robust and Scalable Approaches to Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design - Part II of II

talks.title: Batch-based Optimal Experimental Design in Bayesian Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Tapio Helin
speakers.affiliation: Lappeenranta University of Technology, Finland; Andrew Duncan, Imperial College London, United Kingdom

talks.abstract:
.
Optimal experimental design (OED) aims to optimize the measurement setting of a problem so that the information obtained from observed variables is maximized.  In this presentation, we adopt the Bayesian approach to OED and consider batch-based OED for inverse problems. The aim in batch-based OED is to optimize several design variables simultaneously. Optimizing multiple design variables at once is beneficial in situations where multiple measurements can be carried out at once or the next measurement is not immediately available.  In this presentation, we present a numerical method for the batch-based OED optimization problem and also consider related theoretical aspects.
Authors
Sofia Mäkinen, Lappeenranta University of Technology, Finland, sofia.makinen@lut.fi

talks.title: Non-Intrusive Optimal Experimental Design for Large-Scale Nonlinear Bayesian Inverse Problems Using a Bayesian Approximation Error Approach
talks.abstract:
.
We consider optimal experimental design (OED) for nonlinear inverse problems within the Bayesian framework. We propose an approach based on computing an off-line linearisation to the associated forward problem and then optimizing the experimental design. Replacing an accurate model with a linear surrogate, while justified for certain problems, typically leads to incorrect posteriors and sub-optimal designs if the model discrepancy is ignored. We propose the use of Bayesian approximation error (BAE) approach to account for this while formulating an A-optimal design objective for sensor selection that is aware of the model error. We exploit a key invariance property which leads to a particularly straightforward approach which naturally accommodates marginal OED and also non-differentiable forward models. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on several PDE-governed examples. This is joint work with K. Koval
Authors
Ruanui Nicholson, University of Auckland, New Zealand, ruanui.nicholson@auckland.ac.nz
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Optimal Experimental Design of Bayesian Inverse Problems with Multi Sensor Data: Formulation and Algorithms
talks.abstract:
.
The sensor placement problem is among the most fundamental questions in optimal experimental design for Bayesian inverse problems.  In the common setup, each sensor is identical in terms of quality and cost, and thus the problem is typically constrained by just the total number of sensors. We explore the possibility of different sensor types with varying costs. The resulting optimal experiment design problem is a knapsack constrained optimization problem. We formulate the D-optimal design problem and present a randomized algorithm for solving the it in the case of a linear inverse problem.  In practice, this algorithm has noticeable speed ups against the deterministic greedy algorithm at the expense of a reasonable decrease in the solution quality. For nonlinear inverse problems, we utilize a Bayesian Approximation Error (BAE) approach.
Authors
Steven Maio, North Carolina State University, U.S., smaio@ncsu.edu

talks.title: Approximation of Differential Entropy in Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design

speakers.name: Tapio Helin
speakers.affiliation: Lappeenranta University of Technology, Finland; Nuutti Hyvönen

talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian optimal experimental design provides a framework for selecting experiments that maximize obtained information. We focus on estimating the expected information gain in the setting where the differential entropy of the likelihood is either independent of the design or can be evaluated explicitly. This reduces the problem to maximum entropy estimation. Our study is motivated by large-scale inference problems, such as inverse problems, where the computational cost is dominated by expensive likelihood evaluations. We propose a computational approach in which the evidence density is approximated by a Monte Carlo or quasi-Monte Carlo surrogate, while the differential entropy is evaluated using standard methods without additional likelihood evaluations. We prove that this strategy achieves convergence rates that are comparable to state-of-the-art methods for full expected information gain estimation, particularly when the cost of entropy evaluation is negligible. Our approach relies only on mild smoothness of the forward map and avoids strong technical assumptions. We present numerical experiments that support our theoretical findings.

sessions.title: Recent Advances in Inverse Methods for Complex Systems under Uncertainty - Part II of II

talks.title: Projected Stein Variational Inference for Large-Scale Bayesian Inverse Problems Using Derivative Informed Neural Operators
talks.abstract:
.
Bayesian inversion of parametric PDEs is a powerful tool for quantifying uncertainty in scientific models, but its application is often limited by the high cost of repeated PDE solves in high-dimensional settings. We present a surrogate-assisted projected Stein variational gradient descent (pSVGD) framework that addresses these challenges. Our approach integrates data-driven model reduction with a derivative-informed neural operator that learns the PDE solution operator in reduced space, amortizing the cost of PDE solves across inference iterations. By combining parameter and solution projections with accurate, derivative-informed surrogate training, we obtain efficient and reliable score function evaluations for particle updates. Theoretical error bounds provide control of surrogate accuracy, while numerical results demonstrate substantial computational savings without loss of inference fidelity. This framework opens the door to fast and scalable Bayesian inference for complex PDE models with high-dimensional parameters.
Authors
Benjamin A. Burns, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., bburns46@gatech.edu
Yuan Qiu, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., yuan.qiu@gatech.edu
Peng Chen, Georgia Institute of Technology, U.S., pchen402@gatech.edu

talks.title: UQ for Synthetic Time Series Generated from Multimodal Auxiliary Data Streams

speakers.name: Tian Yu Yen
speakers.affiliation: Jaideep Ray, Amanda Massey, Jason Lund

talks.abstract:
.
Monitoring the health and functioning of physical assets is essential to many industrial and scientific endeavors, but often requires examining data that can be difficult to obtain or potentially cost-prohibitive. Recently, there has been a growth in methods that utilize inexpensive, continuous monitoring tools to obtain alternative data sources as a proxy for such expensive data. By utilizing modern ai/ml approaches, experts seek to create models that can infer and generate synthetic data on key quantities of interest from the fusion of inexpensive, auxiliary sources. However, these black-box models increase the risk of inaccurate quality assessment and inferences without appropriate uncertainty guarantees.
In this talk, we discuss a UQ approach for synthetic time series data generated from auxiliary measurements of a physical ratcheting device. We utilize a conformal predictions approach with our black-box generative model to provide robust UQ for relevant quantities of interest. Comparisons with experimental results are discussed.

talks.title: Bayesian Optimization for Efficient Assimilation and Uncertainty Quantification of Wall-Pressure Measurements in Hypersonic Flow over a Cone
talks.abstract:
.
Data assimilation involves solving an inverse problem to identify the state of a dynamical system from experimental measurements. Solving such inverse problems for complex phenomena, such as transitional hypersonic flows, is computationally costly using direct numerical simulations (DNS). Surrogate models could alleviate the computational costs. However, data generation to train the surrogate models still involves performing expensive DNS. In this work, we implement an active learning (AL) framework and a Bayesian optimization (BO) framework to reduce the computational cost of the data assimilation problem to identify the inflow disturbances for a Mach 6, transitional boundary layer flow over a cone from experimental measurements.  The goal of the AL framework is to learn the surrogate model at a given accuracy with the least number of training data points. The AL framework selects the most informative points to train the surrogate model at every iteration. As such, the number of DNS required to generate the training data is reduced, resulting in a significant reduction of the computational cost. Once the surrogate model is trained using the AL framework, it can be used to solve the inverse problems of interest. In contrast, the goal of the BO framework is to solve a given inverse problem in the smallest number of iterations possible. We demonstrate the computational benefits of the proposed methods and quantify uncertainties in the inversely identified inflow disturbances.
Authors
Ponkrshnan Thiagarajan, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., ponkrshnan@jhu.edu
Michael D. Shields, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., michael.shields@jhu.edu
Tamer A. Zaki, Johns Hopkins University, U.S., t.zaki@jhu.edu

talks.title: Goal-Oriented Model Construction for Multifidelity Approximate Control Variate Estimation

speakers.name: Anthony Gruber
speakers.affiliation: Gianluca Geraci, Patrick J. Blonigan, Eric Parish

talks.abstract:
.
Multifidelity methods for uncertainty quantification are critical for achieving optimal bounds on estimator variance under resource constraints.  When a variety of low-fidelity models are available, approaches such as multifidelity Monte Carlo (MFMC) and approximate control variates (ACV) often achieve orders of magnitude lower variance than standard Monte Carlo estimation for the same computational budget.  However, most low-fidelity model ensembles are subject to additional resource constraints largely ignored by this process.  This talk considers the use of projection-based reduced order models (ROMs) in a multifidelity ACV workflow, where the information cost of training these ROMs is included in the resulting optimization.  By balancing training and sampling costs of the model ensemble, the proposed method automates the construction of ROMs according to their predicted utility in multifidelity estimation.  Results on exemplars from high-speed flows, including the HiFIRE-1 test vehicle, show reduced variance of greater than one order-of-magnitude from this procedure, despite using imperfect estimates of unconstructed ROM statistics.

sessions.title: Inference and Uncertainty Quantification in Mathematical Physiology and Medicine - Part II of II

talks.title: Learned Hemodynamic Coupling Inference in Resting-State Functional MRI
talks.abstract:
.
Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) provides an indirect measurement of neuronal activity via hemodynamic responses that vary across brain regions and individuals. Ignoring this hemodynamic variability can bias downstream connectivity estimates. Furthermore, the hemodynamic parameters themselves may serve as important imaging biomarkers. Estimating spatially varying hemodynamics from resting-state fMRI (rsfMRI) is therefore an important but challenging blind inverse problem, since both the latent neural activity and the hemodynamic coupling are unknown. In this work, we propose a methodology for inferring hemodynamic coupling on the cortical surface from rsfMRI. Our approach avoids the highly unstable joint recovery of neural activity and hemodynamics by marginalizing out the latent neural signal and basing inference on the resulting marginal likelihood. To enable scalable, high-resolution estimation, we employ a deep neural network combined with conditional normalizing flows to accurately approximate this intractable marginal likelihood, while enforcing spatial coherence through priors defined on the cortical surface that admit sparse representations. Uncertainty in the hemodynamic estimates is quantified via a double-bootstrap procedure. The proposed approach is extensively validated using synthetic data and real fMRI datasets, demonstrating clear improvements over current methods for hemodynamic estimation and downstream connectivity analysis.
Authors
William Consagra, University of South Carolina, U.S., consagra@mailbox.sc.edu

talks.title: Navigating the Optogenetic Landscape: How Theory and Experiment Reveal Rules of Recurrent Cortical Computation
talks.abstract:
.
Most neocortical synapses are recurrent, yet the functional logic governing when recurrence promotes cooperation versus competition among neuronal ensembles is poorly understood. Multiphoton holographic optogenetics offers a novel approach to this question, enabling precise activation of defined neuron ensembles in primary visual cortex (V1) without confounding external stimuli. However, these experiments lead to a vast parameter space, as ensemble size, spatial configuration, and tuning composition can all be varied independently.
Here, we combined targeted optogenetic perturbations with computational modeling in an iterative framework. Experiments show that spatial arrangement and stimulus preference of activated ensembles shape network responses. Compact, co-tuned ensembles recruit nearby iso-tuned neurons while suppressing others. These observations motivated a computational model incorporating local excitatory connectivity and selective inhibition. The model recapitulated findings and generated new predictions about the trade-off between excitatory and suppressive pathways, which were tested experimentally. Combining in vivo perturbation and in silico modeling clarifies how recurrent cortical circuits selectively amplify or suppress activity depending on the precise pattern of co-active neurons, demonstrating how theory-experiment dialogue can extract organizing principles from high-dimensional experimental landscapes.
Authors
Gregory Handy, University of Minnesota, U.S., ghandy@umn.edu

sessions.title: Advanced UQ Approaches for Complex PDE and Coupled Systems

talks.title: Joint Parameter and State Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Ice Sheet Inverse Problems

speakers.name: Susan Minkoff
speakers.affiliation: Brookhaven National Laboratory, U.S.; Preskella Mrad, University of Texas at Dallas, U.S.; Noémi Petra, University of California, Merced, U.S.; Benjamin Peherstorfer, Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, New York University, U.S.; Tiangang Cui, University of Sydney, Australia; Ki-Tae Kim, University of California, Merced, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Solving large-scale Bayesian inverse problems governed by complex PDEs suffers from the twin difficulties of the high-dimensionality of the uncertain input parameters and the cost of solving computationally expensive forward models. We present a framework for joint parameter and state dimension reduction that mitigates these computational challenges.  To reduce the parameter dimension we leverage local sensitivity of the data to parameters, the smoothing properties of the forward model, the fact that the data contain limited information about the infinite-dimensional parameter field, and the covariance structure of the prior. The method identifies a likelihood-informed parameter subspace that captures where the change from prior to posterior is most significant. For the state dimension reduction, we employ a proper orthogonal decomposition to reduce the linear part of the forward solve combined with the empirical interpolation method to approximate the nonlinear term in the forward model. We apply this joint input parameter and state space reduction strategy to a Bayesian ice sheet inverse problem in which we recover the basal sliding coefficient using partial surface  velocity data. The input space is reduced from 135 to 15 dimensions, and the state  space is reduced to 15 dimensions from its original size of 6179.  Despite this drastic dimension reduction we see excellent agreement between the MAP point for the recovered basal friction coefficient and the true parameter.

talks.title: Exploring Inputs and Suitability of a Model for Prediction from Deep Learning Models of Post Fire Debris Flows
talks.abstract:
.
In this talk we will discuss recent work on developing deep learning-based surrogates for post fire debris flow models and their use in new sites for which training data is sparse. Topography, post-burn conditions, and rainfall are the primary drivers of such flows. We focus here on strategies for effective prediction at new sites using ideas from optimal transport and spectral clustering.

Abani Patra, Tufts University, U.S., abani.patra@tufts.edu

talks.title: VPPE: Application of Scaled Vecchia Approximations to Parallel Partial Emulation
talks.abstract:
.
Computer models or simulators are widely used across scientific fields, but are computationally expensive limiting their use to explore possible scenarios/outcomes. Gaussian process emulators are statistical surrogates that can rapidly approximate the outputs of computer models at untested inputs and enable uncertainty quantification studies. The parallel partial emulation (PPE) was developed to model simulators with vector-valued outputs. While the PPE is adept at fitting simulator data with multidimensional outputs, the time to fit the PPE increases quickly as the number of training runs increases. The Scaled Vecchia approximation, a fast approximation to multivariate Gaussian likelihoods, makes fitting Gaussian process emulators with large training datasets tractable. Here we introduce the Vecchia Parallel Partial Emulation (VPPE) that utilizes the Scaled Vecchia approximation within the PPE framework to allow for parallel partial emulation with larger training datasets. The VPPE is applied to three computer experiments, a synthetic data set, a hydrology model, and a volcanic flow model, yielding comparable predictive accuracy to the PPE at a fraction of the runtime.
Authors
Joshua Seidman, Marquette University, U.S., josh.seidman@marquette.edu
UQ26 Home 2026

talks.title: Developing a Field-Scale Digital Twin for the Land-Use Response to Agricultural and Environmental Policies in the UK and Giving It to Policymakers
talks.abstract:
.
Understanding the impacts of land-use change in a single field requires well calibrated predictive models for the existing land-use and the potential. Policymakers must hit statutory environmental targets in carbon sequestration, reversing biodiversity loss, water quality and food/energy security. Their use of the land to achieve these goals is limited by the fact that they don’t own the land and can only offer subsidies to the few hundred thousand farmers that do. Our group tries to offer effective decision support to policymakers, model all the current and possible future uses of all fields in all farms in the UK at the same time, quantify uncertainty in the response of the farmers and hence the landscape to any particular policy, and wrap all of that in tools fast enough to enable policymakers to explore their options. Is that even possible? In this talk we look at our attempts to deliver this kind of UQ to the UK government in the last 12 months.
Authors
Daniel Williamson, University of Exeter, United Kingdom, d.williamson@exeter.ac.uk
UQ26 Home 2026

sessions.title: Advances in Sequential Inference for Dynamical Systems - Part II of II

talks.title: A Unified Probabilistic Programming Framework for State Estimation and Dynamics Discovery

speakers.name: Matt Levine
speakers.affiliation: Basis, U.S.; Iñigo Urteaga, Basque Center for Applied Mathematics, Spain; Dan Waxman, Basis, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
Many critical forecasting problems in science and engineering involve partially observed systems with unknown underlying dynamics. Despite their importance, current methodologies for learning such systems remain fragmented across specialized, often incompatible computational frameworks and inference paradigms. This talk introduces a novel probabilistic programming framework for sequential inference that provides a unifying modeling and inference abstraction, enabling joint Bayesian estimation of latent states and governing dynamical laws. By integrating state-space models, stochastic differential equations (SDEs), and machine learning components with filter- and smoother-based approximate likelihoods—including ensemble Kalman and particle filters—the framework facilitates a rigorous approach to system identification. We demonstrate the utility of this interface across SDEs, hidden Markov models, and general discrete-time transition systems, showing its capacity to learn dynamics represented by neural networks, mechanistic equations, sparse functional libraries, and hybrid symbolic-neural architectures. Finally, we illustrate how these tools are wrapped in principled Bayesian uncertainty quantification and model comparison to ensure robust, auditable dynamical forecasting.
Authors
Matt Levine, Basis, U.S., matt@basis.ai
Iñigo Urteaga, Basque Center for Applied Mathematics, Spain, iurteaga@bcamath.org
Dan Waxman, Basis, U.S., dan@basis.ai
