talks.title: PyDPF: A Python Package for Differentiable Particle Filters

speakers.name: John-Joseph Brady
speakers.affiliation: King's College London, United Kingdom; Benjamin Cox, University of Edinburgh, United Kingdom; Yunpeng Li , King's College London, United Kingdom; Víctor Elvira, University of Edinburgh, United Kingdom

talks.abstract:
.
State-space models (SSMs) are a widely used tool in time series analysis. In the complex systems that arise from real-world data, it is common to employ particle filtering (PF), an efficient Monte Carlo method for estimating the hidden state corresponding to a sequence of observations. Applying particle filtering requires specifying both the parametric form and the parameters of the system, which are often unknown and must be estimated. Gradient-based optimisation techniques cannot be applied directly to standard particle filters, as the filters themselves are not differentiable. However, several recently proposed methods modify the resampling step to make particle filtering differentiable. In this talk, we present an implementation of several such differentiable particle filters (DPFs) with a unified API built on the popular PyTorch framework. Our implementation makes these algorithms easily accessible to a broader research community and facilitates straightforward comparison between them. We validate our framework by reproducing experiments from several existing studies and demonstrate how DPFs can be applied to address several common challenges with state space modelling.
Authors
John-Joseph Brady, King's College London, United Kingdom, john-joseph.brady@kcl.ac.uk
Benjamin Cox, University of Edinburgh, United Kingdom, benjamin.cox@ed.ac.uk
Yunpeng Li, King's College London, United Kingdom, yunpeng.li@kcl.ac.uk
Víctor Elvira, University of Edinburgh, United Kingdom, victor.elvira@ed.ac.uk

talks.title: GP4SP: Online and Sequential Inference of Gaussian Processes for Signal Processing

speakers.name: Daniel Waxman
speakers.affiliation: Stony Brook University, U.S.; Fernando Llorente, Brookhaven National Laboratory, U.S.; Petar Djuric, Stony Brook University, U.S.

talks.abstract:
.
The proliferation of capable and efficient machine learning (ML) models marks one of the strongest methodological shocks to signal processing (SP) in its nearly 100-year history. ML models allow complex, nonlinear, and accurate SP systems, yet a common hurdle in adapting them is the necessity of sequential inference, which differs both theoretically and methodologically from the usual train/test-split batch paradigm. Gaussian processes (GPs) are a flexible yet principled framework to model random functions, whose influence has naturally extended to SP as statistical and ML methods become increasingly central. We provide a self-contained, tutorial-style overview of GPs, with particular focus on recent methodological advances in the sequential, incremental, or streaming inference of GPs, and on sequential inference using GPs. We introduce these techniques from a signal-processing perspective while bridging to recent advances in ML. Many of the developments we survey have direct applications to state-space modeling, sequential regression and forecasting, anomaly detection in time series, sequential Bayesian optimization, adaptive and active sensing, and sequential detection and decision-making. By unifying these advances under a signal-processing lens, we aim to equip practitioners with practical tools and a coherent roadmap for deploying sequential GP models in real-world systems.

In this talk, I will survey our group's work on the development of stochastic data-driven reduced order models (ROMs) for turbulent flows.  In particular, I will discuss the construction of ROM closures and stabilizations that leverage data-driven modeling, spatial filtering, stochastic modeling, and data assimilation.  The new ROMs will be assessed in the numerical simulation of challenging test problems that are centered around chaotic fluid flows, such as the turbulent channel flow and the quasi-geostrophic equations that model the large scale ocean circulation.
Authors
Traian Iliescu, Virginia Tech, U.S., iliescu@vt.edu
